definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S is justified in believing that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
counterexample: A man glances at a clock on a public building and forms the belief that it is exactly two o'clock. The clock has, in fact, stopped twelve hours previously, yet by sheer coincidence the man happens to look at precisely two o'clock, so his belief is true and, given the ordinary reliability of public clocks, justified. Nevertheless, one would hesitate to say he knows the time.
missing_condition: The analysis omits any requirement that the justification be appropriately connected to the fact which makes the belief true, thereby permitting cases of epistemic luck to satisfy all three conditions.
passage: One of the most venerable analyses in the theory of knowledge holds that knowledge is nothing other than justified true belief. The proposal may be stated thus: a subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S is justified in believing that p. The appeal of this tripartite analysis is considerable, for each condition appears individually necessary and the conjunction appears sufficient. Truth is plainly required, since one cannot properly be said to know what is false; belief is required, since knowledge is at least a species of assent; and justification is required to distinguish knowledge from mere lucky guessing. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man walking through the town square glances at the large clock mounted upon the post office and reads the time as two o'clock. He thereupon forms the belief that it is two o'clock. The clock has, however, been stopped for exactly twelve hours, having broken at two o'clock the previous morning. By a remarkable coincidence, the man happens to consult the clock at precisely two o'clock in the afternoon, so that his belief is true. Moreover, he is justified in this belief, for he has no reason to suspect the clock is broken, and one is ordinarily entitled to trust the accuracy of public timepieces. Nevertheless, it appears that we should deny that the man knows it is two o'clock, for his being right is a matter of pure accident. The case suggests that justified true belief, whilst perhaps necessary for knowledge, is not sufficient.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) S's justification does not essentially depend upon any false lemma.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
S's justification does not essentially depend upon any false lemma
counterexample: A man believes his colleague owns a Ford motorcar because he has seen him driving one each morning. From this he infers that someone in his office owns a Ford. In fact the colleague's Ford was destroyed last week, but another colleague, quite unknown to the first man, has just purchased a Ford. His belief is true and justified, yet it was inferred from the false lemma that his original colleague owns a Ford.
missing_condition: The no-false-lemma requirement may be too restrictive, for there exist cases in which a belief is justified through a false intermediary yet still constitutes knowledge, provided the falsehood is inessential to the broader evidential support.
passage: It has been proposed that one may rescue the classical analysis of knowledge by supplementing it with a fourth condition, namely that the subject's justification must not rest upon any false lemma. The analysis thus runs: S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) S's justification does not essentially depend upon any false proposition. The motivation for this addition is clear enough, for many of the troublesome cases in the literature involve a subject who reasons correctly from a false premiss to a conclusion which happens, by good fortune, to be true. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis in a different direction. Suppose a man, whom we shall call Jones, believes that his colleague Smith owns a Ford motorcar, having observed Smith driving a Ford to the office every morning for the past year. From this belief Jones deduces that at least one person in the office owns a Ford. Now it transpires that Smith's Ford was destroyed in an accident a week ago, and Smith has since been driving a hired vehicle. However, entirely unknown to Jones, another colleague, Brown, has just that morning purchased a Ford. Jones's belief that someone in the office owns a Ford is true, and his justification proceeds through the false lemma that Smith owns a Ford. The no-false-lemma condition correctly excludes this case. Yet one might observe that the condition is perhaps too stringent, for there appear to be instances where a minor falsehood in one's reasoning does not genuinely undermine the epistemic credentials of one's belief, and one would still wish to ascribe knowledge despite the presence of such an inessential falsity in the inferential chain.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is justified by evidence which, taken together, entails or renders highly probable that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p is justified by evidence which entails or renders highly probable that p
counterexample: A farmer looks across a field and sees what appears to be a sheep. She forms the justified belief that there is a sheep in the field. In fact what she sees is a dog disguised in a sheep's fleece, but behind a hillock out of her sight there happens to be a real sheep. Her belief is true and evidentially well supported by her visual experience, yet she does not know there is a sheep in the field.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the evidence upon which the subject relies be connected in the right way to the fact which renders the belief true, rather than to some misleading surrogate.
passage: Consider an analysis which holds that knowledge is true belief supported by evidence of sufficient strength. More precisely, S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is justified by evidence which, taken together, entails or renders highly probable that p. One might think that the evidential condition is strong enough to exclude cases of epistemic fortune, since the evidence must render the proposition highly probable. Yet a case may be constructed which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a farmer, walking along a country lane, looks across a neighbouring field and observes what she takes to be a sheep grazing near a hedge. She thereupon forms the belief that there is a sheep in the field. Her visual experience is perfectly clear, the light is good, and she is an experienced farmer well acquainted with the appearance of sheep; her evidence, one might say, renders it highly probable that there is a sheep in the field. However, it transpires that the object she is looking at is not a sheep at all but rather a large dog which has been dressed in a fleece by a mischievous child. Yet behind a hillock, quite out of the farmer's line of sight, there does in fact stand a genuine sheep. The farmer's belief is thus true, and her evidence is of the sort that would ordinarily suffice for knowledge, yet it appears that she does not know there is a sheep in the field. The difficulty is that her evidence is connected to the wrong object entirely, and the truth of her belief is secured by a fact of which she is wholly unaware.

definition: A subject S has knowledge of a thing X if and only if (i) S is directly acquainted with X, (ii) S's awareness of X is not mediated by any description or inference, and (iii) X is presented to S in immediate experience.
conditions: S is directly acquainted with X
S's awareness of X is not mediated by any description or inference
X is presented to S in immediate experience
counterexample: A physicist knows a great deal about the interior of the sun — its temperature, its composition, its density — yet she has never been and could never be directly acquainted with it. Her knowledge is mediated entirely by descriptions, theoretical inference, and the reports of instruments. Nonetheless it would be perverse to deny that she possesses genuine knowledge of the sun's interior.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the possibility that knowledge may be attained through well-grounded descriptions and inferences, even when direct acquaintance is impossible, thus unduly restricting the scope of genuine knowledge.
passage: There is a distinguished tradition in epistemology which draws a sharp line between knowledge by acquaintance and knowledge by description. On this view, the paradigmatic form of knowledge is that in which the subject stands in a direct, unmediated cognitive relation to the object known. We may formalise this as follows: S has knowledge of a thing X if and only if (i) S is directly acquainted with X, (ii) S's awareness of X is not mediated by any description or inference, and (iii) X is presented to S in immediate experience. The thought is that one knows one's own sense-data, one's present feelings of pain or pleasure, and perhaps certain abstract objects such as universals, in a manner which is fundamentally different from the way in which one knows, say, the atomic weight of carbon or the population of India. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis as an account of knowledge in general. Consider a physicist who has spent her career studying the interior of the sun. She knows that the core temperature is approximately fifteen million degrees, that hydrogen is fused into helium under enormous pressure, and that certain neutrino emissions confirm her theoretical models. She has never been directly acquainted with the sun's interior, nor could she be, and every item of her knowledge is mediated by complex chains of inference, instrumental readings, and the descriptions furnished by physical theory. Yet it would be exceedingly odd to say that she does not know these things. Her knowledge is as well established as any in natural science. The case reveals that knowledge by description, far from being an inferior species of cognition, can attain a degree of certainty and systematicity which rivals or even surpasses what is available through direct acquaintance.

definition: A subject S knows how to do X if and only if (i) S possesses the relevant propositional knowledge that certain procedures constitute the correct way to do X, (ii) S can articulate or state these procedures, and (iii) S's ability to do X is explained by this propositional knowledge.
conditions: S possesses the relevant propositional knowledge that certain procedures constitute the correct way to do X
S can articulate or state these procedures
S's ability to do X is explained by this propositional knowledge
counterexample: A child who has been riding a bicycle skilfully for years cannot state the physical principles governing balance, nor can she articulate any procedure by which she maintains equilibrium. She simply rides. Her knowledge-how is not explained by any propositional knowledge she possesses, for she possesses none of the relevant propositions.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to recognise that knowledge-how may consist in a practical capacity or disposition to act which is not reducible to, nor dependent upon, the possession of any corresponding propositional knowledge.
passage: It has sometimes been supposed that all knowledge is propositional in character, and that what we call knowing how to do something is, at bottom, knowing that certain propositions about the relevant procedures are true. On this intellectualist view, S knows how to do X if and only if (i) S possesses the relevant propositional knowledge that certain procedures constitute the correct way to do X, (ii) S can articulate or state these procedures, and (iii) S's ability to do X is explained by this propositional knowledge. The attractions of this reduction are plain enough, for it promises to unify the theory of knowledge under a single heading. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a child of seven who has been riding a bicycle with great proficiency for several years. If asked how she maintains her balance, she is quite unable to say. She cannot articulate the principle that one must turn the handlebars in the direction of an incipient fall, nor does she know anything of the physics of angular momentum or gyroscopic precession. She simply gets on the bicycle and rides. It appears that her knowledge-how cannot be explained by any propositional knowledge she possesses, for she possesses none of the relevant propositions. The intellectualist must either deny that the child knows how to ride a bicycle, which seems absurd, or else attribute to her some unconscious propositional knowledge, which appears to be an ad hoc manoeuvre designed solely to preserve the theory. The more natural conclusion, as Ryle urged, is that knowledge-how is a distinct species of knowledge, consisting in a capacity or disposition to perform, and not reducible to the contemplation of truths.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) in most nearby possible worlds in which p is true, S believes that p (safety).
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
In most nearby possible worlds in which p is true, S believes that p
counterexample: A man forms the true, justified belief that there is a red barn before him whilst driving through a region in which, unbeknownst to him, the inhabitants have erected hundreds of convincing red barn facades. He happens to be looking at the one genuine barn. In most nearby possible worlds where a real red barn stands in that spot, he would still believe there is a red barn, since the real barn is always there. Yet intuitively he does not know, because he could so easily have been looking at a facade instead.
missing_condition: The safety condition, framed as requiring belief in nearby worlds where p is true, does not capture cases in which the subject could easily have formed the same belief falsely about a different but similar object in the environment.
passage: The safety condition on knowledge has been advanced as a means of handling certain difficulties which beset the classical justified-true-belief analysis. The proposal adds a modal requirement: S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) in most nearby possible worlds in which p is true, S believes that p. The intuition behind safety is that a belief constitutes knowledge only if it could not easily have been false — that is, the subject's belief must be safe from error across a suitable range of nearby possibilities. This condition handles a good many cases with considerable elegance. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man is driving through a rural district and sees before him what appears to be a handsome red barn. He forms the belief that there is a red barn in front of him, and this belief is true, for the structure is indeed a genuine red barn. Moreover, his belief is justified by his visual experience. Now, unknown to the man, the local inhabitants have erected a great many red barn facades throughout the countryside — convincing from the road but mere flat surfaces supported by scaffolding. The man happens, by good fortune, to be looking at the one real barn among dozens of fakes. If we consider nearby possible worlds in which a real red barn stands at that very location, the man would indeed believe there is a red barn there, since the real barn remains in those worlds. The safety condition thus appears to be satisfied. Yet it seems clear that the man does not know there is a red barn before him, for he might so easily have been deceived by one of the many facades. The difficulty is that safety, as formulated, holds fixed the truth of p at the relevant location but ignores the broader epistemic environment in which the subject is operating.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) if p were not true, S would not believe that p (sensitivity), and (iv) if p were true, S would believe that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
If p were not true, S would not believe that p
If p were true, S would believe that p
counterexample: A man knows that he is not a brain in a vat being fed false sensory experiences by a supercomputer. Presumably he does know this. Yet the sensitivity condition fails: in the nearest possible world in which he is a brain in a vat, he would still believe that he is not, since the deception would be perfect. The analysis thus denies knowledge of propositions we ordinarily take ourselves to know.
missing_condition: The sensitivity condition is too demanding in cases involving sceptical hypotheses, for it requires that the subject's belief would change in counterfactual scenarios which, though logically possible, are so remote as to be epistemically irrelevant.
passage: Robert Nozick's tracking theory of knowledge introduces a pair of subjunctive conditionals designed to capture the intuition that a knower's belief must vary appropriately with the truth. On this account, S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) if p were not true, S would not believe that p, and (iv) if p were true, S would believe that p. Condition (iii) is the sensitivity condition, and it is meant to exclude cases in which the subject's belief is insensitive to the facts — cases, that is, in which the subject would go on believing p even if p were false. The theory yields attractive verdicts in many ordinary cases. If one knows that there is a table before one, then had there been no table, one would not have believed there was. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider the proposition that one is not a brain in a vat, sustained in a tank of nutrients and fed elaborate false sensory data by a powerful computer. Most of us, upon reflection, would affirm that we know we are not brains in vats. Yet the sensitivity condition is not satisfied, for in the nearest possible world in which we are brains in vats, we would still believe that we are not, since the deception would be, by hypothesis, indistinguishable from genuine perception. The tracking theory thus has the unhappy consequence of denying us knowledge of a proposition which we seem perfectly entitled to affirm. This result is particularly troublesome because it appears to lend aid and comfort to the sceptic, which is precisely the opposite of what the theory was designed to achieve.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) there is no true proposition d such that, if d were added to S's evidence, S would no longer be justified in believing that p (no undefeated defeaters).
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
There is no true proposition d such that, if d were added to S's evidence, S would no longer be justified in believing that p
counterexample: A doctor correctly diagnoses a patient with a rare blood disorder on the basis of thorough and competent testing. Unknown to the doctor, a distinguished specialist has published a paper — itself based on faulty data — claiming that the test is unreliable for this disorder. The specialist's paper is a true proposition (it exists and says what it says), and were it added to the doctor's evidence, she might well lose justification. Yet the paper is itself misleading, and the doctor's original diagnosis is both correct and well founded.
missing_condition: The defeasibility condition does not distinguish between genuine defeaters, which reveal a real flaw in the subject's epistemic position, and misleading defeaters, which would undermine justified belief without reflecting any actual deficiency in the subject's evidence.
passage: The defeasibility theory of knowledge attempts to supplement the justified-true-belief analysis with the requirement that no undefeated defeater exists for the subject's justification. Formally, S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) there is no true proposition d such that, if d were added to S's total evidence, S would no longer be justified in believing that p. The thought is that knowledge must be robust in the sense that no further truth could undermine the subject's epistemic position. This approach handles many of the familiar problem cases rather elegantly: in the stopped-clock case, for instance, the true proposition that the clock has been stopped for twelve hours would, if added to the subject's evidence, defeat the justification. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a doctor, Dr. Ellis, performs a battery of well-validated tests upon a patient and correctly diagnoses a rare blood disorder. Her evidence is thorough, her reasoning impeccable, and her conclusion true. Now suppose that a distinguished haematologist, Professor Marsh, has recently published a paper in a reputable journal asserting that the very test Dr. Ellis used is unreliable for this particular disorder. Professor Marsh's paper is, however, based upon faulty experimental data, and its conclusion is quite wrong. The existence of the paper is a true proposition, and were it added to Dr. Ellis's evidence, she might well lose confidence in her diagnosis. The defeasibility condition would thus deny her knowledge. Yet this seems incorrect, for Dr. Ellis's diagnosis is both true and perfectly well supported by her evidence. The paper constitutes what one might call a misleading defeater — a true proposition which would undermine justification without reflecting any genuine flaw in the subject's epistemic situation.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief that p is justified, and (iv) there is an appropriate causal connection between the fact that p and S's belief that p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p is justified
There is an appropriate causal connection between the fact that p and S's belief that p
counterexample: A mathematician knows that there are infinitely many prime numbers. Her belief is true and justified by a valid proof. Yet the fact that there are infinitely many primes — a necessary, abstract truth — does not stand in any causal relation to anything, let alone to her belief. The causal condition thus appears inapplicable to knowledge of mathematical and logical truths.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes that all objects of knowledge are the sort of thing that can enter into causal relations, thereby failing to account for knowledge of necessary truths, abstract objects, and mathematical facts.
passage: Alvin Goldman's causal theory of knowledge proposes that the missing ingredient in the traditional analysis is a causal link between the fact known and the belief in question. On this account, S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief that p is justified, and (iv) there is an appropriate causal connection between the fact that p and S's belief that p. The notion of an appropriate causal connection is intentionally left somewhat flexible; it may involve perception, memory, testimony, or various combinations thereof. The theory yields plausible results in many cases of empirical knowledge: one knows that there is a cat on the mat because the presence of the cat causes certain visual experiences which in turn cause one's belief. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a competent mathematician who, having studied Euclid's proof, comes to believe that there are infinitely many prime numbers. Her belief is true, and it is justified by a perfectly valid deductive argument. But the fact that there are infinitely many primes is a necessary truth, holding in every possible world, and necessary truths of this kind do not appear to stand in causal relations to anything. The infinity of the primes is not the sort of fact which can cause events in the physical world; it is an abstract, timeless truth about the structure of the natural numbers. The causal condition is therefore not satisfied, and the theory must deny the mathematician knowledge of one of the most celebrated results in the whole of mathematics. This seems a reductio of the causal requirement, or at least a demonstration that it can apply, at most, to a restricted domain of empirical knowledge.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p was produced by a reliable cognitive process — that is, a process which tends to produce true beliefs in the relevant range of circumstances.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p was produced by a reliable cognitive process
counterexample: A man possesses, unbeknownst to him, a perfectly reliable capacity for clairvoyance regarding the weather in distant cities. He suddenly finds himself believing that it is raining in Calcutta, and it is. The process is reliable, the belief true, yet he has no evidence for or awareness of his clairvoyant power, and the belief strikes him as entirely unfounded. It seems wrong to say he knows it is raining in Calcutta.
missing_condition: The analysis omits any requirement that the subject have some awareness of, or justification for, the reliability of the process producing the belief, thus counting as knowledge beliefs which the subject themselves would regard as baseless.
passage: The reliabilist account of knowledge, in its simplest formulation, replaces the traditional justification condition with a requirement concerning the reliability of the belief-producing process. S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p was produced by a reliable cognitive process — one which, across the relevant range of circumstances, tends to produce true beliefs and tends not to produce false ones. The theory has a number of attractive features. It accords with the common-sense thought that perception, memory, and sound reasoning are knowledge-conferring precisely because they are reliable, and it avoids some of the notorious difficulties attending the notion of justification. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man, whom we shall call Norman, possesses, quite without his knowledge, a perfectly reliable faculty of clairvoyance with respect to the weather in distant cities. One morning Norman finds himself seized by the conviction that it is raining in Calcutta. He has no evidence for this belief; he has not consulted a weather report, spoken to anyone who has been to Calcutta, or made any inference from which this conclusion could be drawn. He is simply struck by the thought and finds himself believing it. As it happens, it is indeed raining in Calcutta, and Norman's clairvoyant faculty has never once produced a false belief. The reliabilist conditions are thus satisfied: the belief is true and was produced by a reliable process. Yet it seems plain that Norman does not know it is raining in Calcutta. From his own perspective, the belief is entirely groundless — he would be quite unable to offer any reason for holding it — and it would be irresponsible for him to act upon it.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief that p is formed through the exercise of an intellectual virtue (a reliable and truth-conducive cognitive competence), and (iv) S's believing truly is attributable to S's exercise of that virtue (apt belief).
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p is formed through the exercise of an intellectual virtue
S's believing truly is attributable to S's exercise of that virtue
counterexample: An expert archer, exercising her skill with perfect form, releases an arrow towards the target. A sudden gust of wind blows the arrow off course, but then a second gust corrects it, and the arrow hits the bullseye. By analogy, a competent geologist exercises her well-honed perceptual and inferential skills to identify a mineral specimen, arriving at a true belief, but only because two errors in her equipment happened to cancel out. Her believing truly is not properly attributable to her competence but to luck.
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately specify what it means for success to be attributable to competence, leaving open cases in which competence is exercised and truth is reached but the connection between the two is fortuitous.
passage: Ernest Sosa's virtue-theoretic account of knowledge introduces the notion of apt belief. On this analysis, S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief that p is formed through the exercise of an intellectual virtue — a stable, reliable, truth-conducive cognitive competence — and (iv) S's believing truly is attributable to S's exercise of that virtue. The metaphor of the skilled archer is frequently employed: just as a shot is apt when it hits the target because of the archer's skill, so a belief is apt when it is true because of the believer's cognitive competence. The theory is elegant and captures much of what is attractive in both reliabilism and traditional virtue epistemology. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, or at least its need for considerable refinement. Consider a geologist, Dr. Harding, who examines a mineral specimen in her laboratory. She brings to bear her extensive training, her well-calibrated perceptual capacities, and her thorough knowledge of mineralogy. She identifies the specimen as olivine, and she is correct. However, it later transpires that two of her instruments were malfunctioning in precisely compensating ways: her spectrometer gave a reading too high by a certain margin, whilst her refractometer gave a reading too low by exactly the same margin. Had either error occurred alone, she would have reached a false conclusion. The errors cancelled out, and she arrived at the truth. Dr. Harding exercised genuine intellectual virtue — her cognitive competence is beyond question — and her belief is true. Yet it seems doubtful that her believing truly is properly attributable to her competence, for it was the fortuitous cancellation of errors, not her skill, which secured the truth of her belief. The case presses us to ask what precisely is meant by attributability, and whether the notion can be made sufficiently precise to do the work the theory requires of it.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) S is certain that p — that is, S has no doubt whatsoever regarding the truth of p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
S is certain that p
counterexample: A juror, after hearing extensive and compelling evidence, believes that the defendant committed the crime. The evidence is strong, the belief is true, and the juror is justified. Yet the juror retains a faint residual doubt — as any honest and reflective person might — and thus does not satisfy the certainty condition. It would be very odd to deny that the juror knows the defendant is guilty merely because she falls short of absolute certainty.
missing_condition: The certainty condition sets the threshold for knowledge unreasonably high, for it conflates the epistemic standard appropriate to mathematical demonstration with that appropriate to empirical inquiry, where some residual doubt is inevitable and entirely compatible with knowledge.
passage: There is a venerable tradition which ties knowledge to certainty. On this view, to know that p is not merely to believe it truly and with justification, but to be entirely free of doubt regarding it. We may state the analysis thus: S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) S is certain that p, where certainty is understood as the complete absence of doubt. The appeal of this analysis lies in the thought that knowledge ought to be something firm and unshakeable — that if one truly knows, one cannot be wrong, and one knows that one cannot be wrong. Descartes, of course, sought to build the edifice of knowledge upon foundations of just this kind. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis for the vast majority of our epistemic lives. Consider a juror in a criminal trial who has heard several weeks of testimony, examined documentary evidence, and listened to the arguments of both counsel. She believes, on the basis of this evidence, that the defendant committed the offence with which he is charged, and her belief is both true and well justified. Yet she retains, as any thoughtful person might, a faint residual uncertainty — not a specific doubt directed at any particular piece of evidence, but the general awareness that human testimony is fallible and that appearances can deceive. The certainty condition would deny her knowledge. Yet this seems plainly wrong: we routinely ascribe knowledge in legal, scientific, and everyday contexts where absolute certainty is neither attainable nor required. To insist upon certainty is to confine knowledge to the narrow domain of logical and mathematical truths, if indeed even those are held with complete psychological certitude.

definition: Knowledge is closed under known entailment: if (i) S knows that p, (ii) S knows that p entails q, and (iii) S competently deduces q from p, then S knows that q.
conditions: S knows that p
S knows that p entails q
S competently deduces q from p
counterexample: One knows that one has hands. One knows that having hands entails that one is not a handless brain in a vat. One competently deduces that one is not a handless brain in a vat. Yet many epistemologists are reluctant to affirm that one thereby knows one is not a brain in a vat, for this conclusion seems to require an independent refutation of the sceptical hypothesis which mere deduction from hand-knowledge cannot supply.
missing_condition: The closure principle does not account for the possibility that knowledge of certain entailed propositions may require a different or stronger epistemic basis than knowledge of the premiss, particularly when the entailed proposition concerns the denial of a sceptical scenario.
passage: The principle of epistemic closure under known entailment is widely regarded as one of the most natural and compelling principles in the theory of knowledge. It states that if S knows that p, and S knows that p entails q, and S competently deduces q from p, then S knows that q. The principle captures the thought that knowledge is preserved under recognised logical consequence: one cannot know the premisses of a valid argument, recognise the argument's validity, draw the conclusion, and yet fail to know the conclusion. Deduction, on this view, is a means of extending one's knowledge. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this principle, or at least the considerable difficulties which attend its unrestricted application. Consider the following. It appears that one knows that one has hands — this is, after all, one of the most commonplace items of knowledge, and any theory which denied it would thereby discredit itself. It is also clear that having hands entails that one is not a handless brain suspended in a vat of nutrients, receiving simulated sensory experiences from a powerful computer. One recognises this entailment and draws the conclusion. By the closure principle, one therefore knows that one is not a brain in a vat. Yet a great many philosophers have found this conclusion deeply uncomfortable. The denial of the sceptical hypothesis seems to require epistemic resources which go beyond what is available in the mere perception of one's hands. The sceptic will urge that one cannot know one is not a brain in a vat, and the closure principle then forces the unwelcome conclusion that one does not know one has hands either. The principle thus appears to generate a tension between our most basic perceptual knowledge and our inability to rule out radical sceptical alternatives.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief tracks the truth of p — that is, across the relevant range of counterfactual situations, S believes that p when and only when p is true, and (iv) S employs a method M which is truth-tracking with respect to p.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief tracks the truth of p across the relevant range of counterfactual situations
S employs a method M which is truth-tracking with respect to p
counterexample: A man uses a particular thermometer to determine the temperature of a liquid. The thermometer, as it happens, is broken and reads the same number regardless of the actual temperature. By coincidence, the liquid is at exactly the temperature the thermometer displays. If we hold fixed the method (consulting this thermometer), the belief does track the truth in nearby worlds where the liquid is at that temperature, since the thermometer always gives that reading. Yet the man plainly does not know the temperature.
missing_condition: The tracking account's reliance on method-relative counterfactuals introduces a problematic degree of freedom in specifying the relevant method, since narrow individuation of the method can render a belief truth-tracking even when it is clearly not knowledge.
passage: The tracking theory of knowledge, developed in its most detailed form by Nozick, holds that a knower's belief must co-vary with the truth in the appropriate counterfactual manner. S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief tracks the truth of p across the relevant range of counterfactual situations — that is, S believes p when p is true and does not believe p when p is false — and (iv) S employs a method M which is truth-tracking with respect to p. The method-relativity is introduced to handle cases in which a subject might arrive at the same belief by different methods in different circumstances. The theory has considerable power in explaining ordinary cases of perceptual knowledge and in capturing the intuition that knowledge requires more than justified true belief. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a man, Mr. Fletcher, wishes to determine the temperature of a chemical solution in his laboratory. He consults a thermometer immersed in the solution and reads it as showing thirty-seven degrees Celsius. He thereupon believes the solution is at thirty-seven degrees, and this belief is true. However, the thermometer is defective: it is stuck permanently at thirty-seven degrees and will display this reading no matter what the actual temperature of the liquid. Mr. Fletcher is simply fortunate that the solution happens to be at precisely thirty-seven degrees. Now, if we individuate his method narrowly as consulting this particular thermometer, then in nearby possible worlds where the solution is at thirty-seven degrees, Mr. Fletcher believes (using this method) that it is at thirty-seven degrees, since the thermometer always gives that reading. The tracking conditions, relativised to this method, appear to be satisfied. Yet it is manifest that Mr. Fletcher does not know the temperature of the solution. The difficulty lies in the theory's sensitivity to how finely one individuates the method, a problem which threatens to render the tracking conditions either trivially easy or impossibly difficult to satisfy.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is rational — that is, it is the belief which a fully rational agent would hold given S's total evidence.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p is rational given S's total evidence
counterexample: A woman has overwhelming evidence that her business partner is honest — years of trustworthy conduct, impeccable references, transparent bookkeeping. It is entirely rational for her to believe he is honest, and he is in fact honest. Yet she forms this belief not on the basis of the evidence but because a fortune-teller told her so, and she is disposed to believe fortune-tellers. Her belief is true and rational relative to her total evidence, but it is not held for the right reasons.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the subject's belief be held on the basis of the evidence which makes it rational, thus permitting cases in which the right belief is held for entirely the wrong reasons.
passage: One might propose that knowledge is simply true rational belief — that is, true belief which conforms to the standards of rationality given the subject's total evidence. The analysis would then run: S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is the belief which a fully rational agent would hold given S's total body of evidence. This proposal has the merit of connecting knowledge closely to the norms of rational inquiry, and it avoids some of the difficulties which attend the notion of justification construed in more traditional terms. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider Mrs. Whitmore, who has been in a business partnership with Mr. Graves for fifteen years. Over that period, Mr. Graves has conducted himself with complete propriety: the accounts have always balanced, the references have been excellent, and Mrs. Whitmore's solicitor has found nothing amiss. Given this body of evidence, it is entirely rational for Mrs. Whitmore to believe that Mr. Graves is honest, and he is in fact honest. However, Mrs. Whitmore's actual reason for believing in his honesty is not this impressive body of evidence at all. Rather, she believes it because a fortune-teller at a seaside resort once told her that her business partner would prove trustworthy, and Mrs. Whitmore places great store by the pronouncements of fortune-tellers. Her belief is true, and it is rational relative to her total evidence, but she does not hold it on the basis of that evidence. She holds it on the basis of a patently unreliable source. It seems wrong to attribute knowledge to Mrs. Whitmore in such a case, for the connection between her evidence and her belief is, as it were, accidental — the right belief is held for entirely the wrong reasons.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S possesses evidence e such that e entails p or renders p certain.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S possesses evidence e such that e entails p or renders p certain
counterexample: A detective has assembled a mass of evidence — fingerprints, motive, opportunity, witness testimony — which renders it overwhelmingly probable that the suspect committed the crime, yet no single item or combination of items entails or renders certain the suspect's guilt. If knowledge requires evidence amounting to certainty, the detective does not know the suspect is guilty, even though any reasonable person would say she does.
missing_condition: The analysis sets the evidential threshold too high by requiring entailment or certainty, thereby excluding the vast range of cases in which knowledge is grounded in strong but non-conclusive evidence.
passage: There is a tradition in epistemology which ties knowledge very tightly to the possession of evidence, understanding evidence in a strong sense as that which entails or renders certain the proposition believed. On this view, S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S possesses evidence e such that e entails p or renders p certain. The thought is that knowledge must be infallible in the sense that the evidence must logically guarantee the truth of the belief. This is an exceedingly stringent requirement, but it has the advantage of explaining why knowledge seems to carry a special authority which mere probable opinion does not. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a detective, Inspector Leigh, who is investigating a case of burglary. She has assembled an impressive array of evidence: the suspect's fingerprints were found at the scene, the suspect had both motive and opportunity, two independent witnesses place him near the premises at the relevant time, and stolen goods were found in his possession. The evidence renders the suspect's guilt overwhelmingly probable, and Inspector Leigh believes, correctly, that he committed the crime. Yet no item of evidence, nor indeed the totality of the evidence, strictly entails the suspect's guilt, for it remains logically possible, however improbable, that the fingerprints were planted, the witnesses mistaken, and the goods innocently acquired. If knowledge requires evidence amounting to entailment or certainty, Inspector Leigh does not know that the suspect is guilty. But this seems plainly wrong. We should be very reluctant to say that detectives, historians, scientists, and physicians never know the conclusions of their inquiries merely because their evidence falls short of logical entailment.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) it is not possible for S to be mistaken about p — that is, S's epistemic position with respect to p is infallible.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
It is not possible for S to be mistaken about p
counterexample: A chemist knows that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. Her belief is true and well justified by extensive experimental evidence. Yet it is possible, in the logical sense, that she is mistaken — perhaps her instruments are systematically miscalibrated, or perhaps she is the victim of an elaborate deception. If knowledge requires infallibility, she does not know the composition of water, and indeed no empirical knowledge would be possible at all.
missing_condition: The infallibility condition conflates logical possibility of error with genuine epistemic vulnerability, thereby setting an impossible standard which would eliminate virtually all empirical knowledge.
passage: The infallibilist analysis of knowledge holds that genuine knowledge admits of no possibility of error. S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) it is not possible for S to be mistaken about p. The appeal of this position is not difficult to appreciate, for there is something deeply attractive in the thought that knowledge, properly so called, should be a matter upon which one simply cannot be wrong. If one knows, one's epistemic position is, as it were, unassailable. The great rationalist philosophers were drawn to this ideal, and it continues to exert a powerful influence upon philosophical thinking about knowledge. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider Dr. Ashworth, a skilled analytical chemist, who has spent her career investigating the composition of water. She knows — or so we should ordinarily say — that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. Her belief rests upon decades of meticulous experimentation, the convergent testimony of innumerable other chemists, and the extraordinary predictive success of the chemical theory in which this fact is embedded. Nevertheless, it is logically possible that she is mistaken. Perhaps her instruments have been systematically miscalibrated throughout her career; perhaps the chemical theory, despite its success, is fundamentally in error; perhaps she is, in some far-fetched but logically coherent scenario, being deceived. The infallibility condition is not satisfied, and the analysis must deny that Dr. Ashworth knows the composition of water. Indeed, the condition would deny knowledge of virtually every empirical proposition, confining genuine knowledge to the domain of logical and mathematical truths, if even those are immune to every conceivable form of error.

definition: A subject S knows that p on the basis of testimony if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) a speaker T has testified that p, (iv) T knows that p, and (v) S's belief is formed on the basis of T's testimony.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
A speaker T has testified that p
T knows that p
S's belief is formed on the basis of T's testimony
counterexample: A traveller asks a stranger for directions to the railway station. The stranger, who is a compulsive liar, intends to give false directions but makes an error and inadvertently gives correct ones. The traveller follows the directions and arrives at the station. The stranger testified, the belief is true, and it was formed on the basis of testimony, but the stranger did not know the correct route — she thought she was lying. The traveller has a true belief from testimony, yet the conditions are satisfied only vacuously.
missing_condition: The analysis requires that the testifier know that p, but this condition is too strong in one direction and insufficient in another: it excludes cases of warranted testimony from speakers who have justified true belief but not knowledge, and it does not address cases where the testifier's sincerity and competence are compromised yet truth is transmitted by accident.
passage: Knowledge by testimony is pervasive in human epistemic life: the greater part of what any individual knows is learned from the reports of others. A natural analysis of testimonial knowledge runs as follows: S knows that p on the basis of testimony if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) a speaker T has testified that p, (iv) T knows that p, and (v) S's belief is formed on the basis of T's testimony. The requirement that the testifier know that p is intended to ensure the reliability of the testimonial chain: knowledge begets knowledge, as it were, through sincere and competent assertion. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a traveller in an unfamiliar city asks a stranger for directions to the railway station. The stranger is, as it happens, a compulsive liar who takes pleasure in misdirecting visitors. She intends to send the traveller in the wrong direction. However, the stranger is herself confused about the layout of the city, and her attempt to give false directions results, quite by accident, in her stating the correct route to the station. The traveller follows the directions and arrives at the station without difficulty. Now, the stranger has testified that the station is in such-and-such a direction, the proposition is true, and the traveller's belief is formed on the basis of this testimony. But the stranger did not know the correct route — she believed she was giving incorrect directions. The condition that T know that p is not satisfied, and the analysis rightly denies the traveller knowledge. Yet one might feel uneasy, for the traveller is in precisely the same epistemic position as a traveller who receives correct directions from an honest and well-informed stranger. The case reveals a deeper difficulty about whether and when testimony can transmit knowledge, and whether the conditions on the testifier are too stringent or not stringent enough.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief that p is justified, and (iv) S's justification rests upon a chain of reasons which terminates in self-justifying (foundational) beliefs.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p is justified
S's justification rests upon a chain of reasons which terminates in self-justifying beliefs
counterexample: A meteorologist knows that a storm is approaching. Her belief is justified by satellite data, which is justified by the reliability of the instruments, which is justified by calibration procedures, which are justified by physical theory, which is itself justified in part by the observational data which the instruments collect. At no point does the chain of justification terminate in a self-evident or self-justifying belief; instead, it forms a mutually supporting web. Yet the meteorologist plainly knows a storm is approaching.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes that justification must have a linear, foundational structure, but fails to accommodate the possibility that justification may be holistic and mutually supporting, without any single stratum of beliefs serving as an indubitable foundation.
passage: The regress problem for knowledge asks how any belief can be justified, given that every justification appears to require a further justification, generating either an infinite regress, a circle, or a termination in beliefs which are themselves unjustified. The foundationalist response to this problem holds that the regress terminates in a class of foundational beliefs which are self-justifying — beliefs, that is, which do not require justification from any further source. On this view, S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's belief that p is justified, and (iv) S's justification rests upon a chain of reasons which terminates in self-justifying beliefs. Candidates for such foundational beliefs have included reports of immediate sensory experience, self-evident logical truths, and certain introspective judgements. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a meteorologist, Dr. Parsons, who knows that a severe storm is approaching the coast. Her belief is justified by the latest satellite imagery, which shows a distinctive cloud formation. The reliability of the satellite imagery is in turn justified by the known accuracy of the instruments, which is justified by the calibration procedures applied to them, which are justified by the physical theories upon which those procedures rest, which are themselves justified in part by the very sorts of observational data that instruments like the satellite collect. The chain of justification does not terminate in any self-evident or self-justifying belief; rather, it forms a complex web of mutually supporting propositions, each of which draws some of its warrant from the others. Dr. Parsons plainly knows that a storm is approaching, yet her knowledge does not rest upon foundations in the required sense. The case suggests that the foundationalist picture of justification is at best a simplification, and that the actual structure of empirical knowledge may be more akin to a web or a net than to an edifice built upon unshakeable pillars.

definition: A subject S has animal knowledge that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is formed by a competence exercised in appropriate conditions, yielding a true belief (first-order apt belief). Reflective knowledge requires additionally (iv) that S has a justified higher-order belief that their first-order belief is apt.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief is formed by a competence exercised in appropriate conditions
For reflective knowledge: S has a justified higher-order belief that their first-order belief is apt
counterexample: A young child perceives that a dog is in the garden. Her perceptual faculties are functioning well, the conditions are suitable, and her belief is true and apt. She has animal knowledge. Yet she has no concept of perception, no understanding of the reliability of her senses, and no capacity for the sort of epistemic self-assessment required for a justified higher-order belief about her own competence. If reflective knowledge is the higher grade, then children and unreflective adults possess only the lower grade, yet the distinction between the two grades may not carve at a philosophically significant joint.
missing_condition: The two-tiered analysis does not adequately address cases in which the distinction between animal and reflective knowledge appears to be one of degree rather than kind, nor does it explain why reflective knowledge should be regarded as epistemically superior when the first-order competence is already reliable.
passage: Ernest Sosa distinguishes between two grades of knowledge, which he terms animal knowledge and reflective knowledge. Animal knowledge is first-order apt belief: S has animal knowledge that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is formed by a competence exercised in appropriate conditions, yielding a true belief. Reflective knowledge adds a further layer: (iv) S must possess a justified higher-order belief that her first-order belief is apt — that is, she must appreciate, from a suitably informed perspective, that her belief was produced by a reliable competence operating in favourable conditions. The distinction is meant to capture the thought that there is a higher-grade knowledge which involves not merely getting things right but knowing that one is getting things right. Yet one might observe a case which reveals certain difficulties in this analysis. Consider a very young child, perhaps three years of age, who looks out of the window and sees a dog in the garden. Her perceptual faculties are functioning normally, the dog is clearly visible in good light, and she forms the true belief that there is a dog in the garden. She has animal knowledge, for her belief is apt — produced by a reliable perceptual competence in appropriate conditions. But she has no concept of perception as such, no understanding of the conditions under which eyesight is reliable, and no capacity for the sort of epistemic self-reflection which the higher-order condition demands. She cannot form any justified belief about the aptness of her first-order belief, for she lacks the conceptual resources to do so. If reflective knowledge is genuinely the superior grade, then the child possesses only the inferior variety. Yet one might question whether the distinction between the two grades marks a genuine epistemic difference of the kind required. The child's belief is every bit as true, every bit as reliably formed, and every bit as firmly held as the belief of a sophisticated epistemologist who can additionally articulate the grounds of her own reliability.

definition: A subject S has propositional knowledge that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S accepts that p — where acceptance is a matter of adopting p as a premiss for reasoning and action, (iii) S is justified in accepting that p, and (iv) S's acceptance that p is based on adequate grounds.
conditions: p is true
S accepts that p as a premiss for reasoning and action
S is justified in accepting that p
S's acceptance that p is based on adequate grounds
counterexample: A scientist, for practical purposes, accepts that Newtonian mechanics is true when calculating the trajectory of a bridge-building project. She adopts it as a premiss for reasoning and action, and her grounds are adequate for engineering purposes. Yet she does not believe Newtonian mechanics is strictly true — she knows it is superseded by relativity. Her acceptance is pragmatic, not doxastic, and it seems wrong to say she knows Newtonian mechanics is true merely because she accepts it for practical purposes.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates acceptance, which may be a pragmatic attitude adopted for the purposes of reasoning and action, with genuine belief, which involves a commitment to the truth of the proposition, thereby permitting cases of merely instrumental acceptance to count as knowledge.
passage: Some epistemologists have proposed that the doxastic component of knowledge should be understood not in terms of belief but in terms of acceptance, where acceptance is a matter of adopting a proposition as a premiss for one's reasoning and as a basis for action. On this view, S has propositional knowledge that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S accepts that p — that is, S treats p as a premiss in deliberation and acts accordingly, (iii) S is justified in accepting that p, and (iv) S's acceptance is based on adequate grounds. The proposal has certain advantages: it accommodates cases in which knowledge seems to involve a practical commitment to a proposition rather than a mere passive assent, and it avoids some of the difficulties attending the notion of belief as a purely psychological state. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider Dr. Langton, a physicist who is engaged in designing a bridge. For the purposes of her calculations, she accepts Newtonian mechanics as true. She treats Newton's laws as premisses in her reasoning, she bases her engineering decisions upon them, and her grounds for doing so are entirely adequate — Newtonian mechanics yields predictions of extraordinary accuracy at the scales relevant to bridge construction. Moreover, Newtonian mechanics gives correct results for the stresses and loads she calculates, so condition (i) is at least approximately satisfied. Yet Dr. Langton does not believe that Newtonian mechanics is strictly true. She is well aware that it has been superseded by the general theory of relativity and that its predictions, however accurate for practical purposes, are not exactly right. Her acceptance is purely pragmatic: she adopts Newtonian mechanics as a useful approximation, not as a true account of physical reality. It seems wrong to say that Dr. Langton knows Newtonian mechanics is true, for she herself would deny it. The case suggests that acceptance and belief are distinct attitudes, and that knowledge requires the latter, not merely the former.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) there exists no genuine defeater for S's belief that p — that is, no true proposition which, if believed by S, would render S's belief that p unjustified or irrational.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
There exists no genuine defeater for S's belief that p
counterexample: A student correctly believes, on the basis of her competent reading of a reliable history text, that the Battle of Hastings occurred in 1066. However, her teacher — who is himself confused — has told her, incorrectly, that the text she read contains numerous errors about medieval dates. The teacher's testimony is a true proposition (he did say it), and if believed, it would undermine her justification. Yet the teacher is wrong about the text's reliability. The student's belief is true and undefeated in any genuine sense, but the analysis may count this misleading testimony as a defeater.
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately distinguish between genuine defeaters which reveal a real deficiency in the subject's epistemic position and apparent or misleading defeaters which would undermine justification without reflecting any actual error in the subject's grounds.
passage: A streamlined analysis of knowledge dispenses with an explicit justification condition and holds instead that knowledge is true belief in the absence of defeaters. S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) there exists no genuine defeater for S's belief that p — that is, no true proposition which, if believed by S, would render the belief that p unjustified or irrational. The appeal of this analysis lies in its simplicity and in its capacity to handle many of the standard problem cases: in each such case, there is a true proposition the awareness of which would undermine the subject's justification. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider Miss Barton, a diligent student of medieval history, who reads in a well-regarded textbook that the Battle of Hastings took place in the year 1066. She forms the belief that the Battle of Hastings occurred in 1066, and her belief is of course true. Now, her history teacher, Mr. Fenwick, is under the erroneous impression that the textbook Miss Barton has been reading is riddled with errors concerning medieval dates. He has told her, quite sincerely, that the book cannot be relied upon for dates before the twelfth century. Mr. Fenwick's assertion — that the textbook is unreliable about medieval dates — is a proposition which, if believed by Miss Barton, would undermine her justification for believing that Hastings occurred in 1066. One might therefore think it constitutes a defeater. But Mr. Fenwick is simply wrong about the textbook; it is in fact highly reliable, and Miss Barton's belief is well grounded. The case raises the difficult question of whether Mr. Fenwick's misleading testimony should count as a genuine defeater or merely an apparent one, and the analysis as stated provides no resources for drawing this distinction.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is the output of a cognitive process which is, as a matter of empirical fact, reliable — that is, which produces a high ratio of true beliefs to false beliefs in the actual world.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's belief that p is the output of a reliable cognitive process in the actual world
counterexample: A woman has unknowingly been placed in an environment where nearly all the objects she sees are cleverly disguised fakes. By chance, she looks at the one genuine object — a real vase — and forms the true belief that there is a vase before her. Her perceptual process is, in the actual world at large, highly reliable. Yet in this particular environment, it yields almost entirely false beliefs. It seems wrong to say she knows there is a vase, even though her cognitive process is reliable in general.
missing_condition: The analysis does not specify whether reliability is to be assessed globally, across all environments in which the process operates, or locally, in the particular environment in which the belief is formed, and the two assessments may yield conflicting verdicts.
passage: A further articulation of reliabilism about knowledge holds that knowledge is true belief produced by a cognitive process which is, as an empirical matter, reliable in the actual world. S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's belief that p is the output of a cognitive process which produces a high ratio of true beliefs to false beliefs in the actual world. This formulation avoids some of the difficulties of earlier versions by tying reliability to actual-world performance rather than to performance across possible worlds. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, turning on the question of how the relevant environment is to be specified. Suppose Ms. Cartwright, an ordinary man with excellent eyesight, is unwittingly placed in a room filled with highly convincing replicas of various objects — fake vases, fake books, fake flowers — all manufactured to be visually indistinguishable from the genuine articles. Amid this forest of fakes, there stands one genuine vase. Ms. Cartwright looks at the vase and forms the belief that there is a vase before her. Her belief is true. Moreover, her perceptual system is, in the actual world at large, highly reliable: in the ordinary course of events, her visual perception of medium-sized objects yields true beliefs with great frequency. Yet in this particular environment, her perceptual process is almost entirely unreliable — nearly every belief it would produce about the objects in the room would be false. It seems clear that Ms. Cartwright does not know there is a vase before her. The difficulty is that reliability, assessed globally, is high, whilst reliability assessed locally — in the environment in which the belief is actually formed — is low. The analysis, as stated, does not specify which measure of reliability is relevant, and this ambiguity is not merely a technical lacuna but a substantive philosophical problem.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) it is not the case that S's belief is true merely as a matter of epistemic luck — where epistemic luck is understood as the subject's belief being true in the actual world but false in a wide class of nearby possible worlds.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S is justified in believing that p
It is not the case that S's belief is true merely as a matter of epistemic luck
counterexample: A birdwatcher forms the justified belief that the bird she is observing is a goldfinch, on the basis of its yellow plumage, small size, and conical beak. She is correct. However, the area has recently been populated by a species of canary which is visually indistinguishable from a goldfinch to the naked eye. In many nearby possible worlds, she is looking at a canary and her belief is false. Yet one might argue that the presence of look-alikes does not undermine her knowledge if her discriminatory skills, though unable to distinguish goldfinches from canaries, have in fact latched onto a genuine goldfinch through no environmental luck but through a stable and reliable exercise of competence.
missing_condition: The anti-luck condition, formulated in terms of nearby possible worlds, does not adequately distinguish between cases in which the subject's success is genuinely fortunate and cases in which the modal fragility of the belief is an artefact of the description of the environment rather than a reflection of any deficiency in the subject's epistemic position.
passage: Duncan Pritchard's anti-luck epistemology holds that knowledge is, at its core, non-lucky true belief. The analysis runs: S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) it is not the case that S's belief is true merely as a matter of epistemic luck, where epistemic luck obtains when the subject's belief is true in the actual world but false across a wide class of nearby possible worlds. The anti-luck condition is motivated by the thought that what is distinctive about knowledge, as opposed to mere justified true belief, is precisely its resistance to epistemic fortune. If one's being right is a matter of luck, one does not know. The condition handles many of the familiar problem cases and provides a unifying diagnosis of what goes wrong in each. Yet one might observe a case which reveals certain difficulties in this analysis. Consider Mrs. Aldridge, an experienced birdwatcher, who observes a small yellow bird perched on a branch and forms the justified belief that it is a goldfinch. Her belief is true: the bird is indeed a goldfinch. However, the woodland she is visiting has recently been colonised by a species of canary which is visually indistinguishable from a goldfinch to the unaided eye. In many nearby possible worlds, the bird on the branch is a canary rather than a goldfinch, and Mrs. Aldridge's belief is false. The anti-luck condition would thus deny her knowledge. Yet one might resist this verdict. Mrs. Aldridge exercised her considerable ornithological competence in forming her belief, and her competence led her to the truth. The presence of look-alikes in the environment introduces modal fragility into her belief, but it is not obvious that this fragility reflects any genuine deficiency in her epistemic position. She attended carefully to the bird, noted the relevant features, and formed her judgement on the basis of extensive experience. The case suggests that the anti-luck condition, as formulated, may be too sensitive to the structure of the modal neighbourhood and insufficiently attentive to the quality of the subject's epistemic agency.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, (iii) S's believing truly is a cognitive achievement — that is, S's arriving at the truth is the product of, and creditable to, S's own cognitive agency and effort.
conditions: p is true
S believes that p
S's believing truly is a cognitive achievement creditable to S's own cognitive agency
counterexample: A man with no knowledge of ornithology wanders into a bird sanctuary and, upon seeing a brightly coloured bird, guesses at random that it is a scarlet tanager. He is correct. One might suppose this is no achievement. Yet consider a second case: an expert ornithologist studies the bird carefully and correctly identifies it as a scarlet tanager, but only because the sanctuary has placed an identifying label on the branch which she unconsciously reads. Her success is creditable not to her ornithological competence but to the label. It seems she does not know through her own cognitive achievement, yet she would say she does.
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately specify how to determine when a true belief is genuinely creditable to the subject's cognitive agency, as opposed to being the product of fortuitous environmental support which the subject neither recognises nor controls.
passage: The cognitive achievement account of knowledge holds that what distinguishes knowledge from mere true belief is that knowledge involves a genuine cognitive achievement on the part of the subject. S knows that p if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p, and (iii) S's believing truly is a cognitive achievement — that is, S's arriving at the truth is the product of, and creditable to, S's own cognitive agency and effort. The thought is that knowledge is a species of success through ability, analogous to a skilled archer hitting the target through skill rather than luck. This approach is attractive because it connects knowledge to a broader theory of human achievement and explains why knowledge is valued more highly than mere true belief. Yet one might observe a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider Dr. Hargreaves, an expert ornithologist, who visits a bird sanctuary and observes a brightly coloured bird perched in a tree. She studies the bird with care, notes the colour of its plumage, the shape of its beak, and its distinctive song, and concludes that it is a scarlet tanager. She is correct, and her identification appears to be a paradigmatic cognitive achievement. However, it later transpires that Dr. Hargreaves did not in fact identify the bird by means of her ornithological expertise. The sanctuary had placed a small identifying label on the branch directly below the bird, and Dr. Hargreaves, without consciously realising it, had read the label in her peripheral vision. Her identification was driven not by her competence but by this environmental cue. The true belief is perhaps still creditable to her cognitive system in some broad sense — after all, it was her eyes which read the label — but it is not creditable to the specific cognitive competence which she took herself to be exercising. The case raises the difficult question of what it means for a true belief to be genuinely creditable to the subject's cognitive agency, and whether the notion of cognitive achievement can be made precise enough to serve as the distinguishing mark of knowledge.

definition: A belief is foundationally justified if and only if (i) it is a basic belief, not inferred from any other belief, (ii) it is self-evident to the subject who holds it, and (iii) its self-evidence is sufficient to confer positive epistemic status without the support of further propositions.
conditions: The belief is basic and non-inferential
The belief is self-evident to the subject
Self-evidence alone suffices for positive epistemic status
counterexample: A mathematician grasps that a certain axiom of set theory is self-evident and holds it as basic, yet a newly discovered paradox renders that very axiom inconsistent with other self-evident truths she accepts. Her belief remains basic and self-evident to her, yet it can scarcely be regarded as justified given the known inconsistency.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the self-evident belief must not stand in contradiction with other equally self-evident propositions held by the subject.
passage: One might begin by observing that foundationalism, in its classical guise, offers a remarkably tidy picture of the structure of justified belief. On this account, certain beliefs occupy a privileged epistemic station: they are basic, requiring no inferential support from further propositions, and they are self-evident, carrying their warrant, as it were, upon their face. The foundationalist contends that such self-evidence is wholly sufficient for positive epistemic status, so that one who grasps a basic truth thereby possesses a justified belief without the need for any additional reasons. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a mathematician of considerable ability recognises a certain axiom of set theory as self-evident; she holds it as basic, and her apprehension of its truth is immediate and unmediated. However, a paradox is subsequently discovered which demonstrates that this axiom stands in flat contradiction with another proposition she regards as equally self-evident. It appears that her belief, though basic and self-evident in her own estimation, can no longer be deemed justified, for the presence of a known inconsistency among her foundational commitments undermines the epistemic support that self-evidence was meant to provide. The analysis, then, is incomplete: it fails to require that a self-evident basic belief must cohere, at least negatively, with the subject's other self-evident beliefs.

definition: A belief grounded in sense data is incorrigibly justified if and only if (i) the belief concerns the subject's present sensory experience, (ii) the subject cannot be mistaken about the content of that experience, and (iii) the impossibility of error is itself a sufficient ground for the belief's justification.
conditions: The belief concerns the subject's present sensory experience
The subject cannot be mistaken about the content of the experience
The impossibility of error suffices for justification
counterexample: A patient under the influence of a novel anaesthetic sincerely reports that she is experiencing a sharp pain in her left hand, yet careful neurological investigation reveals that the drug has entirely suppressed her pain receptors and she is in fact experiencing only pressure.
