She cannot correctly identify the content of her own experience, though she takes herself to be incorrigible about it.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the subject must possess the conceptual resources necessary to correctly identify and classify the content of them experience.
passage: The doctrine that sense data furnish an incorrigible foundation for empirical knowledge has exercised a powerful influence upon epistemological thought. According to this view, when one attends to the immediate deliverances of sensory experience, one arrives at beliefs about which error is impossible; and this impossibility of error is held to be sufficient for justification. The belief must concern one's present experience, and the subject's awareness of that experience must be such that she cannot be wrong about its content. One might suppose that these conditions adequately capture what it is for a belief to enjoy incorrigible justification. Yet consider a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. A patient is administered a pharmacological agent of a novel sort, the effects of which upon consciousness are not yet fully understood. She sincerely reports experiencing a sharp pain in her left hand. Neurological investigation, however, establishes beyond reasonable doubt that her pain receptors have been entirely suppressed by the drug, and that what she is in fact experiencing is a sensation of pressure, not pain. She believes herself to be in pain, and she takes this belief to be incorrigible, yet she is mistaken about the very content of her experience. The error arises not from any insincerity but from a failure of the conceptual apparatus she brings to bear upon her sensory states. It appears, then, that the analysis must be supplemented with the requirement that the subject possess adequate conceptual resources for correctly classifying the content of her experience.

definition: A belief is justified by coherence if and only if (i) the belief is a member of a set of beliefs held by the subject, (ii) the belief is mutually supported by other beliefs in the set, and (iii) this mutual support is sufficient for the belief's positive epistemic status.
conditions: The belief is a member of a set of beliefs held by the subject
The belief is mutually supported by other beliefs in the set
Mutual support is sufficient for positive epistemic status
counterexample: A paranoid subject constructs an elaborate conspiracy theory in which every belief lends support to every other: the government is watching him, which explains the unmarked vans, which confirms the tapped phone, which supports the surveillance. The beliefs enjoy thorough mutual support, yet one would hesitate to call any of them justified, for the entire system is fabricated without evidential contact with reality.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the mutually supporting set of beliefs must bear some appropriate connection to the subject's evidence or experience.
passage: Coherentism proposes a distinctive picture of epistemic justification, one in which no belief occupies a foundational role but each derives its warrant from the support it receives from other beliefs within the subject's doxastic system. On the coherentist account, a belief is justified provided it belongs to a set of beliefs held by the subject and is mutually supported by the other members of that set, this mutual support being sufficient for positive epistemic status. The appeal of such an account lies in its avoidance of the difficulties attending foundationalism, for it does not require any class of privileged, self-warranting beliefs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily constructed. Imagine a subject of a somewhat paranoid disposition who has fashioned an intricate conspiracy theory. He believes that the government is conducting surveillance upon him, and this belief is supported by his further belief that unmarked vans frequently appear near his residence. The presence of the vans is in turn explained by his belief that his telephone has been tapped, which itself draws support from the original surveillance hypothesis. Each belief lends credence to the others, and the system exhibits a high degree of mutual support. Nevertheless, one would be reluctant to pronounce any of these beliefs justified, for the entire structure has been erected without any genuine evidential contact with the external world. The mutual support is, as it were, a closed circle of confabulation. It appears, therefore, that the coherentist analysis must be supplemented with the requirement that the system of beliefs bear some appropriate relation to the subject's evidence or experience.

definition: A belief is justified by systematic coherence if and only if (i) the belief belongs to a comprehensive system of beliefs, (ii) the system exhibits logical consistency, explanatory integration, and mutual inferential connections, and (iii) the degree of systematic coherence is sufficient for the justification of each member belief.
conditions: The belief belongs to a comprehensive system of beliefs
The system exhibits logical consistency, explanatory integration, and mutual inferential connections
The degree of systematic coherence suffices for justification
counterexample: An inventive novelist constructs a richly detailed fictional world with its own physics, history, and sociology. Her beliefs about this world form a comprehensive, logically consistent, explanatorily integrated system with extensive inferential connections. Yet none of these beliefs are justified, for the entire system is a deliberate fiction bearing no relation to actuality.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the systematically coherent set of beliefs must be appropriately responsive to empirical input or anchored to the subject's actual epistemic situation.
passage: A more sophisticated variant of the coherence theory holds that what justifies a belief is not merely the mutual support it receives from other beliefs but its membership in a comprehensive system characterised by logical consistency, explanatory integration, and rich mutual inferential connections. On this account, the degree of systematic coherence exhibited by the whole is sufficient for the justification of each constituent belief. One might observe that this analysis has considerable attractions, for it captures the intuition that well-integrated bodies of knowledge possess a kind of epistemic virtue that isolated or fragmentary beliefs lack. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be devised without great difficulty. Consider an inventive novelist who, over many years, has constructed a richly detailed fictional world, replete with its own physical laws, a complex history spanning several centuries, and an elaborate sociology governing the interactions of its inhabitants. Her beliefs about this world form a comprehensive system; they are logically consistent, explanatorily integrated, and bound together by extensive inferential connections. The degree of systematic coherence is, one might say, exemplary. Yet it would be absurd to maintain that her beliefs about this fictional world are epistemically justified, for the entire system is a deliberate fabrication, constructed without any intention of representing actuality. The analysis, it appears, must be supplemented with a requirement that the coherent system be appropriately responsive to empirical input or anchored in the subject's actual epistemic situation, lest mere inventive consistency be mistaken for genuine justification.

definition: A subject's belief that p is justified if and only if (i) the subject possesses evidence bearing on p, (ii) the evidence on balance supports p, and (iii) the possession of supporting evidence is both necessary and sufficient for the justification of the belief.
conditions: The subject possesses evidence bearing on p
The evidence on balance supports p
Possession of supporting evidence is necessary and sufficient for justification
counterexample: A juror possesses overwhelming evidence that the defendant is guilty, and the evidence on balance supports the guilty verdict. However, the juror has formed her belief in the defendant's guilt not on the basis of the evidence but solely because of a personal grudge against the defendant. She possesses the right evidence yet her belief is not properly based upon it.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the subject must actually base the belief on the possessed evidence, rather than holding it for some epistemically irrelevant reason.
passage: Evidentialism, in its most perspicuous formulation, maintains that the justification of a belief is wholly a function of the evidence possessed by the subject. A belief that p is justified, on this view, if and only if the subject possesses evidence bearing on p, that evidence on balance supports p, and the possession of such evidence is both necessary and sufficient for justification. The appeal of this doctrine lies in its austere simplicity: it dispenses with considerations of reliability, proper function, and the like, and locates justification entirely in the evidential relation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis presents itself upon reflection. Suppose a juror sits in a criminal trial and has been presented with overwhelming evidence pointing to the defendant's guilt. The forensic testimony, the eyewitness accounts, and the documentary record all converge upon the same conclusion, so that the evidence on balance supports the proposition that the defendant committed the offence. The juror does indeed believe the defendant guilty. However, careful inquiry into the juror's psychology reveals that she has formed this belief not on the basis of the evidence presented at trial but solely on account of a long-standing personal grudge against the defendant. She would have believed the defendant guilty regardless of what the evidence showed. Here, then, is a subject who possesses evidence which on balance supports her belief, and yet whose belief appears not to be justified, for it is not properly grounded in that evidence. The evidentialist analysis, it seems, must be supplemented with a basing condition requiring that the belief actually be held on account of the evidence.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) it is produced by a belief-forming process, (ii) that process is reliable, in the sense that it produces a high ratio of true beliefs to false beliefs, and (iii) the reliability of the process is sufficient for the justification of the resulting belief.
conditions: The belief is produced by a belief-forming process
The process is reliable (high ratio of true to false beliefs)
The reliability of the process suffices for justification
counterexample: A man possesses a brain lesion which, by a freak of neurology, causes him to form the belief that he has a brain lesion. The belief-forming process — the lesion itself — happens to be perfectly reliable in this instance, for it invariably produces the true belief that he has a brain lesion. Yet one would not ordinarily regard this belief as justified, since the process is not of the sort we should recognise as epistemically respectable.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the reliable process must be of a type that is generally recognised as an appropriate or epistemically virtuous method of belief formation.
passage: Process reliabilism offers an elegantly naturalistic account of epistemic justification, holding that a belief is justified just in case it is the product of a reliable belief-forming process — one which yields a suitably high proportion of true beliefs. On this view, it matters not whether the subject is aware of the process or can articulate reasons in its favour; what matters is the track record, as it were, of the cognitive mechanism responsible for the belief. One might initially find this analysis entirely satisfactory, for it accords well with our ordinary practice of distinguishing between reliable and unreliable methods of inquiry. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed. Imagine a person who, owing to a neurological abnormality, possesses a lesion in his brain. This lesion, by a remarkable coincidence of causal pathways, invariably produces in him the belief that he has a brain lesion. The process is, in a straightforward sense, perfectly reliable: every time it operates, it produces a true belief. The subject has never entertained this belief for any other reason; the lesion is the sole cause. Nevertheless, one would hesitate to pronounce this belief justified. The process, though reliable, is not of the sort we should recognise as an epistemically appropriate method of belief formation. It is, one might say, an accident of pathology rather than an exercise of cognitive virtue. The reliabilist analysis, therefore, appears to require supplementation with a condition specifying that the process must be of an epistemically respectable type.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) it is produced by the subject's cognitive faculties, (ii) those faculties are functioning properly according to their design plan, and (iii) the design plan is aimed at truth in the relevant cognitive environment.
conditions: The belief is produced by the subject's cognitive faculties
Those faculties are functioning properly according to their design plan
The design plan is aimed at truth in the relevant cognitive environment
counterexample: A subject has been raised from birth in an environment which systematically differs from the one for which her cognitive faculties were designed. Her faculties function properly according to their design plan, and that plan is aimed at truth, but the environment is so radically different that her properly functioning faculties regularly produce false beliefs. She believes, for instance, that certain harmless plants are poisonous, because her threat-detection faculties are calibrated for a different ecology.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the subject must actually be situated in the cognitive environment for which the design plan was intended, or one sufficiently similar to it.
passage: Proper functionalism, as advanced in various forms, proposes that a belief is justified when it is the product of cognitive faculties operating as they were designed to operate, where the design plan in question is one aimed at the production of true beliefs in the relevant cognitive environment. This account has the merit of connecting justification with the notion of cognitive well-functioning, thereby offering a teleological grounding for epistemic norms. One might suppose that a belief produced by properly functioning faculties, whose design plan is truth-directed, must surely enjoy justification. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be envisaged. Consider a subject who has been transported, through no fault of her own, into an environment which differs systematically and profoundly from the one for which her cognitive faculties were designed. Her faculties continue to function exactly as their design plan specifies; there is no malfunction, no impairment, no deviation from the intended pattern of operation. The design plan is, moreover, genuinely aimed at truth. Nevertheless, because the environment in which she now finds herself diverges so radically from the one her faculties were calibrated to navigate, her beliefs are regularly and extensively false. She mistakes harmless flora for dangerous predators; she misjudges distances, temperatures, and the behaviour of her surroundings. Her faculties are functioning properly and their design plan is truth-aimed, yet her beliefs are not justified, for the environment has rendered her cognitive equipment systematically unreliable. The analysis, it appears, must incorporate a condition requiring that the subject be situated in the environment, or one sufficiently resembling it, for which the design plan was fashioned.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) there exist factors which confer justification upon the belief, (ii) the subject has reflective access to those justifying factors, and (iii) the subject's reflective access is sufficient for the belief's justification.
conditions: There exist factors which confer justification upon the belief
The subject has reflective access to those justifying factors
The subject's reflective access is sufficient for justification
counterexample: A philosopher reflects at length upon her reasons for believing in the external world and identifies what she takes to be adequate justifying factors. However, she has failed to notice that her justifying factors depend upon a suppressed premise which she would, upon examination, recognise as false. She has reflective access to what she takes to be the justifying factors, yet the factors she accesses are not the ones actually doing the justificatory work.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the factors to which the subject has reflective access must be the factors which genuinely confer justification, not merely those the subject mistakenly identifies as justificatory.
passage: Internalism about justification, in one of its more prominent formulations, holds that a belief is justified only if the subject has reflective access to the factors which confer justification upon it. The internalist insists that justification is not something which may operate behind the scenes, as it were, without the subject's awareness; rather, the epistemic agent must be able, upon reflection, to identify and appreciate the grounds of her belief. This access requirement is held to be sufficient, together with the existence of the justifying factors themselves, for the belief's justification. One might observe that this account captures a powerful intuition about the responsibility of the rational agent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be set out as follows. Suppose a philosopher of considerable acuity undertakes a careful examination of her reasons for believing in the reality of the external world. After prolonged reflection, she identifies what she takes to be adequate justifying factors and satisfies herself that she has full reflective access to them. However, her analysis contains a subtle error: her putative justifying factors depend upon a suppressed premise — one she has not made explicit — which she would, upon further examination, recognise as false. The factors to which she has reflective access are not, in truth, the factors which do the justificatory work; the genuine grounds of her belief, if they exist at all, lie elsewhere. She has access, but access to the wrong items. The internalist analysis, therefore, must be supplemented with the requirement that the subject's reflective access must be directed at the factors which genuinely confer justification.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the belief is produced by a reliable cognitive process, (ii) the reliability of the process obtains independently of the subject's awareness of it, and (iii) the external reliability of the process is sufficient for the belief's justification, regardless of whether the subject can identify or access the grounds of justification.
conditions: The belief is produced by a reliable cognitive process
The reliability obtains independently of the subject's awareness
External reliability suffices for justification regardless of the subject's access to the grounds
counterexample: A subject possesses a reliable faculty of mental arithmetic which consistently produces correct results. She is unaware of why her answers are correct; the process is externally reliable. However, she also possesses strong and undefeated evidence that her arithmetic faculty is broken — a trusted authority has told her, falsely, that she suffered brain damage impairing her calculation. She continues to believe her calculations despite this counter-evidence. The external reliability of her process is intact, yet her belief seems unjustified given the undefeated defeating evidence she possesses.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the subject must not possess undefeated evidence which counts against the reliability of the very process producing the belief.
passage: Externalism about justification, in contrast to its internalist rival, maintains that a belief may be justified in virtue of factors to which the subject need have no reflective access. On the externalist account, what matters is that the belief be produced by a reliable cognitive process, and this reliability is held to obtain independently of the subject's awareness of it. The externalist thus divorces justification from the reflective capacities of the agent, locating it instead in the objective features of the belief-forming mechanism. One might find this analysis attractive for its accommodation of the epistemic lives of unsophisticated agents — young children, for instance, or those unversed in epistemological theory. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis merits our attention. Imagine a subject who possesses an unusually reliable faculty of mental arithmetic. She can perform complex calculations swiftly and with consistent accuracy, though she has no insight into the mechanisms underlying this ability. By externalist lights, her arithmetical beliefs are justified, for the process is reliable and its reliability does not depend upon her awareness. Now suppose that a trusted neurologist, whom she has every reason to believe, informs her — falsely, as it happens — that she has suffered brain damage which has severely impaired her calculation abilities. She possesses strong evidence, then, that her arithmetic faculty is unreliable. Yet she persists in believing her calculations. The external reliability remains; her process has not changed. Nevertheless, it appears that her beliefs are not justified, for she holds them in the teeth of undefeated evidence to the contrary. The externalist analysis must, it seems, incorporate a no-defeater condition.

definition: A belief is doxastically justified if and only if (i) the subject holds the belief, (ii) the subject possesses adequate evidence for the belief, and (iii) the subject actually bases the belief upon that adequate evidence.
conditions: The subject holds the belief
The subject possesses adequate evidence for the belief
The subject actually bases the belief on that evidence
counterexample: A detective possesses compelling forensic evidence that the butler committed the crime and does believe the butler is guilty. However, she bases her belief not upon the forensic evidence but upon a hunch she formed when she first saw the butler's face, which reminded her of a villain in a film. She later encounters the forensic evidence and recognises it as adequate, yet her belief was already fixed by the hunch and is not causally sustained by the evidence.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the causal sustaining of the belief by the evidence must be of an epistemically appropriate kind, not merely that the belief and the evidence happen to coexist in the subject's cognitive economy.
passage: The distinction between doxastic and propositional justification occupies an important place in contemporary epistemology. Doxastic justification concerns not merely whether the subject has adequate grounds for a belief but whether the belief is actually held on the basis of those grounds. On the standard analysis, a belief is doxastically justified if and only if the subject holds the belief, possesses adequate evidence for it, and actually bases the belief upon that evidence. One might suppose that these conditions jointly capture what it is for a belief to be properly grounded. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not difficult to construct. Consider a detective investigating a murder. She possesses compelling forensic evidence — fingerprints, fibres, toxicological data — all pointing to the butler as the perpetrator. She does indeed believe that the butler committed the crime. However, a careful examination of the aetiology of her belief reveals that she formed it, not upon encountering the forensic evidence, but upon first seeing the butler's face, which bore an unfortunate resemblance to a villainous character in a film she had recently watched. The hunch thus formed became fixed, and when the forensic evidence subsequently came to her attention, she recognised it as adequate but her belief was not thereby re-grounded. The evidence and the belief coexist, and in some loose sense she might be said to base her belief on the evidence after the fact, yet the causal history is epistemically defective. The analysis, it appears, requires a more exacting specification of what it is for a belief to be properly based upon evidence.

definition: A subject has propositional justification for believing that p if and only if (i) there exists adequate evidence for p, (ii) the evidence is available to the subject, and (iii) the availability of adequate evidence is sufficient for the subject's propositional justification, regardless of whether the subject actually believes p.
conditions: There exists adequate evidence for p
The evidence is available to the subject
Availability of adequate evidence suffices for propositional justification, independent of actual belief
counterexample: A librarian has, among the thousands of volumes in her care, a book containing decisive evidence for a startling historical thesis. The evidence is available to her in the sense that she could consult the book at any time, yet she has no idea the book contains such evidence and has never had occasion to look at the relevant pages. It seems strained to say she has propositional justification for the historical thesis merely because the evidence sits on a shelf she might inspect.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the evidence must not merely be available in some abstract or physical sense but must be available in a sense that the subject could reasonably be expected to recognise and utilise it.
passage: Propositional justification, as distinct from its doxastic counterpart, concerns the question of whether a subject possesses adequate grounds for believing a proposition, irrespective of whether the subject actually forms the belief in question. On the standard analysis, a subject has propositional justification for p provided that adequate evidence for p exists, that this evidence is available to the subject, and that such availability is sufficient for propositional justification. One might observe that this account is appealing in its generality: it allows us to say that a subject is justified in believing something even if she has not yet got round to believing it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be adduced. Consider a librarian who presides over a vast collection of volumes. Among these is a book, tucked away in a seldom-visited section, which contains decisive evidence for a remarkable historical thesis — evidence which, if appreciated, would constitute overwhelming support for the proposition in question. The book is available to the librarian in the straightforward sense that she could, at any moment, walk to the shelf and consult it. Yet she has no notion that the book contains such evidence; she has never examined the relevant pages; and she has no reason to suspect that the evidence is there to be found. To say that she has propositional justification for the historical thesis on the grounds that the evidence is available to her seems to stretch the concept of availability beyond its useful limits. The analysis, then, must incorporate a more demanding notion of availability, one requiring that the subject be in a position to recognise and make use of the evidence in question.

definition: A belief is properly based on evidence if and only if (i) the subject holds the belief, (ii) the subject possesses evidence which supports the belief, and (iii) the evidence is a cause of the subject's holding the belief.
conditions: The subject holds the belief
The subject possesses supporting evidence
The evidence is a cause of the belief
counterexample: A student reads a persuasive journal article presenting strong evidence that a certain chemical compound is toxic. The evidence causes in her a bout of anxiety about laboratory safety, and this anxiety in turn causes her to believe that the compound is toxic. The evidence is a cause of her belief, but only via an epistemically deviant causal chain passing through an emotional state rather than through rational appreciation of the evidential support.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the causal chain from evidence to belief must proceed through an epistemically appropriate route, such as rational appreciation or inference, rather than through a deviant causal pathway.
passage: The basing relation — the relation which must hold between a belief and the evidence upon which it is held — has proved a persistently troublesome notion in epistemology. On a simple causal analysis, a belief is properly based on evidence just in case the subject holds the belief, possesses evidence supporting it, and the evidence is a cause of the subject's holding the belief. This causal account has the virtue of providing a naturalistic characterisation of what it is for a belief to be grounded in reasons. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily available. Suppose a student of chemistry reads a journal article containing strong evidence that a particular compound is toxic to human beings. The evidence presented in the article causes in her a considerable degree of anxiety about the safety of her laboratory work. This anxiety, operating upon her general disposition to worry about hazards, in turn causes her to form the belief that the compound is toxic. Now, one might note that the evidence is indeed a cause of her belief; there is a genuine causal chain running from her encounter with the evidence to her holding of the belief. However, the causal chain passes through an emotional state — the anxiety — rather than through a process of rational appreciation or inference. She does not believe the compound to be toxic because she has weighed the evidence and found it compelling; she believes it because the evidence made her anxious and the anxiety produced the belief. The causal condition is satisfied, yet the basing relation seems not to hold in the epistemically relevant sense. It appears that the analysis must specify that the causal route from evidence to belief must be of an epistemically appropriate kind.

definition: A subject's belief is justified in the absence of defeaters if and only if (i) the subject possesses adequate reasons for the belief, (ii) no undefeated defeaters obtain which undermine or rebut those reasons, and (iii) the absence of undefeated defeaters, together with adequate reasons, is sufficient for justification.
conditions: The subject possesses adequate reasons for the belief
No undefeated defeaters obtain
Adequate reasons plus absence of undefeated defeaters suffice for justification
counterexample: A physician possesses strong diagnostic evidence that her patient has a rare disease, and no undefeated defeater is available to her. However, there exists a newly published study, not yet accessible to any practitioner in her country, which demonstrates conclusively that the diagnostic test she relied upon produces false positives in precisely the circumstances of her patient's case. No defeater is available to her, yet the objective existence of this rebutting evidence seems to cast doubt upon whether her belief is fully justified.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects to specify whether the relevant defeaters must be limited to those accessible to the subject or must include defeaters existing in the broader epistemic environment, regardless of accessibility.
passage: The notion of a defeater plays a central role in contemporary accounts of justification. On one influential analysis, a belief is justified when the subject possesses adequate reasons for it and no undefeated defeaters obtain — that is, there is no available evidence which undermines or rebuts the subject's reasons without itself being defeated by further considerations. The absence of such defeaters, together with adequate reasons, is held to be sufficient for justification. One might observe that this analysis captures an important feature of our epistemic practice, for we do indeed withdraw or modify our beliefs when confronted with defeating evidence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed. Imagine a physician who, on the basis of a well-regarded diagnostic test and her considerable clinical experience, forms the belief that her patient has a rare metabolic disorder. Her reasons are, by any ordinary standard, adequate. No defeater is available to her; she has consulted the relevant literature, sought second opinions, and found nothing to undermine her diagnosis. However, a research team in a distant country has just published a study demonstrating that the diagnostic test in question produces false positives in precisely the circumstances of her patient's case. This study is not yet accessible to any practitioner in her region. No undefeated defeater is available to the physician, yet the rebutting evidence exists. The question which arises is whether her belief is fully justified, given the existence of this evidence in the broader epistemic environment. The analysis, it appears, is incomplete insofar as it does not specify whether the relevant defeaters must be restricted to those accessible to the subject or must encompass those existing at large.

definition: A belief is ultima facie justified if and only if (i) the subject has prima facie justification for the belief, (ii) all potential defeaters have been considered, and (iii) the belief survives the consideration of all defeaters, so that it is justified all things considered.
conditions: The subject has prima facie justification for the belief
All potential defeaters have been considered
The belief survives consideration of all defeaters (justified all things considered)
counterexample: A historian has prima facie justification for a claim about an ancient battle, based on a well-known primary source. She considers all the defeaters of which she is aware and her belief survives. However, she has not considered — because she does not know of — a recently deciphered inscription which constitutes a powerful defeater. She has considered all defeaters available to her, but not all potential defeaters, yet the analysis demands the latter.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to account for the practical impossibility of considering all potential defeaters, thereby setting a standard which no finite epistemic agent could satisfy.
passage: The distinction between prima facie and ultima facie justification marks the difference between initial warrant and warrant which withstands scrutiny. A belief is prima facie justified when the subject has adequate grounds for it, prior to the consideration of possible defeaters. It is ultima facie justified when it survives the consideration of all defeaters — when, having taken everything into account, the subject's reasons remain intact. On the analysis under examination, ultima facie justification requires that the subject have prima facie justification, that all potential defeaters have been considered, and that the belief survive this comprehensive review. One might suppose this analysis to be entirely in order, for it accurately reflects the idea that fully justified belief is belief which has been tested against every relevant challenge. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be adduced. Consider a historian who, on the basis of a well-known primary source, has formed a carefully considered view about the outcome of an ancient battle. She reviews the available secondary literature, consults epigraphic evidence, and considers every objection she can locate. Her belief survives this process and she regards it as justified all things considered. However, a recently deciphered inscription, not yet published and unknown to any scholar in her field, constitutes a powerful defeater of her claim. She has not considered this defeater because she cannot have done so. The analysis requires the consideration of all potential defeaters, yet this is a condition which no finite agent could fulfil, for the space of potential defeaters is not bounded by the subject's epistemic horizon. The analysis, therefore, sets a standard which is practically unattainable and must be revised to reflect the limitations of finite cognition.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the subject has formed the belief through an epistemically responsible inquiry, (ii) the subject has fulfilled all epistemic duties relevant to the belief, and (iii) the fulfilment of these duties is sufficient for the belief's justification.
conditions: The subject formed the belief through epistemically responsible inquiry
The subject has fulfilled all relevant epistemic duties
Fulfilment of epistemic duties suffices for justification
counterexample: A conscientious researcher investigates a medical claim with the utmost epistemic responsibility: she examines every available study, weighs the evidence scrupulously, and fulfils every epistemic duty one could reasonably impose. Yet she is the victim of a systematic fraud — every study she has consulted has been fabricated. Her belief is responsibly formed and her duties are discharged, yet it appears unjustified, for it rests upon a foundation of deceit entirely outside her control.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the epistemic environment must not be so corrupted or deceptive as to render responsible inquiry systematically unreliable.
passage: The concept of epistemic responsibility has been proposed as the foundation of justified belief, the thought being that a subject's belief is justified precisely when she has conducted her inquiry in a responsible manner and has discharged all her epistemic duties. On this account, justification is a matter of epistemic conscientiousness: the subject must have investigated with appropriate diligence, weighed the evidence with due care, and refrained from hasty or negligent judgement. One might observe that this analysis accords well with our intuitive practice of praising those who form their beliefs responsibly and censuring those who do not. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis presents itself. Imagine a medical researcher who undertakes to determine whether a certain drug is efficacious. She proceeds with exemplary responsibility: she examines every published study, evaluates the methodology of each, weighs the statistical evidence with care, and consults experts in the field. She fulfils, one might say, every epistemic duty that could reasonably be imposed upon a researcher in her position. On the basis of this inquiry, she forms the belief that the drug is efficacious. However, it subsequently emerges that every study she consulted was the product of a systematic and elaborate fraud. The data were fabricated, the peer review was corrupted, and the expert opinions she sought were purchased. Her inquiry was responsible in every respect, and her epistemic duties were fully discharged, yet her belief appears not to be justified, for it rests upon a foundation of deliberate deceit wholly beyond her power to detect. The analysis, it seems, must incorporate a condition requiring that the epistemic environment not be so corrupted as to render responsible inquiry systematically unreliable.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the belief is supported by reasons, (ii) those reasons are themselves supported by further reasons, and (iii) this chain of reasons terminates in a finite number of steps at reasons which require no further support.
conditions: The belief is supported by reasons
Those reasons are supported by further reasons
The chain terminates finitely at reasons requiring no further support
counterexample: A theologian believes a certain doctrine, supported by scriptural interpretation, which is supported by a hermeneutic principle, which is supported by a tradition of exegesis, which is supported by the authority of the church, which is itself supported by the doctrine with which we began. The chain of reasons is finite and each link is supported, yet it terminates not at a self-supporting foundation but by circling back upon itself.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the terminal reasons must be genuinely self-supporting or foundational, rather than merely appearing to terminate by virtue of a circular chain.
passage: The regress problem has been a central preoccupation of epistemologists since antiquity. The difficulty is familiar: if every belief requires support from further beliefs, and those beliefs in turn require support, one is threatened with an infinite regress which would render justification impossible. A natural response, associated with foundationalism, holds that the regress terminates: a belief is justified when it is supported by reasons, those reasons by further reasons, and the chain ends, in a finite number of steps, at reasons which require no further support. One might suppose that this analysis resolves the regress in a perfectly satisfactory manner. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be described. Consider a theologian who holds a certain doctrinal proposition. She supports this belief by appeal to a principle of scriptural interpretation, which she in turn supports by reference to a hermeneutic tradition, which she grounds in the authority of a council of the church, which she justifies by appeal to the very doctrine with which she began. The chain of reasons is finite, and each reason is supported by a further reason; moreover, the chain appears to terminate, for there is no point at which the theologian confesses herself unable to provide a reason. Yet the termination is illusory, for the chain does not end at a genuinely self-supporting foundation but rather circles back upon its starting point. The reasons form a closed loop rather than a grounded structure. The analysis, it appears, must be supplemented with the requirement that the terminal reasons be genuinely foundational — self-supporting in a way that does not depend upon the very beliefs they are invoked to justify.

definition: A belief is inferentially justified if and only if (i) the belief is inferred from one or more other beliefs, (ii) the beliefs from which it is inferred are themselves justified, and (iii) the inference preserves justification from the premiss-beliefs to the conclusion-belief.
conditions: The belief is inferred from other beliefs
The premiss-beliefs are themselves justified
The inference preserves justification from premisses to conclusion
counterexample: A logician infers a conclusion from two justified premisses via a valid deductive rule. However, she is entirely unaware that the two premisses, though individually justified, jointly entail a contradiction when combined with a third belief she also holds and is justified in holding. The inference preserves justification in the narrow sense, yet the conclusion inherits a latent inconsistency from the broader set of her justified beliefs.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the premiss-beliefs must be jointly consistent with the subject's other justified beliefs, not merely individually justified.
passage: Inferential justification constitutes the most familiar species of epistemic support: a belief is justified by being inferred from other beliefs which are themselves justified, and the inference is of a kind which transmits or preserves justification. On the standard analysis, the requirements are threefold: the belief must be inferred from premiss-beliefs, those premisses must be justified, and the inference must preserve justification. This analysis captures the common-sense picture of reasoning from established truths to new conclusions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis deserves attention. Suppose a logician of considerable skill holds two premiss-beliefs, each of which is justified by impeccable evidence. She infers a conclusion from these premisses by means of a valid deductive rule, and the inference is, by ordinary standards, justification-preserving. However, unbeknown to her, the two premisses, though individually well-supported, jointly entail a contradiction when taken together with a third belief she is also justified in holding. The conclusion she draws inherits this latent inconsistency: it is the product of a valid inference from individually justified premisses, yet the broader doxastic context renders it problematic. One might observe that the inference preserves justification in the narrow, local sense — each premiss is justified and the rule is valid — yet the conclusion cannot be regarded as justified without qualification, for its derivation depends upon premisses whose joint consistency with the subject's other beliefs has not been secured. The analysis must, it appears, be supplemented with a requirement of joint consistency among the premisses and the subject's broader body of justified beliefs.

definition: A belief is non-inferentially justified if and only if (i) the belief is not derived from any other belief by inference, (ii) the belief arises from a direct and immediate cognitive encounter with its object, and (iii) this immediate encounter is sufficient for the belief's justification.
conditions: The belief is not inferred from other beliefs
The belief arises from a direct and immediate cognitive encounter with its object
The immediate encounter suffices for justification
counterexample: A subject stares at a Müller-Lyer diagram and has a direct, immediate cognitive encounter with the lines, forming the non-inferential belief that one line is longer than the other. The encounter is vivid and unmediated, yet the belief is false and the subject knows, from prior instruction, that such diagrams are illusions. Her immediate encounter produces a belief which she herself has reason to reject.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the subject must not possess background knowledge or evidence which defeats the prima facie justification conferred by the immediate encounter.
passage: Non-inferential or immediate justification is the species of warrant which attaches to beliefs formed not through reasoning from other beliefs but through direct cognitive contact with their objects. On the analysis under consideration, such justification requires that the belief be non-inferential in origin, that it arise from a direct and immediate encounter with its object, and that this encounter be sufficient for justification. The appeal of such an account is evident: it promises to ground certain beliefs without the need for further epistemic support, thereby providing a stopping point for the regress of reasons. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is available. Suppose a subject gazes upon a well-known optical illusion — the Müller-Lyer diagram, let us say. She has a direct and immediate visual encounter with two lines, and she forms the non-inferential belief that one line is longer than the other. The encounter is vivid, unmediated by inference, and produces the belief in the most direct fashion imaginable. The three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. However, the subject is a student of perceptual psychology and is well aware that the Müller-Lyer diagram produces a systematic illusion; she knows that the lines are of equal length. She possesses, in other words, background knowledge which constitutes a powerful defeater of the belief produced by her immediate encounter. It appears that the immediacy of the encounter is not sufficient for justification when the subject possesses countervailing evidence. The analysis must therefore be supplemented with a condition requiring the absence of defeating background knowledge.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the belief has a probability greater than some threshold t (where t > 0.5) on the subject's total evidence, (ii) the subject's total evidence is itself adequate, and (iii) the high probability of the belief on the total evidence is sufficient for its justification.
conditions: The belief has a probability greater than threshold t on the subject's total evidence
The subject's total evidence is adequate
High probability on total evidence suffices for justification
counterexample: A gambler knows that a certain lottery has one million tickets and that exactly one will win. For each individual ticket, the probability that it will lose, given his total evidence, is 0.999999 — far exceeding any reasonable threshold. He is therefore justified, on this analysis, in believing of each ticket that it will lose. Yet he also knows that exactly one ticket must win, so the conjunction of all his individually justified beliefs entails a proposition he knows to be false.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the justification of individual beliefs be consistent with the subject's justified beliefs about the relevant domain taken collectively, thereby generating the lottery paradox.
passage: It has been proposed that justification may be analysed in probabilistic terms: a belief is justified when its probability, conditional upon the subject's total evidence, exceeds some suitable threshold. This account has the attraction of precision, for it replaces vague talk of adequate grounds with the rigorous apparatus of probability theory. On the analysis in question, justification requires that the belief's probability exceed a threshold t on the subject's total evidence, that this evidence be itself adequate, and that high probability be sufficient for justification. One might suppose this analysis to be satisfactory, at least for a wide range of cases. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis has been much discussed. Consider a fair lottery with one million tickets, exactly one of which will win. A subject who knows the structure of the lottery can calculate that, for each individual ticket, the probability that it will lose is 0.999999 — a figure exceeding any reasonable threshold short of certainty. The analysis therefore pronounces him justified in believing, of each ticket, that it will lose. Yet he is also justified in believing that exactly one ticket will win. The conjunction of his individually justified beliefs — that ticket one will lose, that ticket two will lose, and so on for all one million tickets — entails that no ticket will win, which contradicts his equally justified belief that one ticket must win. The subject's individually justified beliefs are collectively inconsistent. It appears that high probability on the total evidence is not sufficient for justification, for the analysis generates a set of justified beliefs which cannot all be true. Some additional condition is required to prevent this paradox.

definition: A perceptual belief is justified if and only if (i) the belief is formed on the basis of a perceptual experience, (ii) the perceptual experience represents the world as being a certain way, and (iii) the representational content of the experience is sufficient to justify the belief that the world is that way.
conditions: The belief is formed on the basis of a perceptual experience
The perceptual experience represents the world as being a certain way
The representational content of the experience suffices to justify the belief
counterexample: A subject enters a room known to contain highly realistic holographic projections. She perceives what appears to be a vase of flowers on the table. Her perceptual experience represents the world as containing a vase of flowers, and she forms the corresponding belief on the basis of this experience. Yet, knowing that the room is filled with holograms, she has strong reason to doubt that any perceived object is real. The representational content of her experience is intact, but the known circumstances of perception undermine its justificatory force.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the conditions under which the perceptual experience occurs must be such that the subject has no reason to believe those conditions are epistemically unfavourable.
passage: Perceptual justification is widely regarded as a fundamental source of empirical knowledge. On one natural analysis, a perceptual belief is justified when it is formed on the basis of a perceptual experience which represents the world as being a certain way, and the representational content of the experience is sufficient to justify the corresponding belief. This account captures the intuition that perception provides prima facie warrant for beliefs about the external world: when one sees a red apple upon the table, one's experience represents the world as containing a red apple, and this representation justifies one's belief that there is a red apple before one. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be described. Suppose a subject enters a room which she has been reliably informed is equipped with sophisticated holographic technology capable of producing highly realistic three-dimensional projections indistinguishable from real objects. Upon entering, she perceives what appears to be a vase of flowers standing upon a table. Her perceptual experience represents the world as containing a vase of flowers in precisely that location, and she forms the belief that there is a vase of flowers on the table, based upon this experience. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Nevertheless, given her knowledge that the room contains holograms, it appears that her belief is not justified, for she has strong reason to doubt that any object perceived in this environment is genuine. The representational content of her experience remains unchanged, yet its justificatory force is undermined by the known circumstances of perception. The analysis must be supplemented with a condition requiring that the perceptual conditions be ones the subject has no reason to regard as epistemically compromised.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the belief meets the epistemic standards operative in the subject's context, (ii) those standards are determined by the practical and conversational features of the context, and (iii) meeting the contextually determined standards is sufficient for justification.
conditions: The belief meets the epistemic standards operative in the subject's context
The standards are determined by practical and conversational features of the context
Meeting contextually determined standards suffices for justification
counterexample: A pilot believes her aircraft is safe to fly, and this belief meets the standards operative in a casual conversational context at a dinner party. However, she is not at a dinner party but in a pre-flight briefing where the practical stakes are extraordinarily high. The casual conversational context in which her belief meets the standards is not the context which should govern, for the practical circumstances demand a far more stringent standard.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to specify which context determines the operative standards when the subject simultaneously occupies multiple contexts with conflicting epistemic demands.
passage: Contextualism about justification holds that the standards a belief must meet in order to count as justified vary with the context in which the question of justification arises. On this analysis, a belief is justified if it meets the epistemic standards determined by the practical and conversational features of the context, and meeting those standards is sufficient for justification. The appeal of this view lies in its accommodation of the observation that we appear to demand more rigorous justification in some situations than in others: what counts as adequate justification for a casual assertion may be wholly inadequate in a court of law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed. Consider a pilot who, at a dinner party, expresses the belief that her aircraft is safe to fly on the morrow. In the relaxed conversational context of the dinner party, the standards for justified belief are relatively modest, and her belief — grounded in her general experience and a cursory inspection of the aircraft that afternoon — meets those standards comfortably. However, she is simultaneously preparing for a pre-flight briefing the following morning, a context in which the practical stakes are of the most serious kind, for lives depend upon the correctness of her assessment. The casual conversational standards of the dinner party are satisfied, but the stringent practical standards of the pre-flight context are not. The analysis does not specify which context governs when the subject simultaneously inhabits multiple contexts with conflicting demands. It appears that the analysis must be supplemented with an account of how the relevant context is to be determined when such conflicts arise.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the subject has fulfilled their epistemic duties with respect to the belief, (ii) epistemic duties are analogous to moral duties and consist in obligations to seek truth and avoid error, and (iii) the fulfilment of these duties is sufficient for the belief's justification.
conditions: The subject has fulfilled their epistemic duties with respect to the belief
Epistemic duties consist in obligations to seek truth and avoid error
Fulfilment of duties suffices for justification
counterexample: A 17th-century natural philosopher fulfils every epistemic duty available in his intellectual context: he observes carefully, reasons diligently, and consults the best authorities. He forms the belief that combustion involves the release of phlogiston. He has done everything that could be asked of a responsible inquirer of his time, yet his belief is false and, by later lights, unjustified. His duties are fulfilled, but the conceptual resources of his era are insufficient for arriving at truth.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the epistemic duties must be calibrated to conceptual and evidential resources adequate for arriving at true beliefs, not merely to the best practices of the subject's historical context.
passage: The deontological conception of justification draws an analogy between epistemic and moral obligation: just as the moral agent has duties to act rightly, so the epistemic agent has duties to seek truth and avoid error. On this analysis, a belief is justified when the subject has fulfilled her epistemic duties — when she has investigated with due diligence, weighed the evidence impartially, and formed her belief in accordance with her obligations as a seeker of truth. The fulfilment of these duties is held to be sufficient for justification. One might observe that this account captures an important feature of our practice of epistemic appraisal, for we do, in common life, evaluate believers in terms of their intellectual conscientiousness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of science. Consider a natural philosopher of the 17th century who sets out to understand the nature of combustion. He observes with the utmost care, reasons with diligence and rigour, and consults the most authoritative sources available to him. He fulfils every epistemic duty that could be imposed upon a responsible inquirer of his period. On the basis of this conscientious inquiry, he forms the belief that combustion involves the release of a substance called phlogiston. His duties are discharged; he has done all that could reasonably be asked. Yet his belief is false, and from the vantage of later chemistry it appears unjustified, for the phlogiston theory is fundamentally mistaken. The difficulty is that his epistemic duties were calibrated to the conceptual and evidential resources of his era, which were not adequate for arriving at the truth about combustion. The analysis must, it appears, require that the duties in question be tied to resources sufficient for truth-attainment, not merely to the best available practices of a given historical moment.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the belief is formed by a method which is truth-conducive, (ii) truth-conduciveness is understood as a tendency to produce true beliefs in the actual world, and (iii) truth-conduciveness of the method is sufficient for the justification of the beliefs it produces.
conditions: The belief is formed by a truth-conducive method
Truth-conduciveness is a tendency to produce true beliefs in the actual world
Truth-conduciveness suffices for justification
counterexample: A clairvoyant possesses a faculty which, as a matter of actual-world fact, invariably produces true beliefs about distant events. The method is truth-conducive in the strongest sense: it never errs. Yet the clairvoyant has no understanding of why her faculty works, possesses no evidence of its reliability, and her wider epistemic community unanimously regards clairvoyance as fraudulent. Her beliefs are produced by a truth-conducive method, yet one would hesitate to call them justified.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the subject must have some positive epistemic reason to regard the belief-forming method as truth-conducive, or at least must not possess strong reasons to doubt its reliability.
passage: Truth-conduciveness has been proposed as the hallmark of epistemically justified belief: a belief is justified, on this view, when it is produced by a method which tends to yield true beliefs in the actual world. The attraction of this account lies in its objectivity, for it ties justification to a genuine relation between the method and the truth, rather than to the subject's perspective or sense of conviction. One might suppose that a method which, as a matter of fact, reliably delivers true beliefs must ipso facto produce justified beliefs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not difficult to conceive. Suppose there exists a subject who possesses a faculty of clairvoyance — a capacity to form beliefs about distant events without any sensory contact or inferential process. As it happens, this faculty is perfectly truth-conducive: every belief it produces is true. The subject herself has no insight into the mechanism by which the faculty operates and possesses no evidence, either first-personal or third-personal, that the faculty is reliable. Moreover, her wider epistemic community unanimously regards clairvoyance as a species of fraud and delusion. She forms the belief, via this faculty, that a certain event is occurring on the far side of the globe. The method is truth-conducive and the belief is true, yet it appears that the belief is not justified, for the subject has no reason to trust the method and strong social evidence against it. The analysis, it seems, must be supplemented with a requirement that the subject have some positive ground for regarding the method as truth-conducive, or at least not possess compelling reasons to distrust it.

definition: A belief is justified a priori if and only if (i) the belief is not justified by empirical evidence, (ii) the justification derives solely from the exercise of rational intuition or conceptual analysis, and (iii) the non-empirical source of justification is sufficient for the belief's positive epistemic status.
conditions: The belief is not justified by empirical evidence
Justification derives solely from rational intuition or conceptual analysis
The non-empirical source suffices for positive epistemic status
counterexample: A philosopher claims to know by rational intuition alone that every event has a cause. She holds this as an a priori truth, justified without empirical evidence, solely through conceptual analysis. Yet developments in quantum mechanics provide strong empirical evidence that certain subatomic events are genuinely uncaused. Her a priori intuition conflicts with robust empirical findings. The non-empirical source of justification appears insufficient when confronted with powerful empirical counter-evidence.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that a priori justification must not be undermined by compelling empirical evidence to the contrary, since even non-empirically grounded beliefs may be defeasible by experience.
passage: The distinction between empirical and a priori justification has been a staple of philosophical inquiry since at least the time of Kant. A belief is justified a priori, on the standard analysis, when its warrant does not derive from empirical evidence but from the exercise of rational intuition or conceptual analysis, and this non-empirical source is sufficient for positive epistemic status. Thus, the truths of logic and mathematics are typically held to be justified a priori: one does not need to examine the physical world to know that two and three make five or that a valid syllogism preserves truth. One might observe that this analysis seems secure, for it reflects a deeply entrenched feature of our epistemic practice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to the relation between a priori intuition and empirical discovery. Consider a philosopher who holds, on the basis of conceptual analysis and rational intuition alone, that every event must have a cause. She regards this as a necessary truth, justified without any appeal to experience. However, developments in quantum mechanics provide strong empirical grounds for the thesis that certain subatomic events — such as the spontaneous decay of a radioactive atom — are genuinely uncaused. The empirical evidence is robust, well-replicated, and accepted by the preponderance of the scientific community. Her a priori intuition that every event has a cause now stands in tension with compelling empirical findings. It appears that her belief, though non-empirically grounded, is no longer fully justified, for the a priori warrant has been undermined by experience. The analysis, then, must accommodate the possibility that a priori justification is defeasible by empirical evidence.

definition: A belief is justified if and only if (i) the belief is integrated into the subject's overall cognitive system, (ii) the integration involves coherent connections with the subject's other beliefs, experiences, and cognitive dispositions, and (iii) cognitive integration of this sort is sufficient for the belief's justification.
conditions: The belief is integrated into the subject's overall cognitive system
Integration involves coherent connections with other beliefs, experiences, and cognitive dispositions
Cognitive integration suffices for justification
counterexample: A subject raised in an isolated community holds a belief in a particular creation myth. The belief is deeply integrated into her cognitive system: it coheres with her other beliefs about the natural world, her cultural experiences, and her cognitive dispositions. It is, as it were, a load-bearing element of her intellectual architecture. Yet the belief is false and is sustained only because her cognitive system has been shaped entirely by the epistemic limitations of her community, with no access to the scientific evidence which would displace it.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that cognitive integration must occur within a system that has had adequate exposure to relevant evidence and is not systematically isolated from sources of correction.
passage: Cognitive integration has been proposed as a criterion of epistemic justification, the idea being that a belief is justified when it is woven into the fabric of the subject's overall cognitive system in a coherent and mutually reinforcing manner. On this analysis, what matters is not merely that the belief cohere with other beliefs but that it be connected with the subject's experiences and cognitive dispositions in a way that reflects genuine integration. This integration, it is held, is sufficient for justification. One might observe that the account has much to recommend it, for it captures the thought that justified beliefs are those which participate in and contribute to a well-functioning cognitive economy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be described. Imagine a subject who has been raised from birth in an isolated community whose members hold a distinctive creation myth. The subject's belief in this myth is deeply integrated into her cognitive system. It coheres with her beliefs about the origin of rivers and mountains, with her experiences of the rituals and narratives of her community, and with her cognitive dispositions — her habits of explanation, her patterns of inference, her expectations about the natural order. The belief is not a peripheral or idle element of her psychology; it is central to her intellectual life. Yet the belief is false, and it is sustained only because her cognitive system has been shaped within the confines of a community that has had no access to the geological, biological, and astronomical evidence which would displace the myth. Her cognitive integration is genuine, but the system into which the belief is integrated has been formed in isolation from relevant sources of correction. The analysis must, it appears, be supplemented with the requirement that cognitive integration occur within a system that has had adequate exposure to the relevant evidence and is not systematically cut off from sources of epistemic correction.

definition: A belief is occurrent if and only if (i) the subject consciously entertains a proposition at a given time, (ii) the subject is introspectively aware of so entertaining it, and (iii) the proposition figures as the content of a present mental episode rather than a mere stored disposition.
conditions: The subject consciously entertains a proposition at a given time
The subject is introspectively aware of entertaining it
The proposition figures as the content of a present mental episode rather than a mere stored disposition
counterexample: A skilled chess player, upon seeing a board position, instantly recognises that a certain move would be catastrophic, yet cannot be said to entertain any explicit proposition at that moment; the recognition is immediate and unreflective. The belief that the move is ruinous is present and operative, but it is not consciously entertained in the manner the analysis requires.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to accommodate beliefs that are cognitively active and causally efficacious in guiding behaviour without being explicitly or reflectively entertained as propositional contents.
passage: One might suppose that the notion of an occurrent belief is tolerably clear: it is a belief that is, so to speak, before the mind at the moment of its operation. The analysis under consideration holds that a belief is occurrent if and only if the subject consciously entertains a proposition, is introspectively aware of doing so, and the proposition figures as the content of a present mental episode rather than a stored disposition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of skilled performance. Consider a chess player of considerable experience who, upon surveying the board, immediately perceives that a particular move would be ruinous. It appears that the player does believe, at that very moment, that the move in question would lead to disaster, and this belief is no mere dormant disposition but an active element in the player's deliberation. Nevertheless, the player need not consciously entertain any proposition, nor be introspectively aware of doing so; the recognition is, as it were, absorbed into the perceptual apprehension of the position. The belief is present and operative, guiding the player's conduct, yet it fails to satisfy the conditions laid down by the analysis. One is thus compelled to recognise that occurrent belief may encompass states that are cognitively active without being reflectively or propositionally entertained in the manner the definition demands.

definition: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
conditions: The subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when suitably questioned
The disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process
The subject would sincerely affirm the proposition under normal conditions upon reflection
counterexample: A person who has never considered the proposition that no elephant weighs less than one gram would, if asked, readily assent to it, and would do so sincerely. Yet the disposition to assent is not grounded in any prior cognitive episode concerning elephants and grams; it arises from general background knowledge that has never been synthesised into that particular proposition.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the possibility that a disposition to assent may arise from the logical or inferential consequences of existing beliefs without any prior cognitive episode specifically directed at the proposition in question.
passage: It is commonly held that many of our beliefs are not at any given moment before the mind but exist, as it were, in a dispositional form. The analysis here considered maintains that a belief is dispositional if and only if the subject is disposed to assent to the relevant proposition when the question is suitably raised, the disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and the subject would sincerely affirm the proposition under normal conditions. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Consider the proposition that no elephant weighs less than one gram. It appears that virtually any rational adult would assent to this proposition if asked, and would do so with complete sincerity. Yet it seems most unlikely that the typical person has ever entertained this precise proposition, or that any cognitive episode specifically concerning elephants and grams has occurred in the person's mental history. The disposition to assent arises not from a prior episode of belief-formation but from the subject's general knowledge of the natural world, which yields the relevant judgement as a matter of simple inference when the occasion demands. The analysis, however, requires that the disposition be grounded in some prior cognitive episode, and this condition is not satisfied. One is therefore led to conclude that the notion of dispositional belief extends beyond what the analysis can accommodate, for it must include propositions to which the subject has never attended but which follow trivially from what the subject already knows.

definition: A subject fully believes a proposition if and only if (i) the subject assigns to the proposition a subjective probability of one, (ii) the subject is prepared to act without hedging on the truth of the proposition, and (iii) the subject would not seek further evidence bearing upon the proposition under ordinary circumstances.
conditions: The subject assigns a subjective probability of one to the proposition
The subject is prepared to act without hedging on its truth
The subject would not seek further evidence under ordinary circumstances
counterexample: A mountaineer fully believes that the rope is secure and proceeds to climb without hesitation or hedging, nor does she seek further evidence of its integrity. Yet if offered a wager at very long odds — say, her life savings against a trivial sum — on whether the rope has been microscopically examined, she would decline, revealing that her subjective probability is not strictly one.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for the distinction between practical certainty, which suffices for unhesitating action, and the strict probabilistic certainty of assigning probability one, which full belief in ordinary contexts rarely attains.
passage: The distinction between full belief and partial credence has exercised philosophers who seek to reconcile the traditional, all-or-nothing conception of belief with the more refined apparatus of subjective probability. The analysis before us holds that a subject fully believes a proposition if and only if the subject assigns it a subjective probability of one, is prepared to act without hedging on its truth, and would not ordinarily seek further evidence concerning it. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the following scenario. Suppose a mountaineer is about to ascend a cliff-face. She fully believes, in every ordinary sense, that her rope is secure. She proceeds to climb without the slightest hesitation, and it would not occur to her under normal circumstances to seek additional evidence of the rope's reliability. By the lights of conditions (ii) and (iii), she fully believes. Yet were she offered a peculiar wager — her life savings against a modest sum — contingent upon whether the rope has been subjected to microscopic examination for hidden defects, she would very likely decline the wager. This reluctance reveals that her subjective probability, though exceedingly high, does not quite reach unity. She is, as one might say, practically certain but not absolutely so. The analysis, however, treats the assignment of probability one as a necessary condition, and this condition is not met. It appears, therefore, that full belief as ordinarily understood admits of a degree of residual uncertainty that the strict probabilistic rendering cannot capture.

definition: A belief is rational if and only if (i) the belief is supported by the available evidence, (ii) the subject has formed the belief through a reliable cognitive process, and (iii) the subject is not aware of any undefeated counter-evidence.
conditions: The belief is supported by the available evidence
The subject has formed the belief through a reliable cognitive process
The subject is not aware of any undefeated counter-evidence
counterexample: A scientist forms the belief that a certain drug is effective on the basis of a well-conducted clinical trial, through reliable methodology, and is unaware of any counter-evidence. Unknown to her, a second trial with equally sound methodology has produced contrary results, but its publication has been suppressed.
