Her belief satisfies every stated condition, yet one might hesitate to call it fully rational, for the total body of evidence — had it been accessible — would not support the belief.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the evidence upon which the belief is based should not be misleadingly incomplete owing to factors beyond the subject's ken, such as the systematic suppression or unavailability of relevant data.
passage: It is widely agreed that not all beliefs are on an equal footing; some are rational and others are not, and the difference is commonly thought to reside in the relationship between the belief and the evidence that bears upon it. The analysis under consideration proposes that a belief is rational if and only if it is supported by the available evidence, has been formed through a reliable cognitive process, and the subject is not aware of any undefeated counter-evidence. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the domain of scientific enquiry. Suppose a medical researcher forms the belief that a certain pharmaceutical compound is effective in treating a particular ailment. She does so on the basis of a carefully conducted clinical trial, employing sound statistical methodology, and she is unaware of any evidence pointing in the contrary direction. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Yet suppose further that a second trial, conducted with equal rigour by an independent team, has yielded results that strongly disconfirm the drug's efficacy, but that this second trial has been suppressed by the drug's manufacturer and never published. The researcher's evidence, though genuine and well-handled, is misleadingly incomplete through no fault of her own. It appears that there is something deficient in calling her belief fully rational, for the total body of evidence — the evidence that exists, whether or not she has access to it — does not support the conclusion she has drawn. The analysis, by restricting its attention to the evidence available to the subject, fails to capture this dimension of rational appraisal.

definition: Doxastic voluntarism obtains if and only if (i) the subject can choose to believe a proposition at will, (ii) this choice is not mediated by any indirect strategy such as seeking evidence or manipulating one's environment, and (iii) the subject exercises the same kind of direct control over belief-formation as over ordinary intentional actions such as raising one's arm.
conditions: The subject can choose to believe a proposition at will
The choice is not mediated by indirect strategies such as evidence-seeking
The subject exercises the same direct control over belief as over ordinary intentional actions
counterexample: A person who desires to believe that a estranged friend bears her no ill will may succeed in bringing herself to believe this by selectively attending to memories of past kindness and avoiding evidence of hostility. She has, in a sense, chosen her belief, yet the choice was mediated by indirect strategies — selective attention and evidence avoidance — rather than exercised directly in the manner of raising one's arm.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to distinguish between direct doxastic control and indirect doxastic influence, thereby excluding cases where beliefs are voluntarily shaped through mediating strategies that fall short of immediate, unmediated choice.
passage: The question whether we can choose our beliefs as we choose our actions has been a matter of persistent philosophical dispute. Doxastic voluntarism, as here analysed, obtains if and only if the subject can choose to believe a proposition at will, this choice is not mediated by any indirect strategy such as the deliberate seeking of evidence or the manipulation of one's epistemic environment, and the subject exercises the same sort of direct control over belief-formation as over ordinary intentional actions. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the familiar phenomenon of motivated belief. Suppose a person wishes very much to believe that an estranged friend harbours no resentment towards her. By a sustained campaign of selective attention — dwelling upon fond memories, avoiding situations in which evidence of hostility might present itself, reinterpreting ambiguous remarks in a favourable light — she succeeds in bringing herself to believe that the friend's attitude is benign. It appears that she has, in some meaningful sense, chosen to believe; the belief is not one that was thrust upon her by the evidence but was actively cultivated in accordance with her desires. Yet the choice was not direct and unmediated in the way that raising one's arm is direct. It was accomplished through indirect strategies of precisely the sort the analysis excludes. One might observe that the analysis, by insisting on direct control, sets the bar so high that it excludes the very cases that make doxastic voluntarism an interesting and plausible thesis, namely those in which beliefs are shaped by the will through the mediation of attention, enquiry, and interpretation.

definition: A subject believes a proposition, as distinct from merely accepting it, if and only if (i) the subject regards the proposition as true, (ii) the subject's attitude towards the proposition is involuntary and not subject to pragmatic considerations, and (iii) the subject would not abandon the attitude merely upon discovering that holding it ceases to be useful.
conditions: The subject regards the proposition as true
The attitude is involuntary and not subject to pragmatic considerations
The subject would not abandon the attitude merely upon its ceasing to be useful
counterexample: A barrister, in the course of defending her client, comes to regard it as true that the client is innocent — not merely as a posture adopted for the courtroom, but as a genuine conviction. Yet this conviction was originally formed under pragmatic pressures: the need to mount a vigorous defence required her to adopt the stance of the client's innocence, and over time the stance became sincere. Were the case to be lost and the pragmatic pressure to vanish, she might well retain the belief. Her belief satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but originated in a manner that violates condition (ii).
missing_condition: The analysis does not accommodate beliefs that originate under pragmatic pressures but subsequently become genuine and involuntary, thereby conflating the genesis of an attitude with its present character.
passage: Philosophers have frequently sought to distinguish belief from acceptance, the latter being understood as a cognitive attitude adopted for practical or theoretical purposes without commitment to truth. The analysis before us holds that a subject believes a proposition, as distinct from merely accepting it, if and only if the subject regards the proposition as true, the attitude is involuntary and not subject to pragmatic considerations, and the subject would not abandon the attitude merely upon its ceasing to be useful. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the legal profession. Suppose a barrister undertakes the defence of a client charged with a serious offence. Initially, she accepts the proposition that her client is innocent as a working hypothesis — a stance adopted for the pragmatic purpose of mounting an effective defence. Over the course of preparing the case, however, she examines the evidence with care and gradually comes to regard it as genuinely true that her client is innocent. The attitude is no longer a mere posture; it is a sincere conviction. Were the case to conclude and the pragmatic pressure to evaporate, she would in all likelihood continue to hold the belief. Thus conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Yet the genesis of the belief was thoroughly pragmatic — it arose from the professional necessity of adopting the client's perspective. Condition (ii) requires that the attitude be involuntary and not subject to pragmatic considerations, and the manner in which the belief was formed appears to violate this requirement. The analysis, it seems, conflates the aetiology of a belief with its present epistemic character, and thereby excludes attitudes that have passed from acceptance into genuine belief.

definition: A subject assumes a proposition, as distinct from believing it, if and only if (i) the subject entertains the proposition without commitment to its truth, (ii) the subject adopts the proposition provisionally for the purposes of reasoning or enquiry, and (iii) the subject is at all times prepared to abandon the proposition without psychological resistance should it prove unhelpful.
conditions: The subject entertains the proposition without commitment to its truth
The proposition is adopted provisionally for purposes of reasoning or enquiry
The subject is prepared to abandon the proposition without psychological resistance
counterexample: A physicist working within Newtonian mechanics assumes, for the purposes of a particular calculation, that space is Euclidean. She does not, strictly speaking, commit herself to the truth of this proposition, for she is aware that general relativity supersedes it. Yet after decades of working within this framework, she finds that she cannot abandon the assumption without considerable psychological resistance; it has become so deeply embedded in her habits of thought that relinquishing it feels like a genuine intellectual loss.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the phenomenon whereby long-standing assumptions become psychologically entrenched, so that the subject can no longer abandon them without resistance, even though they were originally adopted in a spirit of mere provisionality.
passage: It is natural to distinguish between what one believes and what one merely assumes. The analysis here considered holds that a subject assumes a proposition, as distinct from believing it, if and only if the subject entertains the proposition without commitment to its truth, adopts it provisionally for the purposes of reasoning or enquiry, and is at all times prepared to abandon it without psychological resistance should it prove unhelpful. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of the physical sciences. Consider a physicist who has worked for many years within the framework of Newtonian mechanics. She assumes, for the purposes of a great many calculations, that space is Euclidean — a proposition she does not, strictly speaking, endorse as true, since she is well aware that the general theory of relativity provides a more accurate description of spatial geometry. Her adoption of the Euclidean assumption is, in its origin, thoroughly provisional. Yet after decades of employing this assumption, it has become so deeply woven into her habits of thought and modes of reasoning that she cannot relinquish it without considerable intellectual discomfort. It is no longer the case that she is prepared to abandon it without psychological resistance; on the contrary, the assumption has acquired a kind of cognitive inertia that makes it resistant to revision. The assumption thus satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails to satisfy condition (iii). One is led to observe that the analysis does not adequately account for the manner in which long-standing assumptions may become psychologically entrenched, occupying a territory between mere provisional supposition and full-blooded belief.

definition: A belief is implicit if and only if (i) the subject has never consciously entertained the proposition in question, (ii) the proposition is nonetheless a logical consequence of the subject's explicitly held beliefs, and (iii) the subject would assent to the proposition immediately upon its being presented.
conditions: The subject has never consciously entertained the proposition
The proposition is a logical consequence of explicitly held beliefs
The subject would assent immediately upon its being presented
counterexample: A competent speaker of English implicitly believes that no bachelor is married, yet this proposition is not a logical consequence of any set of explicitly held beliefs; it is, rather, a consequence of the subject's grasp of the meanings of the words involved. The subject would assent immediately, and has presumably never consciously entertained the proposition in precisely those terms, but the second condition is not met because the belief arises from linguistic competence rather than logical derivation from other beliefs.
missing_condition: The analysis does not accommodate implicit beliefs that arise from semantic or conceptual competence rather than from logical entailment relations among the subject's explicitly held beliefs.
passage: The notion of implicit belief is invoked to account for the vast number of propositions to which a subject would assent but which the subject has never explicitly considered. The analysis under examination holds that a belief is implicit if and only if the subject has never consciously entertained the proposition, the proposition is a logical consequence of the subject's explicitly held beliefs, and the subject would assent to it immediately upon its being presented. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the domain of linguistic understanding. Consider the proposition that no bachelor is married. It appears that any competent speaker of English would assent to this proposition immediately, and it is plausible that many such speakers have never consciously entertained it in precisely those terms. Conditions (i) and (iii) are thus satisfied. Yet it is far from clear that condition (ii) is met, for the proposition that no bachelor is married is not a logical consequence of any particular set of explicitly held beliefs. It is, rather, a consequence of the subject's grasp of the concepts involved — her understanding of what the words 'bachelor' and 'married' mean. The belief arises from semantic competence, not from logical derivation. One might observe that the class of implicit beliefs is broader than the analysis allows, for it must encompass not only the logical consequences of what one explicitly believes but also the conceptual truths that are, as it were, built into one's mastery of the relevant vocabulary. The analysis, by restricting implicit belief to logical consequence, unduly narrows the extension of the concept.

definition: A subject is self-deceived with respect to a proposition p if and only if (i) the evidence available to the subject strongly supports p, (ii) the subject believes not-p, and (iii) the subject's belief in not-p is motivated by a desire that p be false.
conditions: The evidence available to the subject strongly supports p
The subject believes not-p
The subject's belief in not-p is motivated by a desire that p be false
counterexample: A devoted father believes that his son is a person of good character, despite substantial evidence that the son has been involved in dishonest dealings. The father's belief is not motivated by a desire that his son be honest — he has no particular wish on the matter that precedes his assessment — but rather by an inability to revise deeply held convictions about those he loves. The distortion arises from cognitive rigidity and attachment, not from a prior desire that the contrary proposition be false.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for cases of self-deception driven by cognitive attachment or the inability to revise deeply held convictions, where the motivating factor is not a desire that p be false but rather a structural feature of the subject's psychology.
passage: Self-deception is a phenomenon of considerable interest to epistemologists, for it represents a case in which a subject's beliefs are systematically at odds with the evidence. The analysis before us holds that a subject is self-deceived with respect to a proposition p if and only if the evidence available to the subject strongly supports p, the subject believes not-p, and the subject's belief in not-p is motivated by a desire that p be false. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the following scenario. Suppose a devoted father has a son who has, by any reasonable assessment, been involved in a series of dishonest transactions. The evidence of the son's dishonesty is substantial and readily available to the father. Yet the father believes, with apparent sincerity, that his son is a person of good character. We should, it appears, wish to describe the father as self-deceived. But does his belief satisfy condition (iii)? It is not altogether clear that the father's belief is motivated by a desire that the proposition of his son's dishonesty be false. The father does not first desire that his son be honest and then form a belief accordingly; rather, his conviction of his son's good character is so deeply embedded in his conception of his family and himself that he simply cannot bring himself to revise it. The distortion is a product of cognitive attachment and psychological rigidity, not of a motivating desire in any straightforward sense. The analysis, by requiring that the false belief be desire-driven, fails to capture this important variety of self-deception.

definition: Moore's paradox arises whenever (i) a subject sincerely asserts a proposition p, (ii) the subject simultaneously avows that they do not believe p, and (iii) the conjunction of the assertion and the avowal is absurd in a way that cannot be reduced to a straightforward logical contradiction.
conditions: A subject sincerely asserts a proposition p
The subject simultaneously avows that they do not believe p
The conjunction is absurd but not reducible to a straightforward logical contradiction
counterexample: A weathered diplomat, in the course of delicate negotiations, asserts to her counterpart that a proposed treaty is advantageous to both parties, while privately and simultaneously avowing to herself that she does not believe it. The conjunction of assertion and disbelief seems neither absurd nor paradoxical; it is merely strategic insincerity. The assertion is not sincere in the relevant sense, yet the diplomat would describe her own mental state in precisely the Moorean form: 'The treaty is advantageous, but I do not believe it is.'
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately specify the conditions under which the assertion counts as sincere, thereby admitting cases of strategic or performative assertion where the Moorean conjunction is intelligible rather than absurd.
passage: The paradox associated with G. E. Moore concerns the peculiar absurdity of asserting a proposition whilst simultaneously avowing that one does not believe it. The analysis here considered holds that Moore's paradox arises whenever a subject sincerely asserts a proposition p, simultaneously avows that she does not believe p, and the conjunction of assertion and avowal is absurd in a way that resists reduction to straightforward logical contradiction. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the sphere of diplomatic intercourse. Suppose a seasoned diplomat, engaged in the negotiation of a treaty, asserts to her counterpart that the proposed terms are advantageous to both parties. She does so with every outward mark of sincerity — a measured tone, an appearance of conviction. Yet she privately and simultaneously avows to herself that she does not believe the proposition she has asserted; she regards the terms as disadvantageous to the other party and has made her assertion for purely strategic reasons. If we were to attribute to her the Moorean sentence — 'The treaty is advantageous, but I do not believe it is' — the conjunction would not strike us as absurd in the manner the paradox requires. It would be merely an accurate description of a familiar form of strategic insincerity. The difficulty, it appears, lies in condition (i): the analysis stipulates that the assertion be sincere, but what counts as sincerity is itself a matter of considerable complexity. The diplomat's assertion is offered with every appearance of sincerity and in a context where assertion is expected, yet it is not sincere in the sense the analysis intends. The paradox, properly so called, arises only when the assertion is genuinely sincere, but the analysis does not furnish adequate criteria for distinguishing genuine from apparent sincerity.

definition: Belief revision is rational if and only if (i) the subject abandons a previously held belief upon encountering evidence that contradicts it, (ii) the revision is proportional to the strength of the counter-evidence, and (iii) the revised belief-set is logically consistent.
conditions: The subject abandons a belief upon encountering contradicting evidence
The revision is proportional to the strength of the counter-evidence
The revised belief-set is logically consistent
counterexample: A historian who has long believed that a certain medieval battle took place in 1147 encounters a newly discovered manuscript that dates the battle to 1149. She revises her belief accordingly, and the revision is proportional to the evidence, which is strong. Her revised belief-set is logically consistent. Yet the manuscript was, unbeknownst to her, a forgery. The revision satisfies all three conditions but is not rational, for it was prompted by misleading evidence.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the counter-evidence be genuine or veridical, thereby counting as rational revisions that are prompted by misleading, fabricated, or otherwise defective evidence.
passage: The revision of belief in the light of new evidence is commonly regarded as a hallmark of rational enquiry. The analysis under consideration holds that belief revision is rational if and only if the subject abandons a previously held belief upon encountering evidence that contradicts it, the revision is proportional to the strength of the counter-evidence, and the revised belief-set is logically consistent. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the practice of historical scholarship. Suppose a historian has long held the well-supported belief that a particular medieval battle took place in the year 1147. She then encounters a newly discovered manuscript, apparently of impeccable provenance, which dates the battle to 1149. The evidence is strong, and she revises her belief accordingly, shifting her dating of the battle in a manner proportional to the strength of the new evidence. Her revised belief-set remains logically consistent. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Yet suppose further that the manuscript is, in fact, a clever forgery, unknown to the historian and undetectable by ordinary scholarly methods. Her revision, though it satisfies every condition laid down by the analysis, was prompted by evidence that is misleading and ultimately false. One might hesitate to call such a revision fully rational, for rationality in belief revision seems to require not only that one respond appropriately to the evidence as it appears but also that the evidence itself be of the right sort — that it be genuine rather than specious. The analysis, by attending solely to the subject's response to the evidence and not to the quality or provenance of the evidence itself, fails to capture this requirement.

definition: A group believes a proposition if and only if (i) a majority of the group's members individually believe the proposition, (ii) the members are aware that their fellow members hold the same belief, and (iii) the belief is endorsed through some recognised procedure of collective decision-making.
conditions: A majority of the group's members individually believe the proposition
The members are aware that fellow members hold the same belief
The belief is endorsed through a recognised procedure of collective decision-making
counterexample: A scientific committee, having reviewed the evidence, votes to endorse the proposition that a particular chemical compound is safe for human consumption. The vote passes by the required procedure, and the committee's report states the conclusion. Yet a majority of the individual members privately believe the compound is unsafe; each voted in favour of the safety conclusion because of institutional pressure, funding considerations, or the belief that the others would vote that way. The group believes the compound is safe — it has so declared — yet conditions (i) and (ii) are not satisfied.
missing_condition: The analysis does not accommodate cases in which a group's collectively endorsed belief diverges from the beliefs of the majority of its members, a phenomenon that arises from social pressures, procedural artefacts, or the aggregation of individually insincere endorsements.
passage: The question whether groups, as distinct from their individual members, may be said to hold beliefs is one of considerable philosophical interest. The analysis before us holds that a group believes a proposition if and only if a majority of the group's members individually believe it, the members are aware that their fellow members hold the same belief, and the belief is endorsed through some recognised procedure of collective decision-making. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the workings of expert committees. Suppose a scientific advisory committee is charged with determining whether a certain chemical compound is safe for human consumption. After reviewing the evidence, the committee votes, by its established procedure, to endorse the proposition that the compound is safe. The committee's official report states this conclusion unequivocally. Yet suppose further that a majority of the individual committee members privately believe the compound is not safe. Each member voted in favour of the safety conclusion for reasons unrelated to her private assessment: institutional pressure, concern for the committee's reputation, the desire to secure future funding, or the mistaken belief that all the others genuinely endorsed the conclusion. Conditions (i) and (ii) of the analysis are thus not satisfied — the majority do not individually believe the proposition, and they are not aware of genuine agreement among their fellows. Yet the group, qua group, has arrived at and endorsed the belief through its recognised procedure. It appears that collective belief can obtain even when it is, so to speak, ungrounded in the beliefs of the individual members, and the analysis fails to capture this possibility.

definition: A belief is de re if and only if (i) the belief is about a particular object or individual, (ii) the subject stands in an appropriate epistemic relation to that object — typically acquaintance or causal contact, and (iii) the truth of the belief depends on the properties of the actual object rather than on any mode of presentation under which the subject conceives of it.
conditions: The belief is about a particular object or individual
The subject stands in an appropriate epistemic relation to that object
The truth of the belief depends on the actual object's properties rather than on a mode of presentation
counterexample: An astronomer in antiquity believes of Hesperus — the evening star, with which she has direct perceptual acquaintance — that it is a planet. She also believes of Phosphorus — the morning star — that it is a star, not a planet. Both beliefs are about particular objects, she is perceptually acquainted with both, and the truth of her beliefs depends on the actual properties of the objects in question. Yet Hesperus is Phosphorus; she holds contradictory de re beliefs about one and the same object without any failure of rationality, because the modes of presentation are different. The analysis, by stipulating that truth depends on the actual object rather than a mode of presentation, cannot explain how rational subjects hold inconsistent de re beliefs about a single object.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to account for the role that distinct modes of presentation play in individuating de re beliefs, thereby rendering inexplicable the rationality of holding contradictory beliefs about a single object encountered under different guises.
passage: The distinction between de re and de dicto belief has been a staple of philosophical analysis since at least the middle of the last century. The analysis here considered holds that a belief is de re if and only if it is about a particular object or individual, the subject stands in an appropriate epistemic relation to that object — typically one of acquaintance or causal contact — and the truth of the belief depends on the properties of the actual object rather than on any mode of presentation under which the subject conceives of it. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of astronomy. An astronomer in the ancient world observes a bright celestial body in the evening sky and, being directly acquainted with it, forms the belief that it is a planet. She calls it Hesperus. The same astronomer observes a bright celestial body in the morning sky and forms the belief that it is a star and not a planet. She calls it Phosphorus. Both beliefs satisfy the conditions of the analysis: each is about a particular object, the astronomer is perceptually acquainted with each, and the truth of each belief depends on the properties of the actual celestial body. Yet Hesperus is Phosphorus — they are one and the same object, namely the planet Venus. The astronomer thus holds, of a single object, the belief that it is a planet and the belief that it is not a planet. She is not thereby irrational; her beliefs are perfectly reasonable given her epistemic situation. But the analysis, by insisting that truth depends on the actual object rather than on a mode of presentation, cannot account for how a rational subject may hold contradictory de re beliefs about the same thing. The mode of presentation, which the analysis excludes from relevance, turns out to be essential to the individuation of de re beliefs.

definition: Belief guides action if and only if (i) the subject acts in a manner consistent with the truth of the believed proposition, (ii) the subject would have acted differently had they not held the belief, and (iii) the connection between belief and action is mediated by the subject's practical reasoning.
conditions: The subject acts consistently with the truth of the believed proposition
The subject would have acted differently without the belief
The connection between belief and action is mediated by practical reasoning
counterexample: A person sincerely believes that a particular bridge is unsafe, and her practical reasoning incorporates this belief. She would, under normal circumstances, avoid crossing the bridge. Yet on a particular occasion, being late and seeing no alternative route, she crosses it anyway. Her action is inconsistent with the truth of her belief, and she would not have acted differently had she lacked the belief, for she would have crossed the bridge in any case. The belief nonetheless exists and has guided her reasoning, even though it failed to determine her action.
missing_condition: The analysis does not acknowledge that beliefs may genuinely guide practical reasoning without invariably determining action, since competing practical pressures — urgency, necessity, weakness of will — may override a belief's behavioural influence.
passage: It is a familiar thesis that beliefs serve as guides to action — that to believe a proposition is, in some measure, to be disposed to act as though the proposition were true. The analysis under consideration holds that belief guides action if and only if the subject acts in a manner consistent with the truth of the believed proposition, the subject would have acted differently had she not held the belief, and the connection between belief and action is mediated by the subject's practical reasoning. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the relationship between belief and practical exigency. Suppose a person sincerely believes that a particular bridge is structurally unsound and dangerous to cross. Her practical reasoning incorporates this belief; she deliberates about alternative routes and, under ordinary circumstances, avoids the bridge entirely. Yet on a certain occasion she finds herself late for a matter of some urgency and can discern no alternative route. She crosses the bridge, fully believing it to be unsafe, acting against the content of her belief because the practical pressures of the moment override her doxastic caution. Her action is inconsistent with the truth of her belief, and it is not clear that she would have acted differently without the belief, for the urgency of her situation would have led her across the bridge regardless. Conditions (i) and (ii) are thus not satisfied. Yet it would be strange to deny that her belief about the bridge guided her reasoning; it did so, but its guidance was overridden by competing considerations. The analysis, by requiring that belief invariably issue in consistent action, neglects the possibility that beliefs may genuinely inform practical reasoning without thereby determining conduct.

definition: An assertion is sincere if and only if (i) the speaker believes the proposition asserted, (ii) the speaker intends to convey that they believe the proposition, and (iii) the speaker does not intend to mislead the audience regarding their doxastic state.
conditions: The speaker believes the proposition asserted
The speaker intends to convey that they believe the proposition
The speaker does not intend to mislead the audience regarding their doxastic state
counterexample: A teacher, in the course of a Socratic lesson, asserts to her pupils that the earth is flat. She does not believe this proposition, and she intends her pupils eventually to discover its falsity through their own reasoning. She does not, however, intend to mislead them about her doxastic state in a harmful sense; her intent is pedagogical. The assertion fails condition (i) and arguably (ii), yet it is not a case of lying or insincerity in the ordinary sense — it is an assertion offered in a recognised pedagogical mode.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between contexts of assertion — ordinary, pedagogical, theatrical, and the like — and thereby classifies as insincere any assertion made in a context where the normal norms of sincere assertion are conventionally suspended.
passage: The connexion between sincere assertion and belief is commonly taken to be one of the most fundamental principles governing linguistic communication. The analysis before us holds that an assertion is sincere if and only if the speaker believes the proposition asserted, intends to convey that she believes it, and does not intend to mislead the audience regarding her doxastic state. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the practice of Socratic pedagogy. Suppose a teacher, in the course of a philosophy lesson, asserts to her pupils that the earth is flat. She does not believe this proposition; on the contrary, she holds firmly that the earth is roughly spherical. Her purpose in making the assertion is to provoke her pupils into critical examination of received claims and to lead them, through their own reasoning, to discover the falsity of what she has asserted. She does not intend to leave them permanently misled; her pedagogical aim is the very opposite of deception. Condition (i) of the analysis is plainly not satisfied, and condition (ii) is at best doubtful, for the teacher does not intend to convey that she genuinely believes the earth to be flat. By the lights of the analysis, her assertion is insincere. Yet it appears that calling it insincere does not quite capture the character of the speech act. The assertion is made within a recognised pedagogical context in which the ordinary norms of sincere assertion are conventionally relaxed. It is not a lie, nor is it an attempt at deception in any reprehensible sense. The analysis, by treating all assertions under a single set of conditions, fails to accommodate the diversity of contexts in which assertions may be offered and the corresponding variation in what sincerity requires.

definition: A subject's degree of belief in a proposition is accurately measured by them betting behaviour if and only if (i) the subject is willing to accept a bet at odds that reflect their subjective probability, (ii) the subject's betting behaviour is not distorted by risk aversion or other extraneous psychological factors, and (iii) the subject's preferences over wagers satisfy the axioms of expected utility theory.
conditions: The subject accepts bets at odds reflecting their subjective probability
Betting behaviour is not distorted by risk aversion or extraneous factors
Preferences over wagers satisfy the axioms of expected utility theory
counterexample: A gambler has a degree of belief of 0.5 that a coin is fair, and this is accurately reflected in her willingness to accept even-odds bets on coin-flips involving small sums. Yet when the stakes are raised to very large sums, she refuses to bet at even odds — not because her degree of belief has changed, but because the diminishing marginal utility of money makes the prospective loss loom larger than the prospective gain. Her betting behaviour no longer reflects her subjective probability, though condition (ii) demands it should not be distorted, and arguably it is not 'distorted' but rather rational given her utility function.
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately distinguish between distortions of betting behaviour and rational responses to features of the bet other than the probability in question, such as the magnitude of the stakes and the shape of the subject's utility function.
passage: The operationalist tradition in the philosophy of probability has long maintained that degrees of belief may be measured by betting behaviour: one's subjective probability for a proposition is revealed by the odds at which one is prepared to bet. The analysis under consideration holds that a subject's degree of belief is accurately measured by her betting behaviour if and only if she accepts bets at odds reflecting her subjective probability, her behaviour is not distorted by risk aversion or extraneous psychological factors, and her preferences over wagers satisfy the axioms of expected utility theory. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the familiar phenomenon of stake-sensitivity. Suppose a gambler has a well-calibrated degree of belief of one-half that a particular coin is fair. When invited to bet small sums at even odds on the outcome of a toss, she accepts readily, and her betting behaviour accurately reflects her subjective probability. Yet when the stakes are raised to very large sums — sums whose loss would materially affect her wellbeing — she declines to bet at even odds, despite the fact that her degree of belief in the fairness of the coin has not changed in the least. Condition (ii) of the analysis would classify this as a distortion introduced by risk aversion, an extraneous psychological factor. But one might observe that the gambler's reluctance is not a distortion at all; it is a perfectly rational response to the diminishing marginal utility of money. Her preferences may well satisfy the axioms of expected utility theory when utility rather than monetary value is taken as the relevant quantity. The analysis, by treating risk aversion as a distorting factor rather than a rational feature of decision-making, mischaracterises the relationship between degree of belief and betting behaviour.

definition: A belief is basic if and only if (i) the belief is not inferred from any other belief, (ii) the belief is justified independently of support from further beliefs, and (iii) the belief derives its justification from a direct, non-inferential source such as perceptual experience or rational intuition.
conditions: The belief is not inferred from any other belief
The belief is justified independently of support from further beliefs
The belief derives its justification from a direct non-inferential source such as perception or rational intuition
counterexample: A person looks at a white wall illuminated by red lighting and forms the belief that the wall is red. The belief is not inferred from other beliefs; it arises directly from perceptual experience and appears to be justified independently of further beliefs. Yet the belief is false — the wall is white — and its apparent justification is undercut by the unusual lighting conditions. If background beliefs about the lighting were taken into account, the subject would not form the belief. The belief satisfies all three conditions but is neither justified nor a candidate for a foundation of knowledge.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the non-inferential source from which a basic belief derives its justification be operating under conditions conducive to the formation of true beliefs, thereby admitting as basic beliefs that are formed under epistemically defective conditions.
passage: The foundationalist programme in epistemology rests upon the notion that certain beliefs are basic — that they require no inferential support from other beliefs but are justified by some direct, non-inferential source. The analysis here considered holds that a belief is basic if and only if it is not inferred from any other belief, is justified independently of support from further beliefs, and derives its justification from a direct source such as perceptual experience or rational intuition. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from a familiar scenario involving perceptual illusion. Suppose a subject enters a room in which a white wall is illuminated by a concealed red lamp. She looks at the wall and forms the belief that it is red. This belief satisfies all three conditions of the analysis: it is not inferred from other beliefs but arises directly from perceptual experience, and it appears to be justified independently of support from further beliefs. The non-inferential source — visual perception — is precisely the sort of direct source the analysis has in mind. Yet the belief is false, and its apparent justification is entirely spurious. The perceptual experience, though genuine, is misleading owing to the abnormal lighting conditions. Were the subject to take into account her background beliefs about artificial illumination — or were she even aware that the lighting was unusual — she would not form the belief in question. It appears, therefore, that the analysis is too permissive: it admits as basic any belief that arises from perception or intuition, without requiring that the perceptual or intuitive source be functioning under conditions that are conducive to the production of true beliefs. A more adequate account would impose some condition of epistemic normality upon the circumstances under which the basic belief is formed.

definition: Belief is a mental state if and only if (i) it is an enduring internal condition of the subject, (ii) it has representational content — that is, it represents the world as being a certain way, and (iii) it is causally efficacious in producing behaviour in conjunction with the subject's desires.
conditions: It is an enduring internal condition of the subject
It has representational content representing the world as being a certain way
It is causally efficacious in producing behaviour in conjunction with desires
counterexample: A sophisticated chess-playing computer has internal states that endure over time, represent the positions of pieces on the board, and are causally efficacious in producing moves in conjunction with its objective function (which might be regarded as analogous to desire). All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied, yet we might hesitate to attribute genuine belief to the machine, holding that something essential — consciousness, understanding, or phenomenal awareness — is absent.
missing_condition: The analysis does not include any condition requiring that the subject possess consciousness, phenomenal awareness, or understanding, thereby admitting as believers systems that merely simulate the functional profile of belief without instantiating its essential mental character.
passage: It is a commonplace of folk psychology that belief is a mental state — an enduring condition of the mind that represents the world as being a certain way and, in concert with desire, produces behaviour. The analysis under consideration holds that belief is a mental state if and only if it is an enduring internal condition of the subject, has representational content, and is causally efficacious in producing behaviour in conjunction with the subject's desires. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of artificial systems. Consider a chess-playing computer of considerable sophistication. The computer has internal states that endure over time — stored evaluations of board positions, for example — and these states have representational content, for they represent the positions of the pieces and the relative advantages of various configurations. Moreover, these internal states are causally efficacious in producing the computer's moves, and they operate in conjunction with what might be described, by analogy, as the system's desires — its objective function, which directs it towards checkmate. All three conditions of the analysis appear to be satisfied. Yet one might hesitate, and with some reason, to attribute genuine belief to the chess computer. It appears that something is missing — something that distinguishes a system that genuinely believes from one that merely behaves as if it does. What is missing might be described variously as consciousness, phenomenal awareness, or genuine understanding of the content represented. The analysis, cast as it is in purely functional terms, does not capture this dimension and is therefore too liberal in its application.

definition: Belief differs from imagination in that (i) belief carries with it a commitment to the truth of the proposition entertained, (ii) belief disposes the subject to act in accordance with the proposition's truth, and (iii) belief is involuntary — one cannot believe at will in the way one can imagine at will.
conditions: Belief carries a commitment to the truth of the proposition
Belief disposes the subject to act in accordance with the proposition's truth
Belief is involuntary rather than subject to the will
counterexample: A novelist, in the course of composing a work of fiction, imagines so vividly and persistently that her protagonist is in mortal danger that she finds herself in a state of genuine anxiety, disposed to act as though someone were actually threatened — she catches herself reaching for the telephone to call for help. The imagined scenario disposes her to act in accordance with its content and is not under her voluntary control in the moment, yet she does not commit herself to the truth of the proposition that anyone is in danger. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not — the attitude is imaginative, not doxastic.
missing_condition: The analysis treats involuntariness and behavioural disposition as sufficient to distinguish belief from imagination, but does not account for cases of vivid, immersive imagination that share these features while lacking the commitment to truth that characterises belief.
passage: The distinction between believing a proposition and merely imagining it is one that most philosophers regard as both real and important. The analysis here considered holds that belief differs from imagination in three respects: belief carries a commitment to the truth of the proposition entertained, belief disposes the subject to act in accordance with that truth, and belief is involuntary. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenology of literary creation. Consider a novelist who is deeply absorbed in the composition of a work of fiction. She imagines, with great vividness, that her protagonist is in grave peril — trapped in a burning building, let us say. The imaginative engagement is so intense and sustained that she finds herself genuinely anxious, her heart quickening and her muscles tense, disposed to act as though someone were actually in danger. She catches herself on the point of reaching for the telephone to summon assistance, so completely has the imagined scenario overtaken her practical dispositions. Moreover, the state is not one she can simply switch off at will; the vividness of the imagining resists voluntary control in the moment, much as a belief resists being set aside by a simple act of will. Conditions (ii) and (iii) of the analysis appear to be satisfied. Yet the novelist does not commit herself to the truth of the proposition that anyone is in danger; she knows perfectly well that the scenario is fictional. Her attitude is one of vivid imagination, not of belief. The analysis, by relying upon involuntariness and behavioural disposition as distinguishing marks, fails to capture the distinction it seeks to draw, for these features can be present in imaginative states of sufficient intensity.

definition: Belief perseverance occurs if and only if (i) the subject continues to hold a belief after the evidence that originally supported it has been discredited, (ii) the subject is aware that the original evidence has been discredited, and (iii) the subject has no independent evidence that supports the belief.
conditions: The subject continues to hold the belief after its original evidential support is discredited
The subject is aware that the original evidence has been discredited
The subject has no independent evidence supporting the belief
counterexample: A juror, having been told during trial that a crucial piece of testimony was perjured and should be disregarded, finds that she continues to believe the defendant is guilty. She is fully aware that the testimony has been discredited. However, she possesses independent evidence — the defendant's demeanour, other circumstantial details — that she regards as supporting the belief, though this independent evidence is extremely weak and would not, on its own, have prompted the belief. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, for independent evidence exists, yet the case is a paradigm instance of belief perseverance.
missing_condition: The analysis requires the complete absence of independent evidence, but belief perseverance characteristically occurs when the independent evidence is insufficient to support the belief, not when it is entirely absent.
passage: The phenomenon of belief perseverance — the tendency to continue holding a belief even after the evidence for it has been undermined — has been extensively studied by psychologists and is of considerable interest to epistemologists. The analysis under consideration holds that belief perseverance occurs if and only if the subject continues to hold a belief after its original evidential support has been discredited, is aware of this discrediting, and possesses no independent evidence in favour of the belief. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the setting of a criminal trial. Suppose a juror hears testimony that strongly implicates the defendant, and on the basis of this testimony forms the belief that the defendant is guilty. Subsequently, the testimony is revealed to have been perjured, and the judge instructs the jury to disregard it. The juror is fully aware that the testimony is discredited. Yet she finds that she continues to believe in the defendant's guilt. Is condition (iii) satisfied? It appears not, for the juror does possess some independent grounds for her belief — the defendant's nervous demeanour, certain circumstantial details she noted during the trial — though she would readily concede, if pressed, that these grounds are far too weak to support a finding of guilt on their own. The case is a paradigm instance of belief perseverance, yet the analysis excludes it because the subject possesses some, however inadequate, independent evidence. The analysis is thus too narrow: it requires the total absence of independent evidence, whereas the phenomenon it seeks to describe characteristically involves the retention of belief in circumstances where the remaining evidence is insufficient to warrant it.

definition: A belief is tacit if and only if (i) the subject has never explicitly formulated the belief in linguistic or propositional form, (ii) the belief is nonetheless manifested in the subject's behaviour and cognitive processes, and (iii) the subject would, upon reflection, acknowledge the belief as their own.
conditions: The subject has never explicitly formulated the belief in linguistic or propositional form
The belief is manifested in the subject's behaviour and cognitive processes
The subject would upon reflection acknowledge the belief as their own
counterexample: A person raised in a culture with strong norms of politeness tacitly believes that it is wrong to interrupt others in conversation. She has never formulated this belief explicitly, and it is manifested in her unfailing practice of waiting for others to finish speaking. Yet upon reflection she does not acknowledge the belief as her own; she regards herself as indifferent to conversational norms and attributes her behaviour to mere habit rather than conviction. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not, despite the belief being genuinely operative in her conduct.
missing_condition: The analysis requires that the subject would reflectively acknowledge the belief, but tacit beliefs may be so deeply embedded that the subject fails to recognise them as beliefs, misattributing their behavioural manifestations to habit, temperament, or other non-doxastic sources.
passage: The notion of tacit belief is employed to capture those beliefs that, although never explicitly formulated by the subject, are nonetheless at work in her cognitive life and behaviour. The analysis here considered holds that a belief is tacit if and only if the subject has never explicitly formulated it, the belief is manifested in behaviour and cognitive processes, and the subject would upon reflection acknowledge it as her own. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of social behaviour. Consider a person who has been raised in a culture in which it is considered improper to interrupt others in conversation. She has internalised this norm to such a degree that she invariably waits for others to finish speaking before offering her own remarks. She has never formulated the belief that it is wrong to interrupt, yet the belief is plainly manifested in her conduct and in the cognitive processes that regulate her conversational behaviour — she monitors the speech of others, anticipates the conclusions of their utterances, and inhibits her own impulse to speak prematurely. Conditions (i) and (ii) are clearly satisfied. Yet when the matter is raised with her explicitly — when she is asked whether she believes it is wrong to interrupt — she demurs. She regards herself as rather indifferent to the niceties of conversational etiquette and attributes her behaviour to mere habit, to a kind of social inertia rather than to any conviction about the propriety of waiting one's turn. Condition (iii) is thus not satisfied. Yet it appears that the belief is genuinely operative; it is not mere habit, for she experiences discomfort and disapproval when others interrupt, which suggests a normative commitment rather than a mere behavioural regularity. The analysis, by requiring reflective acknowledgement, excludes beliefs that are too deeply embedded for the subject to recognise.

definition: A belief is core, as opposed to peripheral, if and only if (i) revising it would require substantial changes to the subject's other beliefs, (ii) the belief plays a central role in the subject's reasoning across many domains, and (iii) the subject regards the belief as more certain than most of them other beliefs.
conditions: Revising it would require substantial changes to other beliefs
The belief plays a central role in reasoning across many domains
The subject regards the belief as more certain than most other beliefs
counterexample: A research chemist holds the belief that a particular reagent is inert under standard conditions. This belief plays a central role in her experimental reasoning across many projects and is regarded by her as extremely certain. Yet revising it would require only localised adjustments to her other beliefs — she would need to re-examine certain experimental results, but the bulk of her theoretical commitments would remain untouched. The belief satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) but not condition (i), though it functions, in her intellectual life, very much as a core belief.
missing_condition: The analysis requires that revision would necessitate substantial changes to other beliefs, but a belief may function as core by virtue of its centrality to reasoning and its subjective certainty even when its revision would produce only modest downstream effects.
passage: The distinction between core and peripheral beliefs has been drawn by several philosophers in an effort to capture the observation that not all beliefs are equally deeply held or equally resistant to revision. The analysis under consideration holds that a belief is core if and only if revising it would require substantial changes to the subject's other beliefs, it plays a central role in reasoning across many domains, and the subject regards it as more certain than most of her other beliefs. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns beliefs that are practically indispensable yet theoretically isolable. Consider a research chemist who holds the belief that a particular reagent — one she employs in nearly all her experiments — is inert under standard laboratory conditions. This belief plays a central role in her reasoning across many domains of her research; it is implicated in the design of experiments, the interpretation of results, and the assessment of rival hypotheses. She regards it as extremely certain — as certain as almost anything she believes about chemistry. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are amply satisfied. Yet suppose the belief were to be revised — suppose she discovered that the reagent is not, after all, inert. The downstream effects would be significant but not sweeping. She would need to re-examine certain experimental results and perhaps revise some specific conclusions, but the great body of her theoretical commitments in chemistry would remain intact. Condition (i) is not satisfied, for the revision would not require the wholesale restructuring of her belief system. Nevertheless, the belief functions, in her intellectual life, as a core commitment — one she would be most reluctant to abandon and one whose centrality to her reasoning is beyond dispute. The analysis, it appears, sets the bar for coreness too high by insisting on wholesale downstream revision.

definition: Belief-desire psychology holds that an action is explained if and only if (i) the subject possesses a belief that a certain action will bring about a desired outcome, (ii) the subject possesses a desire for that outcome, and (iii) the belief and desire jointly cause the action through a process of practical reasoning.
conditions: The subject believes a certain action will bring about a desired outcome
The subject desires that outcome
The belief and desire jointly cause the action through practical reasoning
counterexample: A person desires a glass of water and believes that turning on the kitchen tap will produce one. The belief and desire are present, yet instead of reasoning practically about how to obtain water, she finds herself walking to the kitchen and turning on the tap in a wholly automatic, habitual manner, without any intervening process of deliberation or practical reasoning. The action is caused by the belief and desire, but not through practical reasoning as the analysis requires.
missing_condition: The analysis does not accommodate actions that are caused by beliefs and desires operating through habitual or automatic cognitive pathways rather than through explicit practical reasoning.
passage: The belief-desire model of action explanation is among the most venerable frameworks in the philosophy of mind and action. It holds that an action is explained when we identify a belief and a desire that jointly cause it through practical reasoning. The analysis before us states the conditions more precisely: an action is explained if and only if the subject believes that a certain action will bring about a desired outcome, desires that outcome, and the belief and desire jointly cause the action through a process of practical reasoning. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the routine conduct of daily life. Suppose a person desires a glass of water. She believes, as she has believed for many years, that turning on the kitchen tap will produce one. The belief and the desire are both present, and the action — walking to the kitchen and turning on the tap — is performed. Yet the action is not, on this occasion, mediated by any process of practical reasoning. The subject does not deliberate about how to obtain water, weigh alternative means, or reason from her desire and belief to a conclusion about what to do. She acts automatically, out of long-established habit, her belief and desire operating as causes of the action without passing through the mediating process of practical reasoning that the analysis demands. Condition (iii) is thus not satisfied, yet the action is plainly explained by the subject's belief and desire. The analysis, by insisting upon practical reasoning as the mediating process, excludes the large class of habitual actions that are caused by beliefs and desires operating through automatic or sub-personal cognitive mechanisms rather than through explicit deliberation.

definition: Religious belief meets ordinary epistemic standards if and only if (i) the belief is supported by evidence or argument accessible to rational enquiry, (ii) the belief is revisable in the light of counter-evidence, and (iii) the subject holds the belief with a degree of confidence proportional to the evidence.
conditions: The belief is supported by evidence or argument accessible to rational enquiry
The belief is revisable in the light of counter-evidence
The subject holds the belief with a degree of confidence proportional to the evidence
counterexample: A devout person believes in the existence of God on the basis of a profound and life-altering personal experience — a sense of the numinous presence of the divine. She holds this belief with the highest confidence. She would not regard any philosophical argument or empirical finding as capable of overturning it, nor does she consider the evidence — in the sense of publicly accessible reasons — to be the proper basis for her conviction. Her belief fails all three conditions, yet she may be a model of epistemic conscientiousness in every other domain, and the belief may be profoundly rational by the internal standards of her religious tradition.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes that epistemic standards are uniform across all domains of belief, failing to account for the possibility that religious belief may be governed by distinct epistemic norms — such as the authority of personal experience or the primacy of faith — that cannot be assimilated to the standards appropriate to empirical or philosophical enquiry.
passage: The question whether religious belief can meet the epistemic standards applied to beliefs in other domains has been a source of enduring philosophical controversy. The analysis here considered holds that a religious belief meets ordinary epistemic standards if and only if it is supported by evidence or argument accessible to rational enquiry, is revisable in the light of counter-evidence, and is held with a degree of confidence proportional to the evidence. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the phenomenon of religious conviction grounded in personal experience. Suppose a person of considerable intelligence and integrity believes in the existence of God. The basis of her belief is not a philosophical argument or an empirical observation but a profound and transformative personal experience — a sense, overwhelming in its immediacy, of the presence of the divine. She holds this belief with the highest possible confidence, and she would not regard any philosophical argument, however ingenious, or any empirical finding, however well-established, as capable of overturning it. Her belief fails condition (i), for the experience upon which it rests is not accessible to rational enquiry in the sense intended — it is private and unrepeatable. It fails condition (ii), for she does not regard it as revisable. And it fails condition (iii), for her confidence far outstrips what the publicly available evidence would warrant. Yet this person may be, in every other respect, a model of epistemic conscientiousness — careful in her reasoning, attentive to evidence, and scrupulous in her judgements. One might observe that the analysis presupposes the uniformity of epistemic standards across all domains of belief, and that this presupposition is precisely what is in question when the epistemology of religious belief is at issue. Her belief may be profoundly rational by the norms internal to her religious tradition, even as it fails the conditions of the analysis.

definition: An animal believes a proposition if and only if (i) the animal is in an internal state that represents some feature of its environment, (ii) this state is capable of being true or false, and (iii) the state guides the animal's behaviour in a manner analogous to the way human belief guides human action.
conditions: The animal is in an internal state representing some environmental feature
The state is capable of being true or false
The state guides behaviour analogously to human belief guiding action
counterexample: A frog snaps its tongue at a small dark moving object in its visual field. The frog is in an internal state that represents — or appears to represent — the presence of a fly, and this state guides its behaviour in a manner that is, on the surface, analogous to belief-guided action. Yet the frog will snap at any small dark moving object, including lead pellets. The internal state does not represent the presence of a fly but merely the presence of a small dark moving thing. Whether this state is capable of being true or false depends on what we take its content to be, and there appears to be no fact of the matter that settles this question.
missing_condition: The analysis does not specify how the content of an animal's internal state is to be determined, thereby leaving indeterminate whether the state represents a specific environmental feature or merely a proximate stimulus, and consequently whether it is genuinely capable of truth or falsity.
passage: The question whether animals may properly be said to hold beliefs has exercised philosophers of mind for a considerable time. The analysis before us holds that an animal believes a proposition if and only if it is in an internal state that represents some feature of its environment, this state is capable of being true or false, and the state guides the animal's behaviour in a manner analogous to the way belief guides human action. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of amphibian perception. Consider a frog that snaps its tongue at a small dark object moving across its visual field. The frog is in an internal state that appears to represent the presence of a fly and that guides its behaviour — the tongue-snap — in a manner superficially analogous to belief-guided action in humans. Yet the frog will snap with equal readiness at a lead pellet, a small shadow, or any other small dark moving object. The question arises: what does the frog's internal state represent? Does it represent the presence of a fly, or merely the presence of a small dark moving thing? If the former, then the state is false when the object is a pellet; if the latter, then it is true regardless of what the object is, but it does not represent the environmentally significant feature — the presence of food — that would make it analogous to a belief. The analysis requires that the state represent some feature of the environment and be capable of truth or falsity, but it provides no means of determining what the content of the state is. There appears to be no fact of the matter, independent of our interpretive choices, that settles whether the frog represents flies or dark moving things. One is thus led to recognise that the notion of animal belief may be irreducibly indeterminate in a way the analysis cannot accommodate.

definition: A subject S perceives a physical object O by means of sense-data if and only if (i) there exists a sense-datum D which is immediately present to S's consciousness, (ii) D possesses qualities (colour, shape, extension) which represent or correspond to qualities of O, and (iii) D is caused, in the appropriate manner, by the physical object O acting upon S's sense organs.
conditions: There exists a sense-datum D which is immediately present to S's consciousness
D possesses qualities which represent or correspond to qualities of the physical object O
D is caused in the appropriate manner by O acting upon S's sense organs
counterexample: Consider a subject who, owing to a lesion in the visual cortex, experiences a vivid sense-datum of a blue square whenever a red circle is placed before her eyes. There exists a sense-datum immediately present to consciousness, it is caused by a physical object acting upon the sense organs, yet the qualities of the datum bear no correspondence whatever to the qualities of the object.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the representational correspondence between sense-datum and object be systematic or reliable, rather than merely accidental or pathological.
passage: One of the most venerable doctrines in the philosophy of perception holds that we never perceive physical objects directly but only by way of certain mental intermediaries, variously called sense-data, sensa, or sensory appearances. On this view, when one looks at a ripe tomato, what is immediately present to consciousness is not the tomato itself but a red, roughly circular sense-datum which stands as a proxy for the external thing. The analysis, as customarily stated, requires three conditions: the existence of an appropriate sense-datum, a correspondence between its qualities and those of the object, and a causal relation running from object to datum by way of the sense organs. Yet one might observe that these conditions are not jointly sufficient. Suppose a subject has a neurological abnormality such that the stimulation of her retina by a red circular object invariably produces in her a sense-datum of a blue square. All three conditions appear to be met in letter, if not in spirit, for there is a sense-datum present to consciousness, it is caused by the object through the sense organs, and it does possess determinate qualities. Nevertheless, one would scarcely wish to say that the subject perceives the red circle, for the qualities of her sense-datum bear no faithful relation to those of the object before her. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis as stated, for it shows that mere causal production of a sense-datum by an object does not suffice for perception unless the representational correspondence is of the right sort — systematic, reliable, and non-accidental.

definition: A subject S directly perceives a physical object O if and only if (i) O exists independently of S's perceiving it, (ii) S is immediately aware of O without the mediation of any mental intermediary such as a sense-datum or idea, and (iii) O is presented to S as it is in itself, with no distortion or misrepresentation.
conditions: O exists independently of S's perceiving it
S is immediately aware of O without the mediation of any mental intermediary
O is presented to S as it is in itself with no distortion or misrepresentation
counterexample: A subject views a white wall under red lighting and is immediately aware of the wall itself without any intervening sense-datum, yet the wall appears pink rather than white. The object exists independently and is perceived without mental intermediaries, but it is not presented as it is in itself.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to accommodate the possibility that direct perception may involve systematic appearance-reality divergences caused by the conditions of observation, rather than by interposed mental entities.
passage: The doctrine commonly termed naïve or direct realism maintains that in perception we stand in an immediate cognitive relation to the physical objects around us, unmediated by ideas, impressions, or sense-data. It is, one might say, the pre-philosophical view of the ordinary person who supposes that when she sees a table she sees the very table and not some mental copy of it. The analysis requires that the object exist mind-independently, that the subject be aware of it without any intervening mental entity, and that the object be presented as it truly is. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a subject stands before a perfectly white wall which is illuminated by a concealed red lamp. She is, it appears, immediately aware of the wall itself — there is no reason to posit any sense-datum interposed between her and the surface. The wall exists independently of her perceiving it. And yet the wall appears to her to be pink, not white. The third condition is thus violated, for the object is not presented as it is in itself. The naïve realist must either abandon the requirement that objects always appear as they are, or concede that this is not a case of direct perception at all. In either event, the analysis as originally formulated proves too restrictive, for it excludes cases which we should ordinarily wish to count as perfectly good instances of perceiving a physical object.

definition: A subject S perceives a physical object O through a representative idea if and only if (i) S is immediately aware of a mental representation R (an 'idea' in Locke's sense), (ii) R is caused by O through a causal chain involving S's sense organs and nervous system, and (iii) R resembles O with respect to its primary qualities (extension, figure, motion) though not necessarily with respect to its secondary qualities (colour, taste, warmth).
conditions: S is immediately aware of a mental representation R
R is caused by O through a causal chain involving S's sense organs and nervous system
R resembles O with respect to its primary qualities
counterexample: When one views a straight stick half-submerged in water, one's mental representation depicts a bent shape, yet the stick possesses the primary quality of straightness. The representation is caused by the stick through the sense organs, and one is immediately aware of it, but it fails to resemble the object in its primary quality of figure.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the possibility that the causal chain from object to representation may systematically distort even primary qualities, thus undermining the claim that resemblance in primary qualities is guaranteed by the causal relation.
passage: Locke's representative realism, sometimes described as the doctrine of a veil of ideas, holds that our perceptual access to the external world is always mediated by mental representations. These ideas, as Locke terms them, are the immediate objects of awareness, and they are connected to physical things by a causal process running from object through sense organ to mind. Crucially, Locke distinguishes between primary and secondary qualities: the former — extension, solidity, figure, motion — are held to exist in the objects themselves and to be faithfully reproduced in our ideas, whilst the latter — colour, warmth, taste, sound — are powers in objects to produce ideas in us which need not resemble anything in the object. The analysis thus requires awareness of a mental representation, an appropriate causal chain, and resemblance between representation and object with respect to primary qualities. One might observe, however, that this tripartite analysis is not proof against familiar cases of perceptual distortion. Consider the well-known phenomenon of the stick which appears bent when partially immersed in water. Here the subject is immediately aware of a mental representation, and that representation is caused in the standard way by the stick acting upon the visual system. Yet the representation depicts a bent figure, whereas the stick possesses the primary quality of straightness. The resemblance condition is thus violated even with respect to a paradigmatic primary quality, namely shape. This case reveals the insufficiency of the Lockean analysis, for it demonstrates that the causal chain from object to idea may distort primary qualities no less than secondary ones, thereby threatening the very distinction upon which representative realism depends.

definition: A physical object O exists if and only if (i) there is a set of actual sensory experiences which O would produce in a suitably placed observer, (ii) these possible experiences form a stable, law-governed pattern such that any observer satisfying the relevant conditions would undergo them, and (iii) the truth of any statement about O is exhaustively analysable into statements about actual and possible sensory experiences.
conditions: There is a set of actual sensory experiences which O would produce in a suitably placed observer
These possible experiences form a stable law-governed pattern for any observer satisfying the relevant conditions
The truth of any statement about O is exhaustively analysable into statements about actual and possible sensory experiences
counterexample: Consider a distant star which existed for millions of years before any sentient being evolved to observe it. There were, during that epoch, no actual or suitably placed observers, nor any stable community of perceivers whose experiences could ground its existence, yet we have excellent scientific reason to affirm that the star existed nonetheless.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to account for the existence of objects during periods when no observers exist or could exist, thereby rendering it unable to accommodate the unobserved past of the physical universe.
passage: Phenomenalism, as expounded by Mill and later refined by Ayer — and subjected to penetrating criticism by Stebbing — proposes that physical objects are nothing over and above permanent possibilities of sensation.
