To say that a table exists in an unoccupied room is, on this view, merely to say that if one were to enter the room under suitable conditions, one would enjoy certain visual, tactile, and other sensory experiences. The analysis requires that there be a set of possible experiences corresponding to the object, that these experiences form a stable and law-governed pattern, and that all statements about the object be translatable without remainder into statements about actual and possible experiences. One might observe, however, that a case arises which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us consider a star in a remote galaxy which burned brightly for several million years before any sentient creature existed anywhere in the universe. During that vast epoch there were no actual observers, no suitably placed perceivers, and no community of minds whose possible experiences could serve to constitute the star's existence. Yet astrophysics provides us with overwhelming reason to believe that the star existed during that period. The phenomenalist must either deny this — a position which strains credulity — or concede that the existence of the star outruns all possible sensory experience. In either event, the analysis proves inadequate, for it cannot accommodate the plain fact that the physical universe antedates the emergence of percipient beings and existed in a determinate state long before any sensation, actual or possible, could have been grounded.

definition: A perceptual experience E is illusory if and only if (i) E is a genuine perceptual experience produced by a real external object O acting on the subject's sense organs, (ii) E represents O as possessing some property P which O does not in fact possess, and (iii) the misrepresentation is caused by the conditions of observation (medium, context, or physiological state) rather than by any defect in the object itself.
conditions: E is a genuine perceptual experience produced by a real external object O acting on the subject's sense organs
E represents O as possessing some property P which O does not in fact possess
The misrepresentation is caused by the conditions of observation rather than by any defect in the object itself
counterexample: A subject wearing prismatic spectacles that invert the visual field initially sees everything upside-down, satisfying all three conditions. Yet after several days of continuous wear, her visual system adapts and she sees the world right-side-up again, despite the optical conditions of observation remaining unchanged. The 'illusion' vanishes without any alteration in the external conditions that supposedly caused it.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the role of the subject's perceptual learning and neural adaptation, which may cancel or reverse the misrepresentation without any change in the external conditions of observation.
passage: Perceptual illusions have long served as a principal weapon in the armoury of those who wish to challenge naïve realism. The Müller-Lyer lines, the apparent bending of a stick half-submerged in water, the variation of a coin's apparent shape with the angle of regard — all are cited as evidence that perception can systematically misrepresent the properties of real objects. The standard analysis holds that an illusion is a genuine perceptual experience produced by an external object, that it represents the object as possessing some property it lacks, and that the misrepresentation is attributable to the conditions of observation rather than to any defect in the object. One might observe, however, that this analysis encounters difficulty when we consider the phenomenon of perceptual adaptation. It is well established that subjects fitted with prismatic spectacles which invert the visual field initially see the world upside-down. All three conditions of the analysis are met: the experience is caused by real objects, it misrepresents their spatial orientation, and the misrepresentation arises from the conditions of observation, namely the interposition of the prisms. Yet after several days of continuous wear, the subject's visual system adapts, and she comes to see the world right-side-up once more, even though the prisms remain in place and the optical conditions are wholly unaltered. The illusion has vanished without any change in the conditions of observation. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that the conditions of observation are not the sole determinant of misrepresentation; the subject's own perceptual learning and neural plasticity play an indispensable role which the analysis fails to acknowledge.

definition: A subject S hallucinates an object O if and only if (i) S has a perceptual experience as of O, (ii) O does not exist in S's immediate environment, and (iii) S's experience is indistinguishable, from S's own point of view, from a veridical perception of O.
conditions: S has a perceptual experience as of O
O does not exist in S's immediate environment
S's experience is indistinguishable from S's own point of view from a veridical perception of O
counterexample: A subject under the influence of mescaline experiences vivid geometric patterns, kaleidoscopic colours, and impossible spatial configurations which she recognises perfectly well are not real. She has a perceptual experience as of objects which do not exist, yet her experience is entirely distinguishable from veridical perception, for she is at no point tempted to take it for reality.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the existence of hallucinations which the subject recognises as hallucinatory — so-called insight-preserving hallucinations — where the subjective indistinguishability condition is not met yet the experience is plainly hallucinatory.
passage: The concept of hallucination occupies a central place in the philosophy of perception, for it raises in its sharpest form the question of how perceptual experience relates to the external world. On the standard analysis, a hallucination occurs when a subject enjoys a perceptual experience as of some object, the object does not exist in the subject's surroundings, and the experience is subjectively indistinguishable from a veridical perception. These three conditions are typically regarded as jointly sufficient. Yet one might observe that this analysis is too restrictive. Consider a subject who, having ingested mescaline, experiences a spectacular array of geometric patterns, pulsating colours, and spatially impossible configurations. She has a perceptual experience as of objects which assuredly do not exist in her environment, and to that extent the first two conditions are satisfied. But she is entirely aware that what she experiences is not real. Far from being unable to distinguish her experience from veridical perception, she recognises it throughout as a drug-induced phantasm. Her critical faculties remain intact; she does not reach out to touch the kaleidoscopic forms or attempt to interact with them. We should, it appears, still wish to call this a hallucination — indeed, it is a paradigmatic one — yet the third condition is not met, for the experience is perfectly distinguishable from the veridical case. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that subjective indistinguishability from veridical perception is not a necessary feature of hallucination. Many hallucinations, particularly those induced by psychoactive substances, are accompanied by full insight into their non-veridicality.

definition: The argument from illusion establishes that we never directly perceive physical objects if and only if (i) in cases of illusion, what is immediately perceived (the apparent object) has properties which the real object lacks, (ii) since the apparent object has properties the real object lacks, the apparent object cannot be identical with the real object, and (iii) since illusory and veridical experiences are qualitatively continuous, what is immediately perceived in veridical cases must also be something other than the physical object.
conditions: In cases of illusion what is immediately perceived has properties which the real object lacks
Since the apparent object has properties the real object lacks it cannot be identical with the real object
Since illusory and veridical experiences are qualitatively continuous what is immediately perceived in veridical cases must also be something other than the physical object
counterexample: Consider a subject who views a circular coin at an angle so that it appears elliptical. One might deny condition (ii) by noting that the coin itself is the object perceived; it merely appears elliptical from that vantage. No distinct apparent object with the property of ellipticality need be posited, for appearing-elliptical is a relational property of the coin relative to the observer's position, not a property of a numerically distinct entity.
missing_condition: The argument presupposes that if an object appears to have a property P, there must exist some entity which actually has P, yet this inference — sometimes called the sense-datum fallacy — is unwarranted without an additional premise justifying the reification of appearances.
passage: The argument from illusion has been regarded, at least since the time of the Greek sceptics, as one of the most formidable challenges to direct realism. In its modern formulation, the argument proceeds from the observation that in cases of perceptual illusion — the bent stick in water, the apparently elliptical coin, the converging railway lines — what is immediately present to consciousness possesses properties which the real physical object lacks. The stick appears bent though it is straight; the coin appears elliptical though it is round. From this it is inferred that the immediate object of awareness cannot be identical with the physical object, since one entity cannot simultaneously possess and lack the same property. Finally, by an appeal to the qualitative continuity between illusory and veridical experience, the argument concludes that even in veridical cases we perceive not the physical object but some mental intermediary. One might observe, however, that the second step of this argument harbours a questionable assumption. When the coin appears elliptical from an oblique angle, it does not follow that there exists some entity — a sense-datum or apparent object — which actually is elliptical. One may instead maintain that the coin itself is perceived and that it merely appears elliptical from that vantage point. Appearing-elliptical, on this view, is a relational property which the coin bears to the observer's spatial position, not a monadic property of a numerically distinct mental object. This analysis of appearing as a relation rather than a predication upon a sense-datum reveals the insufficiency of the argument, for it shows that the crucial inference from appearance to the existence of a distinct apparent object rests upon an unargued and by no means obvious premise.

definition: A quality Q of a physical object O is a primary quality if and only if (i) Q is inseparable from O in the sense that O cannot be conceived without Q, (ii) Q exists in O independently of any perceiver and would characterise O even if no sentient being existed, and (iii) our ideas of Q resemble Q as it exists in O, so that the structure of our perceptual representation mirrors the structure of the quality itself.
conditions: Q is inseparable from O in the sense that O cannot be conceived without Q
Q exists in O independently of any perceiver
Our ideas of Q resemble Q as it exists in O
counterexample: Consider the property of solidity. On the Lockean analysis it is a paradigmatic primary quality — inseparable from body and mind-independent. Yet modern physics reveals that what we perceive as solidity is the result of electromagnetic repulsion between electron clouds, and that the interior of any solid object is overwhelmingly empty space. Our idea of solidity as impenetrable denseness does not resemble the micro-physical reality of the object at all.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes that resemblance between idea and quality can be straightforwardly assessed, yet it provides no criterion for determining at which level of description — macroscopic or microscopic — the resemblance is to be judged.
passage: Locke's celebrated distinction between primary and secondary qualities has proved one of the most influential doctrines in the empiricist tradition. Primary qualities — extension, figure, motion, number, solidity — are held to exist in bodies as they really are, independently of any perceiver, and our ideas of them are said to resemble the qualities themselves. Secondary qualities — colour, sound, taste, warmth — are by contrast mere powers in objects to produce certain ideas in us, and these ideas bear no resemblance to anything in the object. The analysis of primary qualities thus requires inseparability from the body, mind-independence, and resemblance between idea and quality. One might observe, however, that the third condition encounters a difficulty which Locke does not adequately address. Consider solidity, which Locke regards as a paradigmatic primary quality. Our perceptual idea of solidity presents it as a kind of impenetrable plenitude — the denseness and resistance we feel when we press against a table. Yet physics instructs us that the table is composed overwhelmingly of empty space, with tiny atomic nuclei separated by relatively vast distances, and that the sensation of resistance arises from electromagnetic forces between electron clouds. Our idea of solidity, far from resembling the quality as it exists in the object, misrepresents it in a manner quite as thoroughgoing as the secondary qualities are alleged to do. This case reveals the insufficiency of the Lockean analysis, for it shows that the resemblance condition cannot be sustained even for qualities which Locke himself regarded as the clearest instances of primary qualities.

definition: A subject S has a perceptual experience of a red object if and only if (i) S senses in a particular manner, described adverbially as sensing redly, (ii) this manner of sensing is caused by the appropriate interaction between S's sense organs and the environment, and (iii) no ontological commitment is made to the existence of any mental object (sense-datum) which is itself red.
conditions: S senses in a particular manner described adverbially as sensing redly
This manner of sensing is caused by the appropriate interaction between S's sense organs and the environment
No ontological commitment is made to any mental object which is itself red
counterexample: A subject simultaneously views a red triangle on a blue circular background. The adverbial theory describes her as sensing redly and triangularly and bluely and circularly, but this description fails to capture which colour is bound to which shape. The subject might equally be described as sensing a blue triangle on a red circle, since the adverbs do not encode the structural relations between the qualities experienced.
missing_condition: The analysis lacks any account of how different adverbial modifications are bound together into structured perceptual wholes, leaving it unable to distinguish experiences which differ only in the combination of qualities.
passage: The adverbial theory of perception, championed by Ducasse and refined by Chisholm, was devised in large part to avoid the ontological commitments of the sense-datum theory. Where the sense-datum theorist holds that when one sees something red, there exists a red mental particular of which one is aware, the adverbialist insists that the experience is to be described not by positing a red object of awareness but by modifying the verb of sensing: one senses redly, much as one might dance gracefully without there being an entity called gracefulness which one dances. The analysis requires that the subject sense in a determinate manner, that this manner be caused by sensory interaction with the environment, and that no mental object be posited as the bearer of the experienced quality. One might observe, however, that this analysis encounters a well-known difficulty when we consider the structure of complex perceptual experiences. Suppose a subject views a red triangle set against a blue circular background. On the adverbial account, she is sensing redly, triangularly, bluely, and circularly. But this conjunction of adverbs fails to capture the crucial fact that redness is bound to triangularity and blueness to circularity. The description is equally compatible with her experiencing a blue triangle on a red circular background, or indeed any other permutation of colours and shapes. This is the so-called many-property problem, and it reveals the insufficiency of the adverbial analysis, for it shows that a mere list of adverbial modifications cannot represent the structured character of perceptual experience.

definition: A perceptual experience E is veridical if and only if (i) E involves the subject S standing in a direct perceptual relation to a mind-independent object O, (ii) the phenomenal character of E is constituted, at least in part, by O and its properties, and (iii) no experience which is merely hallucinatory can be of the same fundamental kind as E, since hallucinations lack the relational constituent which defines veridical perception.
conditions: E involves S standing in a direct perceptual relation to a mind-independent object O
The phenomenal character of E is constituted at least in part by O and its properties
No merely hallucinatory experience can be of the same fundamental kind as E
counterexample: A subject looks at a red apple on a table and has a veridical perception of it. Unbeknownst to her, a neuroscientist is monitoring her brain and is prepared, at any moment, to remove the apple and simultaneously stimulate her cortex so as to produce an experience subjectively identical to the veridical one. If the experiences are of fundamentally different kinds, the subject has no means whatsoever of knowing which kind she is undergoing, which threatens the epistemic role that veridical perception is meant to play.
missing_condition: The analysis provides no epistemic criterion by which the subject can determine whether their experience is of the veridical kind or the hallucinatory kind, thus leaving the purported metaphysical distinction without epistemological significance.
passage: Disjunctivism in the philosophy of perception holds that veridical perceptions and hallucinations, despite their possible phenomenal similarity, are experiences of fundamentally different kinds. When one veridically perceives a red apple, one's experience is partly constituted by the apple itself and its redness; the object enters into the very nature of the experience. A hallucination of a red apple, by contrast, merely seems to present an apple but in fact lacks the relational constituent which gives the veridical experience its character. The two experiences share no common experiential factor, or so the disjunctivist maintains. The analysis thus requires a direct perceptual relation to a mind-independent object, the constitutive role of that object in the experience's phenomenal character, and a fundamental kind-distinction between veridical and hallucinatory cases. One might observe, however, that this analysis faces a serious epistemological difficulty. Suppose a subject perceives a red apple whilst a neuroscientist stands ready to remove the apple and simultaneously stimulate the subject's visual cortex in such a way as to produce an experience subjectively indistinguishable from the original. The disjunctivist insists that these two experiences are of fundamentally different kinds, yet the subject herself possesses no means of determining which kind of experience she is enjoying at any given moment. If veridical perception and hallucination are utterly different in metaphysical nature, but the subject cannot tell them apart from within, then the epistemic advantage which veridical perception is supposed to confer — direct acquaintance with reality — becomes inaccessible to the perceiver. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it exposes a gap between the metaphysical distinction upon which disjunctivism insists and the epistemological work which perception is ordinarily taken to perform.

definition: A perceptual experience E is theory-laden if and only if (i) E is shaped or structured by the concepts, beliefs, or theoretical commitments which S brings to the perceptual situation, (ii) two subjects with different conceptual frameworks could, when presented with the same stimulus, have perceptual experiences which differ in content, and (iii) the influence of theory upon perception is not merely post-perceptual or judgmental but operates upon the perceptual experience itself.
conditions: E is shaped or structured by the concepts beliefs or theoretical commitments which S brings to the perceptual situation
Two subjects with different conceptual frameworks could have different perceptual experiences when presented with the same stimulus
The influence of theory upon perception operates upon the perceptual experience itself and is not merely post-perceptual
counterexample: A trained radiologist and a layperson both view the same chest X-ray. The radiologist sees a tumour; the layperson sees only a blur. Yet if the layperson is shown precisely where to look, she can see the same dark mass, despite possessing no medical theory whatever. Her perceptual experience changes not because she has acquired a new theoretical framework but because her attention has been directed to a particular region of the image.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates the role of concepts and theories with the role of perceptual attention and spatial selection, failing to distinguish cases where theory genuinely structures experience from cases where mere attentional direction suffices.
passage: The thesis that perception is theory-laden, advanced most forcefully by Hanson and Kuhn, holds that the concepts and beliefs a subject brings to a perceptual situation enter into the very constitution of her experience. On this view, a trained physicist looking at a cloud chamber does not first have a theory-neutral visual experience and then interpret it as showing the track of a positron; rather, she sees a positron track, and her experience itself differs from that of the untrained observer. The analysis requires that theoretical commitments shape the experience, that differently equipped observers may have different experiences of the same stimulus, and that the influence is genuinely perceptual rather than merely judgmental. One might observe, however, that a certain class of cases reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a radiologist and a layperson examining the same chest X-ray. The radiologist perceives a tumour; the layperson perceives nothing of diagnostic significance. This appears to support the theory-ladenness thesis. But suppose we now direct the layperson's attention to the relevant region of the image, perhaps by pointing or by drawing a circle around the suspicious area. The layperson, who possesses no medical theory whatever, can now see the dark mass perfectly well. Her experience has changed, yet no theoretical commitments have been acquired. What has changed is merely the direction of her attention. This case suggests that what is often attributed to the influence of theory upon perception may in many instances be more parsimoniously explained by differences in attentional deployment, and the analysis fails to distinguish between these quite different sources of perceptual variation.

definition: A subject S has direct perceptual knowledge that P if and only if (i) S perceives that P without relying on any conscious inference from other beliefs or premises, (ii) S's perception that P is caused in the appropriate way by the state of affairs which makes P true, and (iii) S's perceptual state is sufficient by itself, without supplementation by background knowledge, to justify S's belief that P.
conditions: S perceives that P without relying on any conscious inference from other beliefs
S's perception that P is caused in the appropriate way by the state of affairs which makes P true
S's perceptual state is sufficient by itself without supplementation by background knowledge to justify S's belief that P
counterexample: A birdwatcher looks at a bird in a tree and perceives that it is a goldfinch. Her perception is caused by the actual goldfinch before her, and she makes no conscious inference. Yet her perceptual state alone — a visual impression of a smallish yellow bird — is insufficient to justify the belief that it is a goldfinch rather than a canary or a yellow warbler. She relies essentially on her background knowledge of ornithology.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the fact that many perceptual beliefs which appear non-inferential are in fact epistemically dependent on background knowledge and recognitional capacities, without which the perceptual state would be too indeterminate to yield justified belief in the specific proposition perceived.
passage: It is commonly supposed that perception furnishes us with a species of knowledge which is immediate or direct, requiring no inference and no supporting premises. When one sees that there is a cat on the mat, one does not, it is said, first perceive certain colours and shapes and then infer the presence of a cat; rather, one simply sees the cat, and one's perceptual state suffices by itself to justify the resulting belief. The analysis of direct perceptual knowledge thus requires the absence of conscious inference, an appropriate causal connection to the perceived state of affairs, and the self-sufficiency of the perceptual state as a justificatory ground. One might observe, however, that this analysis proves too generous. Consider a seasoned birdwatcher who, glancing at a tree, perceives that the bird perched therein is a goldfinch. She makes no conscious inference; the recognition is, as we say, immediate. And her perception is caused in the right way by an actual goldfinch. Yet her perceptual state — a visual experience as of a smallish yellow bird with certain markings — is not by itself sufficient to justify the belief that it is a goldfinch rather than a canary, a yellow warbler, or any number of superficially similar species. The justificatory work is done in large measure by her extensive background knowledge of ornithology, which enables her to discriminate among similar-looking birds. Without that knowledge, the very same perceptual state would justify at most the belief that there is a small yellow bird in the tree. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that what passes for direct perceptual knowledge frequently depends upon a reservoir of background knowledge which the perceiver has internalised so thoroughly that its contribution goes unnoticed.

definition: A subject S perceives a property P of an object O through sensory modality M if and only if (i) M is the sensory channel through which P is detected (e.g., vision for colour, audition for pitch), (ii) P is a proper sensible of M, that is, P can be detected only through M and not through any other modality, and (iii) S's experience of P is modality-specific and would not occur in the absence of stimulation of M.
conditions: M is the sensory channel through which P is detected
P is a proper sensible of M detectable only through M
S's experience of P is modality-specific and would not occur without stimulation of M
counterexample: A subject hears an object rushing towards her from the left and forms a vivid spatial impression of its trajectory and approximate shape, despite having her eyes closed. Spatial properties like trajectory and shape are supposedly proper sensibles of vision, yet here they are apprehended through audition alone, violating the requirement that proper sensibles be detectable through one modality only.
missing_condition: The analysis assumes a rigid assignment of properties to single modalities, yet neglects the pervasive phenomenon of cross-modal perception whereby spatial, temporal, and other properties are accessible through multiple sensory channels.
passage: The classical doctrine of proper sensibles, traceable to Aristotle and refined by the empiricists, holds that each sensory modality has its own distinctive class of qualities which it alone can detect. Colour is the proper sensible of vision, sound of hearing, flavour of taste, and so forth. The analysis of modality-specific perception requires that the property be detected through the relevant sensory channel, that it be a proper sensible of that channel alone, and that the resulting experience be modality-specific. One might observe, however, that this analysis encounters difficulty when we consider the spatial content of auditory experience. A subject sitting in a darkened room with her eyes shut hears an object moving rapidly from left to right. She forms a vivid impression not merely of a sound but of a spatial trajectory — the object is perceived as moving along a determinate path through three-dimensional space, and she may even gain a rough impression of its size and shape from the character of the sound it produces. Yet spatial properties such as trajectory, location, and shape are traditionally assigned to vision as its proper or at least privileged sensibles. The subject's auditory experience delivers spatial information which, on the classical analysis, ought to be available only through sight. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it demonstrates that the assignment of properties to single sensory modalities is far less rigid than the doctrine of proper sensibles supposes. Spatial properties, and arguably temporal and causal properties as well, are accessible through multiple channels, and any adequate account of perception must accommodate this cross-modal richness.

definition: An experience E is a perception rather than a mere sensation if and only if (i) E has intentional content, that is, it represents or is directed upon an object or state of affairs in the world, (ii) E provides the subject with information about the mind-independent properties of that object, and (iii) E is phenomenally distinct from bodily sensations such as pains and tickles, which lack intentional directedness upon external objects.
conditions: E has intentional content representing or directed upon an object or state of affairs in the world
E provides the subject with information about mind-independent properties of that object
E is phenomenally distinct from bodily sensations which lack intentional directedness upon external objects
counterexample: A subject feels a sharp pain in her phantom limb — a limb which was amputated years ago. The pain appears to be directed upon a specific location in space (the region where the hand used to be), and it seems to convey information about that location (that something is wrong there). Yet there is no object at that location, and the experience is a paradigmatic sensation rather than a perception.
missing_condition: The analysis provides no criterion for distinguishing genuine intentional directedness from merely apparent spatial directedness, thereby failing to exclude sensations which phenomenally seem to be about external locations yet lack any real external object.
passage: Philosophers have long sought to distinguish perception from mere sensation. The prevailing view holds that perception, unlike sensation, is essentially intentional: it is directed upon objects in the world and represents them as being a certain way. When one sees a red apple, one's experience is about the apple; it presents the apple as red, round, and solid. A pain, by contrast, is typically thought to lack this outward directedness — it is simply a modification of one's own consciousness, a way of being appeared to which carries no information about the external world. The analysis thus requires that a perception have intentional content, that it convey information about mind-independent properties, and that it be phenomenally distinct from bodily sensations. One might observe, however, that certain cases threaten the sharpness of this distinction. Consider the well-documented phenomenon of phantom-limb pain. A subject whose arm has been amputated continues to feel vivid pain in the missing hand. The pain appears to be located at a definite point in space — the region formerly occupied by the hand — and the subject may describe it as throbbing, burning, or clenching, as though the hand itself were the source. The experience thus seems to possess a species of intentional directedness: it presents a spatial location and attributes properties to it. Yet there is no object at that location upon which the experience could be directed, and we should ordinarily classify the experience as a sensation rather than a perception. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that the phenomenal appearance of intentional directedness does not suffice to transform a sensation into a perception, and some further criterion is needed to draw the line between the two.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) S has a conscious visual experience as of O, (ii) S can report or describe the presence and properties of O on the basis of that experience, and (iii) S's awareness of O is phenomenally manifest, that is, it is available to introspective access and figures in S's conscious mental life.
conditions: S has a conscious visual experience as of O
S can report or describe the presence and properties of O on the basis of that experience
S's awareness of O is phenomenally manifest and available to introspective access
counterexample: A patient with blindsight, whose primary visual cortex has been destroyed in one hemisphere, sincerely denies seeing anything in the affected portion of her visual field. Yet when forced to guess whether a stimulus is present and what shape it has, she performs well above chance. She processes visual information about the object and can discriminate its properties, but she has no conscious experience of it and cannot report seeing it.
missing_condition: The analysis excludes the well-documented phenomenon of unconscious perception, in which visual information is processed and influences behaviour without producing any conscious experience, report, or introspective awareness.
passage: It has been widely assumed, at least since Descartes, that perception is necessarily a conscious phenomenon. To perceive something, on this view, is to be consciously aware of it — to have it figure in one's phenomenal experience in a way that is accessible to introspection and available for verbal report. The analysis requires a conscious visual experience, the ability to report or describe what is perceived, and the phenomenal manifestness of the perceived object to the subject. One might observe, however, that neuropsychological evidence furnishes a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Patients living with blindsight, a condition typically resulting from damage to the primary visual cortex, sincerely report that they see nothing in the affected region of their visual field. They have no conscious visual experience of stimuli presented there, and they are unable to offer spontaneous reports about the presence or properties of objects in that region. Yet when compelled to make forced-choice guesses — whether a light is flashing, whether a grating is horizontal or vertical, whether an object is moving left or right — they perform at levels far exceeding what chance would predict. The visual information is being processed and is influencing their discriminative responses, yet it does so without producing any phenomenal experience. None of the three conditions of the analysis is met: there is no conscious experience, no spontaneous reportability, and no introspective access. Yet there is, it appears, something which we might reasonably call perception. This case suggests that perception can occur beneath the threshold of consciousness, and any analysis which restricts perception to the consciously experienced is too narrow.

definition: A subject S sees an object O as an F if and only if (i) S visually perceives O, (ii) S's visual experience represents O under the concept or category F, and (iii) S's seeing O as an F is a unitary perceptual act, not decomposable into a bare perception of O followed by a separate judgment that O is an F.
conditions: S visually perceives O
S's visual experience represents O under the concept or category F
S's seeing O as an F is a unitary perceptual act not decomposable into bare perception followed by separate judgment
counterexample: Consider Wittgenstein's duck-rabbit figure. A subject looks at the figure and sees it as a duck. Then, without any change in the visual stimulus, she suddenly sees it as a rabbit. She perceives the same object throughout, and in each case her experience represents the figure under a concept. Yet the shift between the two aspects occurs spontaneously and without any intervening judgment, suggesting that seeing-as involves a dimension of perceptual organisation which is not captured by the mere application of a concept to a perceived object.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to account for aspect-switching — the capacity to see the same object under different aspects without any change in stimulus, judgment, or conceptual repertoire — which reveals that seeing-as involves gestalt-organisational features of experience not reducible to conceptual representation.
passage: The phenomenon of seeing-as, or aspect perception, was brought to philosophical prominence by Wittgenstein's discussion of the duck-rabbit figure in his later work. When one sees the ambiguous figure as a duck, one's experience is not merely of a set of lines to which one subsequently applies the concept duck; rather, the duckishness seems to pervade the experience itself. The analysis of seeing-as requires visual perception of the object, representation under a concept, and the unity of these as a single perceptual act rather than a sequence of perception and judgment. One might observe, however, that the duck-rabbit figure itself provides a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. A subject gazing at the figure may see it as a duck for some time, and then, quite suddenly and without any effort of will, see it as a rabbit. The stimulus has not changed, and the subject has not acquired any new concept — she possessed the concept rabbit all along. Nor is there any judgment mediating the transition; the shift is experienced as immediate and involuntary. What changes is the aspect under which the figure is perceived, and this change involves a reorganisation of the perceptual field — the eye that was the duck's beak becomes the rabbit's mouth, the contour that was the back of the head becomes the front of the face — which is not adequately characterised as the application of a different concept to the same percept. The analysis, by reducing seeing-as to conceptual representation, fails to capture the gestalt-organisational dimension of aspect perception, in which the very structure of the visual experience is reconfigured.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O falls within S's sensory field and produces stimulation of S's sense organs, (ii) S attends to O, selecting it from among the other objects present in the sensory field, and (iii) the content of S's perceptual experience is determined by which objects S selects for attention.
conditions: O falls within S's sensory field and produces stimulation of S's sense organs
S attends to O selecting it from among other objects in the sensory field
The content of S's perceptual experience is determined by which objects S selects for attention
counterexample: A subject is engrossed in conversation at a noisy party, attending exclusively to her interlocutor's voice. Suddenly she hears her own name spoken by someone across the room. She was not attending to that speaker at all — her attention was directed elsewhere — yet she perceives the utterance of her name. The content of her experience is determined not by her attentional selection but by the salience of the stimulus itself.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the role of stimulus-driven or involuntary attentional capture, in which objects are perceived not because the subject selects them but because their intrinsic salience compels attention independently of the subject's voluntary control.
passage: Attention has long been recognised as playing a central role in perception, and some philosophers have gone so far as to make it a necessary condition of perceiving. On this view, the perceptual field presents us with a manifold of potential objects, and it is attention which selects certain of these for determinate experience. What we perceive, it is claimed, is precisely what we attend to; unattended objects, though they may stimulate the sense organs, do not enter into the content of perceptual experience. The analysis requires that the object stimulate the sense organs, that the subject attend to it, and that the content of experience be determined by attentional selection. One might observe, however, that a well-known phenomenon reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. At a crowded and noisy party — the scenario sometimes known as the cocktail party effect — a subject may be deeply absorbed in conversation, attending exclusively to the voice of her interlocutor and filtering out all other auditory input. Yet if someone on the far side of the room utters her name, she becomes immediately aware of it. She hears her name despite having directed no attention to that speaker and despite having no expectation that her name would be spoken. Her attention was, so to speak, captured by the stimulus rather than voluntarily directed towards it. The content of her perceptual experience was determined not by her own attentional selection but by the intrinsic salience and personal significance of the utterance. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that perception can occur without prior attentional selection and that stimuli can force their way into perceptual awareness by their own properties.

definition: A subject S veridically perceives the colour of an object O if and only if (i) O appears to S to have a determinate colour C under, (ii) the conditions of observation are standard or normal (daylight, unobstructed view, functioning visual system), and (iii) under these standard conditions, the colour O appears to have is the colour O actually has.
conditions: O appears to S to have a determinate colour C
The conditions of observation are standard or normal
Under standard conditions the colour O appears to have is the colour O actually has
counterexample: A subject with normal colour vision views a piece of fabric in standard daylight and sees it as green. Another subject, also with normal colour vision, views the same fabric under the same conditions and sees it as blue. Both subjects have functioning visual systems and observe under standard conditions, yet they disagree about the colour. The fabric is one of those objects whose colour falls on the boundary between green and blue, and normal perceivers systematically disagree about it.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes that standard conditions and normal perceivers jointly determine a unique perceived colour, yet it fails to accommodate cases where normal perceivers under standard conditions systematically disagree, revealing that the notion of standard conditions is insufficiently specified.
passage: The phenomenon of perceptual constancy — our ability to perceive the stable properties of objects despite continuous variation in the proximal stimulus — has been regarded as one of perception's most remarkable achievements. Colour constancy is perhaps the most familiar instance: a white shirt appears white in sunlight, in shadow, and under artificial light, despite the dramatic differences in the light reaching the eye. The analysis of veridical colour perception requires that the object appear to have a determinate colour, that the conditions of observation be standard, and that under those conditions the apparent colour coincide with the actual colour. One might observe, however, that this analysis encounters difficulty with certain boundary cases. Consider a piece of fabric whose spectral reflectance falls precisely on the boundary between what English speakers call green and what they call blue. When viewed in standard daylight by subjects with perfectly normal colour vision, it is consistently seen as green by some perceivers and as blue by others. Both groups satisfy all three conditions of the analysis: the fabric appears to have a determinate colour, the conditions are standard, and each perceiver takes the apparent colour to be the actual colour. Yet they disagree, and there is no principled way to adjudicate between them. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that the notion of standard conditions does not by itself determine a unique veridical colour. Perceptual constancy secures stability within a single perceiver's experience, but it cannot resolve inter-personal disagreements at the boundaries of colour categories.

definition: A subject S perceives an event or object E if and only if (i) E is occurring or present at the time of S's perceptual experience, (ii) S's experience represents E as occurring now, that is, as temporally present, and (iii) there is no significant temporal gap between the occurrence of E and S's experience of it.
conditions: E is occurring or present at the time of S's perceptual experience
S's experience represents E as occurring now or as temporally present
There is no significant temporal gap between the occurrence of E and S's experience of it
counterexample: A subject looks up at the night sky and sees a star. The light from that star left its surface eight hundred years ago; the star may well have ceased to exist centuries before the subject was born. The subject's experience represents the star as present, but the star is not occurring or present at the time of the experience, and the temporal gap between the event and the experience spans centuries.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to accommodate cases of perception at a temporal distance, such as seeing distant stars, where the perceived event lies in the remote past and the temporal presentness required by the analysis is systematically violated by the finite speed of light.
passage: It is natural to suppose that perception is essentially perception of the present — that what one perceives is occurring at the very time one perceives it. Memory relates us to the past, expectation to the future, but perception, it is said, anchors us in the now. The analysis requires that the perceived event be temporally present, that the experience represent it as occurring now, and that there be no significant temporal gap between event and experience. One might observe, however, that astronomical observation provides a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. When one looks at the night sky and sees, let us say, the star Betelgeuse, one is seeing light which left that star approximately seven hundred years ago. The event one perceives — the emission of photons from the star's surface — occurred in the Middle Ages, and it is entirely possible that the star has since exploded and ceased to exist. Yet one's experience presents the star as existing now, shining steadily in the constellation of Orion. The first condition is not met, for the event is not present at the time of the experience; and the third condition is flagrantly violated, for the temporal gap extends over centuries. Nevertheless, we should ordinarily say without hesitation that the subject perceives the star. If our analysis entails that she does not, so much the worse for the analysis. This case demonstrates that perception is not confined to the temporally present in the strict sense required, and that any adequate account must allow for perception at a temporal distance.

definition: A subject S has proprioceptive awareness of them own body if and only if (i) S has a non-observational awareness of the position, posture, and movement of them limbs and body parts, (ii) this awareness is produced by proprioceptors (receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints) rather than by the external senses, and (iii) S's proprioceptive states represent the actual configuration of them body and are normally veridical.
conditions: S has a non-observational awareness of the position posture and movement of them limbs and body parts
This awareness is produced by proprioceptors rather than by the external senses
S's proprioceptive states represent the actual configuration of them body and are normally veridical
counterexample: A subject who has suffered complete deafferentation — the destruction of all proprioceptive nerve fibres — retains no proprioceptive input from her limbs whatsoever. Yet she can, with effort and concentration, use vision to monitor and control her body's position and movement. She has awareness of her body's configuration, but it is produced by the external sense of vision rather than by proprioceptors, and she can report her posture accurately.
missing_condition: The analysis assumes that awareness of bodily configuration must be proprioceptive in origin, neglecting the possibility that other sensory modalities, such as vision, can substitute for proprioception and furnish the subject with accurate, though observational, knowledge of them own body.
passage: Proprioception — sometimes called the sixth sense — is the awareness one has of the position and movement of one's own body without relying upon sight, touch, or any of the canonical external senses. When one raises one's arm in complete darkness, one knows that it is raised, and one knows this not by looking at it or by feeling it with the other hand but by a distinctive species of bodily awareness which is mediated by receptors in the muscles, tendons, and joints. The analysis requires non-observational awareness of bodily configuration, production of that awareness by proprioceptors, and normal veridicality of the resulting states. One might observe, however, that neurological case studies reveal the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider the well-documented case of a patient who, owing to a severe polyneuropathy, has lost all proprioceptive input from the neck down. She has no proprioceptive sense of where her limbs are, what posture she is in, or how she is moving. Yet she has learned, through years of painstaking effort, to use vision as a substitute. By watching her limbs she can monitor their position and execute controlled movements. She is aware of her body's configuration, and her awareness is accurate and supports successful action. But it is produced by the external sense of vision, not by proprioceptors, and it is observational rather than non-observational. The first and second conditions of the analysis are thus violated, yet the subject possesses a form of bodily awareness which serves many of the same functional roles as proprioception. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that bodily awareness is not exclusively proprioceptive in origin.

definition: A subject S perceives X if and only if (i) X is a positive entity, event, or property present in S's environment, (ii) S's sensory systems are stimulated by X, and (iii) S has a perceptual experience whose content represents X as being present.
conditions: X is a positive entity event or property present in S's environment
S's sensory systems are stimulated by X
S has a perceptual experience whose content represents X as being present
counterexample: A subject enters a room which she knows is normally filled with the loud ticking of a grandfather clock. Today the clock has stopped, and she immediately perceives the silence — she hears, as we naturally say, that the clock is not ticking. Yet silence is not a positive entity, it does not stimulate the auditory system (it is precisely the absence of stimulation), and her experience represents something as being absent rather than present.
missing_condition: The analysis restricts perception to positive entities and stimulation-based experiences, yet it fails to accommodate the perception of absences, silences, and lacunae, in which the absence of stimulation is itself the content of perceptual experience.
passage: It is commonly assumed, often without explicit argument, that perception is essentially the detection of positive entities and events in the environment. We see objects, hear sounds, smell odours, and feel pressures — all of which are, in the relevant sense, positive realities which act upon our sense organs and produce perceptual experiences. The analysis formalises this assumption by requiring that the object of perception be a positive entity, that the sensory systems be stimulated by it, and that the experience represent it as present. One might observe, however, that ordinary discourse and phenomenological reflection alike suggest that we frequently perceive absences, and these cases reveal the insufficiency of the analysis. Consider a subject who enters a familiar room expecting to hear the ticking of a grandfather clock. The clock has stopped, and she is immediately struck by the silence. She hears the silence, or so it is natural to say; and her perceptual experience is as vivid and determinate as any experience of a positive sound. Yet silence is not a positive entity in her environment — it is precisely the absence of sound. It does not stimulate her auditory receptors, for stimulation requires energy impinging upon the sense organs, and silence furnishes none. And her experience represents not the presence of something but the absence of something, namely the expected ticking. All three conditions of the analysis are violated, yet the subject appears to enjoy a genuine perceptual experience. She does not infer the silence from the absence of auditory experience; she hears it. This case shows that the restriction of perception to positive entities is too narrow, and that an adequate analysis must make room for the perception of absences.

definition: A subject S perceives a temporal duration D if and only if (i) S has a perceptual experience which extends over a temporal interval and is not instantaneous, (ii) S's experience represents the successive parts of D as related to one another in a temporal order (earlier, later, simultaneous), and (iii) the represented duration corresponds to the actual duration of the external event perceived.
conditions: S has a perceptual experience which extends over a temporal interval and is not instantaneous
S's experience represents the successive parts of D as related in a temporal order
The represented duration corresponds to the actual duration of the external event perceived
counterexample: A subject under the influence of certain psychoactive substances, or in a state of extreme danger, reports that a brief event — a falling glass, a near-collision — seemed to last for an enormously long time. Her experience extends over a temporal interval, it represents the successive phases of the event in correct temporal order, yet the represented duration dramatically exceeds the actual duration of the external event.
missing_condition: The analysis assumes a correspondence between represented and actual duration, yet it fails to account for well-documented phenomena of temporal distortion in which subjective duration systematically diverges from objective duration without any disruption of temporal order.
passage: The perception of temporal duration presents peculiar philosophical difficulties, for unlike spatial extension, which can be surveyed at a glance, duration requires that the experience itself unfold in time. When one hears a melody, one does not hear all the notes simultaneously; one hears each note in succession, and yet one perceives the melody as a temporal whole with a determinate duration. The analysis of duration-perception requires that the experience extend over time, that it represent the parts of the duration in temporal order, and that the represented duration match the actual duration of the external event. One might observe, however, that this analysis encounters difficulty with the well-attested phenomenon of temporal distortion. It is widely reported that subjects in states of extreme fear or arousal — soldiers under fire, motorists in the moment of a crash, climbers in the act of falling — experience a dramatic slowing of time. A brief event which objectively lasts two or three seconds may be experienced as lasting twenty or thirty. The subject's experience extends over a temporal interval, and the successive phases of the event are represented in the correct temporal order — the glass tilts, then falls, then shatters — so the first two conditions are satisfied. But the represented duration far exceeds the actual duration of the event. The correspondence condition is violated, yet we should wish to say that the subject perceives the event and perceives its temporal character, albeit with distortion. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that temporal perception can be genuinely perceptual whilst systematically misrepresenting the metric properties of objective duration.

definition: A subject S perceives an object O if and only if (i) O causes S's perceptual experience E through an appropriate causal chain running from O through the intervening medium to S's sense organs and thence to S's brain, (ii) the causal chain does not include any deviant or abnormal links, and (iii) if the causal chain were broken or significantly altered, S would not have experience E.
conditions: O causes S's perceptual experience E through an appropriate causal chain from O to S's sense organs and brain
The causal chain does not include any deviant or abnormal links
If the causal chain were broken or significantly altered S would not have experience E
counterexample: A subject sees a barn facade — a flat painted surface constructed to look exactly like a real barn when viewed from the road. A real barn stands behind the facade and causes light to illuminate the scene, some of which reflects off the facade. The causal chain from barn to experience passes through a deviant link (the facade), yet one might also describe the facade itself as the perceived object with a perfectly normal causal chain. The analysis cannot determine which object is perceived because it provides no criterion for individuating the relevant causal chain.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes that the notion of an appropriate or non-deviant causal chain is well-defined, yet it provides no independent criterion for distinguishing deviant from non-deviant causal processes, leaving the analysis circular or incomplete.
passage: The causal theory of perception, developed in various forms by Grice and Strawson, holds that the relation between a perceived object and the perceiver's experience is fundamentally causal. On this view, what distinguishes perceiving an object from merely thinking about it or imagining it is that in perception the object itself plays an indispensable causal role in producing the experience. The analysis requires an appropriate causal chain from object to experience, the absence of deviant causal links, and a counterfactual dependence of the experience upon the chain's integrity. One might observe, however, that the notion of a deviant causal chain, upon which the analysis crucially depends, is not as transparent as it may seem. Consider a subject driving through the countryside who sees what appears to be a barn. In fact, it is a barn facade — a flat painted surface propped up to look like a barn from the road. Behind the facade stands an actual barn, and light from the sun, reflected in part from the real barn, illuminates the scene in a way that contributes to the appearance of the facade. The causal chain from real barn to experience runs through the facade, which the analysis might classify as a deviant link. But the facade itself is also an object in the scene, and the causal chain from facade to experience is perfectly normal. The analysis cannot, without further supplementation, determine whether the subject perceives the barn, the facade, or both, because it lacks a criterion for individuating the relevant causal chain and for distinguishing deviant from non-deviant causal processes. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that the crucial notion of causal appropriateness is left unanalysed.

definition: A subject S's perceptual belief that P is non-inferential if and only if (i) S arrives at the belief that P directly on the basis of perceptual experience, without any intermediate steps of reasoning, (ii) S is not aware of drawing any conclusion from premises to the belief that P, and (iii) the belief that P does not depend for its justification upon any other belief which S holds.
conditions: S arrives at the belief that P directly on the basis of perceptual experience without intermediate reasoning
S is not aware of drawing any conclusion from premises to the belief that P
The belief that P does not depend for its justification upon any other belief which S holds
counterexample: A subject glances at a weather vane and immediately forms the belief that the wind is blowing from the north. She is not aware of any inferential process; the belief seems to arise directly from her visual experience. Yet the belief plainly depends for its justification upon her background belief that the weather vane is functioning correctly and that it reliably indicates wind direction — beliefs she does not consciously entertain at the moment but without which the perceptual experience would not justify the belief about the wind.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates the absence of conscious inference with genuine epistemic independence, neglecting the possibility that a belief may feel non-inferential to the subject whilst depending for its justification upon tacit background beliefs.
passage: A long tradition in epistemology holds that perceptual beliefs are non-inferential — that they arise directly from sensory experience without passing through any process of reasoning. When one looks at a ripe tomato and forms the belief that there is a red object before one, this belief is not, it is said, the conclusion of a syllogism; it springs immediately from the perceptual encounter. The analysis requires that the belief be formed directly on the basis of experience, that the subject be unaware of any inferential process, and that the belief's justification be independent of other beliefs. One might observe, however, that certain cases reveal the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a subject glances at a weather vane mounted on a rooftop and immediately forms the belief that the wind is blowing from the north. She is not conscious of performing any inference; the belief seems to arise as directly from her visual experience as any paradigmatic perceptual belief. Yet a moment's reflection discloses that the justification of her belief depends upon a number of background beliefs which she holds tacitly: that the object she is looking at is a weather vane, that weather vanes indicate wind direction, that this particular weather vane is functioning correctly and has not been stuck in position. Without these background beliefs, her visual experience — an experience as of an arrow-shaped object pointing in a certain direction — would not justify any belief about the wind at all. The belief thus satisfies the first two conditions, for it is formed directly upon experience and the subject is unaware of any reasoning, yet it violates the third, for its justification is thoroughly dependent upon other beliefs. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it shows that the phenomenal immediacy of a belief is no guarantee of its epistemic independence.

definition: Perception is objective if and only if (i) the content of a perceptual experience represents mind-independent features of the world as they actually are, (ii) the perceptual experience is intersubjectively consistent, meaning that any normally functioning perceiver in the same circumstances would have an experience with the same content, and (iii) the perceptual content is not constitutively dependent upon the idiosyncratic features of the individual perceiver's psychology, physiology, or cultural background.
conditions: The content of the perceptual experience represents mind-independent features of the world as they actually are
The perceptual experience is intersubjectively consistent across normally functioning perceivers
The perceptual content is not constitutively dependent upon idiosyncratic features of the individual perceiver
counterexample: Members of cultures whose languages contain different numbers of basic colour terms perceive colour boundaries differently. Russian speakers, whose language distinguishes between light blue (goluboy) and dark blue (siniy) as separate basic colours, are measurably faster at discriminating between shades that cross this boundary than English speakers, for whom both are simply blue. Both groups are normally functioning perceivers in the same circumstances, yet their perceptual experiences differ owing to the linguistic and cultural categories they bring to perception.
missing_condition: The analysis assumes that the content of perception is independent of cultural and linguistic factors, yet it fails to account for the well-documented influence of language and conceptual repertoire upon the very structure of perceptual experience.
passage: The claim that perception is objective — that it presents the world as it is, independently of the peculiarities of the perceiver — has been a foundational assumption of empiricism and a cornerstone of the scientific worldview. If perception were merely subjective, varying idiosyncratically from person to person, it could hardly serve as a reliable guide to the nature of external reality. The analysis of perceptual objectivity requires that the content of experience represent mind-independent features accurately, that this content be consistent across normally functioning perceivers, and that it be free from constitutive dependence on the individual perceiver's psychology, physiology, or cultural background. One might observe, however, that cross-linguistic research on colour perception reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. It has been demonstrated that speakers of Russian, whose language draws a compulsory distinction between light blue and dark blue as separate basic colour categories, are significantly faster at discriminating between shades of blue that fall on different sides of this linguistic boundary than are speakers of English, for whom no such compulsory distinction exists. Both groups of subjects have normally functioning visual systems and observe under the same conditions, yet their perceptual discriminations differ. The difference is attributable to the linguistic categories they bring to the perceptual task — a cultural and psychological factor which, on the analysis, should be irrelevant to perceptual content. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for it demonstrates that perception is not wholly independent of the perceiver's conceptual and linguistic repertoire and that intersubjective consistency cannot be assumed across perceivers from different cultural backgrounds.

definition: A subject S has testimonial knowledge that p if and only if (i) some speaker T asserts that p to S, (ii) T knows that p, (iii) S comes to believe that p on the basis of T's assertion, and (iv) S has no undefeated defeaters for p.
conditions: Some speaker T asserts that p to S
T knows that p
S comes to believe that p on the basis of T's assertion
S has no undefeated defeaters for p
counterexample: A geologist tells a colleague that there is tin in a particular hillside, knowing this to be so, and the colleague believes it on precisely that basis, possessing no defeating evidence. Yet the geologist's knowledge was acquired through a method that, unbeknownst to either party, has been systematically unreliable in similar geological surveys, so that the colleague's belief, though true and based on testimony from a knower, fails to constitute knowledge in the relevant sense.
missing_condition: The analysis omits any requirement that the method by which the testifier originally acquired knowledge be reliable in the circumstances of its transmission, so that merely having a knower assert p is insufficient to ground testimonial knowledge when the epistemic provenance is defective.
passage: One might suppose that the simplest account of testimonial knowledge would require nothing more than that a knowledgeable speaker communicate a proposition to a hearer, who then forms belief accordingly and encounters no countervailing evidence. Such an analysis, it appears, would capture the ordinary cases tolerably well: when a colleague informs one that the departmental meeting has been moved to Thursday, and one accepts this on his authority, one thereby comes to know the relevant fact, provided the colleague himself knows it and one possesses no reason to doubt him. Yet there exists a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a geologist who tells a fellow researcher that a hillside in Cornwall contains significant deposits of tin. The geologist does indeed know this, having conducted an assay, and the colleague believes the report without reservation. However, the assay method employed, though it happened to yield a correct result on this occasion, has been shown by subsequent investigation to be systematically unreliable in geological contexts of just this sort. The colleague's belief is true, is based upon genuine testimony from a genuine knower, and is undefeated by any evidence available to either party. Nevertheless, one might hesitate to describe the colleague as possessing testimonial knowledge, for the epistemic chain leading from the world to the testifier's own knowledge is flawed in a manner that the simple analysis fails to acknowledge. What appears to be wanting is a condition addressing the reliability of the original method of knowledge-acquisition as it bears upon the transmitted belief.

definition: Reductionism about testimony holds that a hearer S is justified in believing that p on the basis of T's testimony if and only if (i) T testifies that p, (ii) S possesses independent positive reasons for regarding T's testimony as reliable, and (iii) these independent reasons are not themselves ultimately grounded in testimony.
conditions: T testifies that p
S possesses independent positive reasons for regarding T's testimony as reliable
These independent reasons are not themselves ultimately grounded in testimony
counterexample: A young child acquires the knowledge that fire burns by being told so by her mother, and this knowledge proves entirely justified and reliable, yet the child possesses no independent positive reasons whatsoever for trusting the mother's testimony — she simply accepts it as given. The reductionist analysis would deny that the child has justified belief, which seems an unacceptable result.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to accommodate the possibility that certain testimonial beliefs are justified by default or by the nature of the speaker-hearer relationship, without the hearer possessing any independent evidential basis for trust.
passage: The reductionist about testimony insists that one is never justified in accepting another's word unless one has, so to speak, earned the right to trust through independent verification. One must possess reasons — drawn from perception, memory, or inductive inference, but emphatically not from further testimony — for thinking the speaker reliable, before the speaker's assertion can serve as a ground of justified belief. This is, on the face of it, a demanding requirement, and one might observe that it appears to capture something important about the epistemic responsibility of the mature inquirer. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a very young child, perhaps three years of age, whose mother warns her that fire burns and that she must not touch the flame of a candle. The child believes this, and the belief is both true and, by any reasonable standard, justified. The child goes on to act prudently in the vicinity of flames, and her behaviour reflects genuine understanding of the danger. But the child possesses no independent reasons for trusting her mother's testimony. She has not conducted experiments with fire, nor has she observed a correlation between her mother's assertions and subsequent perceptual confirmations. She simply accepts what she is told. The reductionist must either deny that the child's belief is justified — a conclusion which strikes one as deeply counterintuitive — or else relax the requirement of independent positive reasons, thereby abandoning the central tenet of the reductionist programme. It appears, then, that the analysis is too restrictive, failing to account for the manifestly justified beliefs of epistemic agents who have not yet accumulated the evidential resources that the reductionist demands.

definition: Anti-reductionism about testimony holds that a hearer S is justified in believing that p on the basis of T's testimony if and only if (i) T testifies that p, (ii) S has no undefeated defeaters for the proposition that T's testimony is reliable, and (iii) S understands the content of T's assertion.
conditions: T testifies that p
S has no undefeated defeaters for the proposition that T's testimony is reliable
S understands the content of T's assertion
counterexample: A stranger on a train, who is in fact a compulsive fabricator, tells a passenger that the next station is Didcot Parkway. The passenger has no reason to doubt the stranger, understands the assertion perfectly, and believes it. The belief happens to be true, but the stranger's testimony is wholly unreliable, being the product of habitual confabulation rather than knowledge. The anti-reductionist analysis would deem the passenger justified, yet there is something epistemically deficient about believing a compulsive liar who merely happens to speak truly.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects any requirement that the testifier be in fact reliable or that there be some objective connection between the testifier's assertion and the truth, so that the mere absence of defeaters is insufficient to ground justification when the source is systematically untrustworthy.
passage: The anti-reductionist maintains that testimony enjoys a kind of default epistemic authority: one is entitled to believe what one is told unless one has positive reason to withhold credence. This is an attractively liberal view, and it appears to account for the vast quantity of knowledge that we acquire, from earliest childhood, through the reports of others. We do not, in the ordinary course of affairs, subject each informant to a battery of credibility tests before accepting his word, and the anti-reductionist contends that we are right not to do so. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that a traveller on a railway journey asks a fellow passenger — a complete stranger — which station is next. The stranger replies, with apparent confidence, that the next stop is Didcot Parkway. The traveller, possessing no reason to doubt this assertion and understanding it perfectly well, forms the belief accordingly. As it happens, the stranger is a compulsive fabricator who invents answers to questions as a matter of habit; on this occasion, however, his fabrication coincides with the truth. The anti-reductionist analysis, requiring only that there be no undefeated defeaters and that the hearer understand the content, would pronounce the traveller justified. But one might reasonably object that a belief formed on the say-so of a compulsive liar, who merely chances to speak truly, ought not to count as justified, even if the hearer has no access to the deficiency. What seems to be missing is a condition requiring some objective reliability on the part of the testifier, a condition which the anti-reductionist framework, in its eagerness to preserve the default authority of testimony, has set aside.

definition: Testimony is reliable if and only if (i) the testifier sincerely asserts that p, (ii) the testifier believes that p, and (iii) the testifier intends to communicate the truth to the hearer.
conditions: The testifier sincerely asserts that p
The testifier believes that p
The testifier intends to communicate the truth to the hearer
counterexample: A physician sincerely tells a patient that a particular medication has no serious side effects, genuinely believes this, and intends to communicate the truth. Yet the physician's belief is founded upon a misreading of a clinical trial, and the medication does in fact carry serious risks. The testimony satisfies all three conditions of sincerity but is thoroughly unreliable.
missing_condition: The analysis treats sincerity as sufficient for reliability but omits any requirement that the testifier's belief be well-founded or that the testifier possess adequate epistemic grounds for the proposition asserted.
passage: It is natural to suppose that the sincerity of the speaker is among the most important guarantees of testimonial reliability. When one asks a stranger for directions, one assumes that he will tell one what he believes to be the case, and that his intention is to help rather than to mislead. If these assumptions hold, one might think, the testimony is reliable. The analysis under consideration formalises this thought by requiring that the testifier sincerely assert, genuinely believe, and intend to communicate what is true. One might observe, however, that there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Imagine a physician who, with complete sincerity and the best of intentions, informs a patient that a newly prescribed medication carries no risk of serious adverse effects. The physician believes this firmly, having read the summary of a recent clinical trial. Unfortunately, the physician has misread the trial data: the summary in question reported statistically significant adverse effects, but the physician, unfamiliar with certain statistical conventions, interpreted the results as indicating safety. The testimony is sincere, the belief is genuine, and the intention to communicate truth is beyond question, yet the testimony is manifestly unreliable, for it leads the patient to a false and potentially dangerous belief. What the analysis overlooks is that sincerity, belief, and good intention are together compatible with gross epistemic negligence on the part of the testifier.
