A reliable testimony, it appears, requires not merely that the speaker believe what he says, but that his belief be grounded in adequate evidence or competent inquiry.

definition: A testifier T is competent with respect to p if and only if (i) T has undergone training or acquired experience relevant to p, (ii) T's past assertions in the domain of p have been largely accurate, and (iii) T is recognised by other competent persons as an authority on matters pertaining to p.
conditions: T has undergone training or acquired experience relevant to p
T's past assertions in the domain of p have been largely accurate
T is recognised by other competent persons as an authority on matters pertaining to p
counterexample: An amateur astronomer with no formal training and no recognised standing among professional astronomers nevertheless correctly identifies a nova in a region of the sky that professionals had neglected to observe. Her testimony is accurate and reflects genuine competence in this instance, yet she satisfies none of the three conditions laid down by the analysis.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to allow for the possibility that competence may be exhibited in a particular case without any prior track record, formal training, or social recognition, thereby conflating general credentialled authority with the actual epistemic capacity to judge correctly on a given occasion.
passage: One frequently hears it said that a testifier's competence is to be assessed by reference to her training, her track record, and the esteem in which she is held by her peers. These are, to be sure, useful indicators in the ordinary course of inquiry: when one consults a physician about a medical complaint, one is reassured to learn that she has completed the requisite medical training, that her diagnoses have generally proved correct, and that her colleagues regard her as sound. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider an amateur astronomer — a retired schoolmaster, let us say — who spends her evenings observing the heavens through a modest telescope. She has no formal training in astronomy, her past observations have never been subjected to professional scrutiny, and she is entirely unknown to the astronomical community. One night she notices an unfamiliar point of light in the constellation Cygnus and, after careful observation over several evenings, concludes that it is a nova. She reports this to a local astronomical society, and subsequent professional investigation confirms her identification. The amateur's testimony was competent in the fullest epistemic sense: she observed carefully, reasoned correctly, and arrived at a true conclusion. Yet she satisfies none of the three conditions specified in the analysis. She had no relevant training, no established track record, and no professional recognition. It appears, therefore, that the analysis confuses the sociological markers of competence with competence itself, and that a more adequate account must allow for the possibility that an individual may possess genuine epistemic competence in the absence of all three specified conditions.

definition: Hearsay testimony transmits knowledge that p if and only if (i) the original testifier T1 knows that p, (ii) each intermediary in the testimonial chain faithfully reports what the previous link asserted, (iii) the final hearer S believes that p on the basis of the chain, and (iv) the chain contains no link at which the content is distorted.
conditions: The original testifier T1 knows that p
Each intermediary faithfully reports what the previous link asserted
The final hearer S believes that p on the basis of the chain
The chain contains no link at which the content is distorted
counterexample: A scholar tells a colleague that a manuscript dates from the fourteenth century; the colleague tells a student the same; the student tells a librarian. Every link is faithful, and the original scholar knew the fact. Yet the student, when relaying the information, added an explanatory gloss — 'it dates from the fourteenth century, which is to say the 1400s' — thereby introducing a subtle error (the fourteenth century being the 1300s). The librarian receives a distorted proposition, yet every participant believed himself to be faithfully reporting the previous assertion.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between faithful reporting of the words used and faithful preservation of the propositional content, so that a well-intentioned paraphrase or gloss can introduce distortion even when each link sincerely aims at fidelity.
passage: The transmission of knowledge through chains of testimony is a phenomenon of considerable epistemic importance, for much of what we know reaches us not from the original knower but through one or several intermediaries. The analysis considered here attempts to specify the conditions under which such chains preserve knowledge: the original source must know the proposition, each intermediary must faithfully pass on what he received, the final hearer must form belief on this basis, and no distortion may occur at any link. One might suppose that these requirements, taken jointly, would be both necessary and sufficient. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. A palaeographer, examining a manuscript in a university library, determines that it dates from the fourteenth century. He mentions this to a colleague in the history department, who in turn tells a graduate student, who subsequently informs the head librarian. At each stage, the speaker sincerely intends to relay exactly what he was told, and in one sense each succeeds. However, the graduate student, wishing to be helpful, adds a clarificatory remark: 'It dates from the fourteenth century — that is, the 1400s.' The student has confused centuries with their numerical designations, for the fourteenth century in fact comprises the years 1300 to 1399. The librarian, trusting the student's gloss, now believes the manuscript to date from the 1400s. Every link in the chain reported faithfully in the sense of intending to preserve what was said, and yet the propositional content has been distorted. The analysis fails because it does not adequately distinguish between the faithful repetition of words and the faithful preservation of the proposition expressed, a distinction which becomes pressing whenever paraphrase or explanatory gloss enters the chain.

definition: Expert testimony provides the hearer S with justified belief that p if and only if (i) the testifier T possesses specialist knowledge in the domain of p, (ii) T asserts that p within the scope of T's expertise, (iii) S is not in a position to independently evaluate p, and (iv) there is no equally credentialled expert who asserts not-p.
conditions: The testifier T possesses specialist knowledge in the domain of p
T asserts that p within the scope of T's expertise
S is not in a position to independently evaluate p
There is no equally credentialled expert who asserts not-p
counterexample: Two equally credentialled climatologists disagree about whether a particular feedback mechanism will amplify warming by more than two degrees over the next century. A layperson, unable to evaluate the matter independently, hears both views. By the analysis, the layperson is justified in believing neither proposition. Yet one climatologist's argument is demonstrably stronger, resting on more recent and comprehensive data, and it would seem unreasonable to withhold belief entirely merely because an equally credentialled dissenter exists.
missing_condition: The analysis treats the mere existence of an equally credentialled dissenter as a conclusive defeater, without attending to the relative quality of the arguments and evidence adduced by the competing experts.
passage: When a layperson consults an expert, he does so precisely because he is unable to evaluate the matter for himself and must rely upon the specialist's superior knowledge. The analysis under examination captures this dependency by requiring that the expert possess genuine specialist knowledge, that the assertion fall within his competence, that the hearer lack independent evaluative capacity, and that no equally credentialled authority contradict the claim. The last condition is intended to guard against the layperson's blindly accepting one expert's word when the matter is genuinely disputed among those best placed to judge. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that two climatologists, each holding professorships at reputable universities and each possessing impeccable credentials, disagree about the magnitude of a particular climatic feedback mechanism. One argues, on the basis of extensive and recent satellite data, that the mechanism will amplify global warming by more than two degrees Celsius within the century; the other, relying on older modelling assumptions, contends that the amplification will be modest. A member of the public, lacking the scientific training to adjudicate the dispute, hears both views. The analysis would require that, since an equally credentialled expert asserts the contrary, the layperson is not justified in believing either proposition. But this seems an unsatisfactory result. The first climatologist's argument is clearly stronger, resting upon more comprehensive and more recent evidence. A reasonable layperson, apprised of the considerations on each side, might well be justified in crediting the stronger case. The analysis errs in treating the existence of a credentialled dissenter as automatically defeating justification, without regard to the quality of the reasoning and evidence that each expert adduces.

definition: A testifier T's track record is a sufficient ground for trusting T's testimony that p if and only if (i) T has made a significant number of past assertions in the domain of p, (ii) a high proportion of those assertions have proved to be true, and (iii) the present assertion that p falls within the same domain as T's past assertions.
conditions: T has made a significant number of past assertions in the domain of p
A high proportion of those assertions have proved to be true
The present assertion falls within the same domain as T's past assertions
counterexample: A political commentator has an excellent track record of predicting election outcomes over two decades. She now predicts the outcome of an election in a country whose political system has recently undergone a fundamental transformation — a shift from a two-party to a multi-party system. Her prediction falls squarely within the domain of election forecasting, yet the changed circumstances render her track record largely irrelevant to the present case.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the conditions under which past assertions were made be relevantly similar to the conditions under which the present assertion is offered, so that a track record compiled under different circumstances may provide no evidential support.
passage: It is a commonplace of both ordinary and philosophical reflection upon testimony that one ought to attend to the speaker's track record. If a friend has repeatedly given one accurate directions, one has some reason to trust his next set of directions; if a newspaper has frequently published erroneous reports, one has reason to be cautious about its latest offering. The analysis under consideration formalises this thought by requiring a significant number of past assertions, a high proportion of truth among them, and the present assertion's falling within the same domain. One might observe, however, that there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a political commentator who has, over the course of twenty years, built an impressive record of correctly predicting election outcomes in her home country. Her success rate is well above that of her rivals, and her analyses have been praised for their acuity. She now turns her attention to an upcoming election in the same country, but in the intervening period the political landscape has undergone a radical transformation: the long-standing two-party system has fractured into a multi-party configuration, new voting blocs have emerged, and the electorate's behaviour has become far less predictable. Her prediction falls within the same domain — electoral forecasting — and her track record is undeniably strong. Yet the conditions under which that record was compiled are fundamentally different from those now obtaining, and there is little reason to suppose that expertise honed under one set of political arrangements will transfer to another. The analysis, by attending only to domain and past accuracy, neglects the crucial question of whether the circumstances of the present assertion are relevantly similar to those of the past.

definition: A testifier T lies about p if and only if (i) T asserts that p, (ii) T believes that p is false, and (iii) T intends to cause the hearer S to believe that p.
conditions: T asserts that p
T believes that p is false
T intends to cause the hearer S to believe that p
counterexample: A diplomat, when asked whether her government is considering sanctions, replies: 'I am not aware of any such discussions.' She knows perfectly well that sanctions are under active consideration, but she does not assert that no discussions are taking place — she merely reports her own putative state of awareness, choosing her words so as to create a false impression without asserting a falsehood. She satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) in spirit but does not, strictly speaking, assert the proposition she wishes the hearer to believe.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for cases of misleading testimony in which the speaker deliberately creates a false impression by means of implicature, presupposition, or carefully chosen wording, without asserting the false proposition itself.
passage: The distinction between lying and merely misleading is one to which philosophers have rightly drawn attention, for it bears directly upon the analysis of testimonial reliability. The standard account of lying requires that the speaker assert a proposition he believes to be false with the intention of inducing belief in that proposition. This captures the paradigm cases tolerably well: the witness who swears that he was at home on the evening in question, knowing full well that he was elsewhere, is lying in precisely the sense specified. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis as a general account of deceptive testimony. Consider a diplomat who is asked, at a press conference, whether her government is considering the imposition of economic sanctions against a neighbouring state. She is aware that such discussions are well advanced, but she replies, with studied care: 'I am not aware of any such discussions.' Now, it is open to the diplomat to maintain that she has not lied, for she has not asserted that no discussions are taking place; she has merely reported her own state of awareness, and she might even contend, with some ingenuity, that the phrase 'not aware of' is technically compatible with the discussions' existence. Nevertheless, her intention is plainly to cause the journalist to believe that no such discussions are under way, and the journalist, unless unusually astute, will draw precisely that inference. The diplomat has engaged in misleading testimony — she has deliberately created a false impression — without satisfying the conditions of the analysis, for she has not asserted the false proposition. The analysis thus fails to capture an important species of testimonial deception, one which operates through implicature and careful wording rather than outright assertion of a known falsehood.

definition: Collective testimony from an institution I provides justified belief that p if and only if (i) I issues an official report asserting that p, (ii) the report is the product of a properly constituted deliberative process within I, (iii) the individuals who contributed to the report are competent in the relevant domain, and (iv) the report reflects the consensus of those individuals.
conditions: I issues an official report asserting that p
The report is the product of a properly constituted deliberative process
The individuals who contributed are competent in the relevant domain
The report reflects the consensus of those individuals
counterexample: A government health agency issues an official report concluding that a particular pesticide poses no risk to human health. The deliberative process was properly constituted, and the contributing scientists are competent. However, several of the most knowledgeable scientists dissented vigorously, and the reported consensus was achieved only by excluding their objections from the final document. The report thus reflects an artificial consensus rather than a genuine one, yet it satisfies all four conditions as stated.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the consensus be genuine and unmanipulated, so that a report which suppresses significant internal dissent may satisfy the stated conditions while failing to provide the epistemic assurance that collective testimony is supposed to confer.
passage: In modern societies, a great deal of the testimony upon which citizens rely is issued not by individuals but by institutions: government agencies, professional bodies, scientific organisations, and the like. The epistemic authority of such testimony is commonly thought to derive from the collective expertise and deliberative rigour that institutions can bring to bear upon complex questions. The analysis under consideration requires that the institution issue an official report, that the report result from proper deliberation, that the contributors be competent, and that the report reflect their consensus. These conditions appear, at first glance, to be both reasonable and sufficient. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that a government health agency is tasked with assessing the safety of a widely used agricultural pesticide. A committee of toxicologists and epidemiologists is convened, and after months of deliberation, the agency issues an official report declaring the pesticide safe for human use. The process was properly constituted, and the scientists who participated are undeniably competent. However, it emerges that several of the committee's most experienced members — those with the deepest knowledge of the relevant epidemiological data — dissented from the majority view, arguing that the evidence of harm was being systematically underweighted. Their objections were noted in the minutes but were excluded from the final report, which presents the conclusion as a unanimous consensus. The report satisfies every condition of the analysis: it is official, properly deliberated, produced by competent persons, and reflective of what purports to be a consensus. Yet the consensus is manufactured rather than genuine, and the epistemic assurance that collective testimony is meant to provide is correspondingly undermined. What is wanted is a further condition requiring that the consensus be authentic and that significant dissent not be suppressed.

definition: Testimony across languages provides justified belief that p if and only if (i) the original speaker T asserts that p in language L1, (ii) an interpreter or translator renders T's assertion into language L2 for hearer S, (iii) the translation preserves the propositional content of T's assertion, and (iv) S understands the translated assertion.
conditions: The original speaker T asserts that p in language L1
An interpreter renders T's assertion into language L2 for hearer S
The translation preserves the propositional content of T's assertion
S understands the translated assertion
counterexample: A Japanese witness in a legal proceeding testifies that the defendant acted 'shikata ga nai' — a phrase conveying resigned acceptance that what happened was unavoidable. The interpreter translates this as 'he did not care what happened,' which, though not wholly inaccurate, shifts the propositional content from inevitability to indifference. The hearer understands the translated assertion, and the interpreter acted in good faith, yet the propositional content has been subtly but significantly altered.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for the fact that certain propositions are embedded in cultural and linguistic frameworks that resist straightforward translation, so that even a competent and conscientious interpreter may be unable to preserve the propositional content without remainder.
passage: A great deal of the testimony upon which we rely in a globalised world reaches us through the medium of translation, and it is therefore a matter of some epistemic importance to determine the conditions under which translated testimony transmits justified belief. The analysis under consideration specifies four requirements: the original speaker asserts the proposition, an interpreter renders it into the hearer's language, the propositional content is preserved, and the hearer understands the result. These appear to be sensible desiderata, and in many cases they are satisfied without difficulty. When a French scientist reports her experimental results to an English-speaking colleague through an interpreter, the propositional content — concerning temperatures, pressures, and chemical compositions — is readily translatable, and the four conditions are met. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a legal proceeding in which a Japanese witness describes the actions of the defendant using the expression 'shikata ga nai,' a culturally freighted phrase that conveys something like resigned acceptance of an inevitable state of affairs. The court interpreter, working conscientiously but constrained by the resources of the target language, renders this as 'he did not care what happened.' The hearer — the judge — understands the English sentence perfectly well and forms a belief about the defendant's state of mind. But the propositional content has been shifted in a significant respect: what was expressed as a recognition of inevitability has become an ascription of indifference. The translation has not preserved the original proposition, yet condition (iii) is difficult to apply, for the interpreter may reasonably contend that no English phrase exactly captures the Japanese original. The analysis presupposes that propositional content can always be preserved across languages, but there exist cases in which the cultural and conceptual resources of the source language have no precise analogue in the target language, and the analysis offers no guidance for such situations.

definition: Testimony that p is adequately corroborated if and only if (i) at least two independent sources testify that p, (ii) the sources have no common origin or motivation for asserting that p, and (iii) the hearer S has no reason to believe that the sources have coordinated their assertions.
conditions: At least two independent sources testify that p
The sources have no common origin or motivation for asserting that p
The hearer has no reason to believe the sources have coordinated their assertions
counterexample: Two newspapers independently report that a particular bridge has been closed for repairs. They have no common origin and no motivation to coordinate. However, both newspapers obtained the information from the same council press release, which contained an error — the bridge mentioned was not the one actually closed. The corroboration is illusory, for the independence of the sources is undermined by a shared informational origin that the analysis, as stated, fails to detect.
missing_condition: The analysis requires that sources have no common origin but does not extend this requirement to the ultimate informational source from which the testimony derives, so that sources may appear independent whilst drawing upon the same (possibly erroneous) evidence.
passage: Corroboration is widely regarded as a powerful means of strengthening testimonial evidence, and with good reason: when two persons who have no connection with one another both report the same fact, the probability that both are mistaken or dishonest is, ceteris paribus, considerably less than the probability that either alone is so. The analysis captures this thought by requiring that at least two independent sources testify to the same proposition, that they share no common origin or motivation, and that the hearer have no reason to suspect coordination. In many ordinary circumstances, these conditions serve well enough. If two neighbours, who do not know one another, both inform one that the road at the end of the street is flooded, one has good corroborative grounds for believing that it is. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that two local newspapers, operating entirely independently and with no shared editorial agenda, both report that a certain bridge on the outskirts of the town has been closed for structural repairs. A resident, reading both reports and knowing the papers to be unconnected, takes the corroboration as strong evidence that the bridge is indeed closed. In fact, however, both newspapers derived their information from the same source: a press release issued by the county council which, owing to an administrative error, identified the wrong bridge. The bridge reported as closed is perfectly serviceable; it is a different bridge, some miles away, that is under repair. The two sources satisfy the conditions of independence as stated in the analysis — they have no common origin in the sense of shared editorial direction, and no motivation to coordinate. But they share a common informational source, and it is this shared source, rather than any coordination between the papers, that accounts for their agreement. The corroboration is therefore illusory, and the analysis fails because it does not require independence at the level of the ultimate evidential basis.

definition: Anonymous testimony provides justified belief that p if and only if (i) the testifier asserts that p, (ii) the content of the assertion is coherent and internally consistent, (iii) the assertion is not contradicted by the hearer's background knowledge, and (iv) the hearer S has no specific reason to distrust the source.
conditions: The testifier asserts that p
The content of the assertion is coherent and internally consistent
The assertion is not contradicted by the hearer's background knowledge
The hearer has no specific reason to distrust the source
counterexample: An anonymous letter is sent to a local newspaper alleging financial impropriety by a public official. The letter is coherent, internally consistent, not contradicted by the reader's background knowledge, and the reader has no specific reason to distrust the anonymous author. Yet the allegation is grave, and a reasonable person would require more than coherence and the absence of contradiction before forming a justified belief about the official's conduct — precisely because the anonymity of the source prevents any assessment of the testifier's epistemic position or motives.
missing_condition: The analysis omits any requirement that the gravity or consequentiality of the proposition be weighed against the degree of epistemic access the hearer has to the testifier's credentials, motives, and epistemic position, so that coherence and non-contradiction are insufficient for justified belief when the stakes are high and the source is wholly unknown.
passage: Anonymous testimony presents distinctive epistemic challenges, for when one does not know who is speaking, one is deprived of the usual means of assessing credibility: one cannot evaluate the speaker's expertise, his track record, or his possible motives for deception. The analysis under consideration attempts to compensate for this deficit by appealing to the internal qualities of the testimony — its coherence, its consistency, and its compatibility with what the hearer already knows — together with the absence of specific grounds for distrust. These are not unreasonable criteria, and in cases of modest epistemic import they may well suffice. If an anonymous note is left on one's desk informing one that the office will close early on Friday, and the note is coherent and not contradicted by anything one knows, it seems perfectly rational to adjust one's plans accordingly. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose an anonymous letter arrives at the offices of a local newspaper, alleging that a prominent public official has been engaged in systematic financial impropriety. The letter is articulate, internally consistent, and its claims are not contradicted by anything in the public record. The editor has no specific reason to distrust the anonymous source. Nevertheless, it would be epistemically irresponsible to publish the allegations as established fact, or even to form a settled belief in their truth, on the strength of an anonymous communication alone. The gravity of the charge and the impossibility of assessing the source's epistemic position, motives, or relationship to the alleged events demand a higher standard of evidence than mere coherence and non-contradiction. The analysis fails to incorporate any sensitivity to the seriousness of the proposition asserted, treating all anonymous testimony alike regardless of the stakes involved.

definition: A hearer S is justified in accepting testimony that p if and only if (i) a testifier T asserts that p, (ii) S's background knowledge does not render p improbable, and (iii) T's assertion is consistent with S's other beliefs.
conditions: A testifier T asserts that p
S's background knowledge does not render p improbable
T's assertion is consistent with S's other beliefs
counterexample: A layperson in the early twentieth century is told by a physicist that light bends when passing near a massive object. The layperson's background knowledge and existing beliefs — drawn from ordinary experience with light travelling in straight lines — render the proposition improbable and inconsistent with his other beliefs. By the analysis, the layperson is not justified in accepting the testimony. Yet the physicist's assertion is true and well-supported, and the layperson's deference to expert testimony would in fact be epistemically rational.
missing_condition: The analysis gives the hearer's background knowledge and existing beliefs a veto over the acceptance of testimony, without allowing for the possibility that the testifier's epistemic authority may rightly override the hearer's prior expectations, particularly in domains where the hearer is not competent to judge.
passage: It is a natural thought that a hearer ought not to accept testimony which conflicts with what he already knows or believes, for to do so would be to abandon one's own epistemic resources in favour of another's unsupported word. The analysis under consideration gives expression to this thought by requiring that the hearer's background knowledge not render the proposition improbable and that the assertion be consistent with the hearer's other beliefs. In many cases, these conditions serve as useful safeguards: one ought not to believe a stranger who informs one that the moon is made of green cheese, for one's background knowledge renders this proposition exceedingly improbable. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a layperson in the early decades of the twentieth century who is told by a distinguished physicist that light, when passing near a sufficiently massive body such as the sun, does not travel in a straight line but follows a curved path. The layperson's background knowledge, derived from a lifetime of ordinary experience, strongly suggests that light travels in straight lines; the physicist's assertion is not merely improbable on the layperson's evidence but appears to contradict well-established beliefs about the behaviour of light. The analysis would therefore pronounce the layperson unjustified in accepting the testimony. Yet this conclusion is deeply unsatisfactory, for the physicist's claim is both true and supported by the most rigorous theoretical and experimental considerations. A layperson who deferred to the physicist's expertise would, far from being epistemically irresponsible, be exercising a form of rationality that the analysis cannot accommodate — namely, the rational recognition that, in domains where one's own competence is limited, the testimony of those with superior knowledge may rightly override one's prior expectations.

definition: Written testimony is epistemically equivalent to spoken testimony if and only if (i) both convey the same propositional content, (ii) both are produced by a testifier with the same degree of sincerity, and (iii) the hearer or reader has the same access to contextual cues in both cases.
conditions: Both convey the same propositional content
Both are produced with the same degree of sincerity
The hearer or reader has the same access to contextual cues in both cases
counterexample: A witness to an accident gives a spoken account to a police officer, conveying through tone of voice, hesitation, and facial expression a degree of uncertainty about a crucial detail — the colour of the car involved. The same witness later writes a statement containing the same propositional content, expressed with equal sincerity, but the written statement, lacking the paralinguistic cues, reads as confident and unhedged. A reader of the written statement has systematically less access to contextual cues than the officer who heard the spoken account.
missing_condition: The analysis assumes that access to contextual cues can be held constant between written and spoken testimony, but in fact spoken testimony inherently carries paralinguistic information — tone, hesitation, emphasis — that written testimony cannot reproduce, so that condition (iii) is in practice never fully satisfied.
passage: One might be tempted to suppose that the medium through which testimony is transmitted — whether by speech or by writing — is of no epistemic consequence, provided that the content, the sincerity, and the available context remain the same. This is the position captured by the analysis, which treats written and spoken testimony as epistemically equivalent whenever these three conditions are met. The symmetry is appealing in its simplicity, and in many cases it may hold: a mathematical proof communicated in a letter is no less compelling than one delivered in a lecture, provided the content is the same. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Imagine a witness to a road accident who gives a spoken account to a police officer at the scene. In the course of her narrative, she describes the car involved as being, she thinks, dark blue, but her voice wavers, she pauses, and her expression suggests considerable uncertainty. The officer, attending to these paralinguistic cues, records in his notes that the witness was unsure of the colour. The same witness later provides a written statement for the record. She writes: 'The car was dark blue.' The propositional content is the same, and her sincerity is undiminished. But the written statement, stripped of the hesitations and vocal uncertainties that attended the spoken account, reads as a confident assertion. A reader of the statement, encountering no hedging or qualification, naturally takes the witness to be certain of the colour. The analysis requires that the hearer or reader have the same access to contextual cues, but this condition is, in the nature of the case, unsatisfiable when one compares spoken and written testimony, for spoken communication inherently carries paralinguistic information that writing cannot reproduce.

definition: Scientific testimony that p is epistemically warranted if and only if (i) the testifier is a credentialled scientist in the relevant field, (ii) the claim that p has been subjected to peer review, (iii) the result upon which p is based has been independently replicated, and (iv) no significant methodological objections to the supporting research remain unanswered.
conditions: The testifier is a credentialled scientist in the relevant field
The claim has been subjected to peer review
The result has been independently replicated
No significant methodological objections remain unanswered
counterexample: A Nobel laureate in chemistry publishes a paper claiming to have achieved cold fusion. The paper passes peer review at a reputable journal, and two other laboratories report replications. No methodological objections have been sustained. Yet the replications turn out to have been conducted by laboratories with close ties to the original researcher, using his equipment and protocols, so that the supposed independent confirmation is not genuinely independent.
missing_condition: The analysis requires independent replication but does not specify what constitutes genuine independence, so that replications conducted by closely affiliated laboratories, using shared methods and equipment, may satisfy the letter of the condition whilst failing to provide the evidential support that genuine independence would confer.
passage: The authority of scientific testimony is commonly held to rest upon the institutional safeguards of the scientific enterprise: the training of its practitioners, the rigour of peer review, and the discipline of replication. The analysis under consideration incorporates these safeguards as necessary conditions, requiring that the testifier be credentialled, the claim peer-reviewed, the result replicated, and any methodological objections addressed. These are demanding conditions, and one might suppose that their joint satisfaction would be sufficient to warrant belief. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that a distinguished chemist, a laureate of the Nobel Prize, announces that he has achieved sustained nuclear fusion at room temperature. His paper is submitted to a leading journal, where it survives the scrutiny of referees and is published. Within months, two other laboratories report that they have replicated the result, and no reviewer has lodged an unanswered methodological objection. The four conditions of the analysis are satisfied. However, it subsequently emerges that the two replicating laboratories are headed by former students of the original researcher, that they used apparatus of the same design supplied by his laboratory, and that their experimental protocols were drawn directly from his unpublished notes. The replications, though technically conducted by other laboratories, are not genuinely independent: they share too many features of method, equipment, and intellectual provenance with the original experiment to constitute the kind of independent confirmation that the scientific community requires. The analysis specifies replication but does not adequately characterise the independence that replication must possess if it is to serve its epistemic function.

definition: Testimony given under coercion is epistemically worthless if and only if (i) the testifier T is subjected to threats, violence, or other forms of duress, (ii) T's assertion that p is produced as a direct result of that coercion, and (iii) T would not have asserted that p in the absence of coercion.
conditions: The testifier is subjected to threats, violence, or other forms of duress
T's assertion that p is produced as a direct result of that coercion
T would not have asserted that p in the absence of coercion
counterexample: A prisoner of war, subjected to severe interrogation, confesses to having observed troop movements at a particular location. The confession is coerced, produced as a direct result of the duress, and the prisoner would not have volunteered this information otherwise. Yet the prisoner did in fact observe the troop movements, and his coerced testimony happens to be both true and reflective of genuine knowledge. Dismissing it as epistemically worthless would be to discard a veridical report solely because of the circumstances of its extraction.
missing_condition: The analysis treats all coerced testimony as uniformly worthless, without distinguishing between cases in which coercion produces fabrication and cases in which coercion merely compels the disclosure of truths that the testifier genuinely knows but would prefer to conceal.
passage: It is a principle of both law and common sense that testimony extracted under duress is not to be trusted. When a person is threatened, beaten, or otherwise coerced into making a statement, the statement reflects not his honest judgment but his desire to escape the coercion, and its epistemic value is accordingly negligible. The analysis under consideration captures this thought by holding that coerced testimony is worthless whenever the testifier is under duress, the assertion results from that duress, and the testifier would not have spoken in its absence. These conditions, it might be thought, identify precisely the circumstances in which testimony is contaminated by coercion. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a prisoner of war who is subjected to prolonged and severe interrogation. Under this duress, he discloses that he observed enemy troop movements at a particular river crossing three days prior. The disclosure is unquestionably coerced: the prisoner is under extreme duress, his statement is a direct product of the interrogation, and he would not have volunteered the information had he not been compelled. The analysis therefore classifies his testimony as epistemically worthless. But suppose that the prisoner did in fact observe the troop movements exactly as he describes. His testimony is true, it reflects genuine perceptual knowledge, and its content is epistemically valuable — indeed, it may be of the utmost practical importance. The analysis errs in treating all coerced testimony as uniformly worthless, failing to distinguish between coercion that produces fabrication and coercion that compels the reluctant disclosure of genuine knowledge. What is needed is a more nuanced account that recognises the epistemic deficiency introduced by coercion without categorically dismissing every coerced utterance.

definition: Self-serving testimony is epistemically defective if and only if (i) the testifier T has a personal interest in the hearer's believing that p, (ii) T's assertion that p would, if believed, advance T's interests, and (iii) T's interest in the hearer's believing that p constitutes a motive to assert that p regardless of its truth.
conditions: The testifier has a personal interest in the hearer's believing that p
T's assertion would advance T's interests if believed
T's interest constitutes a motive to assert that p regardless of its truth
counterexample: A pharmaceutical executive testifies before a regulatory body that her company's drug is safe and effective. She has an obvious financial interest in the regulator's believing this, and her assertion, if believed, would advance her commercial interests. Yet the drug has in fact passed rigorous clinical trials, and her testimony accurately reflects the scientific evidence. The analysis classifies her testimony as epistemically defective solely because of her interest, yet the testimony is both true and well-supported.
missing_condition: The analysis treats the mere existence of self-interest as sufficient to render testimony defective, without considering whether the testimony is in fact supported by adequate independent evidence, so that truthful and well-grounded assertions are condemned merely because the speaker stands to benefit from their acceptance.
passage: It is a familiar observation that one should be cautious about testimony offered by a person who stands to gain from its acceptance. The vendor who praises his wares, the defendant who protests his innocence, the politician who extols her own record — all have motives that may compromise the reliability of their assertions. The analysis under consideration formalises this caution by identifying three conditions under which self-serving testimony is to be deemed epistemically defective: the testifier has a personal interest, the belief would advance that interest, and the interest provides a motive to assert regardless of truth. One might initially find this analysis compelling, for it identifies a genuine source of epistemic risk. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a pharmaceutical executive who appears before a drug-safety regulatory body to testify that her company's latest medication is both safe and efficacious. Her financial interest in the regulator's accepting this claim is transparent: approval of the drug will generate substantial revenue. Her assertion, if believed, would manifestly advance her commercial interests, and her interest clearly constitutes a motive to testify favourably irrespective of the truth. The analysis therefore pronounces her testimony epistemically defective. But suppose that the drug has in fact undergone extensive, rigorous, and independently audited clinical trials, all of which support the claims of safety and efficacy. The executive's testimony, though self-serving, is true and grounded in robust evidence. To dismiss it as epistemically defective solely on the ground that the speaker has an interest in its acceptance is to confuse a reason for caution with a reason for rejection. What the analysis lacks is any consideration of whether the self-serving testimony is in fact adequately supported by independent evidence.

definition: Testimony based on memory is reliable if and only if (i) the testifier T originally perceived or came to know that p, (ii) T has retained an accurate memory of p, and (iii) T's report faithfully represents the content of T's memory.
conditions: The testifier originally perceived or came to know that p
T has retained an accurate memory of p
T's report faithfully represents the content of T's memory
counterexample: A witness to a burglary, interviewed six months after the event, reports that the intruder was wearing a red jacket. She originally perceived the intruder clearly, her report faithfully represents her current memory, and she sincerely believes her memory to be accurate. Yet her memory has been unconsciously contaminated by a subsequent conversation in which a neighbour mentioned a red jacket, and the intruder was in fact wearing a dark green coat. The memory feels accurate to the testifier but has been subtly altered without her awareness.
missing_condition: The analysis requires that the testifier have retained an accurate memory but provides no means of distinguishing genuine retention from the incorporation of post-event information, so that a memory contaminated by subsequent suggestion satisfies the stated conditions whilst failing to be reliable.
passage: A great deal of testimony concerns not what the speaker presently perceives but what he remembers having perceived or learned at some earlier time. The reliability of such testimony depends, in an obvious way, upon the reliability of the speaker's memory, and the analysis under consideration reflects this by requiring that the testifier originally knew the fact, has retained an accurate memory of it, and reports that memory faithfully. In many cases, these conditions are satisfied and the testimony is reliable: one remembers what one had for breakfast, reports it accurately, and there is no difficulty. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a woman who witnessed a burglary at her neighbour's house. She saw the intruder clearly as he fled through the garden, and at the time she formed a definite impression of his appearance. Six months later, she is interviewed by the police and reports that the intruder was wearing a red jacket. She has a vivid and confident memory of this detail, and her report faithfully represents that memory. However, in the intervening months, she had a conversation with another neighbour who mentioned, in an unrelated context, a man in a red jacket seen in the area. This subsequent information has, without her awareness, become incorporated into her memory of the burglary. The intruder was in fact wearing a dark green coat. The witness originally perceived the intruder, she believes she has retained an accurate memory, and her report is an honest representation of what she remembers. All three conditions of the analysis are, from the testifier's subjective standpoint, satisfied. Yet the memory is not accurate — it has been contaminated by post-event suggestion — and the testimony is unreliable. The analysis fails because it provides no criterion for distinguishing genuine memory retention from the unconscious assimilation of later information.

definition: When testimony that p and testimony that not-p conflict, the hearer S is justified in suspending judgement if and only if (i) both testifiers are equally credible, (ii) the propositions p and not-p are genuinely contradictory, and (iii) S possesses no independent evidence favouring one testimony over the other.
conditions: Both testifiers are equally credible
The propositions are genuinely contradictory
S possesses no independent evidence favouring one testimony over the other
counterexample: Two equally credible historians disagree about whether a particular medieval battle took place in 1138 or 1139. A student, possessing no independent evidence, is told by the analysis to suspend judgement. Yet one historian bases his claim on a recently discovered primary source — a contemporary chronicle — whilst the other relies on a secondary source known to contain dating errors. Even though the student cannot independently evaluate the primary evidence, a reasonable person apprised of the difference in evidentiary basis ought to credit the first historian's testimony rather than suspending judgement.
missing_condition: The analysis treats equal credibility of testifiers as decisive, without considering that the evidential bases underlying conflicting testimonies may differ in quality, so that suspension of judgement is not always warranted even when the testifiers are equally credible in a general sense.
passage: Conflicting testimony presents a familiar epistemic difficulty. When two speakers, both apparently trustworthy, contradict one another, the hearer may find herself at a loss, unable to determine which, if either, to believe. The analysis under consideration addresses this situation by prescribing suspension of judgement whenever the testifiers are equally credible, the conflict is genuine, and the hearer has no independent means of resolution. This is, on the face of it, a reasonable counsel of prudence, and in many cases it captures the right epistemic response. If two strangers give one contradictory directions to the railway station and one has no other information to go on, suspending judgement — and perhaps asking a third person — is entirely sensible. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a student of medieval history consults two senior historians about the date of a particular battle. One asserts that it took place in 1138; the other, that it occurred in 1139. Both are respected scholars with long and distinguished careers, and the student regards them as equally credible. She has no independent knowledge of medieval chronology that would enable her to adjudicate the dispute. The analysis prescribes suspension of judgement. However, the student is informed that the first historian's claim rests upon a recently unearthed contemporary chronicle, whilst the second historian is relying upon a later secondary compilation known to contain chronological errors. The student cannot read the chronicle herself, but she understands that a contemporary primary source is, as a general matter, more reliable than a secondary source with known defects. A reasonable person in her position would, it appears, be justified in crediting the first historian rather than suspending judgement, even though the two historians are equally credible in terms of their general scholarly standing. The analysis fails because it attends only to the credibility of the testifiers and not to the quality of the evidence upon which their respective testimonies are founded.

definition: Testimony about a perceived event is reliable if and only if (i) the testifier T perceived the event in question, (ii) the conditions of perception were adequate (sufficient light, proximity, duration of observation, etc.), (iii) T has no known perceptual deficits relevant to the observation, and (iv) T's report accurately describes what T perceived.
conditions: The testifier perceived the event in question
The conditions of perception were adequate
T has no known perceptual deficits relevant to the observation
T's report accurately describes what T perceived
counterexample: A bystander witnesses a collision between two motor cars at an intersection in broad daylight, from a distance of twenty metres, with no visual impairments. She reports, accurately reflecting her perceptual experience, that the blue car ran a red light. Yet research in the psychology of perception has shown that the stress of witnessing a sudden collision can produce systematic distortions in the perception of rapidly occurring events, and in fact the traffic signal was amber, not red. All four conditions are satisfied, yet the testimony is unreliable.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for the possibility that even under apparently adequate perceptual conditions, psychological factors such as stress, expectation, or attentional narrowing may systematically distort perception, so that the adequacy of external conditions is insufficient to guarantee perceptual accuracy.
passage: When one relies upon the testimony of an eyewitness, one tacitly assumes that the witness saw what he says he saw, that the conditions were favourable for observation, and that his perceptual faculties were functioning properly. The analysis under consideration makes these assumptions explicit, requiring perception of the event, adequate conditions, absence of relevant perceptual deficits, and accurate reporting. These conditions might appear to exhaust the relevant considerations, for if the witness saw the event clearly, has no perceptual impairments, and reports honestly, what more could one ask? Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a woman who is standing on a pavement when two motor cars collide at a nearby intersection. The day is clear, she is twenty metres from the scene, her vision is excellent, and she watches the entire sequence of events. She subsequently tells the police that the blue car proceeded through a red traffic signal and struck the silver car. Her report faithfully represents her perceptual experience: she is quite certain that the light was red. But research in the psychology of perception and attention has established that the sudden onset of a traumatic or startling event can produce systematic distortions in the perception of details that occur in rapid succession. In this instance, the traffic signal was in fact amber — not red — at the moment the blue car entered the intersection, but the witness's perception, shaped by the stress and surprise of the collision, registered it as red. Every condition of the analysis is met: she perceived the event, the conditions were adequate, she has no perceptual deficits, and her report is an honest representation of her experience. Nonetheless, the testimony is unreliable, for her perception itself was distorted by psychological factors that the analysis does not address.

definition: Testimony can transmit moral knowledge that p if and only if (i) the testifier T asserts a moral proposition p, (ii) T has arrived at p through genuine moral reflection or experience, (iii) the hearer S comes to believe that p on the basis of T's assertion, and (iv) p is in fact a correct moral judgement.
conditions: The testifier asserts a moral proposition p
T has arrived at p through genuine moral reflection or experience
The hearer S comes to believe p on the basis of T's assertion
p is in fact a correct moral judgement
counterexample: A moral philosopher, after years of careful reflection, tells a student that promise-keeping is a prima facie duty even when the consequences of keeping a promise are mildly unfavourable. The student believes this on the philosopher's authority, and the judgement is, let us suppose, correct. Yet the student's belief, formed entirely on the basis of another's assertion rather than through any moral reflection of her own, arguably lacks the kind of understanding that moral knowledge requires — she can state the principle but cannot articulate the considerations that support it.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the hearer engage in any moral reflection of them own, so that a belief which is merely parroted from an authority may satisfy all stated conditions whilst failing to constitute the kind of understanding that is plausibly a component of genuine moral knowledge.
passage: The question whether moral knowledge can be transmitted by testimony is one of considerable difficulty, for it engages issues at the intersection of epistemology and moral philosophy. The analysis under examination proposes that such transmission is possible when a testifier asserts a correct moral proposition arrived at through genuine reflection, and the hearer believes it on that basis. One might think this a plausible account, for if a wise and experienced person tells one that a certain action is wrong, and one believes him, and he is right, it may seem natural to say that one has acquired moral knowledge. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a distinguished moral philosopher, after decades of reflection upon the nature of obligation, tells a first-year student that promise-keeping is a prima facie duty — that one ought, other things being equal, to keep one's promises even when doing so is mildly inconvenient or brings about slightly less good consequences than breaking the promise would. The student, deferring to the philosopher's authority, believes this. Let us grant that the moral judgement is correct. All four conditions of the analysis are satisfied. But one might observe that the student's 'moral knowledge' is peculiarly thin: she can assert the proposition, but she cannot explain why it is true, cannot identify the considerations that weigh in its favour, and cannot recognise the circumstances under which it might be overridden by competing duties. Her belief is, in a sense, opaque to her. There is a plausible view — defended by several philosophers — that moral knowledge requires not merely true belief but a kind of moral understanding, a grasp of the reasons and considerations that bear upon the judgement. If this is correct, then the analysis is insufficient, for it allows the transmission of moral belief without the understanding that is arguably constitutive of moral knowledge.

definition: Testimony from an unreliable source is epistemically worthless if and only if (i) the testifier T has a demonstrated pattern of making false assertions, (ii) T's assertion that p falls within a domain in which T has been unreliable, and (iii) S is aware of T's poor track record.
conditions: The testifier has a demonstrated pattern of making false assertions
T's assertion falls within a domain in which T has been unreliable
S is aware of T's poor track record
counterexample: A neighbour who is notoriously unreliable about matters of local gossip — having spread numerous false rumours over the years — tells one that the house at the end of the road is on fire. One is aware of her poor track record, and the assertion concerns a local event of the sort about which she has frequently been wrong. Yet one can see smoke rising from the direction of the house. The testimony, though from an unreliable source in a domain in which she has been unreliable, is in this case both true and immediately confirmable, and it would be irrational to dismiss it as worthless.
missing_condition: The analysis does not allow for the possibility that testimony from a generally unreliable source may be corroborated or confirmed by independent evidence available to the hearer, so that unreliability in the source does not automatically render the testimony epistemically worthless when the hearer has other grounds for belief.
passage: It is a commonplace that one should discount the testimony of a person who has proved himself unreliable. If a colleague has repeatedly made false claims about matters within his sphere — asserting, for example, that meetings have been scheduled when they have not, or that deadlines have been extended when they have not — one learns to treat his assertions with suspicion. The analysis under consideration formalises this prudent attitude by holding that testimony from a demonstrated unreliable source, in a domain in which he has been unreliable, is epistemically worthless, provided the hearer is aware of the track record. In many circumstances, this is sound advice: if the unreliable colleague tells one that the meeting has been moved to Tuesday, and one knows his history of false assertions about scheduling, one is right to withhold belief. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that a neighbour, well known for spreading false rumours about events in the neighbourhood, rushes up to one and declares that the house at the end of the road is on fire. One is fully aware of her lamentable record of inaccuracy concerning local affairs. The analysis classifies her testimony as worthless. But as she speaks, one notices a column of smoke rising above the rooftops in the direction she indicates, and one hears the distant wail of a fire engine. In these circumstances, it would be absurd to dismiss her testimony as epistemically worthless, for it is corroborated by one's own perceptual evidence. The analysis errs in treating the unreliability of the source as a sufficient condition for epistemic worthlessness, without attending to the possibility that independent evidence may rehabilitate even the testimony of a notoriously unreliable witness.

definition: S has second-hand knowledge that p via testimony if and only if (i) some testifier T knows that p through direct acquaintance or perception, (ii) T tells S that p, (iii) S believes that p on the basis of T's testimony, and (iv) S's belief that p is true.
conditions: Some testifier T knows that p through direct acquaintance or perception
T tells S that p
S believes that p on the basis of T's testimony
S's belief that p is true
counterexample: A mountaineer returns from an expedition and tells a friend that the summit of the mountain offers a view of a distant lake to the north. The friend believes this, and it is true. The mountaineer knew it through direct perception. Yet the mountaineer, unbeknownst to the friend, is describing the wrong mountain — he has confused the names of two peaks he climbed on successive days, and the view he describes belongs to the other summit. The friend's belief is true by coincidence: the mountain named in the conversation does happen to afford a view of a lake to the north, but the mountaineer's testimony is based on his perception of a different mountain. The friend has a true belief formed via testimony from a knower, yet something has gone wrong epistemically.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the testifier's direct knowledge pertain to the very state of affairs about which they testify, so that a testifier may know a relevantly similar but distinct fact and inadvertently testify to a different true proposition, yielding true belief in the hearer that is only accidentally connected to the testifier's knowledge.
passage: It is natural to suppose that one acquires second-hand knowledge when a person who knows a fact at first hand communicates it to one and one believes what one is told. The friend who has visited Paris and tells one that the Eiffel Tower is visible from the Champs-Elysees transmits, by this means, knowledge that one could otherwise have obtained only by travelling to Paris oneself. The analysis under consideration attempts to capture this process by requiring that the testifier know the proposition through direct acquaintance, that he communicate it, that the hearer believe it, and that the belief be true. These conditions appear to be jointly sufficient in the standard cases. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. A mountaineer, recently returned from a climbing expedition in the Scottish Highlands, tells a friend at dinner that the summit of Ben Vorlich offers a splendid view of a lake lying to the north. The friend, who has never visited the Highlands, believes this on the mountaineer's authority. As it happens, the mountaineer climbed two peaks on successive days — Ben Vorlich and Ben Vane — and has inadvertently confused the two in his recollection. The view he so vividly remembers belongs not to Ben Vorlich but to Ben Vane. By a fortunate coincidence, however, Ben Vorlich does in fact offer a view of a lake to the north, though a different lake from the one the mountaineer observed. The friend's belief is true, it is based on testimony from a person who has relevant perceptual knowledge, and the testifier did tell the friend the proposition in question. Yet the connection between the mountaineer's first-hand knowledge and the friend's true belief is entirely accidental. The analysis, by failing to require that the testifier's direct knowledge concern the very state of affairs about which he testifies, permits cases in which true belief is generated by a kind of epistemic luck rather than by genuine transmission of knowledge.

definition: S is epistemically dependent on T's testimony that p if and only if (i) S believes that p on the basis of T's testimony, (ii) S has no independent means of verifying or coming to know that p, and (iii) were T's testimony unavailable, S would not believe that p.
conditions: S believes that p on the basis of T's testimony
S has no independent means of verifying or coming to know that p
Were T's testimony unavailable, S would not believe that p
counterexample: A student believes, on the basis of her professor's testimony, that Gödel proved the incompleteness of arithmetic in 1931. She has no independent means of verifying this, and were the professor's testimony unavailable, she would not hold the belief. Yet the same proposition is stated in the introduction to her logic textbook, which she has read but from which she did not form the belief, having not attended to the passage. She satisfies all three conditions of epistemic dependence on the professor, yet her dependence is, in an important sense, contingent upon her own inattention rather than upon a genuine absence of alternative sources.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between the genuine unavailability of independent epistemic resources and the hearer's mere failure to avail themselves of resources that are in fact accessible, so that epistemic dependence may be an artefact of the hearer's epistemic negligence rather than a reflection of them actual epistemic situation.
passage: Epistemic dependence upon testimony is a pervasive feature of our cognitive lives. We depend upon doctors for medical knowledge, upon historians for knowledge of the past, and upon scientists for knowledge of the natural world. The analysis under consideration characterises this dependence by three conditions: the hearer believes on the basis of testimony, has no independent means of verification, and would not hold the belief without the testimony. These conditions appear to capture the phenomenon of dependence accurately enough in the paradigm cases: a patient who knows nothing of oncology and believes her doctor's diagnosis is epistemically dependent in precisely the specified sense. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a student of mathematical logic who believes, on the authority of her professor's lecture, that Kurt Gödel proved the incompleteness of arithmetic in 1931. She has never consulted a primary source on the matter, she possesses no independent means of verifying the historical claim through her own research, and had the professor not mentioned it, she would not have formed the belief. All three conditions of the analysis are met, and the student is classified as epistemically dependent on the professor's testimony. However, the very same proposition is stated, clearly and prominently, in the introduction to the logic textbook that sits on her desk and that she has in fact read, though she did not attend to the relevant passage or form a belief on its basis. In one sense, she has no independent means of verification — she did not independently verify — but in another sense, the means were readily available and she simply failed to use them. Her epistemic dependence on the professor is therefore a product of her own inattention rather than of a genuine absence of alternative epistemic resources. The analysis, by failing to distinguish between the unavailability of independent means and the hearer's neglect of available means, mischaracterises the nature of her dependence.

definition: Memory is a source of knowledge if and only if (i) the subject S presently believes that p, (ii) S's belief that p is derived from a past cognitive episode in which S came to apprehend that p, and (iii) the connection between that past episode and S's present belief is sustained by a reliable preservative process.
conditions: The subject S presently believes that p.
S's belief that p is derived from a past cognitive episode in which S came to apprehend that p.
The connection between the past episode and the present belief is sustained by a reliable preservative process.
counterexample: Consider a mathematician who, years ago, proved a theorem and thereby came to know it. She has since entirely forgotten the proof and retains only the bare conviction that the theorem is true, yet she could not now reconstruct any justificatory route to it. Her belief satisfies all three conditions, yet one might hesitate to say that memory here serves as a source of knowledge, since the epistemic ground has been wholly lost.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that S retain at least some epistemic basis or justificatory ground for the belief, not merely the belief itself.
passage: It is commonly supposed that memory furnishes us with a distinctive species of knowledge, one that differs from perception and inference in that it reaches back, as it were, to episodes which are no longer present to the mind. On the analysis set out above, three conditions are jointly sufficient for memory to serve as a source of knowledge: the subject must presently hold the belief, the belief must trace its origin to a genuine past apprehension, and the link between past and present must be sustained by a reliable process. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily constructed. One might imagine a mathematician who, having once demonstrated a theorem with full rigour, retains after many years only the bare conviction that the theorem holds, without any recollection of the proof or indeed of any reason whatever for accepting the result. The preservative process has, let us grant, been reliable in the sense that the content of the belief has not been distorted. Nevertheless, it appears questionable whether we should credit her with knowledge, for the justificatory basis has been entirely effaced. The belief persists, but the epistemic warrant does not. One is thus led to suspect that the analysis requires supplementation: it is not enough that a belief be reliably preserved; the subject must also retain some vestige of the ground upon which the belief was originally accepted, if memory is to count as a genuine source of knowledge rather than a mere repository of convictions.

definition: Memory is a preserver rather than a generator of knowledge if and only if (i) S knows that p at time t1 through some non-memorial source, (ii) S continues to believe that p at a later time t2 in virtue of memory, and (iii) S's knowledge that p at t2 does not depend upon any new evidence or justification acquired between t1 and t2.
conditions: S knows that p at time t1 through some non-memorial source.
S continues to believe that p at a later time t2 in virtue of memory.
S's knowledge that p at t2 does not depend upon any new evidence or justification acquired between t1 and t2.
counterexample: Suppose a man perceives that a window is open at t1 and thereby knows it. By t2, he has forgotten the perceptual experience but has since overheard a remark confirming that the window was left open. His continued belief satisfies conditions (i) and (ii), yet his present justification partly rests upon testimony received between t1 and t2, so that condition (iii) fails. Curiously, however, he would still be said to remember that the window was open.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to account for cases in which memory and newly acquired evidence jointly sustain a belief, so that memory preserves but also supplements the original epistemic basis.
passage: There is a venerable distinction in the theory of knowledge between those faculties which generate knowledge and those which merely preserve it. Perception and reason are standardly taken to be generative; memory, by contrast, is held to be merely preservative, transmitting to the present what was once acquired by other means. The tripartite analysis given above attempts to capture this preservative character. Yet one might observe that the distinction is not so tidy as it first appears. Consider a person who, on a particular morning, sees that the window of his study is open. He thereby acquires perceptual knowledge of this fact. Some hours later, he can no longer summon any image of the window, but he recalls, in the dispositional sense, that it was open. In the interval, however, a colleague has mentioned in passing that a draught was felt in the study, and this remark has reinforced the man's belief. At t2, his continued acceptance of the proposition rests partly upon the original perception and partly upon the intervening testimony. The case thus reveals the insufficiency of an analysis which insists that memorial knowledge must be wholly independent of new evidence, for here memory and testimony are intertwined in a manner that resists neat separation. One is compelled to acknowledge that the preservative function of memory may be entangled with supplementary epistemic inputs, and that any adequate account must reckon with such hybrid cases.

definition: Justification is retained over time through memory if and only if (i) S was justified in believing that p at t1, (ii) S continues to believe that p at t2 in virtue of memory, (iii) no defeaters for p have become available to S between t1 and t2, and (iv) the memory process has not distorted the content of S's belief.
conditions: S was justified in believing that p at t1.
S continues to believe that p at t2 in virtue of memory.
No defeaters for p have become available to S between t1 and t2.
The memory process has not distorted the content of S's belief.
counterexample: A geographer was once justified in believing that a certain river was the longest in its continent, having consulted authoritative sources. Years later, she retains this belief through memory, and no defeaters have reached her. Yet unbeknownst to her, a more precise survey has since revealed that another river is longer. All four conditions are met, but her justification, though undefeated from her perspective, rests upon evidence that has been superseded.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the original evidence upon which the justification rests must not have been objectively undermined in the interim, even if the subject is unaware of this undermining.
passage: The question of whether justification can survive the passage of time is one that bears directly upon the epistemic status of memory beliefs. If a belief was justified at the moment of its formation, and if memory faithfully preserves it, one might suppose that the justification persists so long as no countervailing considerations intrude. The analysis above codifies this supposition by requiring the absence of defeaters and the integrity of the preserved content. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be described as follows. Let us suppose a geographer who, some decades past, consulted the most authoritative cartographic sources then available and thereby formed the justified belief that a certain river was the longest on its continent. She retains this belief through the intervening years, and no one has informed her of any revision. The memory process has preserved the content of her belief without distortion. All four conditions are thus satisfied. Nevertheless, a more exacting geodetic survey, conducted after her original inquiry, has established that another river exceeds the first in length. Her belief, though subjectively undefeated, is grounded in evidence that the world has, so to speak, overtaken. It appears, then, that the retention of justification cannot be adequately analysed without attending to the objective standing of the original evidence, for justification that was once adequate may be rendered inadequate by developments of which the subject is wholly ignorant.

definition: A memory is reproductive rather than constructive if and only if (i) the content of the memory corresponds to a past experience or cognition of the subject, (ii) the subject's present recollection preserves the structure and detail of that past experience without significant addition or rearrangement, and (iii) the subject does not introduce material drawn from imagination, inference, or external suggestion into the recollected content.
conditions: The content of the memory corresponds to a past experience or cognition of the subject.
The subject's present recollection preserves the structure and detail of the past experience without significant addition or rearrangement.
The subject does not introduce material drawn from imagination, inference, or external suggestion into the recollected content.
counterexample: A woman recalls a holiday she took as a child, and her recollection includes vivid details of the hotel room, the view from the balcony, and the colour of the curtains. She has not consulted photographs or spoken with anyone about the trip. Yet her recollection has, over the years, silently incorporated details from a quite different holiday taken at a later date, blending the two experiences into a single seamless narrative. She is wholly unaware of the admixture.
