missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the subject be able to distinguish genuine preservation from unconscious amalgamation of distinct past experiences, which can occur without any contribution from imagination, inference, or external suggestion.
passage: Philosophers have long distinguished between reproductive memory, which faithfully echoes a past experience, and constructive memory, which reshapes or supplements it. The analysis above endeavours to mark this boundary by requiring fidelity to the original experience and the exclusion of extraneous material. One might, however, observe that there exists a species of memorial distortion which eludes these conditions. Consider a woman who recalls, with considerable vividness, a holiday taken in her childhood. She remembers the hotel room, the prospect from the balcony, the colour of the curtains. She has consulted no photographs and received no prompting from others, so that condition (iii) is ostensibly met. The content does correspond to a genuine past experience, satisfying condition (i). And the recollection presents itself as structurally coherent, apparently satisfying condition (ii). Yet it emerges upon investigation that her memory has silently absorbed details from an entirely different holiday, taken some years later in a different country. The curtains belong to one trip, the balcony to another. No imagination or inference has been exercised; rather, two genuine memories have been unconsciously fused. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for the constructive element here does not consist in the introduction of extraneous material but in the amalgamation of distinct veridical experiences into a composite which corresponds to no single past episode. An adequate account of reproductive memory must therefore reckon with the possibility that construction may proceed by recombination of authentic elements rather than by invention.

definition: A memory is a false memory or confabulation if and only if (i) the subject sincerely reports or inwardly entertains a recollection of an event or experience, (ii) the reported event or experience did not occur as the subject represents it, and (iii) the subject is not aware that the recollection is inaccurate and genuinely takes it to be veridical.
conditions: The subject sincerely reports or inwardly entertains a recollection of an event or experience.
The reported event or experience did not occur as the subject represents it.
The subject is not aware that the recollection is inaccurate and genuinely takes it to be veridical.
counterexample: A man recalls, with full sincerity, an incident in which he was stung by a wasp during a picnic. The incident did in fact occur precisely as he describes it, and he genuinely takes his recollection to be veridical. Yet the causal basis of his present recollection is not the original experience but rather a story told to him by a relative years later; he has no genuine memorial trace of the event itself. His memory satisfies none of the failure conditions and so is not classified as false, yet the epistemic status of his recollection is deeply suspect.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the recollection must be causally connected, in the appropriate way, to the original experience, so that a belief which happens to be accurate but is causally grounded in testimony rather than in genuine recall may escape classification.
passage: The phenomenon of false memory has attracted considerable philosophical attention, not least because it threatens the common assumption that memory is, in the ordinary case, a reliable guide to the past. The analysis above attempts to characterise false memories by three conditions: the subject sincerely entertains a recollection, the recollection does not correspond to what actually occurred, and the subject is unaware of the discrepancy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis presents itself when we consider a person who recalls being stung by a wasp during a childhood picnic. The incident did occur, and his account of it is accurate in every particular. He genuinely takes his recollection to be veridical, and in a sense it is. Nevertheless, the causal provenance of his present belief is not a memorial trace laid down at the time of the experience but rather a narrative communicated to him by a relative many years afterwards. He has, as it were, come to remember the event through testimony rather than through his own past experience, though he cannot now distinguish the two sources. Since condition (ii) is not satisfied — the event did occur as he represents it — his memory is not classified as false on this analysis. Yet one might reasonably doubt whether it constitutes genuine memory at all, for the connection to the original experience is mediated not by a preservative memorial process but by the testimony of another. The analysis thus fails to capture an important class of epistemically deficient memories which are accurate in content but deviant in their causal ancestry.

definition: Memory is constitutive of personal identity if and only if (i) a person P at time t2 is identical with a person P at time t1, (ii) P at t2 possesses memories of experiences had by P at t1, and (iii) these memories provide the psychological continuity that grounds the identity relation between P at t1 and P at t2.
conditions: A person P at time t2 is identical with a person P at time t1.
P at t2 possesses memories of experiences had by P at t1.
These memories provide the psychological continuity that grounds the identity relation between P at t1 and P at t2.
counterexample: An elderly man has lost, through the ordinary ravages of age, all memory of his experiences before the age of twenty. He cannot recall his schooling, his early friendships, or any episode from his youth. Yet no one — neither he nor those who know him — doubts that he is the same person who underwent those experiences. The identity relation holds, but condition (ii) fails, since he possesses no memories of the relevant earlier period.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to accommodate the possibility that personal identity may persist through overlapping chains of memory, where intermediate links connect early and late stages even when direct memory of the earliest stages has been lost.
passage: The Lockean tradition in philosophy has long maintained that memory is the thread which binds together the successive stages of a single person's existence. On this view, what makes a person at one time identical with a person at an earlier time is precisely the presence of memories connecting the two temporal stages. The analysis above formalises this thought by requiring that the later person possess memories of the earlier person's experiences and that these memories furnish the requisite psychological continuity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is easily conceived. One might consider an elderly man of eighty years who has entirely lost all recollection of his life before the age of twenty. He cannot summon any image or narrative from his childhood or youth; those years are, to his present consciousness, a perfect blank. Nevertheless, there is no temptation whatever to deny that he is the same person who, sixty years ago, attended a particular school and formed particular friendships. His identity is not in question, notwithstanding the complete absence of direct memorial connections to the earliest period of his life. One is thus led to suspect that the memory criterion, as stated, is too stringent: it demands direct memory where indirect or overlapping connections might suffice. The analysis must be revised to allow that identity may be sustained by a chain of memories, each link of which connects adjacent periods, even when no single memory spans the entire temporal interval. Without such a revision, the account yields the paradoxical result that persons who have forgotten their youth are not identical with their younger selves.

definition: A memory is episodic rather than semantic if and only if (i) the subject recalls a specific event or experience located at a particular time and place, (ii) the recollection involves a phenomenological re-experiencing or reliving of the event, and (iii) the subject is aware that the recalled event is part of their or their own past.
conditions: The subject recalls a specific event or experience located at a particular time and place.
The recollection involves a phenomenological re-experiencing or reliving of the event.
The subject is aware that the recalled event is part of their or their own past.
counterexample: A historian has read so extensively about a particular battle that she can vividly picture the scene: the terrain, the weather, the disposition of forces, the cries of the wounded. Her mental imagery is rich and detailed, located at a specific time and place, and she is aware that it pertains to a real historical event. Yet she was not present at the battle, and the recalled event is not part of her own past. Conditions (i) and (ii) appear to be met, but (iii) is not, and one would not call this episodic memory.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the subject's recollection be causally grounded in their own perceptual experience of the event, thereby failing to distinguish genuine episodic memory from vivid imaginative reconstruction based on testimony or reading.
passage: The distinction between episodic and semantic memory has become a commonplace of both philosophical and psychological discussion. Episodic memory, we are told, involves the recollection of particular events from one's own past, accompanied by a distinctive phenomenological quality of reliving or re-experiencing. Semantic memory, by contrast, concerns the retention of general knowledge — facts, concepts, and propositions — without any such experiential colouring. The analysis above seeks to capture the episodic category by imposing three conditions: specificity of time and place, phenomenological re-experiencing, and awareness that the event belongs to one's own history. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. One might imagine a historian who has immersed herself so thoroughly in the primary sources concerning a particular battle that she can vividly conjure the scene in her mind's eye. She pictures the rolling terrain, the overcast sky, the movements of the cavalry, and she locates these images at a definite time and place. Her mental imagery possesses a richness and immediacy that closely resembles the phenomenological character of genuine re-experiencing. She is, moreover, fully aware that the event in question actually occurred. What she lacks, of course, is any personal involvement in the event; she was not there, and the recalled scene is not part of her own past. Nevertheless, conditions (i) and (ii) appear to be satisfied by her vivid imaginative reconstruction, and only condition (iii) excludes her case. This suggests that the analysis relies too heavily upon the subject's self-ascription and insufficiently upon the causal provenance of the recollection.

definition: Memory is reliable as a source of belief if and only if (i) the memory process tends to produce true beliefs more often than false ones, (ii) the process operates in a manner that is sensitive to the truth of the remembered proposition, and (iii) the subject has no reason to doubt the deliverances of memory in the relevant domain.
conditions: The memory process tends to produce true beliefs more often than false ones.
The process operates in a manner that is sensitive to the truth of the remembered proposition.
The subject has no reason to doubt the deliverances of memory in the relevant domain.
counterexample: A man reliably remembers factual matters — dates, names, figures — with great accuracy. His memory process produces true beliefs far more often than false ones, and he has never had occasion to doubt it. Yet in one narrow domain, the recollection of conversations, his memory systematically attributes to others remarks that he himself made. He has no awareness of this tendency and no reason to doubt his conversational memories. All three conditions are satisfied in this domain, yet his memory is not reliable there.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the reliability of the memory process be assessed with respect to the specific type of content being recalled, rather than globally across all domains.
passage: It is a familiar thought that we trust our memories in the ordinary course of life, and that this trust is, for the most part, well placed. The analysis above attempts to capture the conditions under which memory may be deemed reliable, requiring that it tend towards truth, that it be sensitive to the facts, and that the subject lack grounds for suspicion. Yet one might observe that reliability is not a monolithic property. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves a man whose memory for factual matters — historical dates, numerical figures, the spellings of words — is exceptionally accurate. In these domains, his memory process produces true beliefs with impressive regularity, and he has never had cause to question it. Yet when it comes to the recollection of conversations, he exhibits a systematic and unconscious tendency to attribute to his interlocutors remarks that were in fact his own. He remembers himself as having listened when he was in truth speaking, and vice versa. He has no awareness of this peculiarity and therefore no reason to doubt his conversational memories. Since the analysis assesses reliability globally — the process tends, on the whole, to produce true beliefs — it classifies his memory as reliable without qualification. Yet in the specific domain of conversational recall, his memory is systematically misleading. The analysis thus proves insufficient because it fails to relativise reliability to the type of content in question, treating memory as uniformly dependable when it may be dependable in some respects and treacherous in others.

definition: A mental state is a genuine memory rather than an act of imagination if and only if (i) the content of the mental state represents a past event or experience, (ii) the subject believes that the represented event actually occurred, and (iii) the mental state possesses a distinctive phenomenological character — a feeling of pastness or familiarity — that marks it as memorial rather than imaginative.
conditions: The content of the mental state represents a past event or experience.
The subject believes that the represented event actually occurred.
The mental state possesses a distinctive phenomenological character — a feeling of pastness or familiarity — that marks it as memorial rather than imaginative.
counterexample: A novelist, in the course of composing a scene, draws upon an experience from her own life. As she dwells upon the scene, she finds that it acquires a vivid feeling of pastness and familiarity; she believes, correctly, that the event occurred. Yet the details she now entertains have been so thoroughly reshaped by her creative activity — characters altered, the setting transposed, the emotional tenor adjusted — that the content no longer faithfully represents the original event. All three conditions are arguably satisfied, yet what she is doing is better described as imaginative elaboration than as remembering.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the content of the mental state be sufficiently faithful to the original event, so that heavily fictionalised elaborations of genuine experiences may satisfy all three conditions whilst constituting acts of imagination rather than memory.
passage: The problem of distinguishing memory from imagination has exercised philosophers at least since Hume, who famously proposed that the two faculties differ in the vivacity of their respective products. The analysis above offers a more refined account, appealing not merely to vivacity but to a distinctive phenomenological character — a feeling of pastness — together with the representational content and the subject's doxastic attitude. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not difficult to construct. One might consider a novelist who, in composing a scene for a work of fiction, draws upon an episode from her own past. She recalls the original event, and as she turns it over in her mind, the feeling of pastness is undeniably present. She believes, and believes truly, that something of the sort occurred. Yet in the course of her creative labour, she has transposed the setting, altered the participants, and adjusted the emotional colouring so extensively that the content of her present mental state corresponds only loosely to what actually took place. The three conditions of the analysis are, at least arguably, met: the content represents a past event (albeit loosely), the subject believes the event occurred, and the phenomenological character of pastness is present. Nevertheless, it appears that what the novelist is engaged in is more properly described as imaginative elaboration than as genuine recollection. The analysis, then, is insufficient in so far as it neglects the degree of fidelity required between the content of the mental state and the original experience.

definition: A memory belief involves inference rather than direct recall if and only if (i) the subject arrives at a belief about the past, (ii) the belief is not the product of a direct memorial impression but is derived from other remembered facts by a process of reasoning, and (iii) the subject is aware that reasoning, rather than direct recollection, is the basis of the belief.
conditions: The subject arrives at a belief about the past.
The belief is not the product of a direct memorial impression but is derived from other remembered facts by a process of reasoning.
The subject is aware that reasoning, rather than direct recollection, is the basis of the belief.
counterexample: A man believes that he attended a particular lecture on a Wednesday. He has no direct recollection of the day of the week, but he remembers that he had lunch with a colleague before the lecture, and he always lunches with that colleague on Wednesdays. He has drawn an inference, yet he is not in the least aware that he has done so; the conclusion presents itself to him with the immediacy of direct recall. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but (iii) is not.
missing_condition: The analysis requires that the subject be aware of the inferential basis of the belief, but many inferences from remembered facts occur unconsciously and present themselves phenomenologically as direct recall.
passage: It is a question of some philosophical interest whether memory beliefs are always the product of direct recall or whether they sometimes rest, wholly or in part, upon inference from other remembered facts. The analysis above attempts to demarcate the inferential case by requiring that the subject arrive at a belief about the past through reasoning from remembered premises and that the subject be aware that reasoning, rather than direct recollection, underlies the belief. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily available. One might imagine a person who believes, with apparent directness, that he attended a certain lecture on a Wednesday. He has no phenomenological impression of the day of the week; the belief simply presents itself to him as something he knows. Upon reflection, however, it emerges that his belief is grounded in an unconscious inference: he remembers lunching with a particular colleague before the lecture, and he invariably lunches with that colleague on Wednesdays. The reasoning has occurred beneath the threshold of awareness, and the conclusion has been delivered to consciousness with the felt immediacy of direct recall. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied — the belief is about the past and is derived from other remembered facts by reasoning — but condition (iii) fails, for the subject is quite unaware that any inference has taken place. The analysis thus proves insufficient because it ties the distinction between recall and inference to the subject's awareness of the process, whereas in fact many memorial beliefs are inferentially grounded without the subject's having any inkling of this.

definition: Justification for a memory belief decays over time if and only if (i) the subject was fully justified in believing that p at time t1, (ii) the subject continues to hold the belief that p at a later time t2 solely in virtue of memory, and (iii) the degree of justification available to S for p at t2 is less than the degree available at t1 owing to the mere passage of time.
conditions: The subject was fully justified in believing that p at time t1.
The subject continues to hold the belief that p at t2 solely in virtue of memory.
The degree of justification available to S for p at t2 is less than the degree available at t1 owing to the mere passage of time.
counterexample: A schoolmistress learns a historical date from a reputable textbook and is fully justified in believing it. Thirty years later, she still believes the date, and her belief is sustained solely by memory. Yet her justification has not diminished with the passage of time; if anything, the repeated use of this fact in her teaching has reinforced her confidence, and the fact has been corroborated by subsequent sources she has encountered. Time has passed, but justification has not decayed.
missing_condition: The analysis assumes that the mere passage of time invariably diminishes justification, but it does not account for cases in which repeated rehearsal or incidental corroboration maintains or even strengthens the justificatory standing of a memory belief.
passage: There is a widely held intuition that the justification attaching to a memory belief tends to weaken as time elapses. The thought is that, even if no positive defeater intervenes, the sheer passage of time introduces a measure of epistemic risk: memories fade, distort, and become confused with other memories, so that a belief held after many years is less well supported than the same belief held shortly after its formation. The analysis above attempts to capture this notion of temporal decay by stipulating that the degree of justification diminishes solely in virtue of the passage of time. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves a schoolmistress who, at the outset of her career, learns from a reputable textbook that a certain battle was fought in a particular year. She is fully justified in believing this date. Over the ensuing thirty years, she teaches this fact to successive classes of pupils, and in the course of her professional life she encounters the same date in several independent and authoritative sources. She continues to hold the belief at t2 solely in virtue of memory, in the sense that she does not at t2 consult any source but merely recalls the date. Yet the passage of time has not diminished her justification; the repeated rehearsal and incidental corroboration have, if anything, fortified it. The analysis thus proves insufficient because it assumes that temporal passage invariably erodes justification, when in truth the epistemic standing of a memory belief may be maintained or even enhanced by factors that accompany the passage of time without constituting fresh evidence in the strict sense.

definition: S genuinely remembers that p, as opposed to merely seeming to remember that p, if and only if (i) p is true, (ii) S believes that p on the basis of what appears to be a recollection, (iii) S did in fact experience or come to know that p in the past, and (iv) there is an appropriate causal connection between S's past experience and S's present recollection.
conditions: p is true.
S believes that p on the basis of what appears to be a recollection.
S did in fact experience or come to know that p in the past.
There is an appropriate causal connection between S's past experience and S's present recollection.
counterexample: A woman seems to remember leaving her umbrella at a restaurant last Tuesday. The proposition is true — she did leave it there. She believes it on the basis of what feels like a recollection. She did experience leaving the umbrella. Yet the causal basis of her present recollection is not the original experience but a note she wrote to herself and later read, the reading of which produced a quasi-memorial impression. The original memory trace was lost; only the testimony of her own note sustains the belief. All four conditions are arguably satisfied, yet her present state is better described as belief based on self-testimony than as genuine remembering.
missing_condition: The analysis specifies an 'appropriate causal connection' without adequately clarifying what counts as appropriate, thereby failing to exclude cases in which the causal route passes through an intermediary such as a written record rather than through the memorial trace itself.
passage: The distinction between genuinely remembering and merely seeming to remember is of the first importance for epistemology, since it bears upon the question of when memory confers knowledge. The analysis above proposes four conditions: truth, belief grounded in apparent recollection, past experience of the relevant fact, and an appropriate causal connection between past experience and present recollection. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves a woman who seems to remember leaving her umbrella at a restaurant on a particular occasion. The proposition is true, and she believes it with the felt conviction characteristic of genuine recall. She did in fact leave the umbrella at the restaurant and was fully aware of doing so at the time. Moreover, there exists a causal chain linking her past experience to her present belief: on the evening in question, she wrote herself a note — 'Left umbrella at restaurant' — and some days later came upon the note, at which point a vivid quasi-memorial impression formed in her mind. The original memory trace, however, has been entirely lost. The causal connection between past and present runs not through the preservative faculty of memory but through the intermediary of a written record and the subsequent perceptual experience of reading it. One might argue that condition (iv) is satisfied, since there is indeed a causal connection of some sort; but it is far from clear that it is an appropriate one. The analysis thus proves insufficient in so far as it leaves the notion of causal appropriateness unspecified, permitting cases in which the causal route is mediated by external artefacts rather than by the internal memorial process.

definition: Memory yields new knowledge if and only if (i) S recalls a set of previously known propositions, (ii) S notices, at the time of recall, a logical or evidential relation among those propositions that S had not previously recognised, and (iii) this newly recognised relation constitutes a fresh item of knowledge not possessed by S at any earlier time.
conditions: S recalls a set of previously known propositions.
S notices, at the time of recall, a logical or evidential relation among those propositions that S had not previously recognised.
This newly recognised relation constitutes a fresh item of knowledge not possessed by S at any earlier time.
counterexample: A detective recalls two witness statements that she had taken on separate days. Upon recollecting them together, she notices that they are mutually inconsistent — a relation she had not previously recognised. Yet the inconsistency is merely apparent, arising from the fact that her memory has subtly altered one of the statements. The 'new knowledge' that the statements conflict is, in truth, an artefact of memorial distortion. All three conditions are satisfied, but no genuine knowledge has been produced.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the recalled propositions be accurately preserved, so that relations noticed among distorted recollections may count as new knowledge even though they obtain only between the distorted, not the original, propositions.
passage: Whether memory can yield genuinely new knowledge, as opposed to merely preserving what was antecedently known, is a question that divides epistemologists. The analysis above attempts to describe a mechanism by which memory might be generative: the subject recalls several previously known propositions and, in the act of recollection, discerns a relation among them that had hitherto escaped notice. The newly discerned relation constitutes a fresh item of knowledge. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. One might imagine a detective who, on two separate occasions, interviews witnesses to a crime and carefully records their testimony. Some time later, while turning the case over in her mind, she recalls the two statements together and is struck by the observation that they are mutually inconsistent — a point she had not previously noticed. She takes herself to have gained new knowledge, namely that the witnesses contradict one another. Yet what has occurred is that her memory has subtly altered one of the statements — a word changed, a detail transposed — so that the apparent inconsistency is an artefact of memorial distortion. The original statements were in fact perfectly compatible. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied: she has recalled previously known propositions, noticed a relation among them, and the relation is one she had not previously recognised. Yet the putative knowledge is spurious, resting upon a foundation of inaccurate recall. The analysis is thus insufficient in so far as it fails to require that the recalled propositions faithfully represent the originals, leaving open the possibility that memorial distortion may masquerade as epistemic discovery.

definition: A memory is a case of habit memory rather than recollective memory if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to perform an action or exhibit a skill that was previously learnt, (ii) the performance does not involve any conscious recollection of the learning episode or of past instances of performance, and (iii) the disposition was acquired through repeated practice or experience rather than through a single episode of learning.
conditions: The subject is disposed to perform an action or exhibit a skill that was previously learnt.
The performance does not involve any conscious recollection of the learning episode or of past instances of performance.
The disposition was acquired through repeated practice or experience rather than through a single episode of learning.
counterexample: A person who grew up speaking French but has lived for decades in an English-speaking country finds that, upon encountering a French speaker, he can still produce fluent French sentences. His ability satisfies conditions (i) and (ii): he is disposed to perform the relevant actions and does so without conscious recollection of any learning episode. Yet his command of French was not acquired through repeated practice in any ordinary sense; rather, it was absorbed through immersion in early childhood without deliberate rehearsal. Condition (iii) is ambiguous: immersion involves repetition in one sense but not the structured practice that the condition seems to envisage.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between deliberate repeated practice and the passive, unreflective absorption characteristic of early linguistic and cultural learning, which shares features of both habit and recollective memory without fitting neatly into either category.
passage: The philosophical tradition has long distinguished between habit memory — the kind involved in knowing how to ride a bicycle or play a musical instrument — and recollective memory — the kind involved in recalling a particular past event. The analysis above characterises habit memory by three conditions: the subject is disposed to perform an action, the performance does not involve conscious recollection, and the disposition was acquired through repeated practice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the phenomenon of early linguistic acquisition. One might imagine a person who grew up in a French-speaking household but has lived for the greater part of his adult life in an English-speaking environment. After many years of disuse, he encounters a French speaker and discovers, somewhat to his surprise, that he is still able to converse fluently in French. His ability satisfies the first two conditions without difficulty: he is disposed to produce French sentences, and he does so without any conscious recollection of having learnt them. The third condition, however, presents a difficulty. His command of French was not acquired through deliberate, repeated practice of the sort we associate with learning a musical instrument or a physical skill. Rather, it was absorbed through immersion in a linguistic environment during early childhood — a process which involves repetition of a sort, but not the structured, intentional practice that the analysis seems to envisage. The case thus sits uneasily between habit and recollective memory, revealing that the analysis draws the boundary too rigidly and fails to accommodate the distinctive character of knowledge acquired through early environmental absorption.

definition: S's memory that p satisfies the causal condition if and only if (i) S experienced or came to know that p at some past time, (ii) S presently recalls that p, and (iii) there exists a continuous causal chain, constituted by memorial processes, linking S's original experience or knowledge of p to S's present recall.
conditions: S experienced or came to know that p at some past time.
S presently recalls that p.
There exists a continuous causal chain, constituted by memorial processes, linking S's original experience or knowledge of p to S's present recall.
counterexample: A woman witnessed a rare astronomical event as a child and formed a memory of it. Over the years, the original memory trace decayed entirely. Decades later, she sees a photograph of the same event, and the photograph triggers what feels like a fresh recollection — she now recalls the event with vivid detail. There is a causal chain from the original event to her present recall, but it passes through the photograph rather than through a continuous memorial process. All three conditions are arguably met, yet the causal chain is not of the right kind.
missing_condition: The analysis does not specify that the causal chain must be constituted exclusively by internal memorial processes, thereby failing to exclude cases in which the chain is sustained by external prompts or artefacts that re-create, rather than preserve, the memory.
passage: The causal condition on memory has been proposed as a means of distinguishing genuine remembering from confabulation and mere true belief about the past. The thought is that a present recollection counts as a genuine memory only if it is causally connected, in the right way, to the original experience. The analysis above formalises this by requiring a continuous causal chain of memorial processes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves a woman who, as a child, observed a rare astronomical event — a transit of Venus, let us say — and formed a vivid impression of it. Over the ensuing decades, the original memorial trace gradually decayed until no vestige of it remained. She could not, by any effort of introspection, summon any recollection of the event. Then, many years later, she comes upon a photograph of the transit taken from a vantage point very near her own. The photograph triggers what feels, to her, like a genuine recollection: she seems to remember the event, with details that go beyond what the photograph depicts. A causal chain does exist from the original event to her present state, but it is not constituted by memorial processes alone; it passes through an external artefact — the photograph — which has, in effect, re-created a memorial impression rather than preserving one. The analysis, by requiring only that a continuous causal chain exist without specifying that it must be wholly internal and memorial in character, fails to exclude this case. One is thus led to conclude that the causal condition must be more precisely formulated if it is to do the philosophical work required of it.

definition: Memory knowledge differs from perceptual knowledge if and only if (i) memory knowledge concerns a state of affairs that is no longer present to the senses, (ii) the justification for a memory belief is derived from a past perceptual or cognitive episode rather than from a current one, and (iii) memory knowledge is epistemically dependent upon the prior acquisition of the relevant information through some non-memorial faculty.
conditions: Memory knowledge concerns a state of affairs that is no longer present to the senses.
The justification for a memory belief is derived from a past perceptual or cognitive episode rather than from a current one.
Memory knowledge is epistemically dependent upon the prior acquisition of the relevant information through some non-memorial faculty.
counterexample: A man looks at a clock and perceives that it reads half past three. He then glances away for a moment and immediately recalls that the clock read half past three. The state of affairs — the clock showing that time — is still present to his senses (he could simply look again), so condition (i) is not clearly satisfied. The temporal gap is so vanishingly small that the distinction between perception and memory becomes unclear. Yet he is relying on memory, not current perception.
missing_condition: The analysis draws a sharp temporal boundary between perception and memory but does not address the continuous transition from one to the other, failing to account for the immediate or 'short-term' retention that blurs the line between perceiving and remembering.
passage: Philosophers have commonly supposed that memory knowledge and perceptual knowledge are distinct species, differing in their temporal orientation and their justificatory structure. Perceptual knowledge is directed at what is present; memory knowledge is directed at what is past. The analysis above formalises this by requiring that memory knowledge concern a state of affairs no longer present to the senses and that its justification derive from a past episode. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from reflection upon what might be called the immediate penumbra of perception. One might consider a person who looks at a clock on the wall and perceives that it reads half past three. He turns his head for the briefest instant — a fraction of a second — and then, without looking back, finds himself in possession of the belief that the clock reads half past three. Is this belief perceptual or memorial? The state of affairs to which it pertains has not ceased to exist; the clock is still there, still showing the same time, and he could verify the matter by a simple glance. Yet he is not now perceiving the clock; his belief is sustained by what he saw a moment ago, and in that sense it is a memory belief. The analysis, however, insists that memory knowledge concerns a state of affairs no longer present to the senses, and this condition is not clearly met. The difficulty reveals that the transition from perception to memory is not a sharp boundary but a continuous gradient, and that any analysis which presupposes a clean division will encounter cases in this penumbral region that resist classification.

definition: A childhood memory is reliable if and only if (i) the remembered event actually occurred, (ii) the child perceived the event at the time of its occurrence and formed a memorial representation of it, and (iii) the memorial representation has been preserved without substantial distortion through the intervening years.
conditions: The remembered event actually occurred.
The child perceived the event at the time of its occurrence and formed a memorial representation of it.
The memorial representation has been preserved without substantial distortion through the intervening years.
counterexample: A man vividly recalls an incident from the age of four in which the family dog knocked him into a pond. The event did occur; he did perceive it; and the core content of his recollection — being knocked into water by the dog — has been preserved accurately. Yet the details he recalls — the colour of his clothing, the expression on his mother's face, the temperature of the water — have been supplied over the years by family photographs and oft-repeated anecdotes. The representation is accurate in outline but has been substantially furnished by external sources rather than by the original perception.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the details of the memorial representation originate from the child's own perceptual experience rather than from subsequent external supplementation through photographs, stories, or family lore.
passage: The reliability of childhood memories has long been a matter of philosophical and psychological concern. It is well known that memories from early life are peculiarly susceptible to distortion, embellishment, and wholesale fabrication. The analysis above attempts to specify the conditions under which such memories may be deemed reliable, requiring that the event occurred, that the child perceived it, and that the representation has been preserved without substantial distortion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is easily described. One might imagine a person who recalls, with considerable vividness, an episode from the age of four in which the family dog knocked him into a garden pond. The event did in fact occur, and the child did perceive it at the time. The core of the memory — being pushed into water by the dog — has been preserved with tolerable accuracy. Nevertheless, the circumstantial details that give the memory its richness and vividness — the colour of his clothing, the expression of alarm on his mother's face, the coldness of the water — have been gradually supplied by family photographs and by the oft-repeated telling of the story at family gatherings. The man cannot distinguish those elements of his recollection that derive from his own infantile perception from those that have been absorbed from external sources. The memorial representation has not been distorted, in the sense that it remains substantially accurate, yet it has been heavily supplemented by material that did not originate in the child's own experience. The analysis, by requiring only that the representation be undistorted, fails to insist that its content derive from the original perception rather than from subsequent external inputs.

definition: A collective memory exists if and only if (i) a group of individuals share a common representation of a past event, (ii) this representation is sustained through social practices such as narration, commemoration, or institutional record-keeping, and (iii) the members of the group accept the representation as an account of their shared past.
conditions: A group of individuals share a common representation of a past event.
The representation is sustained through social practices such as narration, commemoration, or institutional record-keeping.
The members of the group accept the representation as an account of their shared past.
counterexample: The inhabitants of a small town share a vivid collective memory of a great flood that devastated their community in the nineteenth century. The memory is sustained by an annual commemoration and by stories passed from generation to generation. Yet historical investigation reveals that the flood, while real, was a comparatively minor event; the collective memory has greatly magnified its severity. The townspeople accept this inflated account as their shared past. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the collective memory is substantially inaccurate.
missing_condition: The analysis does not include a condition requiring that the shared representation bear an adequate degree of correspondence to the actual historical events, so that a widely accepted but substantially distorted account qualifies as collective memory.
passage: The concept of collective memory has attracted increasing philosophical attention, not least because it raises questions about whether groups, as distinct from individuals, can be said to remember. The analysis above proposes three conditions for the existence of a collective memory: a shared representation, social practices of sustenance, and group acceptance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the following example. One might consider a small town whose inhabitants share a vivid and emotionally charged memory of a great flood that is said to have devastated their community in the latter part of the nineteenth century. The memory is kept alive by an annual ceremony of commemoration, by stories told by the older inhabitants to the younger, and by a plaque affixed to the town hall. The members of the community accept this narrative without question as an account of their shared past. All three conditions are clearly satisfied. Yet when a diligent local historian consults the archival records, she discovers that the flood, though real, was a comparatively minor affair: a few cellars were inundated, some livestock was lost, but no buildings were destroyed and no lives were endangered. The collective memory has, over the generations, magnified the event beyond all recognition. The analysis, by failing to require any correspondence between the shared representation and the historical facts, classifies this grossly inflated narrative as a genuine collective memory, which it is in one sense, but it does not address the epistemic deficiency of a shared representation that bears little resemblance to what actually occurred.

definition: S remembers that p, where p was originally acquired through testimony, if and only if (i) S was told that p by a speaker T at some past time, (ii) S believed that p on the basis of T's testimony, (iii) S continues to believe that p at a later time in virtue of memory, and (iv) S's original acceptance of T's testimony was epistemically justified.
conditions: S was told that p by a speaker T at some past time.
S believed that p on the basis of T's testimony.
S continues to believe that p at a later time in virtue of memory.
S's original acceptance of T's testimony was epistemically justified.
counterexample: A man was told by a trusted colleague that a mutual acquaintance had moved to Edinburgh. He believed this and continues to believe it through memory. His acceptance of the testimony was justified, as the colleague was generally reliable. Yet the colleague was, on this occasion, merely repeating a rumour she had heard at third hand and had not verified. The testimony was, as it happens, true, but the colleague's own epistemic position was thoroughly inadequate. All four conditions are satisfied, yet the testimonial chain is defective.
missing_condition: The analysis requires only that S's acceptance of the testimony be justified but does not require that the testifier T themselves possessed adequate epistemic grounds for asserting that p, so that testimony based on rumour or hearsay may satisfy all conditions.
passage: A great deal of what we remember consists not of events we have ourselves witnessed but of things we have been told by others. The epistemology of testimony and the epistemology of memory thus intersect in cases where a subject remembers a proposition that was originally acquired through the word of another. The analysis above attempts to specify the conditions under which such memorial-testimonial knowledge obtains, requiring that the subject was told, believed on the basis of testimony, continues to believe through memory, and was justified in accepting the testimony. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be described as follows. One might imagine a person who was told by a trusted and, in general, reliable colleague that a mutual acquaintance had moved to Edinburgh. He accepted this testimony without hesitation, and his acceptance was, by ordinary standards, epistemically justified: the colleague was a person of good character and had no motive to deceive. He continues to believe the proposition through memory. All four conditions are satisfied. Yet it transpires that the colleague was, on this particular occasion, merely repeating a rumour she had overheard at a social gathering — a rumour that had passed through several hands and that she had made no effort to verify. The proposition happens to be true, but the colleague's own epistemic position was wholly inadequate. The analysis, by attending only to the justification of the recipient's acceptance and not to the epistemic standing of the testifier, permits knowledge to be transmitted through a chain in which one of the links is epistemically defective. An adequate account must require not merely that the recipient be justified in accepting the testimony but that the testifier possess adequate grounds for the assertion.

definition: S recognises an object or person X without recall if and only if (i) S has a present experience of X that is accompanied by a feeling of familiarity, (ii) S is unable to recall any specific past encounter with X, and (iii) the feeling of familiarity is caused by a genuine past encounter with X that has left a memorial trace, even though S cannot access the details of that encounter.
conditions: S has a present experience of X that is accompanied by a feeling of familiarity.
S is unable to recall any specific past encounter with X.
The feeling of familiarity is caused by a genuine past encounter with X that has left a memorial trace, even though S cannot access the details of that encounter.
counterexample: A woman enters a bookshop and experiences a strong feeling of familiarity upon seeing the proprietor, though she cannot recall any past encounter with him. In fact, she has never met the man before; the feeling of familiarity is produced by his striking resemblance to a former schoolteacher of hers. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but (iii) is not, since the familiarity is caused not by a past encounter with this person but by a resemblance to a different person. Yet the phenomenological character of her experience is indistinguishable from genuine recognition.
missing_condition: The analysis provides no means by which the subject can distinguish genuine recognition, grounded in a past encounter with X, from spurious familiarity produced by resemblance to a different person or object, since the phenomenological character of the two experiences may be identical.
passage: Recognition without recall is a familiar phenomenon: one sees a face and feels certain that one has encountered it before, yet one cannot place it or summon any memory of the previous meeting. The analysis above attempts to characterise this experience by requiring a feeling of familiarity, an inability to recall the specific encounter, and a genuine causal connection between a past meeting and the present feeling. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not difficult to construct. One might consider a woman who enters a bookshop and is immediately struck by a feeling of familiarity upon seeing the proprietor behind the counter. She is quite certain that she has met this man before, though she cannot recall when or where. In truth, she has never encountered him; the feeling of familiarity arises from his marked resemblance to a schoolteacher she knew in childhood. The memorial trace that produces the feeling of familiarity is genuine — it derives from her past experience of the schoolteacher — but it is not a trace of any encounter with the proprietor himself. Condition (iii) is thus not satisfied, strictly speaking, since the familiarity is not caused by a past encounter with X. Nevertheless, the phenomenological character of her experience is, from the inside, indistinguishable from genuine recognition. The analysis, while correctly requiring a causal connection to a past encounter, provides no criterion by which the subject might distinguish veridical recognition from the spurious familiarity produced by resemblance. One is led to conclude that the analysis must be supplemented by some account of how the subject might, at least in principle, discriminate the two cases.

definition: A traumatic memory is epistemically suppressed if and only if (i) the subject experienced a traumatic event at some past time, (ii) the subject formed a memorial representation of the event, and (iii) the memorial representation has been rendered inaccessible to conscious recall by a psychological mechanism of suppression or repression, though it continues to exist as a latent trace.
conditions: The subject experienced a traumatic event at some past time.
The subject formed a memorial representation of the event.
The memorial representation has been rendered inaccessible to conscious recall by a psychological mechanism of suppression or repression, though it continues to exist as a latent trace.
counterexample: A soldier who endured a harrowing combat experience finds that he cannot recall certain details of the event — the faces of those involved, the sequence of actions. He satisfies conditions (i) and (ii): the trauma occurred and a memorial representation was formed. Yet the inaccessibility of certain details is not due to psychological suppression but to the well-documented phenomenon whereby extreme physiological stress disrupts the encoding of memories at the neurological level. The representation was never fully formed, so there is no latent trace to suppress. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, yet the phenomenological result — an inability to recall details of a traumatic event — is indistinguishable from genuine suppression.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between cases in which a fully formed memory has been psychologically suppressed and cases in which the memorial representation was never adequately encoded due to the neurological effects of trauma, yet both present as an inability to recall.
passage: The phenomenon of traumatic memory has occupied a peculiar position in the philosophical and psychological literature, for it appears to challenge the assumption that what has been experienced can, in principle, be recalled. The analysis above characterises epistemic suppression by requiring that a memorial representation exist but be rendered inaccessible by a psychological mechanism. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from a consideration of the neurological effects of extreme stress. One might imagine a soldier who endured a harrowing episode of combat and who subsequently finds himself unable to recall certain critical details of the event — the faces of those involved, the precise sequence of actions, the duration of the engagement. He knows that something terrible occurred, and he retains a general impression of danger and confusion, but the specific details elude him. On the analysis given, one would be inclined to classify this as a case of suppression: the representation was formed but has been rendered inaccessible. Yet the evidence from the neuroscience of stress suggests a different explanation. Under conditions of extreme physiological arousal, the processes responsible for encoding memories into long-term storage are disrupted, so that the detailed representation was never fully formed in the first instance. There is, consequently, no latent trace waiting to be recovered; the information was lost at the point of encoding, not at the point of retrieval. The phenomenological result is the same — the soldier cannot recall the details — but the underlying mechanism is fundamentally different. The analysis, by assuming that a memorial representation was formed and subsequently suppressed, fails to accommodate this alternative aetiology and thereby conflates two importantly distinct phenomena.

definition: Memory contributes to temporal experience if and only if (i) the subject has a present awareness of the passage of time, (ii) this awareness depends upon the retention of immediately preceding experiences in short-term or working memory, and (iii) the retained experiences are apprehended as belonging to the recent past rather than to the present moment.
conditions: The subject has a present awareness of the passage of time.
This awareness depends upon the retention of immediately preceding experiences in short-term or working memory.
The retained experiences are apprehended as belonging to the recent past rather than to the present moment.
counterexample: A musician performing a rapid passage on the piano has a vivid awareness of temporal succession — she hears each note as following the last in a continuous flow. Yet the notes succeed one another so quickly that there is scarcely time for any experience to be apprehended as belonging to the past before the next arrives. Her awareness of temporal passage depends not upon the classification of retained experiences as past but upon the direct perception of succession within the specious present. Condition (iii) is not clearly satisfied, yet her temporal experience is undeniable.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for the perception of temporal succession within the specious present, where the immediate awareness of change does not require that retained experiences be explicitly apprehended as past.
passage: The relationship between memory and the experience of time is a deep and difficult one, touching upon some of the most fundamental questions in the philosophy of mind. It has been urged by a number of philosophers that our awareness of the passage of time depends essentially upon memory: without the retention of what has just occurred, we should be confined to an instantaneous present and could have no sense of temporal flow. The analysis above formalises this thought by requiring that the subject retain immediately preceding experiences and apprehend them as belonging to the recent past. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of musical performance. One might consider a pianist executing a rapid passage in which the notes succeed one another at intervals of a few hundredths of a second. She has a vivid and undeniable awareness of temporal succession: she hears each note as following upon its predecessor in a continuous, directed flow. Yet the rapidity of the succession is such that there is scarcely time for any individual experience to be processed, retained, and apprehended as belonging to the past before the next experience supervenes. Her awareness of temporal passage appears to depend not upon the retrospective classification of retained experiences as past but upon the direct, immediate perception of change and succession within what William James called the specious present. The analysis, by insisting that temporal awareness requires the apprehension of retained experiences as past, fails to accommodate this mode of temporal experience, in which succession is perceived directly rather than inferred from the contrast between present and retained-as-past.

definition: An involuntary memory is epistemically significant if and only if (i) the memory arises spontaneously without deliberate effort on the part of the subject, (ii) the content of the memory is veridical — it faithfully represents a past experience, and (iii) the subject recognises the content as a genuine memory rather than a fantasy or daydream.
conditions: The memory arises spontaneously without deliberate effort on the part of the subject.
The content of the memory is veridical — it faithfully represents a past experience.
The subject recognises the content as a genuine memory rather than a fantasy or daydream.
counterexample: A man walking through a market is suddenly overtaken by an involuntary recollection of a childhood visit to a similar market. The recollection is vivid and arises without any deliberate effort. He recognises it as a genuine memory. Yet upon reflection, he finds that the recollection includes details — the stall-holders' faces, the arrangement of goods — that are not veridical representations of the original experience but rather generic features assembled from many such visits. The memory is not wholly false, but neither does it faithfully represent any single past experience. Condition (ii) is not straightforwardly satisfied, yet the memory is epistemically significant in so far as it conveys general truths about the subject's past.
missing_condition: The analysis requires strict veridicality — faithful representation of a specific past experience — but does not account for involuntary memories that are epistemically valuable because they convey accurate general impressions derived from multiple experiences rather than a faithful record of any single one.
passage: Involuntary memories — those recollections that arise unbidden, often triggered by a sensory cue — have been of interest to philosophers and psychologists alike, not least because of the celebrated descriptions furnished by Proust. The analysis above ascribes epistemic significance to involuntary memories when they are spontaneous, veridical, and recognised as genuine. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be described as follows. One might imagine a man walking through an open-air market who is suddenly overtaken by a vivid and unsought recollection of a childhood visit to a similar market. The recollection presents itself with great immediacy: he seems to see the stalls, the faces of the vendors, the piles of fruit and vegetables. He has no doubt that this is a genuine memory rather than a daydream. Yet upon careful reflection, he realises that the details of the recollection do not correspond to any single past visit. The faces he sees are composites; the arrangement of goods is a generalisation drawn from many occasions. The memory is not veridical in the sense of faithfully representing a particular past experience, for it is an amalgam of many such experiences, distilled into a single composite image. Nevertheless, it would be hasty to dismiss the recollection as epistemically insignificant, for it conveys, in a compressed and impressionistic form, accurate general information about the character of the markets the subject frequented in his youth. The analysis, by demanding strict veridicality with respect to a specific experience, fails to accommodate the epistemic value of such composite involuntary memories, which may be faithful to the general tenor of the past without corresponding to any particular episode within it.

definition: Memory involves narrative construction if and only if (i) the subject recalls a series of past events, (ii) the subject organises these events into a coherent temporal and causal sequence, and (iii) the organising activity is performed by the subject at the time of recall rather than being a feature of the original experience.
conditions: The subject recalls a series of past events.
The subject organises these events into a coherent temporal and causal sequence.
The organising activity is performed by the subject at the time of recall rather than being a feature of the original experience.
counterexample: A woman recalls her experience of a theatrical performance. She remembers the events of the play in their proper temporal and causal sequence: the protagonist's arrival, the confrontation, the resolution. She organises these events coherently at the time of recall. Yet this organisation is not something she imposes upon the material; it is a faithful reproduction of the structure that the playwright and the performance imposed upon her experience at the time. The organising activity at recall merely replicates the original narrative structure rather than constructing one afresh. All three conditions are satisfied, yet no genuine narrative construction has occurred.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between cases in which the subject genuinely constructs a narrative at the time of recall and cases in which the subject merely reproduces a narrative structure that was already present in the original experience.
passage: It has become a commonplace of recent philosophical and psychological discussion that memory is not merely a passive repository of past experiences but an active process of narrative construction. On this view, when we recall the past, we do not simply retrieve a stored record; rather, we organise and shape the recalled material into a coherent story. The analysis above attempts to capture this constructive dimension by requiring that the subject organise recalled events into a temporal and causal sequence at the time of recall. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the recollection of experiences that were already narratively structured at the time of their occurrence. One might consider a woman who recalls a theatrical performance she attended some months ago. She remembers the sequence of events in the play — the arrival of the protagonist, the dramatic confrontation, the dénouement — and she organises these events, at the time of recall, into a coherent temporal and causal sequence. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Yet it would be misleading to say that she has engaged in narrative construction, for the temporal and causal structure she imposes at recall is precisely the structure that was present in the original experience. The playwright and the actors furnished the narrative; she merely reproduces it. Her organising activity at the time of recall is reproductive rather than constructive. The analysis, by failing to distinguish between the imposition of a new narrative structure and the reproduction of an existing one, counts as narrative construction what is in truth mere narrative reproduction. An adequate account must require that the organisation at recall go beyond the structure already present in the original experience.

definition: A memory belief retains its justification despite the subject's having forgotten the original evidence if and only if (i) the subject once possessed adequate evidence for the belief, (ii) the subject has since forgotten the evidence but continues to hold the belief, and (iii) the subject has no positive reason to doubt the belief or to think that the original evidence was defective.
conditions: The subject once possessed adequate evidence for the belief.
The subject has since forgotten the evidence but continues to hold the belief.
The subject has no positive reason to doubt the belief or to think that the original evidence was defective.
counterexample: A philosophy student once read a compelling argument for a particular metaphysical thesis and was persuaded by it. She has since forgotten the argument entirely but retains the belief. She has no reason to doubt the thesis. Yet the argument she read was subsequently shown by a leading philosopher to contain a subtle but fatal error — a fact of which she is unaware. All three conditions are satisfied, but the evidence upon which her belief originally rested has been discredited, though she does not know this.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the original evidence remain undefeated in the broader epistemic community; it attends only to the subject's own epistemic situation, so that a belief grounded in discredited evidence may retain its justification simply because the subject is ignorant of the discreditation.
passage: The problem of forgotten evidence poses a distinctive challenge for the epistemology of memory. It frequently happens that we continue to hold beliefs long after we have forgotten the grounds upon which we originally accepted them. The question is whether such beliefs retain their justification. The analysis above offers an affirmative answer, subject to three conditions: the evidence was once adequate, the belief persists despite the loss of evidence, and the subject has no positive reason for doubt. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves a philosophy student who, during her undergraduate studies, read a seemingly compelling argument for a particular metaphysical thesis — let us say, a form of property dualism. She was persuaded by the argument and adopted the corresponding belief. In the ensuing years, she forgot the argument entirely; she could not now reconstruct even its general outline. She retains the belief, however, and has encountered no reason to question it. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Yet, unbeknownst to her, a distinguished philosopher has since published a devastating critique of the very argument upon which her belief was founded, demonstrating that it rests upon a subtle equivocation. The argument, in the considered judgement of the philosophical community, is no longer regarded as providing adequate support for the thesis. The analysis, by attending solely to the subject's own epistemic position — her lack of positive reason for doubt — and ignoring the objective standing of the original evidence, yields the result that her belief remains justified. One might, however, question whether justification can survive the discreditation of its evidential basis, even when the subject is innocently unaware of this discreditation. The analysis thus proves insufficient in so far as it fails to incorporate an externalist condition requiring that the original evidence retain its standing if the belief is to retain its justification.

definition: A proposition P is known a priori by a subject S if and only if (i) S knows that P, (ii) S's justification for P does not depend upon any sensory experience, and (iii) S's knowledge of P could not be defeated by any empirical evidence.
conditions: S knows that P
S's justification for P does not depend upon any sensory experience
S's knowledge of P could not be defeated by any empirical evidence
counterexample: Consider one who knows, by purely logical reflection, that all bachelors are unmarried. Yet upon encountering a society where the word 'bachelor' has been legally redefined to include certain married persons, one's confidence may be rationally shaken, even though the original justification was wholly non-empirical. Here condition (iii) fails whilst conditions (i) and (ii) appear satisfied.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the concepts employed in P remain stable and are not subject to empirical revision of their conventional meaning.
passage: One might begin by observing that the traditional characterisation of a priori knowledge, as knowledge whose justification is wholly independent of sensory experience, has enjoyed a long and distinguished career in the annals of philosophy. From Leibniz's truths of reason to Kant's celebrated distinction between the a priori and the a posteriori, the notion has served as a cornerstone of epistemological enquiry. The definition under consideration adds to the standard independence condition a further stipulation, namely that a priori knowledge is indefeasible by empirical evidence. It is precisely this additional condition which invites scrutiny. For one might imagine a competent reasoner who grasps an analytic truth through reflection alone, and yet finds herself confronted with testimony or social practice that calls into question the very concepts upon which her reasoning depends. If the meaning of a constituent term shifts under empirical pressure, then what was once a secure piece of a priori knowledge appears vulnerable to a species of empirical defeat. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows: a logician who knows that all vixens are female foxes might encounter a zoological reclassification that alters the extension of 'vixen,' thereby undermining her confidence without impugning her original rational grounds. This suggests that indefeasibility, as stated, is too strong a requirement, or at the very least that it must be carefully circumscribed to exclude cases where empirical revision targets the conceptual apparatus rather than the justificatory structure of the knowledge itself.

definition: A proposition P is analytic if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) P's truth is determined solely by the meanings of the terms composing P, and (iii) the denial of P yields a self-contradiction.
conditions: P is true
P's truth is determined solely by the meanings of the terms composing P
The denial of P yields a self-contradiction
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Nothing is both red and green all over at the same time.' Its truth appears to be determined by the meanings of the colour terms involved, satisfying condition (ii), and yet its denial does not yield a formal self-contradiction in the manner of 'A and not-A.' Here condition (iii) fails whilst the other conditions hold.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to distinguish between logical contradiction and material incompatibility grounded in the internal structure of concepts.
passage: Kant's distinction between analytic and synthetic judgements has exercised a profound influence upon subsequent epistemological thought. The analytic, we are told, is that whose predicate is contained within the concept of the subject; the synthetic is that which genuinely extends our knowledge beyond what is given in the subject-concept alone. The definition here presented refines this Kantian inheritance by requiring that the denial of an analytic truth produce a self-contradiction. One might observe, however, that this refinement is not without its difficulties. There exist propositions whose truth appears to flow from the meanings of their constituent terms, and yet whose denials, whilst absurd, do not constitute contradictions in the narrow logical sense. The proposition that nothing can be simultaneously red and green all over furnishes a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. It is, one might say, a truth about the incompatibility of certain properties, grounded in the nature of colour concepts, but it cannot be reduced to a formal contradiction without supplementary premisses concerning the structure of colour space. Broad, and in a related vein Stebbing, have drawn attention to such cases of material incompatibility, noting that they resist assimilation to the purely formal. If we insist that analyticity requires the generation of strict logical contradiction upon denial, we shall be forced to exclude a range of truths that seem, by any reasonable standard, to be analytic in character. The definition therefore appears to draw the boundaries of the analytic too narrowly.

definition: A statement S is analytic if and only if (i) S is true, (ii) S can be reduced to a logical truth by substitution of synonyms for synonyms, and (iii) there exists a clear boundary between analytic and synthetic statements.
conditions: S is true
S can be reduced to a logical truth by substitution of synonyms for synonyms
There exists a clear boundary between analytic and synthetic statements
counterexample: Consider the statement 'All creatures with a heart are creatures with kidneys.' This is true, and one might attempt to substitute biological synonyms to reduce it to a logical truth, yet the co-extensiveness of 'creature with a heart' and 'creature with kidneys' is an empirical discovery rather than a matter of meaning. There is no clear principled boundary that would classify this as analytic or synthetic, undermining condition (iii).
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes a notion of synonymy that is itself clear and unproblematic, which Quine's arguments show to be question-begging.
passage: Quine's celebrated assault upon the analytic-synthetic distinction has generated a vast literature and remains one of the most consequential episodes in twentieth-century philosophy. The definition under examination captures what one might call the received Fregean picture, according to which analytic truths are those reducible to logical truths by the substitution of synonymous expressions. Quine's objection, in its essentials, is that this characterisation presupposes a prior notion of synonymy that is no less in need of clarification than the concept of analyticity itself. We are invited to believe that 'bachelor' and 'unmarried man' are synonymous, and that the substitution of one for the other transforms a merely analytic truth into a logical one. But upon what ground do we establish this synonymy? If we appeal to interchangeability salva veritate, we find that this criterion is either too weak, admitting co-extensive but non-synonymous terms, or too strong, excluding terms whose interchangeability depends upon the very analytic truths we seek to explain. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the biological domain: the co-extensiveness of 'renate' and 'cordate' is a contingent empirical fact, yet one might be tempted, absent further analysis, to treat the connection as grounded in meaning. The difficulty lies not in any particular substitution but in the absence of a non-circular criterion for the synonymy upon which the entire edifice rests. Quine's challenge thus strikes at condition (iii) with particular force, for it suggests that no sharp boundary between analytic and synthetic can be sustained.

definition: Mathematical knowledge is a priori if and only if (i) the knower possesses justified true belief in a mathematical proposition, (ii) the justification is provided entirely by proof or demonstration, and (iii) no empirical observation plays any role in the justificatory chain.
conditions: The knower possesses justified true belief in a mathematical proposition
The justification is provided entirely by proof or demonstration
No empirical observation plays any role in the justificatory chain
counterexample: Consider a mathematician who arrives at justified true belief in the four-colour theorem, whose proof essentially relies upon extensive computer verification of thousands of configurations. The computer's output constitutes an empirical observation of the machine's behaviour, thereby violating condition (iii), and yet the resulting knowledge appears genuinely mathematical.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the possibility that certain mathematical proofs may legitimately incorporate empirically grounded computational steps without thereby forfeiting their a priori character.
passage: It has long been supposed that mathematical knowledge furnishes the paradigmatic instance of a priori cognition. The geometer's demonstration that the angles of a triangle sum to two right angles, the algebraist's proof that the square root of two is irrational — these appear to be achievements of pure reason, untouched by the contingencies of sensory experience.
