The definition here proposed gives expression to this traditional conviction by requiring that the justificatory chain leading to mathematical knowledge contain no empirical element whatsoever. One might observe, however, that developments in twentieth-century mathematics have placed this requirement under considerable strain. The celebrated proof of the four-colour theorem, first established by Appel and Haken in 1976, relies in an essential and ineliminable fashion upon the output of electronic computing machines. The mathematician who accepts this proof does so, at least in part, on the basis of trust in the physical behaviour of a particular piece of apparatus. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis emerges with clarity here: the knowledge in question is undeniably mathematical in content, and yet the justification incorporates an irreducibly empirical component. If we insist upon the strict exclusion of all empirical observation from the justificatory chain, we shall be compelled to deny a priori status to a growing class of results that the mathematical community regards as fully established. The definition, therefore, appears to demand more than the actual practice of mathematics is able to deliver.

definition: A proposition P is a logical truth known a priori if and only if (i) P is true in all possible interpretations of its non-logical vocabulary, (ii) P's truth is recognisable by reflection upon its logical form alone, and (iii) knowledge of P requires no acquaintance with any particular state of affairs.
conditions: P is true in all possible interpretations of its non-logical vocabulary
P's truth is recognisable by reflection upon its logical form alone
Knowledge of P requires no acquaintance with any particular state of affairs
counterexample: Consider Gödel's incompleteness results, which establish that in any sufficiently powerful consistent formal system there exist truths not derivable within the system. The proposition expressing the system's consistency is true in all interpretations, satisfying condition (i), yet recognising its truth requires metamathematical reasoning that goes beyond mere reflection upon logical form, thereby failing condition (ii).
missing_condition: The analysis omits the distinction between truths recognisable within a given formal system and truths recognisable only by ascending to a metalinguistic or metamathematical standpoint.
passage: The claim that logical truths are known a priori has been regarded, at least since Frege, as among the least controversial theses in epistemology. A logical truth, it is said, is one whose truth depends upon its form rather than its content, and its recognition requires nothing beyond an understanding of the logical constants and the structural relations in which they stand. The definition here presented captures this widely held view with admirable precision. Yet one might observe that the landscape of logic has grown considerably more complex since the days when logical truth could be identified with the theorems of first-order predicate calculus. Gödel's incompleteness theorems reveal the existence of propositions that are true in all models of a given formal system, and yet whose truth cannot be established within that system. To recognise the truth of such propositions, one must ascend to a richer metalanguage or deploy principles that cannot be captured by reflection upon logical form in the narrow sense intended by condition (ii). A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is thus furnished by any Gödel sentence for a consistent extension of Peano arithmetic. Such a sentence is, in a perfectly good sense, a logical truth — it holds in every model — and yet its recognition demands resources that exceed what is available from the contemplation of logical form alone. The epistemological moral is that our grasp of logical truth may outstrip what purely formal reflection can deliver, and the definition must be broadened accordingly if it is to accommodate the full range of a priori logical knowledge.

definition: A subject S possesses a priori knowledge that P through rational intuition if and only if (i) S has an immediate, non-inferential apprehension that P, (ii) this apprehension is produced by the faculty of rational intuition rather than by sensory experience, and (iii) the apprehension is infallible, such that if S rationally intuits that P then P is true.
conditions: S has an immediate, non-inferential apprehension that P
This apprehension is produced by the faculty of rational intuition rather than by sensory experience
The apprehension is infallible
counterexample: Consider a competent mathematician who, upon reflection, intuits that every even number greater than two is the sum of two primes. This apprehension is immediate and non-inferential, and it is produced by rational reflection rather than sensory experience, satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). Yet Goldbach's conjecture remains unproved, and it is conceivable that it is false, so the infallibility requirement of condition (iii) is not met.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to provide a criterion for distinguishing genuine rational intuition from mere intellectual conviction or strong psychological certitude.
passage: The doctrine that rational intuition furnishes a distinctive source of a priori knowledge has a venerable pedigree, stretching from the Cartesian tradition through to the work of contemporary rationalists. On this view, certain truths are grasped directly by the intellect, without the mediation of sensory experience and without the need for discursive reasoning. The definition under scrutiny adds to this picture a requirement of infallibility: what is rationally intuited cannot be false. It is precisely this infallibility condition that proves most vulnerable to objection. For one might observe that the history of mathematics and philosophy is replete with instances in which distinguished thinkers have enjoyed a powerful sense of intellectual self-evidence regarding propositions that were subsequently shown to be false or, at the very least, unprovable. The naive comprehension principle in set theory, for example, struck many as self-evidently true prior to Russell's discovery of the contradiction it engenders. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering any unresolved conjecture — Goldbach's conjecture, say — that strikes the reflecting mind as evidently true. The mathematician who intuits its truth satisfies the first two conditions admirably, yet the infallibility requirement remains unsatisfied, for we possess no guarantee that the conjecture holds. If we insist upon infallibility, we risk emptying rational intuition of nearly all its putative content, retaining only those trivialities whose truth is beyond dispute. A more adequate analysis must distinguish between the phenomenology of rational insight and the guarantee of truth, recognising that the former may obtain without the latter.

definition: A proposition P is contingent a priori if and only if (i) P is knowable independently of experience, (ii) P is true but not necessarily true, and (iii) P's contingency is established by the existence of possible worlds in which P is false.
conditions: P is knowable independently of experience
P is true but not necessarily true
P's contingency is established by the existence of possible worlds in which P is false
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'The standard metre bar in Paris is one metre long.' Kripke argues that this is both contingent and a priori. Yet one might observe that knowing the bar is one metre long arguably requires the experience of stipulating or learning the stipulation of the metre standard — an act embedded in empirical practice. Condition (i) is thereby placed under pressure, for the a priori status depends upon a stipulative act that is itself experiential.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between knowledge that is a priori in virtue of pure reason and knowledge that is a priori only in virtue of a contingent stipulation grounded in empirical practice.
passage: Kripke's introduction of the contingent a priori into philosophical discourse represented a striking departure from the traditional equation of the a priori with the necessary. On the classical view, inherited from Leibniz and Kant, whatever is knowable independently of experience is ipso facto necessary; conversely, whatever is contingent is knowable only a posteriori. Kripke's celebrated example of the standard metre bar challenges this orthodoxy with considerable force. One who fixes the reference of 'one metre' by stipulating that it shall denote the length of a particular bar in Paris thereby comes to know, without further empirical investigation, that the bar is one metre long. Yet the bar might have been longer or shorter — it is a contingent matter that it possesses the length it does. The definition here offered captures the formal structure of such cases admirably. However, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis draws attention to the peculiar character of the stipulative act upon which the a priori knowledge depends. It appears that one must have experience of the bar, or at any rate of the practice of fixing reference, in order to come to know the proposition in question. If this is correct, then the independence from experience required by condition (i) is satisfied only in a highly attenuated sense. The stipulator's knowledge is a priori not because it is grounded in pure reason, but because it is grounded in a linguistic act whose epistemic credentials are themselves partly empirical. One might therefore question whether such knowledge is genuinely a priori or merely a priori relative to a particular stipulative context.

definition: A proposition P is necessary a posteriori if and only if (i) P is true in all possible worlds, (ii) P is knowable only through empirical investigation, and (iii) P's necessity is grounded in the essential nature of the objects or kinds referred to by P.
conditions: P is true in all possible worlds
P is knowable only through empirical investigation
P's necessity is grounded in the essential nature of the objects or kinds referred to by P
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Hesperus is Phosphorus.' This is necessarily true, given that both names designate the same planet, and it was discovered empirically. Yet one might argue that a sufficiently reflective being who grasped the referential mechanism of both names could, in principle, come to know the identity without empirical investigation, thereby challenging condition (ii).
missing_condition: The analysis does not specify whether 'knowable only through empirical investigation' means knowable by all possible epistemic agents or only by agents with our particular cognitive constitution.
passage: The category of the necessary a posteriori, introduced by Kripke in his influential lectures on naming and necessity, has provoked extensive debate concerning the relationship between metaphysical modality and epistemic access. The traditional view, shared by rationalists and empiricists alike, held that necessity and a priority are coextensive: what must be so can be known to be so by reason alone, and what requires experience for its discovery is thereby contingent. Kripke's arguments concerning identity statements involving rigid designators upended this venerable consensus. The proposition that Hesperus is Phosphorus, he argued, is necessarily true — the two names pick out the very same object in every possible world — and yet its truth was an empirical discovery, achieved through astronomical observation rather than armchair reflection. The definition here presented faithfully captures the structure of such cases. Nevertheless, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be developed by considering an idealised epistemic agent possessed of perfect semantic competence. Such an agent, fully grasping the referential mechanism of rigid designation, might in principle determine that two co-referential names pick out the same object without recourse to telescopic observation. If this possibility is coherent, then condition (ii) is too strong, for it asserts that empirical investigation is the only route to knowledge of P. The difficulty lies in the phrase 'knowable only through empirical investigation,' which admits of varying interpretations depending upon the cognitive capacities we ascribe to the knowing subject. A more careful formulation would need to relativise the epistemic requirement to agents of a specified type.

definition: A belief B is innate and thereby a priori if and only if (i) B is present in the mind prior to any sensory experience, (ii) B is not derived from any process of learning or environmental interaction, and (iii) B constitutes knowledge in virtue of its innate origin.
conditions: B is present in the mind prior to any sensory experience
B is not derived from any process of learning or environmental interaction
B constitutes knowledge in virtue of its innate origin
counterexample: Consider an innate fear response to snake-like shapes, which is present prior to experience and not derived from learning. This belief-like state satisfies conditions (i) and (ii), yet it does not constitute knowledge, for it may be triggered by harmless rope-like objects. Condition (iii) is therefore not met, revealing that innate origin alone does not confer epistemic respectability.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the innate belief be reliably truth-tracking or that it bear an appropriate connection to the facts it purports to represent.
passage: The hypothesis that certain ideas or beliefs are innate — present in the mind from birth rather than acquired through experience — has a distinguished philosophical lineage, reaching from Plato's doctrine of recollection through Descartes's innate ideas to contemporary nativist theories in cognitive science. The definition under consideration attempts to forge a link between innateness and a priori status, holding that a belief qualifies as a priori knowledge if it is both innate and constitutive of knowledge in virtue of that innateness. One might observe, however, that this linkage is considerably more problematic than it may initially appear. The mere fact that a belief is innate — that it is, as it were, part of the original furniture of the mind — does not by itself guarantee that the belief is true, let alone that it constitutes knowledge. Evolutionary psychology has furnished numerous examples of innate cognitive dispositions that are systematically misleading, producing beliefs that were adaptive in ancestral environments but are false in current ones. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by innate perceptual biases, such as the tendency to perceive certain ambiguous figures in one way rather than another. Such biases satisfy the conditions of innateness and independence from learning, yet they do not yield knowledge, for they are not reliably connected to the truth. The definition therefore errs in supposing that innate origin can, by itself, ground the epistemic status required for a priori knowledge. What is needed, in addition, is some account of the reliability or truth-conduciveness of the innate mechanism.

definition: A proposition P is a conceptual truth if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the truth of P is wholly determined by the concepts it employs, and (iii) anyone who fully grasps the concepts involved in P is thereby in a position to recognise P's truth.
conditions: P is true
The truth of P is wholly determined by the concepts it employs
Anyone who fully grasps the concepts involved is thereby in a position to recognise P's truth
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Water is H2O.' One might argue that, given the concept of water as necessarily identical to H2O, the truth of this proposition is determined by the concepts involved. Yet a pre-scientific thinker who fully grasps the ordinary concept of water — as a transparent, potable liquid — is manifestly not in a position to recognise that water is H2O. Condition (iii) thus fails.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between the ordinary, phenomenological concept of a natural kind and the scientifically refined concept that incorporates essential microstructure.
passage: The notion of a conceptual truth — a truth whose holding is guaranteed by the very concepts it deploys — occupies a central place in the philosophical tradition. It is closely allied to the analytic, though not, perhaps, co-extensive with it, for there may be conceptual truths that are not reducible to logical truths by the substitution of synonyms. The definition here under scrutiny adds to the requirement of conceptual determination a further epistemic condition: that full conceptual competence suffices for recognition of the truth in question. This is a strong claim, and it is one that faces serious difficulties when applied to propositions involving natural kind terms. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. On one influential view, stemming from Putnam and Kripke, the concept of water is such that it necessarily picks out H2O, for the identity of water with H2O is grounded in the essential nature of the kind. If this is so, then the truth of 'Water is H2O' is, in some sense, determined by the concepts involved. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily constructed: a speaker of ordinary English who possesses full competence with the concept of water, in the sense that she can recognise water, distinguish it from other liquids, and deploy the term correctly in everyday discourse, may nonetheless be entirely ignorant of its chemical composition. Her conceptual grasp is complete by any ordinary standard, yet she cannot recognise the truth that water is H2O. The definition thus conflates two quite different levels of conceptual mastery, and a satisfactory analysis must attend to this distinction.

definition: A subject S has a priori justification for believing P if and only if (i) S has justification for P, (ii) this justification does not depend upon sensory experience, and (iii) S's justification is sufficient, together with the truth of P and S's belief that P, to constitute knowledge.
conditions: S has justification for P
This justification does not depend upon sensory experience
S's justification is sufficient to constitute knowledge when combined with truth and belief
counterexample: Consider a subject who possesses a valid a priori proof of a true mathematical theorem but who also holds the belief on the basis of an unreliable astrologer's testimony. The subject has a priori justification satisfying conditions (i) and (ii), and the belief is true, yet the belief is not based upon the justification in the right way. Condition (iii) fails because the justification, though present, is not what actually grounds the subject's belief.
missing_condition: The analysis omits a basing requirement: that S's belief that P must be held on the basis of the a priori justification rather than on some other ground.
passage: It is important to distinguish between a priori justification and a priori knowledge, for the two notions, though intimately related, are not identical. A subject may possess a priori justification for a proposition without thereby knowing it a priori, if some further condition for knowledge is unmet. The definition here presented attempts to bridge the gap by stipulating that a priori justification, combined with truth and belief, suffices for knowledge. One might observe, however, that this formulation neglects a condition that has been recognised as essential since Gettier's celebrated paper of 1963, namely the requirement that the subject's belief be appropriately based upon the justification in question. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a mathematician has before her a perfectly valid proof that a certain theorem holds. She possesses, therefore, a priori justification for believing the theorem. But suppose further that she has not actually followed the proof; instead, she believes the theorem because a fortune-teller has assured her of its truth. Her belief is true, she possesses a priori justification, and she believes the relevant proposition — yet it would be perverse to say that she knows the theorem a priori, for her belief is not grounded in the justification that is available to her. The lesson is that justification must not merely be present in the subject's epistemic situation; it must be operative in the formation and sustenance of the belief. Without a basing condition, the definition permits cases in which knowledge is attributed where none genuinely obtains.

definition: A priori knowledge is possible within an empiricist framework if and only if (i) the a priori is restricted to relations among ideas or concepts, (ii) a priori truths are devoid of substantive factual content about the external world, and (iii) experience is the ultimate source of all concepts employed in a priori reasoning.
conditions: The a priori is restricted to relations among ideas or concepts
A priori truths are devoid of substantive factual content about the external world
Experience is the ultimate source of all concepts employed in a priori reasoning
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Every event has a cause.' A moderate empiricist might grant that the concepts of event and cause are derived from experience, satisfying condition (iii), and that the proposition concerns the relations among these concepts, satisfying condition (i). Yet the proposition appears to make a substantive claim about the external world, violating condition (ii), whilst arguably being known a priori.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for propositions that are both a priori and substantively informative about the structure of experience, as Kant's synthetic a priori judgements purport to be.
passage: Moderate empiricism, of the sort championed by Hume and refined by the logical positivists, seeks to accommodate a priori knowledge within an empiricist framework by imposing stringent restrictions upon its scope. On this view, the a priori is confined to the sphere of relations among ideas; it yields no information about matters of fact. The truths of logic and mathematics are a priori, to be sure, but they are so only because they are, in some sense, empty — they tell us nothing about how the world is, but only about how our concepts are related to one another. The definition here under examination gives expression to this moderate empiricist position. One might observe, however, that the position faces a serious difficulty when confronted with propositions that appear to be both a priori and substantively informative. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the principle that every event has a cause. This principle has been regarded by many philosophers as knowable a priori — it seems to flow from the very concepts of event and causation — and yet it makes a substantive claim about the structure of the external world. If every event must have a cause, then the world is constrained in a manner that goes beyond the mere relations among our concepts. The moderate empiricist who insists upon condition (ii) must either deny the a priori status of the causal principle or deny that it has substantive factual content. Neither option is free from difficulty, and the tension suggests that the moderate empiricist framework may be too restrictive to accommodate the full range of a priori knowledge.

definition: A logical truth is true by convention if and only if (i) its truth is determined by the linguistic conventions governing the use of logical constants, (ii) these conventions are freely chosen and could have been otherwise, and (iii) no fact about the world independent of these conventions contributes to the truth of the proposition.
conditions: Its truth is determined by the linguistic conventions governing the use of logical constants
These conventions are freely chosen and could have been otherwise
No fact about the world independent of convention contributes to the truth of the proposition
counterexample: Consider the law of non-contradiction: a proposition cannot be both true and false. The conventionalist holds that this is true by convention, yet one might observe that any attempt to adopt an alternative convention — one in which contradictions are permitted — appears to undermine the very practice of assertion and reasoning. If the convention is not genuinely freely chosen, condition (ii) is not satisfied.
missing_condition: The analysis does not address whether certain conventions are constrained by the preconditions of rational discourse itself, such that they are not genuinely free.
passage: Conventionalism about logical truth, associated principally with the early work of the logical positivists and the later reflections of Wittgenstein, maintains that the truths of logic are true in virtue of the conventions we have adopted for the use of logical words. On this view, the law of excluded middle, the law of non-contradiction, and the principles of deductive inference owe their truth not to any feature of mind-independent reality but to our linguistic decisions concerning the meanings of 'not,' 'and,' 'or,' and their fellows. The definition here presented captures the conventionalist position with clarity. One might observe, however, that the freedom condition — the stipulation that the conventions are freely chosen and could have been otherwise — is considerably more difficult to sustain than it may initially appear. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the law of non-contradiction itself. Suppose we attempt to adopt an alternative convention, one under which a proposition may be both true and false. It appears that, in doing so, we do not merely adopt a different logic; rather, we undermine the very possibility of meaningful assertion, for a language in which every proposition and its negation may be simultaneously affirmed is a language in which nothing determinate is said. If this is correct, then the convention governing non-contradiction is not freely chosen in any robust sense — it is, rather, a precondition of the activity of reasoning itself. The conventionalist must therefore either weaken the freedom condition or concede that at least some logical truths are not true by convention in the sense intended.

definition: A proposition P is self-evident if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) understanding P is sufficient for being justified in believing P, and (iii) any rational agent who fully understands P will believe P.
conditions: P is true
Understanding P is sufficient for being justified in believing P
Any rational agent who fully understands P will believe P
counterexample: Consider the axiom of choice in set theory. Many mathematicians fully understand the axiom and regard it as self-evident, yet other equally competent and rational mathematicians understand it fully and yet reject it or remain agnostic. Condition (iii) fails, for rational disagreement persists among those who fully comprehend the proposition.
missing_condition: The analysis does not allow for the possibility that self-evidence may admit of degrees, or that a proposition may be self-evident to some rational agents and not to others depending upon their mathematical training and background assumptions.
passage: The notion of self-evidence has played a significant role in the epistemology of a priori knowledge, particularly in foundationalist theories that seek to identify a class of basic beliefs whose justification requires no further support. A self-evident proposition, on the traditional account, is one that compels assent upon being understood: to grasp it is, eo ipso, to recognise its truth. The definition under examination codifies this traditional account by requiring that every rational agent who fully understands a self-evident proposition will believe it. One might observe, however, that this universality condition is exceedingly strong, and that it is not satisfied even by propositions that have been widely regarded as paradigmatic instances of self-evidence. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is afforded by the axiom of choice, which asserts that for any collection of non-empty sets there exists a function selecting one element from each. This axiom has been accepted by many mathematicians as self-evidently true — it seems to follow from the very concept of a set — and yet it has been rejected or questioned by constructivists and others who possess an equally thorough understanding of its content. The rational disagreement that persists concerning the axiom of choice suggests that full understanding does not invariably compel belief, even among ideally rational agents. A more satisfactory analysis might allow that self-evidence is a matter of degree, or that it is relative to a background of intellectual commitments, rather than an all-or-nothing property that uniformly constrains rational opinion.

definition: A priori justification is indefeasible if and only if (i) S is justified a priori in believing P, (ii) no possible empirical evidence could undermine S's justification for P, and (iii) no possible non-empirical consideration could undermine S's justification for P.
conditions: S is justified a priori in believing P
No possible empirical evidence could undermine S's justification for P
No possible non-empirical consideration could undermine S's justification for P
counterexample: Consider a logician who is justified a priori in accepting a principle of naive set theory — say, the unrestricted comprehension axiom. The discovery of Russell's paradox, a purely non-empirical consideration, defeats this justification. Yet one might have thought that all three conditions were met prior to the paradox's discovery, revealing that a priori justification was defeasible all along despite appearing indefeasible.
missing_condition: The analysis does not recognise that even a priori justification may be provisional, subject to defeat by the discovery of previously unnoticed conceptual or logical difficulties.
passage: The question whether a priori justification is defeasible — whether it can be undermined by subsequent evidence or reflection — is of considerable importance for epistemological theory. If a priori justification is indefeasible, then it furnishes a uniquely secure foundation for knowledge; if it is defeasible, then its epistemic credentials, whilst still significant, are more modest than has traditionally been supposed. The definition here presented characterises indefeasibility in the strongest possible terms, requiring immunity from both empirical and non-empirical defeat. One might observe that this characterisation, whilst admirably clear, is vulnerable to a familiar class of cases drawn from the history of logic and mathematics. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the fate of naive set theory. Prior to the discovery of Russell's paradox, the axiom of unrestricted comprehension — the principle that for every property there exists a set of all and only those things possessing that property — was regarded by many as self-evidently true and its justification as beyond challenge. The justification was a priori, and it was difficult to see what possible consideration, empirical or otherwise, could undermine it. Yet Russell's paradox, itself an exercise in pure logic, did precisely that: it showed that the axiom led to contradiction and was therefore false. The justification that had appeared indefeasible was, in fact, defeated by a non-empirical consideration that simply had not yet been discovered. This episode suggests that indefeasibility, as defined, is a condition that no a priori justification can be guaranteed to meet in advance, for the space of possible non-empirical defeaters is not surveyable in the way the definition requires.

definition: A moral proposition P is known a priori if and only if (i) P is a true moral proposition, (ii) S's justification for P is independent of empirical observation of moral behaviour or social practice, and (iii) P is knowable through pure moral reasoning or rational intuition alone.
conditions: P is a true moral proposition
S's justification for P is independent of empirical observation of moral behaviour or social practice
P is knowable through pure moral reasoning or rational intuition alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Torture of innocents for mere amusement is morally wrong.' This appears to be knowable through moral reasoning alone, satisfying conditions (ii) and (iii). Yet whether it is a true moral proposition, as required by condition (i), presupposes moral realism — a meta-ethical thesis that is itself philosophically contested. An error theorist who denies the existence of moral truths would hold that condition (i) cannot be satisfied by any moral proposition.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes moral realism without acknowledging it, and does not specify how 'true moral proposition' is to be understood if one is not a realist about moral facts.
passage: The question whether there exists a priori knowledge of moral truths has exercised philosophers since at least the time of the Cambridge Platonists, who maintained that moral principles are grasped by reason in much the same manner as mathematical axioms. The definition here proposed captures the rationalist position in ethics with clarity, requiring that moral knowledge be independent of empirical observation and accessible through pure moral reasoning. One might observe, however, that the definition rests upon a substantial meta-ethical presupposition that is by no means universally shared. Condition (i) requires that the moral proposition in question be true, but this requirement is non-trivial in the context of ongoing disputes between moral realists, constructivists, and error theorists. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by considering a seemingly paradigmatic moral truth — that the torture of innocents for mere entertainment is wrong. A rationalist about moral knowledge will find conditions (ii) and (iii) readily satisfied: one need not observe actual instances of torture to know that the practice is morally abhorrent. Yet the error theorist, following Mackie, will deny that any moral proposition is true in the correspondence sense required, and the constructivist will insist that moral truths are constituted by the outcomes of idealised deliberative procedures — procedures that may themselves have an empirical dimension. The definition, by simply assuming the truth of the moral proposition, fails to engage with these deeper meta-ethical questions, and a more adequate analysis would need to specify the sense in which moral propositions can be true before claiming that they can be known a priori.

definition: Geometric knowledge is a priori if and only if (i) the known propositions concern the necessary properties of spatial figures, (ii) the justification for these propositions derives from pure spatial intuition or logical deduction from axioms, and (iii) no empirical measurement or observation contributes to the justification.
conditions: The known propositions concern the necessary properties of spatial figures
The justification derives from pure spatial intuition or logical deduction from axioms
No empirical measurement or observation contributes to the justification
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the angles of a triangle sum to 180 degrees. In Euclidean geometry this is deduced from the parallel postulate, but the discovery of non-Euclidean geometries reveals that the proposition holds only relative to a particular set of axioms. Whether physical space is Euclidean is an empirical question, settled by measurement. Condition (i) fails because the properties in question are not necessary simpliciter but contingent upon the choice of axioms.
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between knowledge of a particular geometric system's theorems and knowledge of which geometric system correctly describes physical space.
passage: The epistemic status of geometry has been a matter of intense philosophical debate since antiquity. Kant regarded geometric knowledge as synthetic a priori, holding that the truths of geometry are necessary and yet informative about the structure of space as intuited by us. The definition here under consideration reflects this Kantian heritage, requiring that geometric knowledge derive from pure spatial intuition or logical deduction and that it concern the necessary properties of spatial figures. One might observe, however, that the development of non-Euclidean geometry in the nineteenth century, and the subsequent application of Riemannian geometry in Einstein's general theory of relativity, has placed this traditional view under severe strain. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. The proposition that the interior angles of a triangle sum to two right angles is, within Euclidean geometry, a theorem deducible from the axioms by purely logical means. It might therefore appear to satisfy all three conditions. Yet the very same proposition is false in hyperbolic and elliptic geometries, where the angle sum is respectively less than and greater than two right angles. Whether the geometry of physical space is Euclidean, hyperbolic, or elliptic is a question that cannot be settled by pure intuition or deduction alone; it requires empirical investigation of the sort carried out by astronomers and physicists. The definition, by requiring that geometric propositions concern necessary properties, presupposes that there is a single geometry whose truths are necessary, when in fact there are many geometries, and the question of which applies to the physical world is irreducibly empirical.

definition: Experience plays only an enabling role in a priori knowledge if and only if (i) experience is causally necessary for the acquisition of the concepts employed in the known proposition, (ii) experience provides no evidential support for the truth of the proposition, and (iii) the justification for the proposition is wholly non-experiential once the relevant concepts have been acquired.
conditions: Experience is causally necessary for the acquisition of the relevant concepts
Experience provides no evidential support for the truth of the proposition
The justification is wholly non-experiential once concepts have been acquired
counterexample: Consider one who learns the concept of addition through experience with physical objects and subsequently comes to know that 7 + 5 = 12. The experience of grouping objects may not merely enable the acquisition of the concept but may also serve as evidence that the arithmetic claim is correct, blurring the boundary between enabling and evidential roles. Condition (ii) is thus difficult to sustain in practice.
missing_condition: The analysis does not provide a criterion for distinguishing cases where experience merely triggers concept acquisition from cases where it simultaneously provides evidential support for the resulting beliefs.
passage: The distinction between the enabling and the evidential roles of experience in a priori knowledge is of considerable importance for the defence of the a priori against empiricist scepticism. The enabling role is uncontroversial: one must have certain experiences in order to acquire the concepts necessary for entertaining a priori propositions. A being who had never experienced redness could not, perhaps, grasp the concept of red and hence could not entertain the a priori truth that nothing is both red and green all over. The evidential role, by contrast, is what the a priori theorist denies: experience may supply the concepts, but it does not supply the grounds for believing the proposition true. The definition here presented gives expression to this distinction with commendable precision. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be developed by attending to the way in which mathematical concepts are actually acquired. A child who learns to count by manipulating physical objects — beads, blocks, fingers — acquires the concept of number through experience. But it is far from clear that the experience plays a merely enabling role. The child's repeated observation that two beads and three beads invariably yield five beads is plausibly part of the evidence upon which the child's belief that 2 + 3 = 5 rests, at least initially. If the experiential evidence is not subsequently replaced by purely rational justification, then the distinction between enabling and evidential roles is less sharp than the definition assumes. The philosophical task of distinguishing the two roles with precision remains incomplete, and the definition is insufficient precisely because it presupposes a clean separation that may not obtain in actual epistemic practice.

definition: A proposition P is knowable a priori with respect to possible worlds if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) P is true in all possible worlds accessible from the actual world, and (iii) S can determine P's truth-value for any possible world by a priori reflection alone.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
P is true in all possible worlds accessible from the actual world
S can determine P's truth-value for any possible world by a priori reflection alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'I exist.' In the actual world this is true, and arguably one can know it by a priori reflection. Yet it is not true in all accessible possible worlds — there are worlds in which the actual thinker does not exist. Conditions (i) and (iii) may be satisfied whilst condition (ii) fails, showing that a priori knowability does not require truth across all accessible worlds.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates a priori knowability with necessary truth by requiring truth in all accessible possible worlds, when in fact some a priori knowable propositions are contingent.
passage: The relationship between a priori knowledge and the framework of possible worlds has been the subject of extensive investigation in recent decades. The definition here proposed links a priori knowability to truth across all accessible possible worlds, thereby tying the a priori to the necessary in a manner reminiscent of the pre-Kripkean tradition. One might observe, however, that this linkage is precisely what Kripke's work has called into question. The contingent a priori — propositions that are knowable independently of experience yet true in some possible worlds and false in others — constitutes a direct challenge to condition (ii). A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the Cartesian tradition. The cogito — the proposition that I exist — is plausibly knowable a priori, for one can determine its truth by reflection alone, without recourse to sensory experience. Yet it is not true in all possible worlds accessible from the actual world, for there are surely possible worlds in which the particular individual who is reflecting does not exist. The definition, by requiring truth in all accessible worlds, would exclude the cogito from the class of a priori knowable propositions, and this seems an unacceptable result. More generally, the definition conflates two notions that ought to be kept distinct: being knowable a priori and being necessarily true. Kripke's insights concerning the contingent a priori and the necessary a posteriori have shown that these notions can come apart, and a satisfactory analysis of a priori knowledge in the possible worlds framework must accommodate this separation.

definition: Conceptual analysis yields a priori knowledge if and only if (i) the analysis consists in the decomposition of a concept into its constituent necessary and sufficient conditions, (ii) the analysis is conducted by reflection upon the concept without recourse to empirical investigation, and (iii) the results of the analysis are true of all and only those things falling under the concept.
conditions: The analysis decomposes a concept into necessary and sufficient conditions
The analysis is conducted by reflection without empirical investigation
The results are true of all and only those things falling under the concept
counterexample: Consider the analysis of the concept of knowledge as justified true belief. This analysis was conducted by purely reflective means and appeared to provide necessary and sufficient conditions. Yet Gettier's cases demonstrate that justified true belief is not sufficient for knowledge, thereby showing that the analysis fails condition (iii) despite satisfying conditions (i) and (ii).
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the proposed decomposition be verified against the full range of possible and actual cases, including unusual or deviant ones, before it is accepted as yielding knowledge.
passage: Conceptual analysis, understood as the decomposition of a concept into its constituent conditions, has long been regarded as the distinctive method of armchair philosophy. From Socrates' search for definitions to the analytic philosophers' attempts to provide necessary and sufficient conditions for knowledge, justice, causation, and the like, the method has been applied with varying degrees of success to a wide range of concepts. The definition here proposed identifies conceptual analysis as a source of a priori knowledge, provided that the analysis correctly captures the extension of the concept in question. One might observe, however, that the history of philosophy furnishes numerous cases in which carefully conducted analyses have turned out to be incorrect, despite having been arrived at by impeccable reflective means. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the Gettier problem. For more than two millennia, the analysis of knowledge as justified true belief was widely accepted as correct. It was arrived at by reflection, it appeared to provide necessary and sufficient conditions, and it seemed to be true of all cases of knowledge. Yet Gettier's ingeniously constructed scenarios showed that justified true belief is not sufficient for knowledge: a subject may hold a justified true belief that does not constitute knowledge because the justification is, in a certain sense, disconnected from the truth. The analysis satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) — it is a reflective decomposition into putative necessary and sufficient conditions — but it fails condition (iii), for the conditions it identifies are not, in fact, sufficient. This failure suggests that conceptual analysis, even when conducted with the greatest care, is fallible, and that the a priori knowledge it yields is provisional rather than certain.

definition: A proposition P is a priori knowable through conceivability if and only if (i) P's truth can be established by conceiving of a state of affairs in which P holds, (ii) the inconceivability of not-P suffices for establishing P's necessity, and (iii) what is conceivable is a reliable guide to what is metaphysically possible.
conditions: P's truth can be established by conceiving of a state of affairs in which P holds
The inconceivability of not-P suffices for establishing P's necessity
What is conceivable is a reliable guide to what is metaphysically possible
counterexample: Consider the proposition that zombies — beings physically identical to conscious humans but wholly lacking phenomenal experience — are conceivable. If conceivability is a reliable guide to possibility, then zombies are metaphysically possible, which would refute physicalism. Yet many philosophers maintain that zombies are conceivable but not genuinely possible, thereby undermining condition (iii).
missing_condition: The analysis does not distinguish between prima facie conceivability and ideal conceivability, nor does it account for the possibility that our imaginative faculties may be systematically misleading regarding metaphysical possibility.
passage: The appeal to conceivability as a guide to possibility, and thence to a priori knowledge, has a long and contentious history in philosophy. Descartes employed it in his argument for mind-body dualism; Hume invoked it as a test for necessary connection. The definition here under scrutiny formalises the intuition that what we can conceive is possible and what we cannot conceive is necessary. One might observe, however, that the reliability of conceivability as a guide to metaphysical possibility has been subject to sustained criticism. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is afforded by the zombie argument in the philosophy of mind. It appears that we can conceive of a being that is physically identical to a conscious human in every respect and yet wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness. If condition (iii) of the definition is correct — if conceivability is a reliable guide to possibility — then such zombies are metaphysically possible, and physicalism about the mind is false. Yet many philosophers, including those sympathetic to physicalism, maintain that zombie scenarios, whilst conceivable in some sense, do not represent genuine metaphysical possibilities. The conceivability, they argue, is merely prima facie: it reflects the limits of our imaginative faculties rather than the structure of modal reality. If this is correct, then conceivability is not the reliable guide to possibility that condition (iii) requires, and the a priori knowledge supposedly grounded in conceivability arguments is correspondingly less secure than the definition would have us believe. A satisfactory analysis must attend to the distinction between various grades of conceivability and their respective credentials as guides to what is genuinely possible.

definition: A thought experiment yields a priori knowledge that P if and only if (i) the thought experiment involves the imaginative consideration of a hypothetical scenario, (ii) the consideration of the scenario does not depend upon empirical observation, and (iii) the conclusion P drawn from the thought experiment is both justified and true.
conditions: The thought experiment involves imaginative consideration of a hypothetical scenario
The consideration does not depend upon empirical observation
The conclusion P drawn is both justified and true
counterexample: Consider Galileo's thought experiment concerning falling bodies, in which he imagines a heavy and a light body joined together. The thought experiment yields the conclusion that all bodies fall at the same rate regardless of weight. Yet the justification arguably depends upon tacit empirical assumptions about the behaviour of falling bodies — for instance, that the medium does not differentially affect objects of different density. Condition (ii) is thus violated.
missing_condition: The analysis does not require that the background assumptions informing the thought experiment be themselves a priori, allowing tacit empirical premisses to contaminate the supposedly a priori conclusion.
passage: Thought experiments have occupied a distinctive place in the methodology of philosophy and science alike. From Plato's ring of Gyges to Einstein's riding on a beam of light, the imaginative consideration of hypothetical scenarios has yielded insights that might have been difficult or impossible to obtain by observation alone. The definition here presented characterises the epistemic contribution of thought experiments in terms that align them with a priori knowledge: the scenarios are imagined rather than observed, and the conclusions drawn are justified and true. One might observe, however, that this characterisation underestimates the extent to which thought experiments rely upon tacit empirical assumptions. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by Galileo's celebrated thought experiment concerning the rate of fall of heavy and light bodies. Galileo invites us to imagine a heavy body and a light body joined by a string and dropped from a height. If the heavier body falls faster, then the compound body should both fall faster (because it is heavier) and fall slower (because the lighter body retards the heavier one). From this contradiction, Galileo concludes that all bodies fall at the same rate. The reasoning is conducted in the imagination, without direct recourse to observation, and the conclusion is true. Yet the thought experiment depends upon empirical assumptions — about the nature of gravity, the role of air resistance, and the behaviour of connected bodies — that are not themselves a priori. If these background assumptions are false, as they would be in a medium where viscosity is significant, the conclusion of the thought experiment would be mistaken. The definition therefore fails to ensure that the a priori status of the thought experiment is not undermined by the empirical character of its tacit premisses.

definition: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject, (ii) the judgement is known independently of experience, and (iii) the judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding.
conditions: The predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject
The judgement is known independently of experience
The judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding
counterexample: Consider the judgement 'Every effect has a cause.' One might grant that this is synthetic and a priori, satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). Yet an empiricist may deny that there exist pure forms of intuition or categories of the understanding in Kant's sense, holding instead that the judgement's apparent a priori status is explicable by deeply entrenched habits of thought. Condition (iii) thus depends upon a contested metaphysical framework.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes the truth of Kant's transcendental idealism and does not provide an account of synthetic a priori knowledge that is neutral between competing epistemological frameworks.
passage: Kant's doctrine of the synthetic a priori stands as one of the most ambitious and controversial contributions to epistemology in the history of the subject. Against both the rationalist, who holds that a priori knowledge is invariably analytic, and the empiricist, who denies the possibility of substantive a priori knowledge altogether, Kant maintains that there exist judgements which are both informative and knowable independently of experience. The examples he adduces — '7 + 5 = 12,' 'every event has a cause,' and the principles of Euclidean geometry — have been the subject of vigorous debate ever since. The definition here under examination gives expression to the Kantian position by grounding the synthetic a priori in the pure forms of intuition and the categories of the understanding. One might observe, however, that this grounding is itself a substantive philosophical commitment, and one that is by no means universally accepted. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the perspective of the empiricist who rejects the apparatus of Kantian transcendental philosophy. Such a philosopher may grant that certain judgements — 'every effect has a cause,' for instance — appear to be synthetic and a priori, but will deny that their a priori character is explained by pure forms of intuition or categories. She may offer an alternative explanation in terms of psychological entrenchment, evolutionary inheritance, or pragmatic indispensability. Condition (iii), which ties the synthetic a priori to a specifically Kantian metaphysical framework, thus renders the definition partisan where it ought to be neutral. A satisfactory account of the synthetic a priori, if such knowledge exists, must be compatible with a range of epistemological frameworks, rather than presupposing the truth of one particular philosophical system.

definition: A subject S acquires a priori knowledge that P through testimony if and only if (i) the testifier T has a priori knowledge that P, (ii) T communicates P to S, and (iii) S comes to know P on the basis of T's communication without requiring independent a priori justification.
conditions: The testifier T has a priori knowledge that P
T communicates P to S
S comes to know P on the basis of T's communication without requiring independent a priori justification
counterexample: Consider a student who learns from a trusted mathematics teacher that Fermat's Last Theorem is true. The teacher possesses a priori knowledge (via Wiles's proof), and the student comes to believe the theorem on the basis of the teacher's testimony. Yet the student's justification depends upon the empirical assessment of the teacher's reliability — the student trusts the teacher because of past experience. The resulting knowledge, if it is knowledge, appears to be a posteriori rather than a priori, undermining the claim that testimony can transmit a priori status.
missing_condition: The analysis does not address the epistemic status of the recipient's grounds for trusting the testifier, which are typically empirical and may thereby convert what was a priori knowledge in the testifier into a posteriori knowledge in the recipient.
passage: The question whether a priori knowledge can be transmitted through testimony has received comparatively little attention in the epistemological literature, yet it raises issues of considerable subtlety. On the face of it, the matter seems straightforward: if a mathematician proves a theorem and communicates it to a colleague, the colleague comes to know the theorem. But in what sense does the colleague's knowledge qualify as a priori? The definition here presented takes the optimistic view that a priori knowledge can be transmitted via testimony, provided the testifier herself possesses such knowledge. One might observe, however, that the transmission of knowledge through testimony introduces an essentially empirical element into the justificatory chain. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering the epistemic situation of the recipient. When a student accepts a mathematical theorem on the authority of her teacher, her grounds for believing the theorem include her assessment of the teacher's reliability, her perception of the teacher's utterance, and her background beliefs about the trustworthiness of mathematical authorities. All of these are empirical grounds. The student does not grasp the proof herself; she relies upon the teacher's word. If we ask what justifies the student in believing the theorem, the answer must include these empirical considerations, for without them the student would have no reason to accept the teacher's testimony rather than, say, the pronouncements of a random stranger. The a priori character of the teacher's knowledge is not, it appears, automatically inherited by the student. Rather, the testimony introduces a new justificatory pathway that is irreducibly empirical in character, and the student's knowledge, if it qualifies as knowledge at all, appears to be a posteriori. The definition therefore fails to reckon with the transformation of epistemic status that occurs in the transmission from testifier to recipient.

definition: A priori knowledge is compatible with naturalism if and only if (i) a priori justification can be explained in terms of natural cognitive processes, (ii) a priori knowledge does not require the existence of abstract non-natural entities, and (iii) the reliability of a priori methods can be empirically confirmed.
conditions: A priori justification can be explained in terms of natural cognitive processes
A priori knowledge does not require the existence of abstract non-natural entities
The reliability of a priori methods can be empirically confirmed
counterexample: Consider a priori knowledge of logical laws, such as the law of non-contradiction. A naturalist may attempt to explain our grasp of this law in terms of evolved cognitive mechanisms. Yet if the reliability of these mechanisms is to be empirically confirmed, as condition (iii) demands, we must use logic in designing and interpreting the confirming experiments — thereby presupposing the very a priori knowledge we seek to naturalise. Condition (iii) thus involves a vicious circularity.
missing_condition: The analysis does not address the circularity that arises when the empirical confirmation of a priori methods itself presupposes the validity of those very methods.
passage: The relationship between naturalism and the a priori constitutes one of the most vexed problems in contemporary epistemology. Naturalists, broadly construed, maintain that all genuine knowledge can be accommodated within a scientific framework and that the methods of the natural sciences provide the ultimate standard of epistemic respectability. The a priori, with its suggestion of knowledge obtained independently of empirical enquiry, sits uneasily within this framework. The definition here proposed attempts a reconciliation, requiring that a priori justification be explicable in natural terms, that it not presuppose non-natural entities, and that the reliability of a priori methods be empirically confirmable. One might observe, however, that the third condition raises a difficulty of a peculiarly intractable kind. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be developed by considering the attempt to empirically confirm the reliability of logical reasoning. Suppose we wish to confirm, by empirical means, that the law of non-contradiction is reliable — that beliefs formed in accordance with it are generally true. To carry out this confirmation, we must design experiments, collect data, and draw inferences. But the design of experiments presupposes logic, the interpretation of data presupposes logic, and the drawing of inferences is itself an exercise of the very logical capacity whose reliability we seek to confirm. The circularity is manifest: we cannot empirically confirm the reliability of logic without employing logic, and we cannot employ logic for this purpose without presupposing its reliability. This suggests that the a priori resists naturalisation at a fundamental level, for the tools of empirical confirmation are themselves dependent upon the a priori methods they are meant to underwrite. The naturalist who insists upon condition (iii) thus finds herself caught in a circle from which there is no obvious escape.

definition: A belief is immune to Cartesian sceptical doubt if and only if (i) the belief is held with subjective certainty by the agent, (ii) no logically possible scenario exists in which a deceiving demon could cause the agent to hold the belief falsely, and (iii) the agent can rule out every deceiving-demon hypothesis by the exercise of reason alone.
conditions: The belief is held with subjective certainty by the agent
No logically possible scenario exists in which a deceiving demon could cause the agent to hold the belief falsely
The agent can rule out every deceiving-demon hypothesis by the exercise of reason alone
counterexample: Consider a mathematician who holds with complete subjective certainty the proposition that seven is a prime number. No deceiving demon could render this false, for its truth is a matter of logical necessity. Yet the mathematician cannot, by reason alone, rule out the hypothesis that a demon is causing her to misidentify which propositions she is entertaining — she may believe she is contemplating the primality of seven when the demon has substituted the thought of nine.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the agent must also be able to verify, independently of the demon hypothesis, that their mental content is what they take it to be.
passage: One of the most celebrated challenges in the history of philosophy is that which Descartes posed in the first of his Meditations, wherein he entertained the possibility that an omnipotent malicious demon might be deceiving him about the whole of his experience. The analysis above attempts to characterise those beliefs which would survive such radical doubt. It appears, at first glance, that necessary truths of mathematics and logic would furnish the paradigm cases: no demon, however powerful, could render seven composite rather than prime. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers not the truth of the proposition itself but the agent's capacity to be certain that the proposition she is entertaining is indeed the one she takes herself to be contemplating. For the demon's powers, as Descartes himself would acknowledge, extend not merely to the fabrication of sensory experience but potentially to the corruption of thought itself. If one's very act of thinking could be tampered with, then subjective certainty about the content of one's beliefs becomes suspect, and the third condition — the ability to rule out the demon hypothesis by reason alone — cannot be satisfied in the manner the definition requires. One might observe, therefore, that the analysis fails to account for a deeper level of sceptical vulnerability, namely, the possibility that the demon attacks not the objects of belief but the medium of cognition itself.

definition: A perceptual experience provides conclusive evidence that one is awake if and only if (i) the experience possesses a phenomenal vividness that dreams characteristically lack, (ii) the experience coheres with the totality of one's other current experiences, and (iii) the agent is unable to recall any transition from a sleeping state to the present experience.
conditions: The experience possesses a phenomenal vividness that dreams characteristically lack
The experience coheres with the totality of one's other current experiences
The agent is unable to recall any transition from a sleeping state to the present experience
counterexample: Consider a lucid dreamer who, within a dream of extraordinary vividness, finds that each element of the dream environment coheres perfectly with every other — the light falls consistently, conversations follow rational patterns, and physical laws appear to hold. Moreover, since the dream began seamlessly from an apparent waking state, the dreamer has no recollection of falling asleep. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the agent is not awake.
missing_condition: The analysis omits any criterion requiring that the experience be causally produced by actual external objects rather than by the dreaming mind itself.
passage: The argument from dreaming, which one encounters in various forms from Plato through to Descartes, raises the disquieting possibility that one might at any moment be asleep and merely dreaming that one is perceiving the world. The analysis offered above attempts to specify conditions under which a perceptual experience would constitute conclusive evidence of wakefulness. It appears, upon initial reflection, to capture something important: ordinary dreams are often fragmentary, incoherent, and lacking in the vivid specificity of waking perception. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the well-documented phenomenon of lucid dreaming, in which the dreaming subject achieves a degree of awareness and experiential richness that rivals or exceeds ordinary waking life. In such episodes, the phenomenal character of the experience satisfies condition (i), its internal coherence satisfies condition (ii), and the absence of any remembered transition to sleep satisfies condition (iii). The difficulty, one might observe, lies in the fact that the proposed criteria are all internal to the experience itself and provide no external anchor by which the agent could distinguish a sufficiently elaborate dream from genuine perception. What is wanted, it seems, is a condition that reaches beyond the phenomenology of the experience to its causal provenance — but it is precisely this that the dreaming argument calls into question.

definition: A subject S has knowledge of the external world if and only if (i) S holds beliefs about mind-independent objects on the basis of sensory experience, (ii) those beliefs are true, and (iii) the sensory experiences which ground those beliefs are caused, in the normal way, by the mind-independent objects they purport to represent.
conditions: S holds beliefs about mind-independent objects on the basis of sensory experience
Those beliefs are true
The sensory experiences which ground those beliefs are caused, in the normal way, by the mind-independent objects they purport to represent
counterexample: Suppose a subject perceives a red barn in a landscape that happens to be filled with elaborate barn facades. By sheer luck, the subject is looking at the one genuine barn. Her belief that there is a barn before her is true, and her experience is caused in the normal way by an actual barn. Yet we should hesitate to ascribe knowledge, for had she looked in any other direction, she would have formed an equally confident but false belief.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the subject's belief be safe or modally robust — that is, that the subject would not easily have formed a false belief by the same method in relevantly similar circumstances.
passage: The sceptic about the external world contends that we can never possess genuine knowledge of mind-independent reality, since our only access to such reality is mediated by sensory experience, and sensory experience might, in principle, be systematically misleading. The analysis given above attempts to rebut this scepticism by specifying conditions that, if met, would constitute knowledge of external objects. It appears to capture the commonsensical view: if one's beliefs about the world are true and are produced in the standard causal fashion by the very objects they concern, then surely one knows. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in what we might call the landscape of facades. In such a scenario, the subject happens to fix her gaze upon the sole genuine barn in a county otherwise populated entirely by convincing barn facades. Each of the three conditions is satisfied: she believes there is a barn, there is indeed a barn, and her experience is caused in the perfectly ordinary manner by that barn. Nevertheless, it appears that she does not genuinely know that she sees a barn, for the epistemic environment is one in which she could very easily have been mistaken. What the analysis neglects, one might observe, is a modal dimension of knowledge — the requirement that the agent's belief-forming method be reliable not merely in the actual case but across a suitable range of nearby possible situations. The external-world sceptic may seize upon precisely this lacuna, arguing that we can never be confident that our epistemic environment is not similarly inhospitable.

definition: A subject S knows that another mind M exists if and only if (i) S observes behaviour in another organism that is relevantly similar to behaviour S would exhibit when in certain mental states, (ii) S infers by analogy that M possesses corresponding mental states, and (iii) no alternative explanation of the observed behaviour is more plausible than the attribution of mentality.
conditions: S observes behaviour in another organism that is relevantly similar to behaviour S would exhibit when in certain mental states
S infers by analogy that M possesses corresponding mental states
No alternative explanation of the observed behaviour is more plausible than the attribution of mentality
counterexample: Consider a sophisticated automaton — a robot designed to simulate human behaviour so perfectly that its responses to stimuli are indistinguishable from those of a sentient being. An observer satisfies all three conditions: the behaviour is relevantly similar, the analogical inference is natural, and no more plausible alternative explanation presents itself. Yet the automaton, by hypothesis, possesses no mental states whatsoever.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the entity whose behaviour is observed must actually possess the kind of internal constitution — biological, computational, or otherwise — that is capable of giving rise to genuine mentality.
passage: The problem of other minds has exercised philosophers at least since Descartes observed that, whilst he could be certain of his own consciousness, the inner lives of others remained forever beyond his direct inspection. The argument from analogy, which the above analysis codifies, represents the most venerable attempt at a solution: we observe that others behave as we do, and we infer that they think and feel as we do. It appears, on first consideration, to be a reasonable inference. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by imagining an artificial creature whose external behaviour perfectly mimics that of a conscious being. Such an automaton would satisfy every condition the analysis requires. Its movements would be relevantly similar to those of a person in pain, joy, or contemplation; the analogical inference would seem compelling; and absent any knowledge of the creature's internal constitution, the attribution of mentality would be at least as plausible as any rival hypothesis. Nonetheless, we should not wish to say that the observer knows another mind exists, for there is no mind to be known. One might observe that the difficulty lies in the fact that the analysis appeals exclusively to external, behavioural criteria and provides no foothold for distinguishing genuine mentality from its perfect simulacrum. The sceptic about other minds will note that if our criteria for the presence of minds are purely behavioural, then knowledge of other minds rests on foundations no stronger than the resemblance between outward deportment and inner life — a resemblance that admits of no independent verification.

definition: An inductive inference from observed instances to a general law is rationally justified if and only if (i) a sufficiently large and varied sample of instances has been observed, (ii) all observed instances conform to the proposed generalisation, and (iii) there exists no known defeater — that is, no observed instance that contravenes the generalisation.
conditions: A sufficiently large and varied sample of instances has been observed
All observed instances conform to the proposed generalisation
There exists no known defeater — that is, no observed instance that contravenes the generalisation
counterexample: Consider the generalisation, drawn from centuries of European ornithological observation, that all swans are white. Prior to the discovery of Australia, an enormous and varied sample of swans had been observed, every observed swan was white, and no swan of any other colour had ever been recorded. All three conditions were satisfied, yet the generalisation was false: black swans exist in considerable numbers in the Antipodes.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the observed sample be drawn from the full range of environments or populations over which the generalisation is intended to hold.
passage: Hume's celebrated argument concerning induction draws attention to the apparent impossibility of furnishing a non-circular justification for our practice of inferring general laws from particular observations. The analysis above endeavours to specify conditions under which such inferences would be rationally warranted. It appears, upon initial inspection, to codify the working methodology of empirical science: observe widely, confirm repeatedly, and watch for exceptions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the history of European ornithology. For centuries, naturalists observed swans throughout the breadth of Europe and recorded, without exception, that every swan was white. The sample was both large and varied, encompassing different regions, seasons, and habitats; every observed instance conformed to the generalisation; and no defeater had ever been encountered. The analysis would therefore pronounce the inference to the universal whiteness of swans rationally justified. It was, however, false, as the subsequent discovery of black swans in Australia demonstrated. One might observe that the difficulty here is not merely incidental but structural: the conditions as stated provide no assurance that the sample, however large, is representative of the domain over which the generalisation ranges. Hume's sceptic would press the point further, noting that no finite body of evidence can ever logically entail a universal proposition, and that the demand for representativeness itself presupposes knowledge we do not yet possess.

definition: A subject S has justified belief about the past if and only if (i) S possesses an apparent memory of a past event E, (ii) S has no reason to doubt the reliability of this memory, and (iii) the memory is consistent with other evidence available to S concerning the past.
conditions: S possesses an apparent memory of a past event E
S has no reason to doubt the reliability of this memory
The memory is consistent with other evidence available to S concerning the past
counterexample: Suppose the universe came into existence five minutes ago, complete with all present traces of an apparent past — fossils, documents, and the apparent memories of every living being. A subject would possess vivid memories, would have no reason to doubt them, and would find them perfectly consistent with all available evidence, since all the evidence was itself created five minutes ago. All three conditions are met, yet the subject's beliefs about the past are entirely without foundation.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the apparent memories and corroborating evidence must have been produced by the actual past events they purport to represent, rather than having come into existence independently of any such events.
passage: Scepticism about the past takes a distinctive and peculiarly troubling form, for it challenges not merely our knowledge of what has occurred but the very coherence of the evidence upon which all historical knowledge depends. The analysis above seeks to specify conditions under which beliefs about the past would be justified: the agent remembers, has no grounds for suspicion, and finds her memories concordant with other available evidence. It appears, on its face, to be a reasonable account of ordinary epistemic practice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by entertaining Russell's well-known hypothesis that the world might have been created five minutes ago, replete with every trace and vestige of a far longer history. In such a scenario, a subject would possess apparently genuine memories of a childhood, an education, and a career.
