She would have no reason to doubt these memories, for every piece of corroborating evidence — photographs, letters, the testimony of others — would likewise have been created in the same moment and would therefore cohere perfectly with her recollections. The three conditions of the analysis are all satisfied, yet the beliefs they are meant to justify are, by hypothesis, entirely baseless. One might observe that the analysis is deficient because it treats consistency among present evidence as a substitute for a genuine causal connection between present memories and past events. The sceptic about the past exploits precisely this gap, contending that no amount of present evidence can, by itself, guarantee its own provenance.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) p is true, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) S is in a position to rule out every proposition q that is known to entail not-p (the closure condition).
conditions: S believes that p
p is true
S is justified in believing that p
S is in a position to rule out every proposition q that is known to entail not-p
counterexample: Consider an ordinary person who knows that she has hands. Her belief is true, she is justified, and she satisfies conditions (i) through (iii) unproblematically. Yet condition (iv) requires that she be in a position to rule out the proposition that she is a handless brain in a vat being fed the illusion of having hands — for that proposition is known to entail that she does not have hands. Since she cannot rule this out on independent grounds, the closure condition forces the conclusion that she does not know she has hands, despite its being one of the most certain things she could claim to know.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to restrict the closure condition to relevant alternatives, thereby allowing far-fetched sceptical hypotheses to undermine even the most mundane and well-supported knowledge claims.
passage: The epistemic closure principle asserts that if one knows a proposition p, and knows that p entails q, then one is in a position to know q. Taken in its contrapositive form, it yields a powerful sceptical argument: if one cannot know that sceptical hypothesis q is false, and q entails not-p, then one does not know that p. The analysis above incorporates this closure condition as the fourth requirement for knowledge. It appears, at first blush, to express a perfectly rational demand — that the knower be able to follow through the logical consequences of what she claims to know. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is as near to hand as one's own hands. An ordinary person, sitting at her desk, believes with excellent justification that she has hands. The proposition that she is a handless brain in a vat entails that she does not have hands. Condition (iv) therefore requires that she be able to rule out the brain-in-a-vat hypothesis, which she manifestly cannot do by any empirical or a priori means available to her. The analysis thus yields the absurd result that she does not know she has hands. One might observe that the difficulty lies in the unrestricted generality of the closure condition: it permits remote and fantastic hypotheses to defeat knowledge claims that are, by any reasonable standard, as well established as any claim could be. The sceptic, of course, finds this result congenial; but it suggests that the analysis has failed to capture the concept of knowledge as it is ordinarily employed.

definition: A subject S can distinguish their actual epistemic situation from a sceptical scenario H if and only if (i) there exists some experiential or evidential difference between the actual situation and H, (ii) S has access to this difference, and (iii) S can use this difference to determine which situation they are in.
conditions: There exists some experiential or evidential difference between the actual situation and H
S has access to this difference
S can use this difference to determine which situation they are in
counterexample: Consider the brain-in-a-vat hypothesis in its strongest formulation, wherein a disembodied brain is stimulated so as to produce experiences qualitatively identical to those of an embodied person in a genuine physical world. By the very construction of the scenario, there is no experiential or evidential difference between the actual situation and the sceptical one; condition (i) is not met, yet the analysis presupposes it can be. This renders the analysis vacuously inapplicable to the very cases it was designed to address.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes that there must be some accessible difference between the actual and sceptical scenarios, thereby begging the question against the sceptic who contends that no such difference exists.
passage: The brain-in-a-vat scenario, which has become a staple of contemporary epistemology in much the way that Descartes's evil demon was a staple of its predecessors, invites us to consider the possibility that our entire experiential life is the product of artificial stimulation. The analysis above attempts to specify conditions under which an agent could distinguish her genuine situation from such a sceptical alternative. It appears to offer a straightforward approach: if there is a difference, and the agent can find it and exploit it, then the distinction can be drawn. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers the brain-in-a-vat hypothesis in its canonical form. The hypothesis is constructed precisely so that there shall be no experiential difference between the vatted brain's situation and that of an ordinary embodied agent. Every sensation, every memory, every piece of apparent evidence is, by stipulation, exactly replicated. The first condition of the analysis therefore fails, not because the analysis has been carelessly stated, but because the sceptical hypothesis is specifically designed to eliminate the very foothold the analysis requires. One might observe that the analysis is question-begging in a particularly instructive way: it assumes that distinguishing one's actual situation from a sceptical scenario is the sort of thing that can be accomplished by detecting differences, when the whole force of the sceptical argument lies in the claim that no such differences are available. The sceptic does not deny that differences would be helpful; she denies that they exist.

definition: A sceptical argument is sound if and only if (i) its premises are more plausible than their negations, (ii) its logical form is valid, and (iii) its conclusion — that we lack knowledge of the relevant domain — is more plausible than the denial of any of its premises.
conditions: Its premises are more plausible than their negations
Its logical form is valid
Its conclusion — that we lack knowledge of the relevant domain — is more plausible than the denial of any of its premises
counterexample: Consider the standard sceptical argument: if I cannot rule out that I am a brain in a vat, then I do not know I have hands; I cannot rule out that I am a brain in a vat; therefore I do not know I have hands. The argument is formally valid, and each premise, taken individually, appears plausible. Yet, following Moore, one might judge that the conclusion — that one does not know one has hands — is less plausible than the denial of the second premise. Thus condition (iii) fails: the conclusion is not more plausible than every premise-denial, and the Moorean reversal proceeds.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the possibility that the comparative plausibility of premises and conclusion might shift depending on whether one treats the argument as a reductio of the sceptic's position or as a straightforward proof, thus rendering the soundness criterion indeterminate.
passage: G. E. Moore famously responded to sceptical arguments not by identifying a flaw in their logic but by observing that, since the conclusion — that we do not know the most obvious facts about the physical world — is less certain than the premises from which it is derived, we are entitled to reject a premise rather than accept the conclusion. The analysis above attempts to specify when a sceptical argument should be regarded as sound, incorporating a plausibility constraint as its third condition. It appears, on its face, to be an admirably balanced proposal, for it recognises that validity alone does not settle the matter. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by Moore's own celebrated proof of the external world. When Moore holds up his hands and declares that he knows they exist, the plausibility ordering inverts: the conclusion of the sceptical argument becomes less credible than the denial of one of its premises. The difficulty, one might observe, is that condition (iii) treats plausibility as though it were a fixed and determinate quantity, when in fact the comparative plausibility of premises and conclusion depends upon the dialectical context and the antecedent commitments of the reasoner. The Moorean respondent and the sceptic each find a different element of the argument most resistible, and the analysis provides no principled means of adjudicating between them. What is wanted is not a static comparison of plausibilities but an account of the epistemic norms that should govern the resolution of such conflicts.

definition: A subject S knows that p if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) p is true, (iii) S is justified in believing that p, and (iv) S can rule out all relevant alternatives to p, where an alternative is relevant just in case it is a realistic possibility given S's actual circumstances.
conditions: S believes that p
p is true
S is justified in believing that p
S can rule out all relevant alternatives to p, where an alternative is relevant just in case it is a realistic possibility given S's actual circumstances
counterexample: Consider a birdwatcher who, in a region where both goldfinches and canaries are common, identifies a bird as a goldfinch on the basis of its plumage. The canary alternative is relevant and she has ruled it out by noting distinctive markings. However, suppose that in this particular region, a rare species of warbler has recently been introduced whose plumage is virtually identical to a goldfinch's. This alternative is a realistic possibility given her circumstances, yet she has never heard of the species and cannot rule it out. Her belief is true — the bird is indeed a goldfinch — but the relevant alternatives theory denies her knowledge.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to specify who determines which alternatives count as relevant — the subject, the attributor, or some objective standard — and thereby leaves the boundary of knowledge fatally vague.
passage: The relevant alternatives theory of knowledge arose in large part as a response to the perceived excesses of closure-based sceptical arguments. Its central insight is that a knower need not rule out every logically possible alternative to what she believes, but only those alternatives that are genuinely relevant — that is, those that represent realistic possibilities in the agent's actual situation. The analysis above codifies this approach by adding a fourth condition requiring the elimination of relevant alternatives. It appears, upon first consideration, to strike a sensible balance between the demands of the sceptic and the deliverances of common sense. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from ordinary perceptual identification. A birdwatcher in a region rich in both goldfinches and canaries correctly identifies a bird as a goldfinch, having ruled out the canary hypothesis by attending to specific markings. But if a nearly indistinguishable species of warbler has recently been introduced to the region, this too is a realistic possibility given her circumstances, and she cannot rule it out because she is ignorant of the species' existence. Her belief is true and well justified by the evidence available to her, yet the analysis denies her knowledge. One might observe that the difficulty is not simply the existence of an unconsidered alternative but the deeper question of how relevance is to be determined. If relevance depends on the subject's actual circumstances, then alternatives she cannot even conceive of may nonetheless be relevant, which threatens to reinstate the very scepticism the theory was designed to avoid.

definition: A knowledge attribution 'S knows that p' is true in a context C if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) p is true, (iii) S's evidence for p meets the epistemic standards operative in C, and (iv) the epistemic standards operative in C are determined by the practical interests and conversational purposes of those making the attribution.
conditions: S believes that p
p is true
S's evidence for p meets the epistemic standards operative in C
The epistemic standards operative in C are determined by the practical interests and conversational purposes of those making the attribution
counterexample: Suppose a bank customer, asked casually by a friend whether the bank is open on Saturday, says 'I know it is — I was there last Saturday.' In this low-stakes context, the attribution seems true. But now consider a philosopher in a seminar who, discussing the very same customer and the very same evidence, says 'She does not really know the bank is open, since she cannot rule out a change in hours.' The contextualist analysis implies that both attributions are true in their respective contexts, yet this seems to entail that there is no fact of the matter about whether the customer knows — a conclusion that sits uncomfortably with the ordinary conception of knowledge as a determinate relation between a subject and a proposition.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects to provide a criterion for when a shift in conversational context constitutes a genuine change in epistemic standards rather than a mere change in the looseness or strictness with which a single fixed standard is applied.
passage: Contextualism about knowledge attributions holds that the truth conditions of sentences of the form 'S knows that p' vary with the context in which the attribution is made. In conversational settings where little is at stake and sceptical possibilities are not salient, the standards for knowledge are comparatively modest; in philosophical discussions or high-stakes practical deliberations, they become more exacting. The analysis above attempts to codify this view by relativising the epistemic standards in condition (iii) to the practical interests and conversational purposes of the attributors in condition (iv). It appears, at first glance, to provide an elegant dissolution of the sceptical problem: the sceptic and the ordinary person are simply operating with different standards, and each is correct relative to her own context. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers two attributors evaluating the same subject and the same evidence in contexts with differing standards. The casual friend and the seminar philosopher reach opposite verdicts on whether the customer knows the bank is open on Saturday, and the analysis pronounces both verdicts true. One might observe that this result is deeply problematic, for it suggests that knowledge is not a genuine property of the subject but an artefact of the attributor's conversational situation. It is one thing to say that the word 'knows' is used more or less strictly in different contexts; it is quite another to say that the underlying epistemic relation itself shifts with the interests of distant conversationalists who may never interact with the subject. The analysis thus appears to confuse the semantics of knowledge talk with the metaphysics of knowledge itself.

definition: A sceptic is a Pyrrhonian if and only if (i) the sceptic suspends judgement on all propositions regarding how things really are, (ii) the sceptic does not assert that knowledge is impossible, and (iii) the sceptic lives by appearances without committing to any theoretical beliefs about the nature of reality.
conditions: The sceptic suspends judgement on all propositions regarding how things really are
The sceptic does not assert that knowledge is impossible
The sceptic lives by appearances without committing to any theoretical beliefs about the nature of reality
counterexample: Consider a sceptic who suspends judgement on all first-order propositions about reality and refrains from asserting that knowledge is impossible, yet who holds, as a matter of firm theoretical conviction, that equipollence — the equal balance of opposing arguments — is an objective feature of the dialectical landscape rather than merely how things appear to her. She satisfies conditions (i) and (ii), and she lives by appearances in practical matters, yet her commitment to the objectivity of equipollence is itself a theoretical belief about the nature of reality, violating condition (iii) in a manner the analysis fails to detect because it does not distinguish between first-order and higher-order theoretical commitments.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the suspension of judgement and the commitment to appearances extend reflexively to the sceptic's own methodological principles, rather than applying only to first-order propositions about the external world.
passage: The distinction between Pyrrhonian and Academic scepticism, which Sextus Empiricus took such pains to articulate, turns on the difference between suspending judgement and denying the possibility of knowledge. The Academic sceptic, as characterised in antiquity, holds that nothing can be known; the Pyrrhonian, by contrast, declines even this much commitment, preferring to suspend judgement on the question of whether knowledge is possible. The analysis above attempts to capture the Pyrrhonian position by specifying three conditions: suspension of judgement, refusal to assert impossibility, and practical reliance upon appearances. It appears, on first examination, to delineate the position with reasonable precision. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers the status of the Pyrrhonian's own methodological commitments. A sceptic who satisfies all three conditions with respect to ordinary propositions about the world may nonetheless harbour a theoretical conviction about the nature of sceptical enquiry itself — for instance, the belief that for every argument there is an equally compelling counter-argument, understood not as an appearance but as a truth about the structure of rational discourse. Such a conviction would constitute a theoretical belief about the nature of reality, broadly conceived, yet the analysis as stated does not clearly demand that the sceptic's suspension extend to these higher-order methodological principles. One might observe that Pyrrhonism, if it is to be internally consistent, must apply its own medicine to itself, and the analysis fails to ensure this reflexive application.

definition: A belief qualifies as certain knowledge if and only if (i) the proposition believed is true, (ii) the subject cannot conceive of any circumstances under which the proposition would be false, and (iii) the subject's confidence in the proposition is maximal and unshakeable.
conditions: The proposition believed is true
The subject cannot conceive of any circumstances under which the proposition would be false
The subject's confidence in the proposition is maximal and unshakeable
counterexample: Consider a subject in the seventeenth century who believes with maximal and unshakeable confidence that Euclidean geometry is the one true geometry of physical space. She cannot conceive of any circumstances under which it would be false, for non-Euclidean geometries have not yet been developed. The proposition, as applied to physical space, is in fact false — space, as we now understand it, is non-Euclidean in the presence of mass. Condition (i) is not met, but the more instructive point is that conditions (ii) and (iii) are fully satisfied despite the proposition's falsehood, revealing that inconceivability and psychological unshakeability are no guarantee of truth.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the subject's inability to conceive of falsifying circumstances be grounded in the logical or metaphysical character of the proposition rather than in the limitations of the subject's imagination or the state of contemporary knowledge.
passage: The relationship between scepticism and certainty is among the most enduring themes in epistemology. Descartes sought a foundation of certainty immune to every possible doubt; his sceptical method was the instrument by which all merely probable beliefs were to be stripped away, leaving only what could not be doubted. The analysis above attempts to characterise what it would be for a belief to constitute certain knowledge, requiring truth, inconceivability of falsehood, and maximal psychological confidence. It appears, at first blush, to capture the Cartesian aspiration: a belief that could not, even in principle, be otherwise, and of which the subject is perfectly assured. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of mathematics. A seventeenth-century geometer, schooled in Euclid, would have found it inconceivable that space might possess a non-Euclidean structure. Her confidence would have been maximal; she could not have imagined any alternative. Yet the proposition she held — that physical space is Euclidean — turned out to be false. One might observe that the difficulty lies in treating inconceivability as an epistemic criterion, for what a subject can conceive depends not upon the nature of the proposition but upon the conceptual resources available to her. The sceptic will note with some satisfaction that certainty, so analysed, is a psychological state that floats free of truth, and that the demand for certainty is therefore either unsatisfiable or trivially satisfiable by sufficiently confident but mistaken believers.

definition: A belief B is adequately justified if and only if (i) B is supported by a reason R1, (ii) R1 is itself supported by a further reason R2, (iii) this chain of reasons terminates in a foundational belief that is self-justifying or self-evident, and (iv) no link in the chain is circular.
conditions: B is supported by a reason R1
R1 is itself supported by a further reason R2
This chain of reasons terminates in a foundational belief that is self-justifying or self-evident
No link in the chain is circular
counterexample: Consider a subject who believes that she is in pain. This belief is typically regarded as self-justifying: it does not require further support. Suppose she then reasons from this to the belief that her body is damaged, and from that to the belief that she should seek medical attention. The chain terminates in a self-justifying belief and contains no circularity. Yet the inference from 'I am in pain' to 'my body is damaged' is defeasible — phantom limb pain, for example, involves pain without bodily damage — so the chain as a whole may be unjustified despite satisfying all four conditions.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the inferential connections between successive links in the chain be themselves justified or truth-preserving, rather than merely present.
passage: The regress of justification, which Sextus Empiricus wielded as a weapon against the dogmatists, poses a fundamental challenge to the possibility of justified belief. If every belief requires justification by a further belief, the chain of justification either extends infinitely, circles back upon itself, or terminates in a belief that requires no further justification. The analysis above endorses the foundationalist solution: the chain terminates in a self-justifying belief, and no circularity is permitted. It appears, upon initial reflection, to provide a tidy resolution. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one examines not the terminal belief but the inferential connections between the links. A subject who begins from the genuinely self-justifying belief that she is in pain and infers from this that her body must be damaged satisfies all four conditions: there is a supporting reason, the chain terminates in a self-evident belief, and no circularity is involved. However, the inference itself is problematic, for pain does not entail bodily damage. One might observe that the analysis attends exclusively to the structural properties of the justificatory chain — its termination point and its freedom from circularity — whilst ignoring the quality of the inferential links that compose it. The sceptic may exploit this gap by noting that even a chain anchored in self-evidence can transmit no justification if its links are defective, and that the analysis provides no resources for evaluating those links. The regress problem, it seems, is not solved merely by finding a stopping place; one must also ensure that the path from the stopping place to the belief in question is epistemically sound.

definition: A sceptical challenge is merely local if and only if (i) it targets beliefs within a specific domain D while leaving beliefs in other domains unchallenged, (ii) the grounds for doubt are specific to the features of D and do not generalise to other domains, and (iii) there exists at least one domain whose beliefs the challenge leaves entirely intact.
conditions: It targets beliefs within a specific domain D while leaving beliefs in other domains unchallenged
The grounds for doubt are specific to the features of D and do not generalise to other domains
There exists at least one domain whose beliefs the challenge leaves entirely intact
counterexample: Consider scepticism about perceptual knowledge, which appears local: it targets beliefs formed through sensory experience and seems to leave mathematical and logical beliefs untouched. However, if one's reason for doubting perceptual beliefs is that one might be systematically deceived by a malicious agent, this ground for doubt generalises naturally to mathematical beliefs — for the deceiver might corrupt one's reasoning as well. Condition (ii) fails, yet the challenge was initially presented as domain-specific, and the analysis provides no means of detecting that its grounds will metastasise.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects to require an independent demonstration that the grounds for doubt are genuinely contained within the target domain, rather than merely appearing so upon initial presentation.
passage: The distinction between local and global scepticism is of considerable philosophical importance, for it determines whether a sceptical challenge threatens the entirety of our epistemic edifice or merely a portion of it. A local sceptic questions our knowledge in a particular area — perception, testimony, or memory, for instance — whilst granting that knowledge is attainable elsewhere. The analysis above attempts to characterise local scepticism by three conditions: domain-specificity of target, domain-specificity of grounds, and the existence of at least one untouched domain. It appears, upon first consideration, to draw the distinction clearly. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the seemingly local challenge to perceptual knowledge. When one enquires into the basis of doubt about perception, one frequently discovers that the grounds — the possibility of systematic deception, the underdetermination of theory by evidence, or the fallibility of human cognitive faculties — are not in fact confined to the perceptual domain. The hypothesis of a malicious deceiver, for example, which is introduced to cast doubt on perceptual beliefs, extends with equal force to beliefs in mathematics, logic, and metaphysics, for the deceiver's powers are limited only by logical possibility. One might observe that what begins as an apparently local challenge has a natural tendency to become global, and that the analysis provides no criterion for determining whether the grounds of doubt are genuinely containable. The boundary between local and global scepticism is thus far less stable than the analysis suggests, and the sceptic may exploit this instability to parlay a modest doubt into a comprehensive one.

definition: A subject S is justified in believing a proposition p on the basis of testimony if and only if (i) a testifier T asserts that p, (ii) S has no specific reason to regard T as untrustworthy, and (iii) the content of T's testimony is not intrinsically implausible given S's background beliefs.
conditions: A testifier T asserts that p
S has no specific reason to regard T as untrustworthy
The content of T's testimony is not intrinsically implausible given S's background beliefs
counterexample: Consider a subject who receives testimony from a stranger that a certain road is safe to travel. She has no specific reason to regard the stranger as untrustworthy, and the claim is not intrinsically implausible. All three conditions are satisfied. However, the stranger, though sincere, is speaking of a different road with a similar name, and the road in question is in fact extremely dangerous. The subject's belief, formed on the basis of this testimony, is unjustified despite meeting the analysis's requirements.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the testifier's assertion be about the same state of affairs the subject takes it to be about, and more broadly, that the communicative exchange be free from relevant misunderstanding.
passage: The epistemology of testimony is a subject that has received increasing attention in recent decades, though its roots may be traced at least to Reid's defence of a principle of credulity. The sceptic about testimony contends that we are never justified in accepting the word of another, since we have no independent access to the facts the testifier reports. The analysis above attempts to resist this scepticism by specifying conditions under which testimonial belief is justified. It appears, on its surface, to capture the default position of ordinary epistemic practice: we accept what others tell us unless we have reason not to and unless what they say strikes us as implausible. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when the testimony is sincerely given but concerns a different state of affairs from the one the hearer takes it to concern. A stranger who says that a particular road is safe may be speaking truthfully of a road she has in mind, while the hearer, through an innocent confusion, applies the testimony to a quite different road. Every condition of the analysis is met from the hearer's perspective — the assertion is made, no grounds for distrust are present, and the claim is not implausible — yet the resulting belief is unjustified because it rests on a misidentification. One might observe that the analysis treats testimony as a simple transfer of information and neglects the many ways in which the communicative context can introduce distortions that neither party intends. The sceptic about testimony will note that such possibilities of miscommunication are endemic and largely undetectable from within the testimonial exchange.

definition: A subject S has a justified belief about a past event E on the basis of memory if and only if (i) S has an apparent memory of E, (ii) the apparent memory was originally formed through a reliable cognitive process, and (iii) the memory has not been significantly altered or distorted since its formation.
conditions: S has an apparent memory of E
The apparent memory was originally formed through a reliable cognitive process
The memory has not been significantly altered or distorted since its formation
counterexample: Consider a subject who witnessed a minor traffic accident. Her original perception was accurate and formed through a reliable process. Over the following weeks, she discussed the event with other witnesses, and through the well-documented phenomenon of memory conformity, her recollection came to incorporate details from their accounts — details that are false but that she now sincerely recalls as part of her own experience. Her memory has been altered, but the alteration is not one she can detect: subjectively, her recollection appears seamless and undistorted. Condition (iii) is violated, but since the subject has no way of knowing this, the analysis provides no guidance as to whether she is justified from her own perspective.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the requirement that the subject have some means, however fallible, of monitoring or detecting whether their memories have been altered, so that the justificatory status of the belief can be assessed from within them epistemic situation.
passage: Scepticism about memory strikes at the very heart of our epistemic lives, for an enormous proportion of what we claim to know rests upon what we recall rather than upon what we presently perceive. The analysis above attempts to specify conditions under which memory-based beliefs are justified, requiring that the memory be genuine, reliably formed, and undistorted. It appears, at first glance, to be a sensible externalist account: if the causal history of the memory is in good order, then the belief it supports is justified. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the phenomenon of memory conformity, which has been extensively studied by psychologists. When a subject discusses a remembered event with others, her recollection is liable to absorb details from their reports, producing a composite memory that she cannot distinguish from her original experience. In such a case, condition (iii) is violated — the memory has been altered — but the violation is invisible to the subject. One might observe that the analysis is deficient in a way that parallels a familiar difficulty with externalist theories of justification more generally: it locates the justificatory conditions entirely in the causal history of the belief and provides no resources for the subject to assess, from her own epistemic vantage point, whether those conditions are met. The sceptic about memory will seize upon this, arguing that if we cannot tell from the inside whether our memories have been corrupted, then we can never be justified in trusting them — precisely the sceptical conclusion the analysis was designed to forestall.

definition: A subject S has justified moral belief that action A is wrong if and only if (i) S believes that A is wrong on the basis of a coherent moral framework, (ii) this framework is endorsed by a significant portion of reflective moral agents, and (iii) S has subjected the belief to critical scrutiny and found no compelling objection.
conditions: S believes that A is wrong on the basis of a coherent moral framework
This framework is endorsed by a significant portion of reflective moral agents
S has subjected the belief to critical scrutiny and found no compelling objection
counterexample: Consider a nineteenth-century moralist who believes that racial segregation is permissible. Her moral framework — which includes notions of social order, natural hierarchy, and communal welfare as she understands them — is internally coherent. The framework is endorsed by a very significant portion of the reflective moral agents of her time and place. She has subjected the belief to critical scrutiny as she understands it and has found no compelling objection, for the objections that would later become decisive have not yet gained currency. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the belief is one we should wish to call unjustified — indeed, egregiously so.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the moral framework itself be responsive to considerations of impartiality, human dignity, or some other substantive moral constraint, rather than merely internally coherent and socially endorsed.
passage: Moral scepticism of the epistemic variety does not deny that moral propositions have truth values but questions whether we are ever justified in our moral beliefs. The analysis above attempts to specify conditions for justified moral belief, appealing to coherence, social endorsement, and critical scrutiny. It appears, upon initial examination, to capture much of what we ordinarily regard as responsible moral reasoning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the moral convictions of past ages. A thoughtful and reflective individual in a slaveholding society might well have believed, on the basis of a coherent moral framework endorsed by the preponderance of her peers, that certain forms of racial subjugation were morally permissible or even required. She might have considered and dismissed the objections available to her, finding them unpersuasive by the lights of her framework. Every condition of the analysis is satisfied, yet we should wish to say that her moral belief was not merely false but unjustified — that she ought to have seen what she failed to see. One might observe that the difficulty lies in the analysis's reliance on coherence and consensus as surrogates for moral truth. A framework can be internally coherent whilst being substantively monstrous, and a belief can enjoy widespread endorsement amongst reflective agents who share a common blindness. The moral sceptic will note that if our best account of justified moral belief permits the justification of abhorrent conclusions, then either the account is defective or the sceptical worry that we cannot reliably distinguish justified from unjustified moral beliefs has more force than we had supposed.

definition: An empirical hypothesis H is confirmed by a body of evidence E if and only if (i) E is logically consistent with H, (ii) E would be expected if H were true, and (iii) no rival hypothesis H* is equally consistent with E and equally predictive of E.
conditions: E is logically consistent with H
E would be expected if H were true
No rival hypothesis H* is equally consistent with E and equally predictive of E
counterexample: Consider the hypothesis that the earth orbits the sun and the rival hypothesis that the earth is stationary whilst the heavens rotate around it, with all observable motions adjusted accordingly. For a pre-telescopic observer, both hypotheses are logically consistent with the evidence, and both predict the observed motions of the celestial bodies with equal accuracy. Condition (iii) fails — there is a rival hypothesis equally consistent and predictive — and the analysis therefore pronounces the heliocentric hypothesis unconfirmed, despite its being true and despite its possessing theoretical virtues (simplicity, unifying power) that the geocentric hypothesis lacks.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects to include theoretical virtues such as simplicity, explanatory unification, and ontological parsimony as criteria for preferring one hypothesis over an empirically equivalent rival.
passage: The underdetermination of theory by evidence is one of the most powerful instruments in the sceptic's arsenal. If two hypotheses are equally consistent with all available evidence and equally predictive of future observations, then, the sceptic contends, we have no rational basis for preferring one to the other. The analysis above attempts to codify when evidence confirms a hypothesis, including a condition that no equally good rival exists. It appears, at first blush, to be a reasonable formalisation of confirmatory reasoning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the historical competition between geocentric and heliocentric astronomy. Prior to the development of the telescope and the discovery of stellar parallax, the two hypotheses were empirically equivalent: both accounted for the observed motions of the heavenly bodies with comparable accuracy, and neither predicted any phenomenon the other could not accommodate. The analysis would therefore declare the evidence insufficient to confirm either hypothesis over the other. Yet most philosophers of science would maintain that even at this stage, the heliocentric hypothesis enjoyed a measure of rational support that the geocentric hypothesis lacked, by virtue of its greater simplicity, its capacity to unify disparate phenomena, and its avoidance of ad hoc epicyclic constructions. One might observe that the analysis restricts itself to the logical and predictive relations between evidence and hypothesis and takes no account of the theoretical virtues that scientists routinely invoke when choosing among empirically equivalent theories. The sceptic about underdetermination will argue that such virtues are merely aesthetic preferences rather than truth-tracking features, but even this argument concedes that the analysis as stated is incomplete.

definition: A subject S means addition by the symbol '+' if and only if (i) S's past uses of '+' have been consistent with the addition function, (ii) S has an inner state — a mental content or disposition — that determines that '+' refers to addition rather than to any alternative function, and (iii) S would, if presented with novel cases, apply '+' in accordance with the addition function.
conditions: S's past uses of '+' have been consistent with the addition function
S has an inner state — a mental content or disposition — that determines that '+' refers to addition rather than to any alternative function
S would, if presented with novel cases, apply '+' in accordance with the addition function
counterexample: Consider Kripke's quus function, defined so that x quus y = x + y for all cases where x and y are less than 57, but x quus y = 5 for all other cases. Every past use the subject has made of '+' has involved numbers less than 57 and is therefore equally consistent with quaddition as with addition. The subject may possess a disposition, but dispositions are finite and fallible — they do not determine a unique infinite function. And the counterfactual in condition (iii) is itself in dispute: the sceptic asks what fact determines that the subject would add rather than quadd in novel cases. All three conditions are either met by both functions or are indeterminate between them.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to specify a fact — neither dispositional, behavioural, nor phenomenological — that could determine meaning uniquely, as required to answer the sceptical challenge about rule-following.
passage: The sceptical paradox concerning rule-following, which Kripke extracted from Wittgenstein's later writings, poses a challenge not to our knowledge of the external world but to the determinacy of meaning itself. If no fact about a speaker's past usage, mental states, or dispositions can distinguish her meaning addition by '+' from her meaning some deviant function that coincides with addition over the cases so far encountered but diverges elsewhere, then, the sceptic contends, there is no fact about what she means. The analysis above attempts to resist this scepticism by specifying three conditions that would constitute the speaker's meaning addition. It appears, upon first examination, to marshal the obvious candidates: past usage, inner states, and dispositions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by Kripke's celebrated quus function. Every computation the speaker has performed is consistent with her meaning quus rather than plus, for the two functions coincide over the domain she has explored. Her inner states, whatever they may be, are finite and do not, by themselves, determine a unique function over infinitely many arguments. And her dispositions are equally compatible with both functions, since she has not yet encountered a case where they diverge. One might observe that the difficulty is not merely that the analysis fails to pick out addition uniquely but that no analysis of this form — appealing to past usage, inner states, or dispositions — could do so, for the sceptic's challenge is directed at the very notion that there are facts about meaning of the sort the analysis presupposes.

definition: A subject S is rationally justified in expecting the future to resemble the past if and only if (i) S has observed a regular conjunction of events of type A followed by events of type B across a wide range of circumstances, (ii) S has no evidence that the underlying causal mechanisms have changed, and (iii) the regularity A-then-B admits of a plausible causal explanation.
conditions: S has observed a regular conjunction of events of type A followed by events of type B across a wide range of circumstances
S has no evidence that the underlying causal mechanisms have changed
The regularity A-then-B admits of a plausible causal explanation
counterexample: Consider the observation, made for billions of years, that the sun has risen each morning. The conjunction has been observed across an immense range of circumstances, there is no evidence that the relevant mechanisms have changed, and astrophysics provides a causal explanation. All three conditions are abundantly satisfied. Yet astrophysics also tells us that the sun will eventually cease to rise — it will exhaust its fuel and the earth will be consumed or cast adrift. The very causal explanation that satisfies condition (iii) entails that the regularity will eventually fail, yet the analysis counts the expectation as justified without qualification.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects to require that the causal explanation invoked in condition (iii) not itself predict the eventual breakdown of the observed regularity, or at minimum that the justification be qualified by temporal scope.
passage: The problem of induction, which Hume bequeathed to subsequent generations of philosophers, calls into question our right to expect that future events will conform to past regularities. The analysis above attempts to specify conditions under which such expectations are rationally justified, supplementing the Humean observation of constant conjunction with the requirements that underlying mechanisms be unchanged and that a causal explanation be available. It appears, upon first consideration, to improve upon bare induction by incorporating explanatory considerations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the most familiar regularity of all: the daily rising of the sun. For billions of years, this conjunction has held without exception, and modern astrophysics furnishes a detailed causal account of why it occurs. No evidence suggests that the relevant mechanisms have recently changed. All three conditions are satisfied in an exemplary fashion. Yet the very causal account that satisfies condition (iii) also entails that the sun is a finite resource and that the regularity will eventually cease. One might observe that the analysis treats a causal explanation as though it guaranteed the perpetuation of the regularity it explains, when in fact a thorough causal explanation may reveal the conditions under which the regularity will break down. The sceptic about induction will note that every natural regularity, however well explained, is contingent upon conditions that may alter, and that the analysis provides no means of assessing how long the justification it confers may be expected to last. The difficulty, it seems, is not that causal explanations are irrelevant to inductive inference but that they do not play the simple confirmatory role the analysis assigns them.

definition: A criterion of truth C is epistemically adequate if and only if (i) C can be stated without presupposing the truth of any proposition whose truth depends on C, (ii) C is applicable to the full range of propositions it purports to adjudicate, and (iii) the correctness of C can be verified independently of C itself.
conditions: C can be stated without presupposing the truth of any proposition whose truth depends on C
C is applicable to the full range of propositions it purports to adjudicate
The correctness of C can be verified independently of C itself
counterexample: Consider the criterion that a proposition is true if and only if it is clearly and distinctly perceived. One can state this criterion without presupposing the truth of any particular clearly and distinctly perceived proposition, satisfying condition (i). The criterion appears applicable to the full range of propositions one might entertain, satisfying condition (ii). But to verify that clear and distinct perception is a reliable guide to truth, one must rely upon some further criterion — and the correctness of that further criterion must itself be verified, generating a regress. Condition (iii) cannot be satisfied without invoking another criterion that faces the same difficulty.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to address the regress that arises when one demands independent verification of each criterion, offering no account of how the chain of verification could be brought to a non-circular and non-arbitrary halt.
passage: The problem of the criterion, which the ancient sceptics deployed with devastating effect, asks how we can identify truths without first possessing a criterion of truth, and how we can validate a criterion of truth without already knowing which propositions are true. The analysis above attempts to specify conditions under which a criterion of truth would be epistemically adequate, imposing requirements of non-circularity, broad applicability, and independent verifiability. It appears, on its face, to set a reasonable standard. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the Cartesian criterion of clear and distinct perception. Descartes proposed that whatever one clearly and distinctly perceives is true, and in stating this criterion he does not obviously presuppose the truth of any particular clear and distinct perception. The criterion is apparently applicable to any proposition one might consider. But condition (iii) demands that the criterion's correctness be verifiable independently of the criterion itself, and here the analysis collapses under its own weight. For any attempt to verify that clear and distinct perception tracks truth must employ some standard of verification — some further criterion — and that further criterion must itself be independently verified, and so on without end. One might observe that the analysis has incorporated a demand — independent verification — that no criterion can satisfy in a non-circular manner, for every act of verification presupposes some evaluative standard. The sceptic will conclude that the problem of the criterion is insoluble, and that the analysis, by making its conditions jointly unsatisfiable, has inadvertently demonstrated this very point.

definition: A response to scepticism is pragmatically adequate if and only if (i) it demonstrates that sceptical doubt has no practical consequences for the conduct of inquiry or daily life, (ii) it shows that the beliefs challenged by the sceptic are indispensable for any coherent practice, and (iii) it establishes that the absence of practical consequences renders the sceptical doubt theoretically idle and therefore dismissible.
conditions: It demonstrates that sceptical doubt has no practical consequences for the conduct of inquiry or daily life
It shows that the beliefs challenged by the sceptic are indispensable for any coherent practice
It establishes that the absence of practical consequences renders the sceptical doubt theoretically idle and therefore dismissible
counterexample: Consider the Humean observation that inductive scepticism has no practical consequences: no one ceases to expect the sun to rise tomorrow, regardless of the sceptic's arguments. The doubt is practically inert, and inductive beliefs are indispensable for every form of rational conduct. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the sceptic may reply that the question at issue is not whether we can refrain from believing but whether our beliefs are justified, and that the practical indispensability of a belief provides no evidence whatsoever of its truth or rational warrant.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates the practical indispensability of a belief with its epistemic justification, failing to require any account of how practical necessity could confer or constitute rational warrant for a belief's truth.
passage: Pragmatic responses to scepticism, which find their roots in the writings of Hume, James, and Peirce, seek to dissolve rather than to refute the sceptic's challenge by showing that sceptical doubt makes no practical difference. If the sceptic's conclusions, even if accepted, would leave our conduct and our enquiries entirely unchanged, then, the pragmatist contends, the doubt is empty and may be set aside. The analysis above attempts to codify this strategy by specifying three conditions for a pragmatically adequate response. It appears, on first consideration, to capture the pragmatist's central insight: beliefs that are indispensable for practice and whose denial alters nothing cannot be genuinely doubtful. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the very example the pragmatist most frequently invokes. Hume observed that the sceptic about induction continues, upon leaving his study, to expect bread to nourish and fire to burn. The doubt has no practical consequences, and inductive beliefs are indispensable. But the sceptic's question was never whether we could live without inductive beliefs; it was whether those beliefs are rationally justified. One might observe that the analysis substitutes a practical criterion for an epistemic one: it shows that we cannot help believing, not that we are right to believe. The sceptic may cheerfully grant that her doubts are practically idle — indeed, the Pyrrhonians made much of the compatibility of their scepticism with ordinary life — whilst insisting that the theoretical question of justification remains entirely open. What the analysis lacks, it seems, is a bridge principle connecting practical necessity with epistemic warrant, and it is far from clear that any such principle can be supplied without begging the very question at issue.

definition: The problem of the criterion is solved if and only if (i) one identifies a set of particular knowledge claims that are antecedently credible, (ii) one derives from these a general criterion of knowledge, and (iii) the criterion so derived validates precisely the particular knowledge claims from which it was derived, without circularity.
conditions: One identifies a set of particular knowledge claims that are antecedently credible
One derives from these a general criterion of knowledge
The criterion so derived validates precisely the particular knowledge claims from which it was derived, without circularity
counterexample: Consider the particularist strategy of Chisholm, who begins with cases we are confident we know — that we have hands, that there is a desk before us — and derives from these a general criterion. The criterion, applied back to the original cases, validates them. But this method satisfies conditions (i) through (iii) even if the antecedently credible claims are all deliverances of a particular culture or tradition that happen to cohere yet are systematically mistaken — as, for instance, the confident astronomical beliefs of the Ptolemaic tradition. The analysis cannot distinguish between a virtuous circle grounded in genuine knowledge and a vicious one grounded in shared error.
missing_condition: The analysis provides no independent constraint ensuring that the antecedently credible claims from which the criterion is derived are themselves actually true or reliably produced, rather than merely widely accepted or intuitively compelling.
passage: The problem of the criterion, in its classical formulation, asks whether we must first know particular truths in order to derive a general criterion of knowledge, or whether we must first possess a criterion in order to identify particular truths. Chisholm's particularist response begins with cases of putative knowledge that strike us as antecedently credible and works outward to a general principle. The analysis above codifies this approach, requiring that the criterion be derived from antecedently credible cases and that it validate precisely those cases. It appears, upon first examination, to chart a coherent path between the horns of the dilemma. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by imagining a community whose antecedently credible claims are systematically in error. The Ptolemaic astronomers, for instance, regarded as antecedently credible a range of astronomical propositions — that the earth is stationary, that the heavenly bodies move in perfect circles, and so forth. From these they derived criteria of astronomical knowledge (coherence with the model, predictive accuracy within certain limits) that validated the very claims from which they were derived. The analysis's three conditions are all satisfied, yet the resulting criterion is built upon false foundations. One might observe that the circularity here is not merely formal but substantive: the criterion inherits whatever errors are present in the initial cases, and the mutual validation between criterion and cases creates the illusion of security without providing any genuine epistemic purchase. The sceptic will note that the particularist method, as the analysis presents it, lacks any external check — any point at which reality, as opposed to the community's confident assumptions, constrains the outcome.

definition: A doubt is rationally warranted if and only if (i) it is based on a specific ground or reason, rather than on a mere psychological inclination to disbelieve, (ii) the ground cited is logically relevant to the truth or falsity of the doubted proposition, and (iii) the ground has not been conclusively rebutted by available evidence.
conditions: It is based on a specific ground or reason, rather than on a mere psychological inclination to disbelieve
The ground cited is logically relevant to the truth or falsity of the doubted proposition
The ground has not been conclusively rebutted by available evidence
counterexample: Consider a conspiracy theorist who doubts the safety of a well-tested vaccine. He cites as his ground the logical possibility that the testing data have been fabricated — a possibility that is logically relevant to the truth of the safety claim. This ground has not been conclusively rebutted, in the strict sense that no finite body of evidence can logically exclude the possibility of wholesale fabrication. All three conditions are satisfied, yet we should hesitate to call his doubt rationally warranted, for the ground he invokes, while logically relevant, is practically negligible and is not proportionate to the overwhelming evidence in favour of the vaccine's safety.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to require that the ground for doubt be proportionate in evidential weight to the evidence supporting the doubted proposition, rather than merely logically relevant and unrefuted.
passage: The distinction between rational scepticism and irrational doubt is of considerable importance both in philosophy and in the conduct of ordinary life. We do not wish to say that every doubt is equally warranted; the person who doubts the safety of a bridge because an engineer has identified a structural flaw is in a different epistemic position from the person who doubts it because of a vague anxiety. The analysis above attempts to distinguish rational from irrational doubt by requiring a specific ground, logical relevance, and the absence of conclusive rebuttal. It appears, upon first consideration, to draw the line in a plausible place. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers a doubt based on the bare logical possibility of systematic deception. A person who doubts a well-established scientific finding on the ground that the entire body of supporting evidence might have been fabricated satisfies all three conditions: he has a specific ground (the possibility of fabrication), it is logically relevant (fabricated evidence would not support the conclusion), and it has not been conclusively rebutted (logical possibility cannot be eliminated by empirical evidence). Nonetheless, we should be reluctant to call this doubt rational, for it fails to take account of the overwhelming preponderance of evidence on the other side. One might observe that the analysis treats logical relevance and absence of rebuttal as sufficient for rational doubt, when what is required is a more demanding standard: that the ground for doubt carry evidential weight commensurate with the evidence favouring the proposition. Without this proportionality requirement, the analysis licenses a form of scepticism that is indistinguishable, in its practical effects, from the irrational doubt it was designed to exclude. The upshot, it seems, is that the line between rational scepticism and mere obstinacy cannot be drawn by logical criteria alone; it requires a sensitivity to evidential weight that the analysis does not provide.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) there exists a fact F in the world, (ii) P stands in a relation of structural correspondence to F such that the constituents of P map onto the constituents of F, and (iii) the arrangement of the constituents in P mirrors the arrangement of the constituents in F.
conditions: There exists a fact F in the world
P stands in a relation of structural correspondence to F such that the constituents of P map onto the constituents of F
The arrangement of the constituents in P mirrors the arrangement of the constituents in F
counterexample: Consider the true proposition that there are no unicorns. There is no positive fact in the world to which this negative existential corresponds, nor any arrangement of constituents to be mirrored. The proposition is true precisely because something is absent, yet the analysis demands the presence of a structured fact.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to account for negative existential truths, where the truth of a proposition consists not in correspondence with an existing fact but in the absence of any fact of a certain kind.
passage: One of the most venerable accounts of truth holds that a proposition is true when it corresponds to a fact, and false when no such correspondence obtains. On this view, the world furnishes a domain of facts, and truth consists in a structural mirroring between the proposition and the relevant fact, so that the components of the one may be placed in systematic alignment with the components of the other. It appears, however, that this analysis encounters a serious difficulty when one considers propositions of a negative existential character. Suppose we affirm that there are no unicorns. This proposition, we should all agree, is true. Yet it is far from clear what fact in the world it might be said to correspond to. There is no entity, no arrangement of objects, which constitutes the fact of there being no unicorns; rather, the truth of the proposition seems to consist precisely in the absence of any such entity. One might observe that the correspondence theorist is thus compelled either to postulate curious negative facts — facts whose esse is, so to speak, a form of non-esse — or to concede that the analysis as stated does not capture all the cases. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is therefore furnished by the humble negative existential, which is true not by virtue of matching something that is there, but by virtue of there being nothing of the relevant sort to match.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) P belongs to a system of propositions S, (ii) P is consistent with every other member of S, and (iii) P is entailed by the conjunction of sufficiently many other members of S such that removing P would diminish the overall coherence of S.
conditions: P belongs to a system of propositions S
P is consistent with every other member of S
P is entailed by the conjunction of sufficiently many other members of S such that removing P would diminish the overall coherence of S
counterexample: Consider a comprehensive and internally coherent system of propositions constituting a well-constructed work of fantasy fiction. Every proposition in the system is consistent with every other, and each is entailed by the others. Yet the propositions of this fiction are not true. Coherence is perfectly achieved, but truth is entirely absent.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the system S must itself be anchored to reality or to some criterion external to mere internal consistency, so that coherence alone cannot manufacture truth from wholecloth.
passage: The coherence theory of truth maintains that to be true is nothing other than to occupy a fitting place within a body of mutually supporting propositions. On this account, the truth of any single proposition is not determined by its relation to some extra-propositional reality, but by its relations of consistency and mutual entailment with the other members of the system to which it belongs. One might observe that this analysis possesses a certain elegance: it avoids the obscurities sometimes attendant upon the notion of correspondence, and it makes the concept of truth an affair wholly internal to our system of beliefs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed without great difficulty. Let us suppose that a novelist of considerable ingenuity has devised a fictional world whose internal history, geography, and natural laws are described with perfect consistency. Every proposition belonging to this fictional system coheres with every other; the removal of any single proposition would diminish the unity of the whole. The conditions of the analysis are thus satisfied in full. Nevertheless, we should not wish to say that the propositions of this fiction are true, for they describe a world that does not exist. The coherence theorist appears, therefore, to require some further condition — some anchor to actuality — which the analysis as formulated does not provide. Without such an anchor, coherence is compatible with the most elaborate falsehood.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) P is such that acting upon it leads to successful practical outcomes, (ii) the successful outcomes are experienced by the agent who accepts P, and (iii) the success obtained by acting on P is not merely accidental but is reliably connected to the acceptance of P.
conditions: P is such that acting upon it leads to successful practical outcomes
The successful outcomes are experienced by the agent who accepts P
The success obtained by acting on P is not merely accidental but is reliably connected to the acceptance of P
counterexample: A patient who believes, falsely, that a sugar pill is a powerful medicine may reliably experience genuine physiological improvement owing to the placebo effect. The belief leads to successful outcomes for the agent, and the success is reliably connected to the acceptance of the belief. Yet the belief is false: the pill has no medicinal properties whatsoever.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to distinguish between beliefs whose practical success derives from the actual state of affairs described and beliefs whose practical success derives from the psychological consequences of holding them, irrespective of their descriptive accuracy.
passage: William James famously urged that the true is that which works, and one formulation of the pragmatic theory accordingly identifies truth with reliable practical success. A proposition is true, on this view, when the agent who accepts it is thereby led to successful outcomes, and when that success is not a matter of mere luck but stands in some stable relation to the acceptance of the proposition itself. One might initially find this account attractive, for it ties the somewhat ethereal notion of truth to the tangible business of getting on in the world. Yet it appears that the analysis is vulnerable to a class of cases in which false beliefs yield perfectly reliable practical benefits. Consider a patient who is given an inert substance and told, falsely, that it is a potent medicine. The patient, believing this, experiences genuine and repeatable physiological improvement — the well-documented placebo effect. Here all three conditions of the analysis are satisfied: the belief leads to successful outcomes; the outcomes are experienced by the agent; and the success is reliably, not accidentally, connected to the holding of the belief. Nonetheless, we should be most reluctant to pronounce the belief true, for the pill is in fact pharmacologically inert. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis thus shows that practical efficacy may flow from the mere holding of a belief, quite apart from whether the content of that belief accurately describes the world. The pragmatist, it seems, must distinguish the sources of practical success more carefully than the analysis permits.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) P is the sort of proposition that could be investigated by an indefinitely prolonged process of inquiry, (ii) the community of inquirers, if it were to persist long enough, would converge upon the acceptance of P, and (iii) this convergence would be the result of the self-correcting nature of the method of inquiry rather than of external compulsion or accident.
conditions: P is the sort of proposition that could be investigated by an indefinitely prolonged process of inquiry
The community of inquirers would converge upon the acceptance of P if inquiry were prolonged indefinitely
This convergence would result from the self-correcting nature of the method of inquiry rather than from external compulsion or accident
counterexample: Consider the proposition that a particular radium atom decayed at a specific microsecond on the third of January in the year 45,000 BCE. This proposition is either true or false. Yet no conceivable process of inquiry, however prolonged, could converge upon its acceptance or rejection, for the relevant evidence has been irrecoverably lost. The self-correcting method of inquiry has, as it were, nothing upon which to operate.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects propositions whose truth-value is determinate but whose subject-matter is such that no evidence could, even in principle under idealised conditions, be recovered — rendering convergence through inquiry impossible.
passage: Charles Sanders Peirce advanced a conception of truth according to which the true is that opinion upon which the community of inquirers would ultimately converge, were inquiry to be pursued without limit. On this account, truth is not a static correspondence but the endpoint of an ongoing, self-correcting process: errors are progressively eliminated, methods are refined, and the community of investigators draws ever nearer to a final, settled opinion. One might observe that this account has the considerable merit of connecting truth to the actual practice of rational investigation. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis can be produced by attending to propositions concerning events that have left no recoverable trace. Suppose that a particular atom of radium underwent radioactive decay at a perfectly specific instant, many tens of thousands of years ago. The proposition that the atom decayed at that instant is, one should think, either true or false. Yet the evidence pertaining to this singular micro-event has been utterly and irrecoverably lost; no future refinement of method could reconstitute it. The community of inquirers, however long it persists and however scrupulously it applies the self-correcting method, would never converge upon either the acceptance or the rejection of this proposition. If truth is convergence under ideal inquiry, then such propositions appear to lack a truth-value — a consequence which most philosophers would find unacceptable. The analysis, it seems, fails to accommodate those truths which are, by their very nature, beyond the reach of any possible investigation.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) the predicate 'is true' as applied to P adds no content beyond what P itself asserts, (ii) the assertion of 'P is true' is logically equivalent to the assertion of P, and (iii) the concept of truth serves no explanatory role beyond its use as a device of generalisation or endorsement.
conditions: The predicate 'is true' as applied to P adds no content beyond what P itself asserts
The assertion of 'P is true' is logically equivalent to the assertion of P
The concept of truth serves no explanatory role beyond its use as a device of generalisation or endorsement
counterexample: Consider a person who says, 'Everything the witness told the court is true,' without knowing what the witness said. The truth predicate here does substantive work: it enables the speaker to endorse an unknown set of propositions. The predicate is not merely redundant, for the speaker cannot replace the sentence with a conjunction of the propositions themselves, since she does not know what they are.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for blind endorsement cases in which the truth predicate is applied to propositions whose content is unknown to the speaker, thereby performing a function that goes beyond mere logical equivalence or redundancy.
passage: The deflationary or redundancy theory of truth, as advanced by Ramsey and elaborated by later writers, holds that the predicate 'is true' is, in the strictest sense, otiose. To say that it is true that snow is white is simply to say that snow is white; the truth predicate, on this view, adds nothing to the content of the proposition to which it is applied. The concept of truth is retained, if at all, merely as a convenient device of generalisation — enabling one to say, for instance, 'Everything he said is true' as a compendious substitute for a possibly infinite conjunction. One might observe that this is an appealingly parsimonious account. Yet it appears that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises precisely where the truth predicate is put to its most natural use in everyday discourse. Suppose a juror, having been told that a witness gave testimony but not having heard that testimony herself, remarks: 'Everything the witness said is true.' Here the truth predicate is performing a function that cannot be reduced to the mere reassertion of propositions, for the juror does not know which propositions the witness asserted. She cannot replace her remark with a list of the endorsed propositions; the truth predicate is doing work that outstrips its supposed role as a mere stylistic abbreviation. This suggests that the concept of truth, even in its generalising function, plays a more substantive logical role than the deflationist acknowledges, and that the equivalence between 'P is true' and 'P' does not exhaust the uses to which the predicate is put.

definition: A sentence S in a language L is true if and only if (i) there is a metalanguage M in which the truth-conditions of S can be stated, (ii) the T-sentence 'S is true in L if and only if p' holds, where p is a translation of S into M, and (iii) the metalanguage M is expressively richer than L so as to avoid semantic paradox.
conditions: There is a metalanguage M in which the truth-conditions of S can be stated
The T-sentence 'S is true in L if and only if p' holds, where p is a translation of S into M
The metalanguage M is expressively richer than L so as to avoid semantic paradox
counterexample: Consider natural languages such as English, which serve simultaneously as their own metalanguages. English can state its own truth-conditions: 'Snow is white' is true in English if and only if snow is white. There is no distinct, expressively richer metalanguage standing above English, yet truth-ascriptions in English are perfectly intelligible. The requirement for a separate, richer metalanguage is not met, and the analysis cannot be straightforwardly applied.
missing_condition: The analysis does not accommodate semantically closed languages — languages that contain their own truth predicate — and thereby fails to apply to natural languages, which are the very languages in which truth-talk most commonly occurs.
passage: Alfred Tarski's celebrated semantic theory of truth provides a formally precise account of what it is for a sentence to be true. On this approach, truth is defined for sentences of a given object language by means of a metalanguage, and the hallmark of an adequate definition is that it entails every instance of the T-schema: the sentence 'S' is true if and only if p, where p is the translation of S into the metalanguage. A crucial constraint, as Tarski herself emphasised, is that the metalanguage must be expressively richer than the object language, for otherwise the liar paradox threatens to render the definition inconsistent. One might observe that this framework has proved extraordinarily fruitful in mathematical logic and formal semantics. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis emerges when we turn to natural languages. English, for example, is a semantically closed language: it contains its own truth predicate, and its speakers routinely employ that predicate to state truth-conditions for English sentences within English itself. There is no distinct metalanguage, expressively richer than English, to which one might ascend. The third condition of the analysis is thus not satisfied, and yet truth-ascriptions in English are perfectly well understood and, one should think, often correct. Tarski herself recognised this difficulty and declined to apply her definition to natural languages, regarding them as too unruly for formal treatment. But if the analysis cannot be applied to the very languages in which questions about truth are most urgently raised, one may reasonably doubt whether it captures the full concept of truth rather than merely a technically useful surrogate.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) there exists some entity or state of affairs E that is a truthmaker for P, (ii) E necessitates the truth of P, such that it is impossible for E to exist and P to be false, and (iii) E is a positive, concrete entity or state of affairs rather than a mere absence or privation.
conditions: There exists some entity or state of affairs E that is a truthmaker for P
E necessitates the truth of P such that it is impossible for E to exist and P to be false
E is a positive, concrete entity or state of affairs rather than a mere absence or privation
counterexample: Consider the true proposition that this room contains no elephants. There is no positive, concrete entity whose existence necessitates the truth of this proposition. One cannot point to a thing or a state of affairs whose being there makes it true that elephants are absent.
