The truth of the proposition consists precisely in the non-existence of something, yet the analysis demands a positive existent as the truthmaker.
missing_condition: The analysis does not provide for truths that are made true by the absence or non-existence of entities, since it requires a positive, concrete truthmaker and thereby excludes negative truths from the domain of the analysis.
passage: Truthmaker theory contends that every truth is made true by something in the world — that for every true proposition, there is some entity whose existence necessitates its truth. This view offers an attractive metaphysical grounding for truth: propositions do not float free of reality but are tethered to it by the existence of their truthmakers. The analysis further stipulates that these truthmakers are positive, concrete entities or states of affairs, thereby maintaining a robust connection between truth and the furniture of the world. One might observe that this is an admirably ontologically committed position. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily available. Consider the proposition that this room contains no elephants. One would agree, upon reflection, that this proposition is true. Yet what entity, what positive state of affairs, serves as its truthmaker? One cannot identify a particular thing in the room whose existence makes it true that there are no elephants present. The room contains chairs, tables, and so forth, but none of these individually or jointly necessitates the absence of elephants, for one can conceive of a room containing both chairs and elephants. The truth of the proposition appears to rest not upon what exists but upon what does not exist — and this is precisely what the analysis, with its insistence on positive, concrete truthmakers, cannot accommodate. The truthmaker theorist is thus confronted with a dilemma: either admit negative or absential truthmakers, which many find ontologically suspect, or concede that not every true proposition has a truthmaker in the sense required.

definition: A speaker S asserts a proposition P correctly if and only if (i) S believes that P is true, (ii) S has adequate grounds for taking P to be true, and (iii) P is in fact true, such that the norm of assertion is truth.
conditions: S believes that P is true
S has adequate grounds for taking P to be true
P is in fact true such that the norm of assertion is truth
counterexample: Consider a scientist who, on the basis of the best available evidence, asserts that a particular drug is safe. The scientist believes this, and her grounds are as strong as could reasonably be demanded. Yet unbeknownst to her, the drug has a rare and as-yet-undiscovered side effect, and the proposition is in fact false. Intuitively, her assertion was perfectly proper, though the third condition is not satisfied.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates the propriety of an assertion with the truth of its content, failing to recognise that an assertion may be entirely proper even when the proposition asserted turns out to be false, provided the speaker's grounds were adequate.
passage: It has been maintained by a number of writers that the norm governing assertion is truth: one ought not to assert what is not true. On this view, a correct assertion requires not merely that the speaker believe the proposition and have adequate grounds for it, but that the proposition be, as a matter of fact, true. The three conditions — belief, adequate grounds, and truth — must all be satisfied for the assertion to meet the standard that governs the speech act. One might observe that this is a demanding standard, and indeed it is sometimes advanced precisely because of its demandingness. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis can be found in the ordinary practice of conscientious inquiry. Suppose a pharmaceutical researcher, having conducted extensive and methodologically sound trials, asserts that a certain drug is safe for human consumption. She believes this proposition, and her evidential grounds are by any reasonable measure excellent. Yet the drug has a rare side effect that no trial of practicable size could have detected, and the proposition she asserts is, strictly speaking, false. Should we say that her assertion was incorrect — that she violated the norm of assertion? This seems unduly harsh, for she did everything that could reasonably be expected of a responsible inquirer. The case suggests that the propriety of an assertion may come apart from the truth of its content, and that an account which makes truth the sole norm of assertion conflates two things that ought to be kept distinct: the epistemic responsibility of the speaker and the alethic status of the proposition.

definition: A theory of truth is adequate if and only if (i) it assigns a determinate truth-value to every well-formed declarative sentence of the language, (ii) it does not generate contradictions from sentences that refer to their own truth-value, and (iii) it preserves the unrestricted applicability of the T-schema to all sentences of the language.
conditions: It assigns a determinate truth-value to every well-formed declarative sentence of the language
It does not generate contradictions from sentences that refer to their own truth-value
It preserves the unrestricted applicability of the T-schema to all sentences of the language
counterexample: The liar sentence — 'This sentence is false' — is a well-formed declarative sentence that refers to its own truth-value. If the T-schema is applied to it without restriction, a contradiction results. Any theory that satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) will violate condition (ii), and any theory that satisfies (ii) must restrict either (i) or (iii). The three conditions cannot be jointly satisfied.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to acknowledge that the three conditions are jointly inconsistent in any language that permits self-reference, and that an adequate theory of truth must therefore sacrifice or weaken at least one of them rather than requiring all three.
passage: Theories of truth are typically evaluated against certain desiderata: they should assign truth-values comprehensively, avoid paradox, and respect the T-schema, which is widely regarded as a minimal condition on any satisfactory account of truth. One might suppose that these requirements are individually so modest that any competent theory ought to satisfy all three simultaneously. It appears, however, that the liar paradox furnishes a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis — or, more precisely, reveals that the conditions as jointly stated are unrealisable. Consider the sentence 'This sentence is false.' It is a well-formed declarative sentence and thus falls within the scope of the first condition. The T-schema, applied without restriction, yields: 'This sentence is false' is true if and only if this sentence is false. If the sentence is true, then what it says obtains, and it is false; if it is false, then what it says does not obtain, and it is true. A contradiction is generated, in violation of the second condition. We are thus compelled to recognise that the three conditions cannot be jointly maintained in any language that permits self-reference. Tarski's response was to restrict the third condition, confining the T-schema to languages that do not contain their own truth predicate. Other writers have proposed restricting the first condition, allowing truth-value gaps for pathological sentences. In either case, the original analysis must be weakened, for its conditions as formulated describe an ideal that logic itself forbids us from realising in full generality.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) P is such that it would be assertible by an ideally rational agent under epistemically ideal conditions, (ii) the ideal conditions include possession of all relevant evidence and the absence of any cognitive distortion, and (iii) the assertibility of P under such conditions is stable in the sense that no further evidence could overturn it.
conditions: P would be assertible by an ideally rational agent under epistemically ideal conditions
The ideal conditions include possession of all relevant evidence and absence of cognitive distortion
The assertibility of P is stable such that no further evidence could overturn it
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the total number of grains of sand on all the beaches of the world at this precise moment is odd. This proposition is either true or false. Yet even an ideally rational agent, under the best conceivable conditions, could not possess all the relevant evidence, for the counting of every grain at a single instant is a practical impossibility even in principle. The conditions of the analysis cannot be satisfied, yet the proposition has a determinate truth-value.
missing_condition: The analysis does not accommodate propositions whose truth-value is determinate but whose subject-matter is such that no epistemic conditions, however idealised, could render them stably assertible, because the requisite evidence is uncollectable even in principle.
passage: The epistemic theory of truth identifies truth with what would be warranted assertible under ideal epistemic conditions. On this account, a proposition is true not by virtue of corresponding to some mind-independent fact but by virtue of being the sort of thing that an ideally rational agent, possessed of all relevant evidence and free from every form of cognitive distortion, would be warranted in asserting, and would remain warranted in asserting no matter what further evidence might come to light. One might observe that this view has the advantage of connecting truth intimately with our epistemic practices and thereby avoiding the alleged obscurities of the correspondence relation. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis can be constructed by considering propositions of a certain arithmetical character concerning the physical world. Take the proposition that the total number of grains of sand on the world's beaches at this very instant is an odd number. We should, one thinks, accept that this proposition is determinately either true or false. Yet the relevant evidence — a complete and simultaneous enumeration of every grain — is not merely difficult to obtain but is, even under the most extravagant idealisation, impossible to collect, for the notion of a simultaneous count of every grain is incoherent at the required precision. No ideal agent, however perfectly rational and however favourably situated, could be warranted in asserting or denying this proposition. If truth is ideal warranted assertibility, the proposition lacks a truth-value; but this seems plainly wrong. The analysis thus appears to confuse what is true with what is knowable, conflating an ontological status with an epistemic one.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) P belongs to a particular domain of discourse D, (ii) the property in virtue of which P is true is the truth property appropriate to D, and (iii) the truth property appropriate to D is determined by the subject-matter of D, so that different domains may have different truth properties.
conditions: P belongs to a particular domain of discourse D
The property in virtue of which P is true is the truth property appropriate to D
The truth property appropriate to D is determined by the subject-matter of D so that different domains may have different truth properties
counterexample: Consider a mixed proposition such as 'This act of charity is morally good and it occurred on a Tuesday.' This proposition belongs simultaneously to the moral domain and the domain of ordinary empirical fact. The analysis requires that the truth property be determined by the domain, but this proposition spans two domains with, on the pluralist's own account, different truth properties. It is unclear which truth property applies, or how two distinct truth properties could combine to yield a single truth-value for one proposition.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to specify how propositions that span multiple domains of discourse are to be evaluated, since a mixed proposition cannot be assigned to a single domain and thus cannot be governed by a single domain-appropriate truth property.
passage: Truth pluralism is the view that truth is not a single, uniform property but rather a family of properties, each appropriate to a particular domain of discourse. In the domain of empirical science, truth might consist in correspondence with physical fact; in the domain of mathematics, it might consist in provability within a formal system; in the domain of morals, it might consist in coherence with a set of reflective judgements. On this view, the question 'What is truth?' does not admit of a single answer, for the answer varies with the domain. One might observe that this is an attractively tolerant position, accommodating diverse intuitions about different kinds of truth-claim. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider propositions that straddle the boundaries between domains. Suppose someone asserts: 'This act of charity is morally good and it occurred on a Tuesday.' This is a single proposition — a conjunction — whose components belong to different domains. The first conjunct, concerning moral goodness, belongs to the moral domain; the second, concerning a day of the week, belongs to the domain of ordinary empirical fact. The pluralist holds that these domains have different truth properties. But what truth property governs the conjunction as a whole? If the moral conjunct is true in virtue of one property and the empirical conjunct in virtue of another, it is unclear how the conjunction could be true in virtue of any single property. The pluralist must either provide an account of how truth properties compose across domains or accept that certain perfectly ordinary propositions fall outside the scope of the analysis — neither option being without difficulty.

definition: A moral claim M is truth-apt if and only if (i) M expresses a proposition capable of being true or false, (ii) there exist moral facts in virtue of which M may be true, and (iii) the truth or falsity of M is independent of the attitudes or sentiments of any particular agent or community.
conditions: M expresses a proposition capable of being true or false
There exist moral facts in virtue of which M may be true
The truth or falsity of M is independent of the attitudes or sentiments of any particular agent or community
counterexample: Consider the claim that gratuitous cruelty to children is morally wrong. Nearly everyone would affirm that this claim is true. Yet it appears impossible to identify a moral fact — analogous to a physical fact — in virtue of which it is true. One cannot observe moral wrongness as one observes redness or squareness. The second condition demands moral facts, but their existence and nature remain deeply contested, and the claim seems to have its truth-aptness independently of the resolution of that metaphysical dispute.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes a particular metaethical position — moral realism — by requiring the existence of moral facts, and thereby fails to accommodate the possibility that moral claims may be truth-apt without there being mind-independent moral facts of the sort the analysis demands.
passage: The question whether moral claims are truth-apt — whether they are the sort of thing that can be true or false — is one of the central issues of metaethics. On one influential account, a moral claim is truth-apt if it expresses a genuine proposition, if there exist moral facts that could render it true, and if its truth does not depend upon the feelings or preferences of any individual or group. One might observe that this analysis captures the commitments of moral realism, according to which morality is a domain of objective, mind-independent fact. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis emerges when we consider a moral claim that virtually everyone would regard as truth-apt and true — for example, that gratuitous cruelty to children is morally wrong. The claim certainly appears to express a proposition. But what moral fact makes it true? Moral facts, unlike physical facts, are not the sort of thing that can be detected by observation or experiment. Their existence is not a datum but a philosophical thesis, and a contested one at that. Expressivists, for instance, maintain that moral claims express attitudes rather than describe facts, and yet many expressivists wish to preserve the appearance of truth-aptness for moral discourse. The second condition of the analysis thus builds into the very definition of truth-aptness a substantive metaphysical commitment that many competent philosophers reject. A moral claim may be truth-apt, it seems, even if the notion of a moral fact remains elusive — and the analysis, by requiring moral facts as a condition of truth-aptness, is accordingly too restrictive.

definition: An aesthetic claim A is truth-apt if and only if (i) A expresses a proposition about an aesthetic property of some object or event, (ii) the aesthetic property attributed is a real feature of the object or event rather than a projection of the observer's response, and (iii) competent observers would converge in their attribution of the aesthetic property under suitable conditions of perception.
conditions: A expresses a proposition about an aesthetic property of some object or event
The aesthetic property attributed is a real feature of the object or event rather than a projection of the observer's response
Competent observers would converge in their attribution of the aesthetic property under suitable conditions of perception
counterexample: Consider the claim that Beethoven's late string quartets are sublime. Many competent listeners regard them as supreme achievements; others, equally competent and attentive, find them forbidding and resistant to the attribution of sublimity. There is no convergence among competent observers, and yet the claim seems to be more than a mere report of personal feeling — it appears to say something about the music itself. The third condition is unsatisfied, yet the claim seems truth-apt.
missing_condition: The analysis makes the truth-aptness of aesthetic claims contingent upon the convergence of competent observers, yet persistent disagreement among such observers may reflect the nature of the aesthetic domain rather than the non-truth-aptness of its claims.
passage: Whether aesthetic claims — claims about beauty, sublimity, elegance, and the like — are truth-apt is a question that has exercised philosophers since antiquity. One account holds that an aesthetic claim is truth-apt when it attributes a genuine property to its object, one that is not merely projected by the observer, and when competent observers would agree upon the attribution under suitable conditions. The convergence requirement is intended to distinguish objective aesthetic properties from mere subjective responses: if trained and attentive observers agree, the property is plausibly in the object; if they do not, the claim is merely expressive. One might observe that this analysis has a certain initial plausibility. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the long and instructive history of aesthetic disagreement. Consider the claim that Beethoven's late string quartets are sublime. Many competent musicians and listeners affirm this without hesitation; yet others of equal competence and sensitivity find these works perplexing, austere, and resistant to easy categorisation, declining to attribute sublimity to them. The convergence that the analysis demands is not forthcoming, and yet one would hesitate to conclude that the claim is not truth-apt — that it is a mere exclamation of preference. The claim appears to be about the music, not about the speaker, and it invites reasoned discussion, counter-argument, and the giving of grounds. To deny its truth-aptness on the basis of non-convergence seems to mislocate the source of the difficulty, for the disagreement may reflect the genuine complexity of the aesthetic domain rather than the absence of an objective matter about which to disagree.

definition: A proposition P is true relative to a framework F if and only if (i) P is evaluated within a particular conceptual, cultural, or theoretical framework F, (ii) the standards of truth operative in F determine whether P counts as true, and (iii) there is no framework-independent standpoint from which the truth of P can be assessed.
conditions: P is evaluated within a particular conceptual, cultural, or theoretical framework F
The standards of truth operative in F determine whether P counts as true
There is no framework-independent standpoint from which the truth of P can be assessed
counterexample: Consider the proposition that torturing an innocent person for amusement is morally wrong. A relativist must allow that this proposition may be true in one framework and false in another. Yet if two frameworks deliver opposed verdicts, there is no way to say that one is correct, for the analysis denies any framework-independent standpoint. This yields the consequence that there is nothing wrong, absolutely speaking, with torture for amusement — a consequence which most would regard as a reductio of the view.
missing_condition: The analysis provides no means of adjudicating between frameworks that deliver incompatible verdicts on the same proposition, and thus cannot account for the strong intuition that certain propositions are true absolutely and not merely relative to a framework.
passage: Relativism about truth holds that truth is always truth-within-a-framework: there is no Archimedean point from which propositions can be assessed independently of all conceptual, cultural, or theoretical commitments. On this analysis, to ask whether a proposition is true simpliciter is to ask a question without a determinate answer; one can only ask whether it is true relative to this or that framework, and the standards internal to the framework settle the matter. One might observe that this view has a certain appeal, particularly in the face of apparently irresolvable disagreements across cultures. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider propositions of a morally fundamental character. Take the proposition that torturing an innocent person purely for the torturer's amusement is morally wrong. In one framework, the standards of truth operative may deliver the verdict that this is true; in another framework, with different moral commitments, the same proposition may be judged false. The relativist, by the terms of the analysis, must hold that neither framework enjoys a privileged position, and that there is no framework-independent fact of the matter. But this entails that there is no non-relative truth about whether torturing innocents for fun is wrong — a consequence that strikes most reasonable persons as deeply objectionable, and which many philosophers would treat as a decisive reason for rejecting the analysis. The difficulty is not merely that the consequence is unpalatable; it is that the analysis lacks the resources to say what seems plainly correct: that some propositions are true, not relative to a framework, but absolutely.

definition: A proposition P is meaningful and hence truth-apt if and only if (i) there exists, at least in principle, some set of sensory observations that would verify P, (ii) the verification procedure is specifiable in advance such that one could say what observations would count for or against P, and (iii) the meaning of P is exhausted by its method of verification.
conditions: There exists, at least in principle, some set of sensory observations that would verify P
The verification procedure is specifiable in advance such that one could say what observations would count for or against P
The meaning of P is exhausted by its method of verification
counterexample: Consider the proposition that every even number greater than two is the sum of two prime numbers — Goldbach's conjecture. This proposition is meaningful and truth-apt: mathematicians understand it perfectly and devote considerable effort to proving or refuting it. Yet there is no set of sensory observations that could verify it, for it concerns an infinite domain of abstract objects. The verification conditions of the analysis are not satisfied, and yet the proposition is plainly meaningful.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to accommodate propositions of pure mathematics and logic, whose meaningfulness and truth-aptness do not depend upon any possible sensory verification but upon relations among abstract entities.
passage: The verification principle, associated with the logical positivists and with Ayer's formulation in particular — a principle that Stebbing examined with rigour and that Warnock later placed in broader philosophical context, maintains that a proposition is meaningful — and thereby truth-apt — only if it is at least in principle verifiable by sensory observation. The meaning of a proposition, on this account, is nothing other than its method of verification: to understand a proposition is to know what observations would confirm or disconfirm it. Propositions that fail this test are dismissed as pseudo-propositions, devoid of cognitive content. One might observe that this principle was introduced with the laudable aim of purging philosophy of empty metaphysics. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the propositions of pure mathematics. Consider Goldbach's conjecture: the proposition that every even number greater than two is the sum of two prime numbers. This proposition is perfectly meaningful; mathematicians understand it with complete clarity and have devoted great ingenuity to its investigation. Yet there are no sensory observations by which it could be verified, for it makes a claim about an infinite totality of abstract objects. One cannot observe numbers, nor can one complete an infinite series of checks. The verification conditions of the analysis are therefore unsatisfied, and yet no one would wish to say that Goldbach's conjecture is meaningless or that it lacks a truth-value. The verificationist must either broaden the notion of verification until it loses its empiricist bite, or concede that a substantial class of undeniably meaningful propositions falls outside the scope of the analysis.

definition: A true proposition P is identical with a fact F if and only if (i) P and F are not merely correlated but are one and the same entity, (ii) the content of P — what P says — is not distinct from the worldly state of affairs F, and (iii) there is no ontological gap between P's being true and F's obtaining, since they are the very same thing.
conditions: P and F are not merely correlated but are one and the same entity
The content of P is not distinct from the worldly state of affairs F
There is no ontological gap between P's being true and F's obtaining since they are the very same thing
counterexample: Consider a false proposition, such as the proposition that the Earth is flat. This proposition has content — it says something perfectly definite — and yet there is no fact with which it could be identical, for there is no fact of the Earth's being flat. If true propositions are identical with facts, then false propositions must be a different kind of entity entirely, since they lack a fact-partner. This asymmetry is puzzling, for true and false propositions seem to be entities of the same logical kind, differing only in truth-value.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to provide a unified account of propositions as such, since it treats true propositions as identical with facts while leaving false propositions without a corresponding ontological status, thus creating an unexplained asymmetry between the two.
passage: The identity theory of truth holds that when a proposition is true, it is not merely correlated with or corresponding to a fact; it is the fact. Truth, on this account, is not a relational property that obtains between two distinct items — proposition and fact — but the identity of the proposition with a worldly state of affairs. The appeal of this view is that it dissolves the problem of explaining the correspondence relation: if the true proposition simply is the fact, there is no gap between them that needs bridging. One might observe that this is an impressively economical ontology. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis presents itself when we turn our attention to false propositions. Consider the proposition that the Earth is flat. This proposition is false, and yet it is certainly a proposition: it has a definite content, it can be entertained, believed, and denied, and it stands in logical relations to other propositions. On the identity theory, a true proposition is identical with a fact. But a false proposition has no fact with which to be identical, for the fact of the Earth's being flat does not obtain. What, then, is a false proposition? It cannot be an entity of the same kind as a true proposition, for true propositions are facts and false propositions are not. The analysis thus generates a troubling asymmetry: true and false propositions, which in every other respect seem to be entities of the same logical category, turn out, on this view, to be fundamentally different kinds of thing. This consequence requires either an independent account of the ontology of false propositions or an acknowledgement that the identity theory is incomplete as an analysis of truth.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) the singular terms in P successfully refer to objects in the world, (ii) the predicates in P correctly apply to those objects, and (iii) the truth of P is fully determined by the reference of its terms and the satisfaction of its predicates.
conditions: The singular terms in P successfully refer to objects in the world
The predicates in P correctly apply to those objects
The truth of P is fully determined by the reference of its terms and the satisfaction of its predicates
counterexample: Consider the true proposition that the average British household has 2.4 children. The singular term 'the average British household' does not refer to any particular household in the world; there is no object that is the average household. Yet the proposition is true and widely understood. The first condition — successful reference — is not met, and yet truth obtains.
missing_condition: The analysis does not accommodate propositions containing terms that function grammatically as referring expressions but do not in fact refer to any object in the world, yet which contribute to the truth of the proposition in which they occur.
passage: It is a natural thought that the truth of a proposition depends upon its terms latching onto the world in the right way: the singular terms must refer to objects, and the predicates must correctly characterise those objects. On this view, truth is a function of reference and predicate satisfaction, and nothing more is needed to explain what makes a true proposition true. One might observe that this analysis works well for a large class of straightforward empirical propositions. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis can be found by attending to propositions that employ terms which do not refer in any straightforward sense. Consider the proposition that the average British household has 2.4 children. This proposition is true — it accurately reports a statistical fact — and it plays a legitimate role in demographic reasoning. But the term 'the average British household' does not refer to any household. There is no door one might knock upon and find the average household at home with its 2.4 children. The term functions as a kind of convenient fiction, a device for summarising a body of data, and it would be a logical error to treat it as a genuine referring expression. The first condition of the analysis is therefore unsatisfied: the singular term does not refer to any object in the world. Yet the proposition is true. This suggests that truth cannot in every case be decomposed into reference plus predicate satisfaction, and that some true propositions achieve their truth by mechanisms that the analysis does not capture — mechanisms involving statistical abstraction, idealisation, or other non-referential modes of discourse.

definition: A scientific theory T is approximately true if and only if (i) the central claims of T are close to what is actually the case, (ii) the deviations between what T asserts and what is actually the case are minor and quantifiable, and (iii) the respects in which T is inaccurate do not affect its core explanatory and predictive success.
conditions: The central claims of T are close to what is actually the case
The deviations between what T asserts and what is actually the case are minor and quantifiable
The respects in which T is inaccurate do not affect its core explanatory and predictive success
counterexample: Consider Newtonian mechanics. It is extraordinarily successful in its predictions for a vast range of phenomena. Yet its central claims — that space and time are absolute, that mass is invariant, that gravitational influence is instantaneous — are not close to what is actually the case according to general relativity. The deviations are not minor matters of degree but fundamental matters of kind. Nevertheless, Newtonian mechanics is paradigmatically the sort of theory one would wish to call approximately true.
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately distinguish between quantitative inaccuracy, which may indeed be minor and measurable, and qualitative or structural inaccuracy in the foundational claims of a theory, which may be profound even when the theory's predictions are excellent.
passage: The notion of approximate truth is of considerable importance in the philosophy of science, for it is widely held that our best scientific theories, while not perfectly accurate, are nonetheless close to the truth. One analysis of this notion holds that a theory is approximately true when its central claims are near to what is actually the case, when the deviations are small and quantifiable, and when the inaccuracies do not undermine the theory's explanatory and predictive achievements. One might observe that this captures a common-sense understanding of what it is for a theory to be roughly but not exactly right. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by Newtonian mechanics — the very theory most often cited as a paradigm of approximate truth. Newtonian mechanics is spectacularly successful in its predictions: it guides the construction of bridges, the launching of rockets, and the calculation of planetary orbits with extraordinary accuracy. Yet its foundational ontological commitments are profoundly mistaken by the lights of general relativity. Space and time are not absolute but are aspects of a unified and curved spacetime; mass is not invariant but varies with velocity; gravitational effects propagate at a finite speed. These are not minor deviations that can be measured in small percentages; they are fundamental reconceptions of the physical world. The analysis, by requiring that the deviations be minor and quantifiable, cannot accommodate this case, for the deviations are qualitative and structural rather than quantitative. The approximately true theory may get the numbers nearly right whilst getting the ontology entirely wrong, and the analysis has no way of accommodating this possibility.

definition: A proposition P is objectively true if and only if (i) the truth of P does not depend upon any subject's beliefs, desires, or perspective, (ii) P would remain true even if no conscious beings existed to entertain it, and (iii) the fact that makes P true is part of mind-independent reality.
conditions: The truth of P does not depend upon any subject's beliefs, desires, or perspective
P would remain true even if no conscious beings existed to entertain it
The fact that makes P true is part of mind-independent reality
counterexample: Consider the proposition that pain is unpleasant. This proposition is, one should think, objectively true — its truth does not depend on any particular person's opinion and it would be unreasonable to deny it. Yet it cannot satisfy the second or third conditions, for pain is an essentially subjective phenomenon. If no conscious beings existed, there would be no pain and hence no fact of pain's being unpleasant. The fact that makes the proposition true is not part of mind-independent reality but is constitutively dependent upon the existence of minds.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates objectivity with mind-independence, failing to recognise that some propositions may be objectively true even though the facts that make them true are essentially mind-dependent, as with propositions about the character of conscious experience.
passage: Objectivity is often understood as mind-independence: a proposition is objectively true when its truth does not depend on anyone's perspective and when it would remain true even in a world devoid of minds. This analysis is intended to capture the intuition that objective truths are not up to us — that they are features of reality rather than reflections of our attitudes. One might observe that this account works well for a wide range of truths in the natural sciences: the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen, for example, would remain true regardless of whether any conscious being existed to entertain it. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider truths about the nature of conscious experience itself. Consider the proposition that pain is unpleasant. Virtually everyone would agree that this is true, and that its truth is not a matter of opinion or perspective; it seems as objective as any truth could be. Yet pain is an essentially subjective phenomenon — it exists only insofar as it is experienced by a subject. In a world without conscious beings, there would be no pain, and the proposition that pain is unpleasant would have no subject-matter to which it could pertain. The second and third conditions of the analysis are thus unsatisfied, for the fact that pain is unpleasant is not part of mind-independent reality. The analysis, it seems, has drawn the boundary of objectivity too narrowly, excluding propositions about the phenomenal character of experience that are, by any reasonable standard, objectively true.

definition: A sentence S is meaningful if and only if (i) S has determinate truth-conditions, (ii) the truth-conditions of S are graspable by a competent speaker of the language, and (iii) the meaning of S is identical with its truth-conditions, such that to know the meaning of S is to know what would make it true.
conditions: S has determinate truth-conditions
The truth-conditions of S are graspable by a competent speaker of the language
The meaning of S is identical with its truth-conditions such that to know the meaning of S is to know what would make it true
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Hello!' This sentence is meaningful — every competent English speaker understands it — and yet it lacks truth-conditions entirely, for it is not the sort of utterance that can be true or false. Greetings, commands, and questions are meaningful linguistic acts, but they do not possess truth-conditions. The first condition is not met, yet the sentence is not meaningless.
missing_condition: The analysis restricts meaning to declarative sentences with truth-conditions, thereby excluding the meaningfulness of non-declarative speech acts — interrogatives, imperatives, greetings, and exclamations — which are meaningful without being truth-evaluable.
passage: The truth-conditional theory of meaning, elaborated with great rigour by Davidson and others, holds that the meaning of a sentence is its truth-conditions. To understand a sentence, on this view, is to know under what circumstances it would be true; and a theory of meaning for a language is, in effect, a theory that pairs each sentence with its truth-conditions. One might observe that this approach has yielded important results in formal semantics and has provided a fruitful framework for the study of natural language. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily available in the most ordinary uses of language. Consider the sentence 'Hello!' Every competent speaker of English understands this utterance and knows how to use it. It is a paradigm of meaningful linguistic behaviour. Yet it does not have truth-conditions. A greeting is neither true nor false; it is not the sort of thing that describes a state of affairs. The same applies to commands such as 'Close the door' and to questions such as 'Is it raining?' These sentences are meaningful — their users understand them perfectly — and yet they lack the truth-conditions that the analysis identifies with meaning. The theory, in its strong form, must therefore be restricted to declarative sentences, which constitute only a portion of meaningful language use. But this restriction is itself an acknowledgement that the identification of meaning with truth-conditions does not capture meaning in its full generality, and that a complete theory of meaning must accommodate speech acts that contribute to communication without possessing truth-values.

definition: The primary bearers of truth are propositions, and a proposition P bears truth if and only if (i) P is an abstract, language-independent entity that can be expressed by different sentences in different languages, (ii) P is the content of beliefs, assertions, and other intentional attitudes, and (iii) the truth-value of P is invariant across all linguistic expressions of P.
conditions: P is an abstract, language-independent entity that can be expressed by different sentences in different languages
P is the content of beliefs, assertions, and other intentional attitudes
The truth-value of P is invariant across all linguistic expressions of P
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'I am hungry,' uttered by two different speakers at different times. Each utterance expresses a different proposition — one about the first speaker's hunger, the other about the second's — and these propositions may differ in truth-value. Yet both utterances are instances of the same sentence. The identity conditions of the proposition depend on contextual features that the sentence alone does not determine, and the analysis provides no account of how propositions are individuated when indexical expressions are involved.
missing_condition: The analysis does not specify how propositions are individuated in the presence of indexical and context-dependent expressions, where the same sentence may express different propositions with different truth-values depending on the context of utterance.
passage: A familiar doctrine in the philosophy of truth holds that propositions — abstract, language-independent entities — are the primary bearers of truth. Sentences may express propositions, and beliefs may have propositions as their content, but it is the proposition itself, not the sentence or the belief, that is true or false. The advantage of this view is that it provides a stable locus for truth: the proposition that snow is white is the same proposition whether it is expressed in English, French, or Mandarin, and its truth-value does not fluctuate with the language of expression. One might observe that this is a tidy and attractive account. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in connection with indexical and context-dependent expressions. Consider the sentence 'I am hungry.' When uttered by Smith at noon, it expresses the proposition that Smith is hungry at noon. When uttered by Jones at midnight, it expresses the proposition that Jones is hungry at midnight. These are different propositions, and they may well differ in truth-value. Yet the sentence is the same on both occasions. The analysis tells us that propositions are abstract, language-independent entities that serve as the contents of intentional attitudes, and that their truth-values are invariant across linguistic expression. What it does not tell us is how to individuate propositions when the same sentence, depending on who utters it and when, expresses different propositions. The problem of indexicality thus reveals a lacuna in the account: the analysis presupposes that we can identify propositions, but it provides no resources for doing so in the very cases where identity is most in question.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in every possible world, (ii) the truth of P does not depend on any contingent features of the actual world, and (iii) the denial of P is logically impossible.
conditions: P is true in every possible world
The truth of P does not depend on any contingent features of the actual world
The denial of P is logically impossible
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. According to Kripke's analysis, this is a necessary truth — true in every possible world — for 'water' rigidly designates the substance whose chemical composition is H2O. Yet the truth of this identity was discovered empirically, and its denial is not logically impossible in the sense that it produces no formal contradiction. Someone who denies that water is H2O is mistaken, but the denial is not a logical absurdity of the sort one finds in denying that bachelors are unmarried.
missing_condition: The analysis conflates logical necessity with metaphysical necessity, failing to accommodate necessary truths that are known a posteriori and whose denials, whilst metaphysically impossible, do not involve logical contradiction in the narrow sense.
passage: The notion of necessary truth has occupied philosophers since Leibniz, and a standard analysis holds that a proposition is necessarily true when it holds in every possible world, depends on no contingent features of actuality, and is such that its denial is logically impossible. This analysis neatly captures the status of propositions such as 'All bachelors are unmarried' and 'Nothing is both red all over and green all over at the same time,' whose denials produce what one might call a logical absurdity. One might observe that the analysis thus characterises a familiar and important class of truths. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the Kripkean necessary a posteriori. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. If Kripke's arguments are sound, this proposition is necessarily true: in every possible world, the substance that is water has the chemical composition H2O. The proposition does not depend on contingent features of the actual world in the relevant sense, for it holds of water as such. Yet its truth was discovered by empirical investigation, not by logical analysis, and its denial — the claim that water is not H2O — is not logically impossible in any formal sense. One can consistently entertain the denial without falling into self-contradiction; it is merely that the denial, upon investigation, turns out to be false in every possible world. The third condition, which requires that the denial be logically impossible, is thus not satisfied in the relevant sense, and the analysis proves too narrow to accommodate all necessary truths. The distinction between logical and metaphysical necessity, which the analysis obscures, turns out to be philosophically significant.

definition: Every well-formed declarative proposition P satisfies the principle of bivalence if and only if (i) P is determinately true or determinately false, (ii) there is no third truth-value that P might possess, and (iii) the truth-value of P is fixed independently of our knowledge or ability to ascertain it.
conditions: P is determinately true or determinately false
There is no third truth-value that P might possess
The truth-value of P is fixed independently of our knowledge or ability to ascertain it
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the present King of France is bald. This is a well-formed declarative proposition, and yet, as Russell observed, it seems to be neither determinately true nor determinately false, for there is no present King of France. If the proposition is not true (since no bald king exists) and not false (since no non-bald king exists either), then the first condition is not met, and the principle of bivalence fails for a perfectly grammatical declarative proposition.
missing_condition: The analysis does not account for propositions that contain non-referring singular terms, in which the failure of reference may render the proposition neither determinately true nor determinately false, thereby constituting a class of well-formed declarative propositions to which bivalence does not straightforwardly apply.
passage: The principle of bivalence — the thesis that every well-formed declarative proposition is either true or false, with no third possibility — has long been regarded as a cornerstone of classical logic. On the analysis here considered, every such proposition has a determinate truth-value, there is no intermediate status between truth and falsehood, and the matter is settled by reality itself, independently of what we happen to know. One might observe that this principle underwrites much of our ordinary reasoning, and that its denial would require substantial revision of our logical practices. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by propositions containing non-referring singular terms. Consider the proposition that the present King of France is bald. The sentence is well-formed and declarative. Yet, as Russell's celebrated analysis brought to light, there is no present King of France. Is the proposition true? It cannot be, for there is no bald king. Is it false? To say that it is false might seem to commit one to the view that the present King of France is not bald — that is, that he has hair — which is equally unsatisfying, for there is no such person at all. On Strawson's rival account, the proposition is neither true nor false; the question of its truth-value does not arise, because the presupposition of existence has failed. If Strawson is right, then we have a well-formed declarative proposition that violates the first condition of the analysis: it is not determinately true and not determinately false. Bivalence, it appears, cannot be sustained without restriction in the face of reference failure.

definition: A proposition P may be true even if P is evidence-transcendent, where evidence-transcendence obtains if and only if (i) the truth-value of P cannot be determined by any evidence available to human inquirers, (ii) the evidence required to settle P is not merely practically inaccessible but is in principle beyond human cognitive reach, and (iii) the truth of P is nonetheless fully determinate.
conditions: The truth-value of P cannot be determined by any evidence available to human inquirers
The evidence required to settle P is in principle beyond human cognitive reach
The truth of P is nonetheless fully determinate
counterexample: Consider a proposition about an observable but unrecorded historical event: that exactly seventeen raindrops fell on a particular stone in an unpopulated region on a specific afternoon two million years ago. The evidence has been lost irrecoverably, yet it was not in principle beyond human cognitive reach — had an observer been present, the matter could have been settled by counting. The proposition is evidence-transcendent in practice but not in principle, and the analysis does not clearly distinguish these two situations.
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately distinguish between propositions that are evidence-transcendent in principle — those whose subject-matter is inherently beyond any possible human epistemic access — and those that are evidence-transcendent only in practice, owing to the contingent loss of evidence that was once available or could have been collected.
passage: The notion of evidence-transcendent truth lies at the heart of the realism-antirealism debate. The realist maintains that a proposition may be fully determinately true or false even if no evidence, however ingeniously sought, could settle the matter — even if the truth-value of the proposition is, in a strong sense, beyond the reach of human inquiry. The analysis thus distinguishes evidence-transcendent propositions from merely undecided ones: it is not that we have not yet found the evidence, but that no evidence is available even in principle. One might observe that this notion is central to the realist's conception of a mind-independent reality that outstrips our capacity to know it. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider propositions about past events that were, at the time, perfectly observable. Suppose we consider the proposition that exactly seventeen raindrops fell upon a particular stone in a remote and unpopulated region on a specific afternoon two million years ago. The evidence pertaining to this event has been irretrievably lost — no record, no trace, no fossil remains. The proposition is, in practice, completely beyond our epistemic reach. Yet it is not in principle beyond human cognitive capacity: had an observer been stationed at the stone on the relevant afternoon, she could have counted the raindrops. The evidence was once available, at least in a counterfactual sense, and its absence is a contingent matter of history rather than a principled impossibility. The analysis, however, does not clearly distinguish this case from one in which the evidence is in principle uncollectable, and thus conflates two quite different species of epistemic inaccessibility under a single heading.

definition: A proposition P is true if and only if (i) P is the object of a consensus among all members of the relevant community of inquirers, (ii) the consensus is reached through free, open, and rational deliberation, and (iii) the consensus is stable in the sense that it would not be overturned by the introduction of further relevant considerations.
conditions: P is the object of a consensus among all members of the relevant community of inquirers
The consensus is reached through free, open, and rational deliberation
The consensus is stable in the sense that it would not be overturned by further relevant considerations
counterexample: For centuries, the relevant community of inquirers — astronomers, natural philosophers, and educated persons generally — maintained a stable consensus, arrived at through the best available methods of rational deliberation, that the Sun revolves around the Earth. This consensus satisfied all three conditions. Yet the proposition was false. Copernicus, Galileo, and Kepler overturned it, but until they did so, the consensus was as stable and rationally grounded as any could be.
missing_condition: The analysis provides no mechanism for distinguishing a genuine consensus about the truth from a consensus that is the product of shared but systematic error, since the conditions of free, open, rational deliberation and stability are compatible with the community's being uniformly mistaken.
passage: The consensus theory of truth holds that a proposition is true when it is the object of agreement among all members of the relevant community, provided that the agreement has been reached through free and rational deliberation and is stable under the introduction of further considerations. This view has the appeal of grounding truth in social practices of inquiry and avoiding appeal to inaccessible facts lying beyond the reach of human knowledge. One might observe that it captures something important about the role of communal discussion in the pursuit of truth. It appears, however, that a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis can be found by consulting the history of science. For many centuries, the geocentric model of the cosmos commanded the assent of every qualified inquirer. Astronomers from Ptolemy to the late mediaeval period affirmed, on the basis of the best observations and the most sophisticated theoretical reasoning available to them, that the Sun and the planets revolve around the Earth. This consensus was not coerced; it was the product of genuinely open and rational investigation. Nor was it unstable: it persisted for well over a millennium, surviving every challenge until the work of Copernicus and her successors. All three conditions of the analysis were satisfied. And yet the proposition that the Sun revolves around the Earth was false. The consensus theory, by identifying truth with rational communal agreement, lacks the resources to explain how a community of inquirers, deliberating freely and carefully, can arrive at a stable consensus on a falsehood. The difficulty is not that such cases are rare; it is that the analysis provides no way of even recognising the possibility of systematic communal error, for by its own lights such error is a contradiction in terms.

definition: A person exhibits intellectual courage if and only if (i) the person holds a belief which conflicts with the prevailing orthodoxy of the relevant community, (ii) the person is willing to assert or defend this belief publicly despite the risk of social or professional censure, and (iii) the person's willingness to do so arises from a genuine concern for truth rather than from contrarianism or desire for notoriety.
conditions: The person holds a belief which conflicts with the prevailing orthodoxy of the relevant community
The person is willing to assert or defend this belief publicly despite the risk of social or professional censure
The person's willingness to do so arises from a genuine concern for truth rather than from contrarianism or desire for notoriety
counterexample: Consider a junior academic who, upon discovering statistical irregularities in the published findings of a senior colleague, resolves to raise the matter at a departmental colloquium. She does so from a sincere concern for the integrity of the discipline and at considerable personal risk. Yet one would hesitate to call this intellectual courage, for she does not in fact hold a belief which conflicts with the prevailing orthodoxy; rather, she applies the very standards the community professes to uphold.
missing_condition: The analysis fails to accommodate cases in which courageous epistemic action consists not in challenging orthodoxy but in insisting upon the faithful application of shared standards against those who have tacitly abandoned them.
passage: One might suppose that intellectual courage is adequately analysed as the willingness to challenge received opinion from a sincere desire for truth, and indeed this captures a great many of the cases we should wish to describe as courageous. The scientist who proposes a novel hypothesis contrary to the established paradigm, knowing full well that her career may suffer, appears to satisfy all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a junior researcher discovers that a widely cited paper by a senior figure in her department contains fabricated data. She resolves to present her findings at a departmental colloquium, fully aware that the senior colleague wields considerable influence over hiring and promotion decisions. Her motivation is entirely proper: she is moved by a genuine concern for the integrity of the scientific enterprise. Nevertheless, she does not hold a belief which conflicts with the prevailing orthodoxy. On the contrary, she appeals to the very methodological standards which the community professes to endorse. It appears, then, that the definition is too narrow, for it excludes precisely those cases in which courage consists not in the proposal of heterodox views but in the insistence upon standards which others have quietly ceased to honour. One might observe that the risk she undertakes and the sincerity of her motivation are no less than those of the paradigm-challenger, and it would be peculiar to deny her the attribution of intellectual courage on so technical a ground.

definition: A person is open-minded if and only if (i) the person is willing to consider beliefs, arguments, or evidence which conflict with the person's currently held views, (ii) the person does not dismiss such conflicting material without first giving it a fair hearing, and (iii) the person is disposed to revise or abandon previously held beliefs in the light of sufficiently strong countervailing evidence.
conditions: The person is willing to consider beliefs, arguments, or evidence which conflict with the person's currently held views
The person does not dismiss such conflicting material without first giving it a fair hearing
The person is disposed to revise or abandon previously held beliefs in the light of sufficiently strong countervailing evidence
counterexample: A philosophy lecturer prides herself on her open-mindedness: she reads every objection to her published work with care, discusses rival theories at length with colleagues, and has on several occasions altered her position in response to compelling arguments. Yet she extends this willingness only to views advanced by persons she regards as intellectually serious, and categorically refuses to entertain objections raised by students or members of the public, regardless of their merit. She satisfies all three conditions with respect to a restricted class of interlocutors, yet we should not wish to call her genuinely open-minded.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the willingness to consider conflicting material must extend to all sources irrespective of the social or professional standing of the person offering the objection.
passage: It is commonly held that open-mindedness consists in a readiness to entertain views at variance with one's own and to revise one's beliefs accordingly. We might formalise this as the conjunction of three conditions: a willingness to consider opposing material, a refusal to dismiss it without due examination, and a genuine disposition to change one's mind when the evidence warrants. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may, however, be readily constructed. Imagine a philosopher of some distinction who takes great care to engage with the published criticisms of her peers. She reads every review, attends every symposium at which her work is discussed, and has in fact changed her position on two significant matters over the course of her career. By any reasonable measure she satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet she exhibits a curious limitation: she will not entertain objections raised by her own students or by persons outside the academy, however cogently they may be formulated. When a particularly able undergraduate presents a devastating objection to her central thesis during a tutorial, she dismisses it with the remark that he has not yet read enough to contribute usefully. It appears, then, that the definition fails to capture an important dimension of open-mindedness, namely that it must be exercised without regard to the credentials or social standing of the person from whom the conflicting view originates. A person who is willing to reconsider only when challenged by those she already respects is open-minded merely in a truncated and ultimately self-serving sense.

definition: A person displays intellectual thoroughness if and only if (i) the person investigates a question to a degree of depth and breadth proportionate to its importance, (ii) the person does not rest content with superficial or incomplete evidence when further inquiry is feasible, and (iii) the person systematically considers the relevant alternatives before arriving at a conclusion.
conditions: The person investigates a question to a degree of depth and breadth proportionate to its importance
The person does not rest content with superficial or incomplete evidence when further inquiry is feasible
The person systematically considers the relevant alternatives before arriving at a conclusion
counterexample: A medical researcher conducting a review of drug efficacy investigates the literature with extraordinary care, searches every relevant database, considers all rival hypotheses, and produces a report of exemplary comprehensiveness. Yet she undertakes this painstaking inquiry not because the question is important — she regards it as trivial — but because she is constitutionally incapable of producing work that is anything less than exhaustive, and she applies the same disproportionate effort to questions of no consequence whatever. She satisfies all three conditions, yet her behaviour seems better described as compulsiveness than as intellectual thoroughness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the degree of investigative effort be appropriately calibrated to the significance of the question, such that the agent exercises judgement about when further inquiry ceases to be warranted.
passage: We are inclined to praise intellectual thoroughness as one of the chief virtues of the inquiring mind, and we might attempt to analyse it as a disposition to investigate questions in depth, to refuse superficial evidence, and to consider rival hypotheses before settling upon a conclusion. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis proceeds as follows. Consider a medical researcher who has been asked to conduct a routine review of the literature on a well-understood class of drugs. The matter is of modest clinical significance and the existing consensus is well supported. Nevertheless, she devotes six months to the task, searching every conceivable database, corresponding with researchers in adjacent fields, and producing a report of several hundred pages in which every alternative hypothesis, however remote, is meticulously evaluated. She does this not because she regards the question as important — she freely admits that it is not — but because she finds it psychologically impossible to produce work that falls short of exhaustiveness. She applies precisely the same disproportionate effort to every question she encounters, regardless of its significance. It appears that she satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition, for she investigates the question in extraordinary depth, she does not rest content with incomplete evidence, and she considers every relevant alternative. Yet we should hesitate to call her intellectually thorough in the virtue-theoretic sense. Her behaviour is better described as a species of compulsiveness, an inability to calibrate effort to importance, and thoroughness so understood is not a virtue but a deficiency of practical judgement. The definition, it seems, requires supplementation with a condition requiring that the agent exercise appropriate discrimination in the allocation of investigative effort.

definition: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
conditions: The person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities
The person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence
The person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others
counterexample: A distinguished mathematician knows precisely the boundaries of her expertise, never overstates her competence, and is punctilious in acknowledging errors and crediting collaborators. Yet she regards her own limitations with serene indifference rather than with any sense that they call for further learning or deference to those who know more. When confronted with a question outside her expertise, she simply declares it uninteresting. She satisfies all three conditions yet displays a complacency that seems antithetical to genuine intellectual humility.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the agent regard the limits of them knowledge as reasons for further inquiry or appropriate deference, rather than treating them as matters of indifference.
passage: Intellectual humility is widely regarded as a central epistemic virtue, and one might be tempted to analyse it as an accurate self-assessment of the limits of one's knowledge conjoined with a refusal to overclaim and a readiness to acknowledge error. Such an analysis captures a good deal of what we ordinarily mean by humility in epistemic contexts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed without great difficulty. Imagine a mathematician of considerable distinction who possesses a remarkably accurate picture of the boundaries of her own competence. She knows precisely which areas of mathematics she commands and which lie beyond her training. She never ventures opinions on matters outside her expertise, and when she makes an error she acknowledges it promptly and without defensiveness. She is generous in crediting the contributions of her collaborators. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Yet when one examines her attitude more closely, one discovers that she regards the limits of her knowledge with a kind of serene unconcern. She does not treat them as deficiencies to be remedied or as reasons to seek guidance from those more competent; rather, she treats them as simple facts about herself, no more calling for a response than the colour of her eyes. When a colleague attempts to explain a result in an adjacent field, she waves him off, declaring that she has no interest in what she does not already know. It appears, then, that accurate self-assessment and a refusal to overclaim are insufficient for intellectual humility. What is additionally required is a certain orientation towards one's own limitations — a recognition that they constitute reasons for further inquiry or for deference to those whose knowledge exceeds one's own.

definition: A person is epistemically responsible if and only if (i) the person forms beliefs on the basis of adequate evidence and sound reasoning, (ii) the person takes reasonable steps to ensure that the person's belief-forming processes are reliable, and (iii) the person is willing to revise beliefs when presented with sufficient countervailing evidence.
conditions: The person forms beliefs on the basis of adequate evidence and sound reasoning
The person takes reasonable steps to ensure that the person's belief-forming processes are reliable
The person is willing to revise beliefs when presented with sufficient countervailing evidence
counterexample: A conscientious journalist always verifies facts through multiple sources, checks her reasoning for fallacies, and revises her views when new evidence emerges. Yet she confines these epistemic efforts entirely to her professional work. In her private life she forms beliefs on the basis of rumour, superstition, and unexamined prejudice, and sees no inconsistency in doing so. She satisfies all three conditions within one domain but not across the whole of her epistemic life, and it seems wrong to call her epistemically responsible simpliciter.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the responsible belief-forming dispositions be exercised across the full range of the agent's epistemic activities, not merely within a circumscribed professional domain.
passage: Epistemic responsibility is ordinarily understood as a matter of forming beliefs with due care — attending to evidence, ensuring the reliability of one's methods, and remaining open to revision. One might formalise this intuition by requiring that the agent form beliefs on the basis of adequate evidence, take steps to ensure the reliability of her processes, and stand ready to revise when countervailing evidence is presented. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis involves a journalist who is renowned for the rigour of her investigative work. Every claim she publishes is supported by multiple independent sources; she subjects her own reasoning to careful scrutiny for bias and fallacy; and she has on numerous occasions retracted or corrected published claims in response to new evidence. Within the domain of her professional practice, she satisfies all three conditions admirably. Yet in her private life she is a different person altogether. She believes in astrological predictions, accepts the testimony of friends without question, and forms political opinions on the basis of whatever she happens to have heard most recently, without any attempt at verification. When challenged on this discrepancy, she replies that epistemic standards are a feature of professional life and have no bearing on one's personal beliefs. It appears, then, that the proposed definition is too permissive, for it would allow us to attribute epistemic responsibility to a person who exercises the relevant dispositions only within a narrow domain. What seems to be required in addition is that the responsible dispositions extend across the whole of the agent's epistemic life, such that she does not treat the demands of evidence and reasoning as confined to particular contexts.

definition: A belief is apt, in the reliabilist sense associated with Sosa, if and only if (i) the belief is true, (ii) the belief is produced by a cognitive competence of the agent, and (iii) the belief is true because it is produced by that competence — that is, the truth of the belief is attributable to the exercise of the competence rather than to luck.
conditions: The belief is true
The belief is produced by a cognitive competence of the agent
The belief is true because it is produced by that competence rather than to luck
counterexample: An experienced ornithologist glances at a bird in flight and, exercising her well-honed perceptual competence, judges it to be a peregrine falcon. Her belief is true, and it is produced by a genuine competence. Yet unbeknownst to her, the bird she observes is in fact a cleverly constructed mechanical decoy; the real peregrine falcon is perched out of sight directly behind the decoy and would have been visible a moment later. Her belief is true and competence-produced, and the truth is attributable to her competence in a superficial sense, yet the alignment between competence and truth seems accidental in a way the definition does not capture.
missing_condition: The analysis does not adequately specify the manner in which the success must be attributable to the competence, leaving room for cases in which the competence produces a true belief but the particular route from competence to truth is fortuitous.
passage: The reliabilist virtue epistemology associated with Sosa proposes that a belief merits the honorific 'apt' when its truth is attributable to the exercise of a cognitive competence rather than to mere fortune. The analysis requires three conditions: the belief must be true, it must issue from a genuine competence, and its truth must be because of that competence. One might suppose that this tripartite condition suffices to exclude epistemic luck of every relevant variety. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed as follows. An experienced ornithologist, walking along a coastal path, catches a brief glimpse of a bird in flight and, drawing upon decades of field experience, judges it to be a peregrine falcon. Her belief is true: there is indeed a peregrine falcon in precisely the location at which she looked. Moreover, her belief is produced by a genuine cognitive competence — her perceptual skill in identifying raptors at a distance. Yet the bird she actually observed was not a peregrine falcon at all but a remarkably realistic mechanical decoy placed there by a wildlife photographer. The real peregrine falcon happened to be perched out of sight directly behind the decoy, and it was this bird, not the one she saw, which made her belief true. It appears that the truth of her belief is in some sense attributable to her competence, for had she not possessed the relevant skill she would not have formed the belief at all. Yet the particular path from competence to truth involves a coincidence that seems to undermine the aptness of the belief. The definition, it appears, requires a more precise specification of the manner in which success must be attributable to competence.

definition: A person possesses an epistemic virtue in the responsibilist sense associated with Zagzebski if and only if (i) the person possesses a stable character trait that reliably leads to the acquisition of true beliefs, (ii) the trait is motivated by a love of truth and a desire for genuine understanding, and (iii) the person exercises this trait in a manner that is praiseworthy and reflective of good epistemic character.
conditions: The person possesses a stable character trait that reliably leads to the acquisition of true beliefs
The trait is motivated by a love of truth and a desire for genuine understanding
The person exercises this trait in a manner that is praiseworthy and reflective of good epistemic character
counterexample: A historian possesses an extraordinary attentiveness to archival detail, driven entirely by a love of truth and a desire for genuine understanding of the past. This trait reliably leads her to acquire true beliefs about historical events, and her exercise of it is universally praised. Yet she directs this trait exclusively towards questions that confirm her existing theoretical commitments, systematically neglecting archives that might yield disconfirming evidence. She satisfies all three conditions but deploys her virtue selectively in a manner that seems epistemically vicious rather than virtuous.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the trait be exercised impartially across the full range of relevant evidence, rather than being directed selectively towards truth-conducive inquiries that happen to confirm the agent's prior commitments.
passage: Zagzebski's responsibilist account of epistemic virtue locates the source of epistemic merit not in the mere reliability of a cognitive process but in the motivational structure of the agent. On this view, a trait counts as an epistemic virtue only if it is animated by a love of truth and exercised in a praiseworthy manner.
