One might formalise this by requiring that the trait be stable, truth-conducive, motivated by the right ends, and praiseworthy in its exercise. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis proceeds as follows. Consider a historian who possesses an extraordinary capacity for close attention to archival documents. Her attentiveness is a stable trait, deeply rooted in her character, and it reliably leads her to discover truths about the past that other scholars have overlooked. She is motivated entirely by a love of truth and a desire for genuine understanding; there is nothing mercenary or self-serving in her inquiries. Her exercise of this trait is universally praised by her peers, who regard her as a model of scholarly conscientiousness. Yet upon closer examination one discovers a curious pattern in her research: she directs her formidable attentiveness exclusively towards archives and sources that are likely to confirm her existing theoretical commitments. When colleagues point her towards materials that might disconfirm her views, she declines to examine them, not from laziness but from a sincere conviction that they are unlikely to contain anything of value. It appears, then, that she satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition and yet her trait, as actually exercised, functions as an instrument of confirmation bias rather than of genuine inquiry. The definition, it seems, must be supplemented with a requirement that the virtuous trait be exercised impartially across the full range of relevant evidence.

definition: A person is dogmatic, in the epistemically vicious sense, if and only if (i) the person holds certain beliefs with a degree of conviction that is disproportionate to the available evidence, (ii) the person refuses to revise these beliefs in the face of compelling countervailing evidence, and (iii) the person's refusal to revise is not the result of having carefully weighed the evidence but of an unwillingness to entertain the possibility that the person may be wrong.
conditions: The person holds certain beliefs with a degree of conviction disproportionate to the available evidence
The person refuses to revise these beliefs in the face of compelling countervailing evidence
The person's refusal to revise is not the result of having carefully weighed the evidence but of an unwillingness to entertain the possibility of being wrong
counterexample: A logician holds with absolute conviction that the law of non-contradiction is true. When a proponent of dialetheism presents arguments for the existence of true contradictions, she refuses to revise her belief. Her conviction might be called disproportionate to the evidence in the sense that she cannot produce a non-circular proof of the law, and her refusal stems not from careful weighing of the dialetheist's arguments but from a deep unwillingness to entertain the possibility that she is wrong. She satisfies all three conditions, yet it seems perverse to call her dogmatic, for there may be beliefs whose abandonment would undermine the very framework within which evidence is assessed.
missing_condition: The definition does not account for the possibility that certain foundational beliefs may rationally resist revision despite satisfying the formal conditions for dogmatism, because their rejection would undermine the coherence of the evidential framework itself.
passage: Dogmatism is widely regarded as an epistemic vice, and one might analyse it as the holding of beliefs with disproportionate conviction, coupled with a refusal to revise them that stems not from a considered judgement but from psychological rigidity. Such an analysis captures the paradigmatic cases: the ideologue who clings to a political doctrine in the face of overwhelming contrary evidence, the conspiracy theorist who treats every refutation as further confirmation. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by attending to a rather different sort of belief. Consider a logician who holds with unwavering conviction that the law of non-contradiction is true. A colleague who is sympathetic to dialetheism presents her with a series of ingenious arguments purporting to demonstrate the existence of true contradictions — the liar paradox, certain results in naive set theory, and so forth. She refuses to revise her belief in the law. Now, one might argue that her conviction is disproportionate to the evidence, inasmuch as she cannot produce a non-question-begging proof of the law of non-contradiction, and that her refusal stems not from a careful weighing of the dialetheist's arguments but from a deep-seated unwillingness to entertain the possibility that so fundamental a principle might be false. She thus appears to satisfy all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet it seems quite wrong to classify her as dogmatic in the epistemically vicious sense. There are, it appears, certain beliefs so fundamental to our epistemic framework that their abandonment would undermine the very standards by which evidence is assessed, and steadfast adherence to such beliefs is not dogmatism but a precondition of rational inquiry.

definition: A person is gullible if and only if (i) the person accepts testimony or claims without requiring adequate evidence or corroboration, (ii) the person fails to exercise appropriate critical scrutiny in evaluating the credibility of sources, and (iii) this pattern of acceptance is a stable disposition of the person rather than an isolated lapse.
conditions: The person accepts testimony or claims without requiring adequate evidence or corroboration
The person fails to exercise appropriate critical scrutiny in evaluating the credibility of sources
This pattern of acceptance is a stable disposition of the person rather than an isolated lapse
counterexample: A patient in a hospital accepts her doctor's diagnosis without requiring independent corroboration, does not exercise critical scrutiny of the doctor's credentials or reasoning — she simply trusts him — and this is her stable disposition whenever she interacts with medical professionals. She satisfies all three conditions, yet it would be peculiar to call her gullible. In the context of a well-functioning institution with appropriate checks, her unscrutinised acceptance of expert testimony appears to be a perfectly rational default rather than an epistemic vice.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify that the lack of critical scrutiny must be inappropriate given the epistemic context, leaving it unable to distinguish gullibility from the rational default trust that is warranted in institutional settings with reliable expert testimony.
passage: Gullibility is ordinarily counted among the epistemic vices, and one might analyse it as a stable disposition to accept claims without adequate evidence or critical evaluation of their sources. Such an analysis seems to capture the intuitive core of the concept: the person who believes whatever she reads in the tabloid press, the investor who entrusts his savings to every plausible-sounding scheme, the student who accepts every assertion of her tutor without question. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be found in contexts where uncritical acceptance appears not merely innocent but rational. Consider a patient who visits her general practitioner complaining of persistent headaches. The doctor, after conducting an examination, informs her that she has a tension-type headache and prescribes a mild analgesic. She accepts this diagnosis without seeking a second opinion, without examining the doctor's medical school transcripts, and without conducting her own research into the differential diagnosis of headaches. This pattern of unscrutinised acceptance is, moreover, a stable disposition: she invariably trusts the judgement of medical professionals. She thus satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet it would be manifestly absurd to call her gullible. In the context of a well-functioning medical system with institutional safeguards — licensing requirements, peer review, malpractice liability — her default trust in expert testimony is not a vice but a rational and indeed epistemically efficient strategy. The definition, it appears, must be supplemented with a condition specifying that the absence of critical scrutiny be inappropriate relative to the epistemic context in which the testimony is received.

definition: A person is closed-minded if and only if (i) the person systematically avoids exposure to views, arguments, or evidence that conflict with the person's existing beliefs, (ii) the person is unwilling to engage seriously with such material even when it is presented, and (iii) this avoidance and unwillingness are not the product of rational judgement about the material's quality but of a desire to protect existing beliefs from challenge.
conditions: The person systematically avoids exposure to views, arguments, or evidence that conflict with existing beliefs
The person is unwilling to engage seriously with such material even when it is presented
This avoidance and unwillingness are not the product of rational judgement about the material's quality but of a desire to protect existing beliefs from challenge
counterexample: A theoretical physicist declines to read papers advocating perpetual motion machines and refuses to engage with their authors at conferences. She systematically avoids exposure to these views and is unwilling to engage with them when presented. Her avoidance is not based on a detailed rational assessment of each paper's quality — she dismisses them categorically without reading them — but rather on a prior conviction that the second law of thermodynamics is inviolable. She satisfies all three conditions, yet we should not wish to call her closed-minded; her categorical refusal seems an entirely reasonable allocation of finite epistemic resources.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between avoidance motivated by an unreasonable desire to protect cherished beliefs and avoidance motivated by a well-founded prior assessment that an entire class of claims is not worth engaging with, given limited epistemic resources.
passage: Closed-mindedness is generally reckoned a serious epistemic vice, and an analysis of it might proceed by identifying three conditions: systematic avoidance of conflicting views, unwillingness to engage with them when presented, and a motivation rooted not in rational assessment but in a desire to shield one's beliefs from challenge. This analysis appears to capture the standard cases — the partisan who reads only newspapers sympathetic to his cause, the religious fundamentalist who refuses to consider evolutionary biology. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by considering the epistemic practices of a theoretical physicist of acknowledged competence. She categorically refuses to read papers advocating the possibility of perpetual motion machines. She does not examine them individually and find them wanting; she dismisses the entire class of claims without engagement. When a colleague presses a particularly sophisticated version of such a paper upon her at a conference, she declines to discuss it. Now, one might argue that her avoidance is not the product of a detailed rational assessment of each paper's quality — she has not, after all, read them — but rather of an antecedent conviction that the second law of thermodynamics is true and that any paper purporting to demonstrate its violation is thereby refuted. She thus appears to satisfy all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet it seems quite wrong to describe her as closed-minded. Given the overwhelming evidential support for the second law and the finite character of any scholar's time and attention, her categorical refusal to engage with a class of claims she has excellent reason to regard as mistaken seems not a vice but a perfectly rational economy of epistemic effort. The definition, it appears, must be refined to distinguish between avoidance that serves to protect fragile beliefs and avoidance that reflects a reasonable prior assessment of an entire domain of inquiry.

definition: A person is intellectually honest if and only if (i) the person does not intentionally misrepresent the person's own beliefs, evidence, or reasoning to others, (ii) the person does not deliberately conceal evidence that is relevant to the inquiry at hand, and (iii) the person presents the strongest available versions of opposing views rather than weakened or distorted versions.
conditions: The person does not intentionally misrepresent the person's own beliefs, evidence, or reasoning to others
The person does not deliberately conceal relevant evidence
The person presents the strongest available versions of opposing views rather than weakened or distorted versions
counterexample: A barrister defending a client in a criminal trial does not misrepresent her own beliefs — she genuinely believes her client is innocent. She does not conceal evidence, for she has disclosed everything required by law. And she presents the prosecution's case at its strongest before attempting to rebut it, as a matter of forensic strategy. She satisfies all three conditions, yet there is a sense in which her intellectual honesty is entirely instrumental: she exercises it because it serves the adversarial process, and she would abandon it immediately if doing so would better serve her client's interests. We should hesitate to attribute genuine intellectual honesty to a person whose honest practices are wholly contingent upon strategic advantage.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent's honest epistemic practices be motivated by a concern for truth or epistemic integrity rather than by strategic or instrumental considerations.
passage: Intellectual honesty is widely admired as a cardinal epistemic virtue, and one might analyse it as a tripartite disposition: not to misrepresent one's own views, not to conceal relevant evidence, and to present opposing views in their strongest form. Such an analysis seems to capture the essential features of honest inquiry. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be found in the domain of adversarial argumentation. Consider a barrister defending a client whom she genuinely believes to be innocent of the charge brought against him. In the course of her defence, she does not misrepresent her own beliefs — she sincerely holds that her client is not guilty. She does not conceal evidence, having disclosed everything that the rules of procedure require. And she takes care to present the prosecution's case at its strongest before attempting to dismantle it, for she has learnt that this forensic strategy is more effective with juries than attacking a straw man. She thus satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet one may observe that her intellectual honesty is entirely instrumental. She exercises these dispositions not from any concern for truth as such but because they happen to serve the adversarial interests of her client. If she believed that misrepresentation or concealment would secure an acquittal, she would not hesitate to adopt those strategies instead. It appears, then, that the definition captures only the outward behaviour of intellectual honesty and not its motivational core. Genuine intellectual honesty, one might argue, requires that the agent's honest practices be animated by a concern for truth or for the integrity of the epistemic enterprise, rather than by considerations of strategic advantage.

definition: A person displays intellectual perseverance if and only if (i) the person continues to pursue an inquiry in the face of significant obstacles, difficulties, or frustrations, (ii) the person does not abandon the inquiry prematurely when progress is slow or uncertain, and (iii) the person's persistence is directed towards the resolution of a genuine epistemic question rather than towards the mere continuation of activity.
conditions: The person continues to pursue an inquiry in the face of significant obstacles, difficulties, or frustrations
The person does not abandon the inquiry prematurely when progress is slow or uncertain
The person's persistence is directed towards the resolution of a genuine epistemic question rather than towards the mere continuation of activity
counterexample: A graduate student has been working on her doctoral thesis for nine years. She has encountered every conceivable obstacle — failed experiments, inconclusive data, the dissolution of her research group — and has persisted through all of them. Her inquiry is directed at a genuine and important question in organic chemistry. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet every qualified person who has examined her work agrees that her approach is fundamentally misconceived and cannot yield the answer she seeks. She has been told this repeatedly by her supervisors and examiners, and the evidence supports their judgement. Her persistence, far from being a virtue, has become an inability to recognise when an inquiry ought to be abandoned.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the agent's persistence be responsive to evidence about the viability of the chosen line of inquiry, such that they are capable of recognising when persistence has ceased to be rational.
passage: Intellectual perseverance is counted among the epistemic virtues, and one might analyse it as the disposition to continue pursuing an inquiry through difficulty, to resist the temptation of premature abandonment, and to direct one's efforts towards the resolution of a genuine epistemic question. Such an analysis seems to distinguish virtuous persistence from mere stubbornness and from aimless activity. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed as follows. Consider a graduate student in chemistry who has been working on her doctoral thesis for the better part of a decade. Her project concerns the synthesis of a novel class of compounds, and the question she is attempting to answer is both genuine and important. She has encountered a remarkable series of obstacles: experimental failures, funding difficulties, the departure of her principal supervisor, and years of inconclusive results. Through all of this she has persisted, driven by a sincere desire to resolve the question that motivates her research. She satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition admirably. Yet there is a difficulty. Every qualified chemist who has examined her work — her new supervisor, her external examiners, several distinguished colleagues — has concluded that her synthetic approach is fundamentally misconceived and that no amount of further effort along these lines can succeed. She has been informed of this assessment on multiple occasions and has been urged to adopt an alternative strategy or to redirect her research entirely. The evidence overwhelmingly supports their judgement. Yet she persists. It appears, then, that the definition fails to capture an essential feature of virtuous perseverance, namely that it must be tempered by a responsiveness to evidence about the viability of the inquiry itself. Persistence that is blind to compelling evidence of futility is not a virtue but a deficiency of judgement.

definition: A person possesses epistemic autonomy if and only if (i) the person forms beliefs on the basis of the person's own reasoning and evaluation of evidence rather than merely deferring to the judgement of others, (ii) the person is capable of critically assessing the testimony and arguments of others before accepting or rejecting them, and (iii) the person's belief-forming practices are not unduly influenced by social pressure, authority, or the desire for conformity.
conditions: The person forms beliefs on the basis of the person's own reasoning and evaluation of evidence rather than merely deferring to the judgement of others
The person is capable of critically assessing the testimony and arguments of others before accepting or rejecting them
The person's belief-forming practices are not unduly influenced by social pressure, authority, or the desire for conformity
counterexample: An amateur enthusiast with no formal training in epidemiology reads the primary literature on vaccine safety, evaluates the evidence according to his own reasoning, critically assesses the arguments of both proponents and sceptics, and arrives at his conclusions entirely free from social pressure. He satisfies all three conditions. Yet his own reasoning is demonstrably incompetent in this domain: he lacks the statistical training to evaluate clinical trials, misinterprets confidence intervals, and draws conclusions that every qualified epidemiologist would recognise as erroneous. His epistemic autonomy, so defined, leads him systematically away from truth and towards beliefs that would have been better formed by deferring to expert consensus.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the agent possess the relevant competence to evaluate the evidence independently, without which epistemic autonomy degenerates into uninformed self-reliance that is worse, from a truth-conducive standpoint, than judicious deference.
passage: Epistemic autonomy is widely valued as a mark of the mature and responsible epistemic agent, and one might analyse it as the disposition to form beliefs through one's own reasoning rather than through deference, to critically assess the testimony of others, and to resist undue social influence. Such an analysis has an appealing democratic flavour and seems to capture the Enlightenment ideal of thinking for oneself. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, is readily available. Consider an amateur who, having read several popular accounts of a scientific controversy, resolves to form his own view on the safety of a particular medical intervention. He obtains copies of the relevant clinical trials, reads them with great care, and evaluates the evidence according to his own best reasoning. He critically assesses the arguments advanced by both proponents and sceptics, weighing each on what he takes to be its merits. His conclusions are not influenced by social pressure or the desire to conform; indeed, they place him at odds with nearly everyone he knows. He satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet his own reasoning is demonstrably incompetent in this domain. He lacks the statistical training necessary to interpret the studies he has read. He misunderstands the concept of a confidence interval, confuses relative and absolute risk reduction, and draws inferences that no qualified researcher would endorse. His beliefs, formed with admirable independence, are systematically false, and he would have arrived at truer beliefs had he simply deferred to the expert consensus. It appears, then, that the definition captures only the formal structure of epistemic autonomy and not its substance. Genuine autonomy, one might argue, requires not merely that one reason for oneself but that one possess the competence to do so fruitfully in the domain in question.

definition: Epistemic deference is appropriate if and only if (i) the person to whom one defers possesses genuinely superior knowledge or expertise in the relevant domain, (ii) the deferring agent has good reason to believe that the expert's judgement is reliable, and (iii) the deferring agent lacks the capacity or resources to evaluate the matter independently to an adequate standard.
conditions: The person to whom one defers possesses genuinely superior knowledge or expertise in the relevant domain
The deferring agent has good reason to believe that the expert's judgement is reliable
The deferring agent lacks the capacity or resources to evaluate the matter independently to an adequate standard
counterexample: A competent statistician consults a senior colleague about the appropriate method for analysing a complex dataset. The colleague possesses genuinely superior expertise, the statistician has good reason to trust her judgement, and the statistician lacks the specialised knowledge needed to resolve the question independently. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the colleague's recommendation is influenced by a financial interest in a software product that implements the method she recommends, a fact the statistician does not know. The deference is inappropriate despite satisfying all three conditions, because the expert's judgement is compromised by a conflict of interest that the deferring agent cannot detect.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the expert's judgement not be compromised by conflicts of interest or other factors that would undermine its reliability, independently of whether the deferring agent has grounds to suspect such compromise.
passage: The question of when it is appropriate to defer to the epistemic authority of another is of considerable practical importance, and one might analyse appropriate deference as requiring that the expert possess genuine expertise, that the deferring agent have reason to trust the expert, and that the agent lack the capacity to evaluate the matter independently. Such an analysis seems to capture the standard cases: the layperson who defers to her physician on a medical question, the citizen who defers to the climatologist on matters of climate science. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, arises when the expert's judgement is compromised by factors unknown to the deferring agent. Consider a statistician of moderate ability who encounters a methodological question that exceeds her competence. She consults a senior colleague who is widely recognised as one of the foremost authorities in the relevant area of statistical methodology. She has every reason to trust her colleague's judgement: the colleague has an impeccable publication record, has served on numerous editorial boards, and has never, to the statistician's knowledge, given unreliable advice. The statistician herself lacks the specialised knowledge required to resolve the question independently. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the colleague's recommendation is influenced, consciously or otherwise, by a financial interest in a commercial software product that implements the method she recommends. This conflict of interest is not disclosed and is not apparent from the recommendation itself. The statistician, having no grounds for suspicion, follows the advice and employs a method that is, in fact, inferior to an available alternative. It appears, then, that the definition is insufficient, for it does not require that the expert's judgement be uncompromised by conflicts of interest or other corrupting factors. Appropriate deference, one might argue, requires not merely that the deferring agent have reason to trust but that the expert be in fact trustworthy.

definition: An inquiry is virtuous if and only if (i) it is directed towards the acquisition of truth or understanding concerning a genuine question, (ii) it is conducted by means of methods that are appropriate to the subject-matter, and (iii) the inquirer is motivated by a sincere desire for knowledge rather than by the wish to confirm a predetermined conclusion.
conditions: The inquiry is directed towards the acquisition of truth or understanding concerning a genuine question
It is conducted by means of methods that are appropriate to the subject-matter
The inquirer is motivated by a sincere desire for knowledge rather than by the wish to confirm a predetermined conclusion
counterexample: A sociologist undertakes a study of income inequality in a particular region. The question is genuine, her statistical methods are impeccable, and she is sincerely motivated by a desire for knowledge. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet she conducts her study in complete isolation, refusing to consult the existing literature, to seek peer review, or to discuss her findings with colleagues before publication. Her results, though obtained by sound methods from sincere motives, contain an elementary error that any reviewer would have caught. The inquiry satisfies the conditions for virtuousness yet is deficient in a way the definition does not capture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the inquiry be conducted in a manner that is appropriately responsive to the epistemic community, including the willingness to subject one's methods and conclusions to scrutiny by competent others.
passage: We may wish to distinguish virtuous inquiry from inquiry that is merely competent or merely sincere, and an analysis might proceed by requiring that the inquiry address a genuine question, employ appropriate methods, and be motivated by a love of knowledge rather than a desire for confirmation. Such an analysis captures much of what we admire in the practice of conscientious research. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by considering the role of the epistemic community in the conduct of inquiry. Suppose a sociologist sets out to investigate patterns of income inequality in a rural region. The question she addresses is both genuine and important. Her methods — a combination of quantitative analysis of census data and qualitative interviews — are well suited to the subject-matter and executed with considerable technical skill. She is motivated entirely by a sincere desire to understand the economic dynamics of the region; she has no ideological agenda and no predetermined conclusion she wishes to confirm. She thus satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet she conducts her entire study in complete intellectual isolation. She does not consult the existing literature on income inequality, does not seek feedback on her research design, does not submit her preliminary findings for peer review, and does not discuss her work with any colleague at any stage. The result is a study that, while methodologically sound in most respects, contains an elementary statistical error — a confusion of median and mean income — that any competent reviewer would have detected immediately. It appears, then, that the definition omits an important dimension of virtuous inquiry, namely the willingness to engage with the epistemic community and to submit one's work to the scrutiny of competent others.

definition: The aim of belief is truth if and only if (i) a belief is properly formed only when the agent's primary goal in forming it is to believe what is true, (ii) the agent regards the acquisition of false beliefs as a failure of the belief-forming process, and (iii) the agent is willing to sacrifice other desirable features of a belief — such as simplicity, comfort, or practical utility — when these conflict with truth.
conditions: A belief is properly formed only when the agent's primary goal in forming it is to believe what is true
The agent regards the acquisition of false beliefs as a failure of the belief-forming process
The agent is willing to sacrifice other desirable features of a belief when these conflict with truth
counterexample: A theoretical physicist aims at truth above all else in forming her beliefs about quantum mechanics. She regards false beliefs as failures and is willing to sacrifice simplicity and comfort for truth. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet the beliefs she arrives at, while true, consist of isolated, disconnected propositions — a catalogue of verified facts with no unifying structure. She has true beliefs about quantum phenomena but no understanding of quantum mechanics. It appears that an epistemic agent who aims solely at truth, as the definition requires, may achieve truth while missing something of at least equal epistemic value, namely understanding.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that understanding, which involves grasping the systematic connections among truths, may be an independent or even a primary aim of belief that is not reducible to the mere accumulation of true propositions.
passage: It is a venerable thesis in epistemology that the aim of belief is truth, and one might formalise this by requiring that beliefs be properly formed only when truth is the agent's primary goal, that false beliefs be regarded as failures, and that truth take precedence over competing desiderata such as simplicity or comfort. Such an analysis has a certain austere plausibility, and it coheres with the widely held view that belief is, as it were, answerable to the facts. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by considering the distinction between truth and understanding. Imagine a theoretical physicist who forms beliefs about quantum mechanics with the sole aim of believing what is true. She reads the experimental literature with extraordinary care, accepts only those propositions that are well supported by the evidence, and is prepared to abandon cherished theories when the facts require it. She regards every false belief as a failure and willingly sacrifices elegance and simplicity when they conflict with truth. She satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet the body of beliefs she has accumulated consists of a vast number of isolated, disconnected propositions — verified experimental results, confirmed predictions, established correlations — with no unifying theoretical structure. She knows a great many truths about quantum phenomena but has no understanding of quantum mechanics in any substantive sense. She cannot explain why the phenomena she has catalogued occur, cannot see the connections among them, and cannot deploy her knowledge to generate novel predictions. It appears, then, that an agent who aims solely at truth may achieve truth while missing something of at least equal epistemic value. Understanding, which consists in grasping the systematic connections among truths, seems to be an aim of belief that the proposed analysis entirely neglects.

definition: A hearer practises testimonial justice, in the sense articulated by Fricker, if and only if (i) the hearer assigns a degree of credibility to a speaker that is proportionate to the evidence of the speaker's competence and sincerity, (ii) the hearer does not allow prejudice based on the speaker's social identity to deflate or inflate the credibility assigned, and (iii) the hearer's credibility assessment is the product of a virtuous disposition rather than an isolated or accidental correct judgement.
conditions: The hearer assigns a degree of credibility to a speaker that is proportionate to the evidence of the speaker's competence and sincerity
The hearer does not allow prejudice based on the speaker's social identity to deflate or inflate the credibility assigned
The hearer's credibility assessment is the product of a virtuous disposition rather than an isolated or accidental correct judgement
counterexample: A hiring committee chair assigns credibility to every candidate's testimony strictly in proportion to evidence of competence, without any prejudice based on social identity, and does so as a matter of settled disposition. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet she employs a rigid scoring rubric that assigns credibility solely on the basis of formal credentials — degree, institution, publication count — and ignores contextual evidence such as the coherence and detail of a candidate's verbal account. A candidate from a disadvantaged background who has acquired genuine expertise through unconventional means receives a low credibility score not because of prejudice but because the credibility-assessment method is insensitive to legitimate forms of evidence. The result is testimonial injustice that the definition cannot diagnose.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the evidence used to assess competence and sincerity be itself sufficiently inclusive, so that legitimate but unconventional indicators of epistemic credibility are not systematically excluded by the assessment method.
passage: Fricker's influential account of testimonial justice requires that hearers assign credibility in proportion to evidence of competence and sincerity, free from identity-based prejudice, and as a matter of virtuous disposition rather than accident. One might formalise this in three conditions and suppose that their conjunction suffices for testimonial justice. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed as follows. Consider the chair of a hiring committee who prides herself on the fairness of her credibility assessments. She employs a rigorous scoring rubric that assigns credibility to each candidate's testimony on the basis of formal indicators of competence: the prestige of the candidate's doctoral institution, the number and quality of published papers, and the recommendations of established scholars. She applies this rubric without regard to the candidate's gender, race, or social background, and she does so consistently as a matter of settled disposition. She thus satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet the rubric she employs is sensitive only to conventional indicators of competence and is systematically blind to evidence that may be equally probative. A candidate who has acquired genuine expertise through self-directed study, professional experience outside the academy, or training at an under-resourced institution will receive a low credibility score not because of any prejudice on the chair's part but because the method of assessment excludes the kinds of evidence that would reveal the candidate's actual competence. The result is a form of testimonial injustice — a systematic deflation of credibility — that arises not from prejudice in the ordinary sense but from the narrowness of the evidential base employed in the credibility assessment. The definition, it appears, must be supplemented with a requirement that the evidence used to assess competence be itself sufficiently inclusive to capture the full range of legitimate indicators of epistemic credibility.

definition: Testimonial injustice occurs if and only if (i) a hearer assigns a deflated level of credibility to a speaker's testimony, (ii) this deflation is caused by a prejudice related to the speaker's social identity, and (iii) the speaker is thereby wronged in the speaker's capacity as a knower.
conditions: A hearer assigns a deflated level of credibility to a speaker's testimony
This deflation is caused by a prejudice related to the speaker's social identity
The speaker is thereby wronged in the speaker's capacity as a knower
counterexample: A witness in a legal proceeding gives accurate testimony about a traffic accident. The presiding magistrate harbours a prejudice against persons of the witness's ethnic group, which would ordinarily lead him to discount the testimony. However, upon learning that the witness is a senior physician at the local hospital, the magistrate's deference towards the medical profession — an irrelevant positive bias — precisely cancels out his ethnic prejudice, with the result that he assigns the witness exactly the degree of credibility that the testimony warrants. The witness receives the credibility she deserves, and so condition (i) is not satisfied. She is not wronged in her capacity as a knower. Yet an identity-based prejudice is operative and would, but for a countervailing irrelevant factor, have produced testimonial injustice. The definition cannot capture the wrong that is present in the structure of the magistrate's reasoning.
missing_condition: The definition fails to capture cases in which an identity-based prejudice is operative in the hearer's reasoning but is masked by a countervailing irrelevant factor — such as social deference towards the speaker's profession — such that the speaker receives appropriate credibility for the wrong reasons, and the underlying structure of injustice persists undiagnosed.
passage:
Testimonial injustice, as Fricker characterises it, involves a hearer's prejudicial deflation of a speaker's credibility, resulting in a wrong to the speaker in her capacity as a knower. One might formalise this by requiring that credibility be deflated, that the deflation be caused by identity-based prejudice, and that the speaker be thereby wronged as a knower. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering circumstances in which multiple biases interact.

Suppose a witness in a legal proceeding gives accurate testimony about a traffic accident she observed. The presiding magistrate harbours a prejudice against persons belonging to the witness's ethnic group — a prejudice that would ordinarily lead him to discount her testimony. However, upon learning that the witness is a senior physician at the local hospital, the magistrate finds himself deferring to her professional standing. This deference towards the medical profession is itself an irrelevant factor — the witness's competence as a physician has no bearing upon the reliability of her observation of a traffic accident — yet it happens to cancel out the magistrate's ethnic prejudice, with the result that he assigns her precisely the degree of credibility that her testimony warrants.

The witness is not, in the event, wronged in her capacity as a knower: she receives the credibility she deserves, her testimony is accepted, and justice is done in the particular case. None of the three conditions of the proposed definition is satisfied, for there is no deflation of credibility and hence no wrong. Yet it seems clear that something is epistemically amiss in the magistrate's reasoning. An identity-based prejudice is operative; it is merely masked by a countervailing irrelevant factor — deference to professional status. Had the witness been a person of lower social standing, or had the magistrate been less impressed by medical credentials, the prejudice would have produced a clear case of testimonial injustice. The definition, it appears, is unable to capture the wrong that inheres in the structure of the magistrate's credibility-assessment process, independently of whether that wrong is manifested in any particular case.

definition: Hermeneutical injustice occurs if and only if (i) there is a significant gap in the collective interpretive resources available to a social group, (ii) this gap is caused by the structural marginalisation of that group from the practices through which shared meanings are generated, and (iii) as a result of this gap, members of the group are unable to render some significant area of their social experience intelligible, either to themselves or to others.
conditions: There is a significant gap in the collective interpretive resources available to a social group
This gap is caused by the structural marginalisation of that group from the practices through which shared meanings are generated
As a result of this gap, members of the group are unable to render some significant area of their social experience intelligible, either to themselves or to others
counterexample: Consider a community of long-haul lorry drivers who suffer from a distinctive pattern of chronic fatigue, social isolation, and psychological distress arising from the specific conditions of their occupation. No medical or psychological vocabulary exists to describe this occupational syndrome as a unified phenomenon, and the drivers lack the conceptual resources to articulate their experience in a way that would render it legible to healthcare professionals. There is a genuine gap in the collective interpretive resources. Yet this gap is not caused by the structural marginalisation of lorry drivers from meaning-making practices; it arises simply because the syndrome has not yet been identified and studied. All three conditions are not satisfied, yet the epistemic harm suffered by the drivers — their inability to make their experience intelligible — is phenomenologically indistinguishable from paradigmatic hermeneutical injustice.
missing_condition: The definition does not account for cases in which a hermeneutical gap produces the same epistemic harm — rendering a group's experience unintelligible — without being caused by structural marginalisation, suggesting that the harm may be partially independent of its causal aetiology.
passage: Fricker's account of hermeneutical injustice identifies a distinctive form of epistemic harm that arises when gaps in collective interpretive resources prevent members of a marginalised group from making sense of their own experience. The analysis requires three conditions: a gap in interpretive resources, a causal connection between the gap and structural marginalisation, and a resulting inability to render significant experience intelligible. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering a group whose hermeneutical disadvantage arises from a different source. Imagine a community of long-haul lorry drivers who suffer from a distinctive pattern of chronic fatigue, social isolation, and psychological distress that is peculiar to the conditions of their occupation — the extended periods of solitary driving, the disruption of circadian rhythms, the separation from family and community. This syndrome is not recognised by any medical or psychological framework. No term exists for it, no diagnostic criteria have been developed, and the drivers themselves lack the conceptual resources to describe their experience as a unified phenomenon. When they visit their general practitioners, they can report only isolated symptoms — tiredness, low mood, difficulty sleeping — and they receive fragmented and inadequate treatment. There is, it appears, a genuine gap in the collective interpretive resources. Yet it is far from clear that this gap is caused by the structural marginalisation of lorry drivers from the practices through which shared meanings are generated. Lorry drivers are not, in the relevant sense, a marginalised group excluded from meaning-making institutions. The gap exists simply because the syndrome has not yet been identified and studied by the medical profession. The epistemic harm, however, is phenomenologically indistinguishable from that suffered in paradigmatic cases of hermeneutical injustice. The definition, it appears, ties the concept too closely to a particular causal aetiology and thereby fails to capture cases in which the same harm arises from different sources.

definition: Epistemic paternalism is justified if and only if (i) the paternalistic agent restricts or manipulates the epistemic environment of another person for the purpose of promoting that person's epistemic welfare, (ii) the person whose epistemic environment is restricted would not, in the absence of the intervention, have arrived at true beliefs or avoided false ones on the matter in question, and (iii) the restriction does not undermine the person's capacity for autonomous epistemic agency in the long term.
conditions: The paternalistic agent restricts or manipulates the epistemic environment of another person for the purpose of promoting that person's epistemic welfare
The person whose epistemic environment is restricted would not, in the absence of the intervention, have arrived at true beliefs or avoided false ones on the matter in question
The restriction does not undermine the person's capacity for autonomous epistemic agency in the long term
counterexample: A well-meaning science teacher, concerned that her pupils will be confused by reading about fringe theories, removes all books mentioning cold fusion from the school library and presents only the orthodox account of nuclear physics. Her aim is to promote her pupils' epistemic welfare; without the intervention, some pupils might have formed false beliefs about cold fusion; and since they are young and will later encounter a full range of views at university, her restriction does not undermine their long-term epistemic autonomy. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet by shielding them from heterodox claims, she deprives them of the opportunity to develop the critical skills needed to evaluate such claims, which seems to be a significant epistemic harm that the definition does not capture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the epistemic value of encountering false or misleading claims as a means of developing critical evaluation skills, such that the restriction may promote true belief in the short term while impeding the development of capacities that are essential to long-term epistemic flourishing.
passage: The question of whether epistemic paternalism can be justified has attracted considerable philosophical attention, and one might attempt to specify the conditions under which it is permissible by requiring that the intervention promote the epistemic welfare of its target, that the target would otherwise have formed false beliefs, and that the intervention not undermine long-term epistemic autonomy. Such an analysis seems to capture the intuitive justification for certain common practices — the teacher who simplifies a complex topic for young pupils, the editor who withholds graphic images that might distort public understanding of an event. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by attending to the epistemic value of exposure to error. Consider a conscientious science teacher who, alarmed by the prevalence of pseudoscientific claims in popular media, resolves to protect her pupils from confusion by removing all books that discuss fringe theories from the school library. She replaces them with carefully curated materials that present only the well-established scientific consensus. Her aim is entirely benign: she wishes to ensure that her pupils form true beliefs about physics, chemistry, and biology. Without her intervention, some pupils would undoubtedly have encountered claims about cold fusion, homoeopathy, or perpetual motion machines and might have been taken in by them. Since her pupils are young and will later have access to a full range of views at university, her restriction does not, on any plausible account, undermine their capacity for autonomous epistemic agency in the long term. She thus satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet by shielding her pupils from heterodox and erroneous claims, she deprives them of precisely the material they need in order to develop the critical skills required to evaluate such claims for themselves. The ability to recognise a bad argument presupposes some acquaintance with bad arguments, and a pupil who has never encountered a pseudoscientific claim is ill equipped to resist one when she finally does.

definition: A person displays intellectual generosity if and only if (i) the person is disposed to share knowledge, evidence, and intellectual resources with others, (ii) the person actively assists others in their epistemic endeavours without expectation of reciprocal benefit, and (iii) the person credits the intellectual contributions of others rather than appropriating them.
conditions: The person is disposed to share knowledge, evidence, and intellectual resources with others
The person actively assists others in their epistemic endeavours without expectation of reciprocal benefit
The person credits the intellectual contributions of others rather than appropriating them
counterexample: A senior professor freely shares her data, spends hours helping junior colleagues with their research, expects nothing in return, and is scrupulous in crediting the contributions of others. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet she shares her knowledge only with those she judges likely to agree with her theoretical framework and systematically withholds assistance from those pursuing rival approaches. Her generosity, though genuine within its scope, functions as a means of intellectual empire-building rather than as an impartial contribution to the collective epistemic enterprise.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that intellectual generosity be exercised impartially, extending to all inquirers regardless of whether they share the generous agent's theoretical commitments or intellectual allegiances.
passage: Intellectual generosity is often cited as a virtue of the academic life, and one might analyse it as a disposition to share knowledge freely, to assist others without expectation of return, and to credit the contributions of colleagues. Such an analysis captures the salient features of many admirable epistemic agents: the senior scholar who mentors junior colleagues, the researcher who makes her data freely available, the reviewer who provides constructive criticism without thought of personal advantage. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by attending to the selectivity with which generosity is sometimes exercised. Consider a professor of considerable eminence who is celebrated for her intellectual generosity. She shares her unpublished data with any colleague who requests it, devotes many hours each week to helping junior researchers design their studies and interpret their results, expects nothing in return, and is punctilious in crediting the ideas of others. She satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet upon closer examination one discovers that her generosity is not impartial. She extends it freely to those who work within her theoretical framework or who are likely to produce results congenial to her views, but she systematically withholds assistance from those who pursue rival approaches. When a junior colleague embarks upon a research programme that threatens to undermine her central theory, she does not share data, does not offer advice, and does not assist in any way. Her generosity, though genuine and admirable within its scope, functions as a mechanism for consolidating her intellectual influence rather than as a disinterested contribution to the progress of knowledge. It appears, then, that the definition must be supplemented with a requirement that intellectual generosity be exercised without regard to the theoretical allegiances of its beneficiaries.

definition: A person displays epistemic temperance if and only if (i) the person proportions the degree of confidence in a belief to the strength of the available evidence, (ii) the person withholds judgement on matters where the evidence is insufficient to warrant a determinate conclusion, and (iii) the person avoids both excessive credulity and excessive scepticism.
conditions: The person proportions the degree of confidence in a belief to the strength of the available evidence
The person withholds judgement on matters where the evidence is insufficient to warrant a determinate conclusion
The person avoids both excessive credulity and excessive scepticism
counterexample: A civil servant responsible for public health policy proportions her confidence to the evidence, withholds judgement where evidence is insufficient, and avoids both credulity and excessive scepticism. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet her temperance leads her to withhold judgement on whether a novel infectious disease poses a serious threat, precisely because the early evidence is ambiguous and insufficient for a determinate conclusion. By the time sufficient evidence accumulates to warrant a confident belief, the opportunity for effective intervention has passed. Her epistemic temperance, impeccable from a purely evidential standpoint, is a practical catastrophe.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for contexts in which the practical stakes of a question require that judgement be made under conditions of evidential insufficiency, so that epistemic temperance must be tempered by sensitivity to the costs of withholding belief.
passage: Epistemic temperance is a virtue that occupies a mean between the extremes of credulity and scepticism, and one might analyse it as the disposition to proportion confidence to evidence, to withhold judgement when evidence is lacking, and to avoid epistemic excess in either direction. Such an analysis has a venerable pedigree, echoing Locke's counsel that assent should be proportioned to the evidence. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by attending to the practical contexts in which epistemic temperance is exercised. Consider a civil servant in the department of public health who is responsible for formulating the government's response to a novel infectious disease. In the early weeks of the outbreak, the evidence concerning the disease's transmissibility, severity, and likely trajectory is fragmentary and contradictory. Some studies suggest a high fatality rate; others are more reassuring. The available data are insufficient to support a confident conclusion in either direction. The civil servant, exercising exemplary epistemic temperance, withholds judgement. She does not declare the disease a serious threat, for the evidence does not yet warrant such a conclusion, and she does not declare it benign, for the evidence does not warrant that conclusion either. She proportions her confidence precisely to the strength of the available evidence, which is to say that her confidence is low. She avoids both credulity and excessive scepticism. She satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet by the time the evidence has accumulated to a point sufficient to warrant a determinate judgement, several weeks have elapsed, the disease has spread widely, and the window for effective public health intervention has closed. Her epistemic temperance, though impeccable from a purely evidential standpoint, has proved a practical catastrophe. It appears that the definition must be supplemented with a sensitivity to the practical stakes of the question at hand, recognising that in certain contexts the cost of withholding judgement may exceed the cost of forming a belief on insufficient evidence.

definition: A true belief constitutes knowledge derived from epistemic virtue if and only if (i) the belief is true, (ii) the belief is formed through the exercise of an epistemic virtue of the agent, and (iii) the truth of the belief is not attributable to epistemic luck — that is, the belief would not easily have been false in nearby possible worlds.
conditions: The belief is true
The belief is formed through the exercise of an epistemic virtue of the agent
The truth of the belief is not attributable to epistemic luck — the belief would not easily have been false in nearby possible worlds
counterexample: An archaeologist, exercising exemplary intellectual thoroughness and interpretive skill, analyses a set of artefacts and concludes that the site was occupied in the twelfth century. Her belief is true. It is formed through genuine epistemic virtues. And in nearby possible worlds — worlds in which the artefacts are slightly different or her analysis slightly less careful — she would have reached a different and false conclusion, so it seems her belief could easily have been false. Yet the reason it could easily have been false is that the evidence was intrinsically ambiguous, not that some Gettier-like accident intervened. Her belief seems to be a genuine case of knowledge despite satisfying the luck condition, because the fragility of the belief reflects the difficulty of the subject-matter rather than any defect in her epistemic situation.
missing_condition: The definition's anti-luck condition does not distinguish between fragility that arises from the intrinsic difficulty of the subject-matter and fragility that arises from Gettier-type accidents, with the consequence that it excludes beliefs that should count as knowledge in epistemically challenging domains.
passage: The relationship between epistemic virtue and epistemic luck has been much discussed in recent epistemology, and one might attempt to specify the conditions under which a true belief formed through virtue constitutes knowledge by adding an anti-luck condition: the belief must not easily have been false in nearby possible worlds. Such an analysis is designed to handle Gettier cases, in which a true belief is formed through competent methods but is true only by accident. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by considering domains in which competent inquiry characteristically yields fragile conclusions. Consider an archaeologist of acknowledged expertise who is excavating a site of uncertain date. She analyses the pottery fragments, the stratigraphic layers, and the few surviving organic remains with exemplary thoroughness and interpretive skill. She concludes that the site was occupied during the twelfth century. Her belief is true: the site was indeed occupied during that period. It is formed through the exercise of genuine epistemic virtues — intellectual thoroughness, careful reasoning, attention to detail. Yet in nearby possible worlds — worlds in which one of the pottery fragments is slightly more weathered, or in which one stratigraphic layer is marginally less distinct — her analysis would have pointed to the thirteenth century, and her belief would have been false. Her true belief is, in the modal sense, fragile: it could easily have been false. The anti-luck condition thus appears to be violated. Yet it seems wrong to deny that she knows the site's date. The fragility of her belief reflects not a Gettier-type accident but the intrinsic difficulty of archaeological interpretation. In domains where evidence is inherently ambiguous and even the most competent inquiry yields conclusions that are sensitive to small variations in the data, the anti-luck condition threatens to exclude beliefs that we should wish to count as knowledge. The definition, it appears, must distinguish between fragility that arises from epistemic accident and fragility that is an unavoidable feature of the subject-matter.

definition: A person exercises intellectual charity (the principle of charity in interpretation) if and only if (i) the person interprets the statements and arguments of others in the strongest reasonable way consistent with what they have said, (ii) the person does not attribute to others positions that are weaker, less coherent, or more absurd than what their words actually support, and (iii) the person applies this interpretive generosity as a general policy rather than selectively.
conditions: The person interprets the statements and arguments of others in the strongest reasonable way consistent with what they have said
The person does not attribute to others positions that are weaker, less coherent, or more absurd than what their words actually support
The person applies this interpretive generosity as a general policy rather than selectively
counterexample: A philosophy lecturer interprets every student essay in the strongest possible way, never attributes weaker positions than the text supports, and applies this policy uniformly. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet her charitable interpretations are sometimes so strong that they bear no recognisable relation to what the student actually meant. When a student writes a confused paragraph gesturing vaguely at a point about free will, she reconstructs it as a sophisticated compatibilist argument the student has never heard of. The student receives high marks but learns nothing, for the feedback addresses an argument he did not make and could not have made. Her charity, though genuine and uniformly applied, has ceased to be interpretive in any meaningful sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the charitable interpretation remain constrained by a plausible account of what the speaker or writer actually intended, so that the interpretation strengthens the argument without departing from the communicative intentions of its author.
passage: The principle of charity in interpretation is widely regarded as an intellectual virtue, and one might analyse it as the disposition to interpret others' arguments in their strongest form, to refrain from attributing absurd positions, and to apply this generosity uniformly. Such an analysis captures the standard rationale for charitable interpretation: it promotes productive discourse, prevents the waste of time on straw men, and shows proper respect for one's interlocutors. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, concerns the limits of charitable reconstruction. Consider a philosophy lecturer who is known for her extraordinary generosity in interpreting student work. She reads every essay with the aim of finding the strongest possible argument consistent with what the student has written, never attributes a weaker position than the text can bear, and applies this policy uniformly to all students without exception. She satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet her interpretive charity sometimes produces results that are, in a certain sense, too good. When a student submits a muddled paragraph that gestures vaguely at some point about the relationship between determinism and moral responsibility, she reconstructs it as a subtle and original compatibilist argument of a kind the student has certainly never encountered and could not have intended. The student receives an excellent mark and glowing feedback, but the feedback addresses an argument that is entirely the lecturer's own creation. The student learns nothing from the exercise, for he cannot recognise his own thought in the interpretation offered. It appears, then, that the definition captures only the formal structure of charity and not the constraint that makes it genuinely interpretive. Charitable interpretation, one might argue, must remain anchored to a plausible account of what the speaker or writer actually intended, so that the reconstruction strengthens the argument without departing from the communicative purposes of its author.

definition: A person is epistemically conscientious if and only if (i) the person takes care to form beliefs in accordance with appropriate epistemic standards, (ii) the person monitors the person's own belief-forming processes for errors, biases, and lapses of attention, and (iii) the person feels a sense of obligation towards epistemic norms and is disposed to experience guilt or discomfort when the person falls short of them.
conditions: The person takes care to form beliefs in accordance with appropriate epistemic standards
The person monitors the person's own belief-forming processes for errors, biases, and lapses of attention
The person feels a sense of obligation towards epistemic norms and is disposed to experience guilt or discomfort when falling short of them
counterexample: A financial analyst takes great care to form beliefs about market trends in accordance with rigorous evidential standards. She continuously monitors her own reasoning for cognitive biases, keeping a detailed log of her predictions and their outcomes. She feels genuine guilt when she falls short of her epistemic standards, and this discomfort motivates her to improve. She satisfies all three conditions. Yet the epistemic standards to which she holds herself are those of her professional subculture, which systematically overweights short-term quantitative indicators and ignores qualitative evidence about structural economic conditions. Her conscientiousness is directed towards the wrong norms, and her scrupulous adherence to them leads her reliably to form false beliefs about long-term market trends.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the epistemic standards to which the agent holds themselves be themselves appropriate or truth-conducive, leaving room for conscientiousness directed towards defective norms that systematically produce false beliefs.
passage: Epistemic conscientiousness is often regarded as a foundational epistemic virtue, inasmuch as it disposes the agent to take the demands of epistemic norms seriously and to hold herself accountable when she fails to meet them. One might analyse it as a tripartite disposition: to form beliefs carefully, to monitor one's own cognitive processes, and to feel a sense of obligation towards epistemic standards. Such an analysis captures much of what we admire in the scrupulous inquirer. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, however, may be constructed by attending to the quality of the standards to which the agent holds herself. Consider a financial analyst who is renowned for the rigour of her work. She forms beliefs about market trends only after careful examination of the available data, subjects her own reasoning to continuous scrutiny for cognitive biases, and keeps a meticulous record of her past predictions and their outcomes so that she may learn from her errors. When she discovers that she has formed a belief carelessly or under the influence of a bias, she experiences genuine guilt and resolves to do better. She satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition admirably. Yet the epistemic standards she applies are those prevailing in her professional subculture, which privileges short-term quantitative indicators — price movements, trading volumes, technical patterns — and systematically disregards qualitative evidence about underlying economic structures, regulatory changes, and social conditions. Her conscientiousness, though entirely sincere, is directed towards norms that are themselves defective, and her scrupulous adherence to them leads her reliably to form false beliefs about long-term market developments. It appears, then, that the definition captures the form of epistemic conscientiousness but not its substance. Genuine conscientiousness requires not merely that the agent hold herself to standards but that the standards themselves be appropriate — that they be, in some relevant sense, truth-conducive and epistemically sound.
